# The Rust Reference

[Print this book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html "Print this book")[Git repository](https://github.com/rust-lang/reference/ "Git repository")

# Introduction

This book is the primary reference for the Rust programming language.

> Note
>
> For known bugs and omissions in this book, see our [GitHub issues](https://github.com/rust-lang/reference/issues). If you see a case where the compiler behavior and the text here do not agree, file an issue so we can think about which is correct.

## Rust releases

Rust has a new language release every six weeks.
The first stable release of the language was Rust 1.0.0, followed by Rust 1.1.0 and so on.
Tools (`rustc`, `cargo`, etc.) and documentation ( [Standard library](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/index.html), this book, etc.) are released with the language release.

The latest release of this book, matching the latest Rust version, can always be found at [https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/).
Prior versions can be found by adding the Rust version before the “reference” directory.
For example, the Reference for Rust 1.49.0 is located at [https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.49.0/reference/](https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.49.0/reference/).

## What _The Reference_ is not

This book does not serve as an introduction to the language.
Background familiarity with the language is assumed.
A separate [book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/index.html) is available to help acquire such background familiarity.

This book also does not serve as a reference to the [standard library](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/index.html) included in the language distribution.
Those libraries are documented separately by extracting documentation attributes from their source code.
Many of the features that one might expect to be language features are library features in Rust, so what you’re looking for may be there, not here.

Similarly, this book does not usually document the specifics of `rustc` as a tool or of Cargo.
`rustc` has its own [book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/index.html).
Cargo has a [book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/index.html) that contains a [reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/index.html).
There are a few pages such as [linkage](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#linkage) that still describe how `rustc` works.

This book also only serves as a reference to what is available in stable Rust.
For unstable features being worked on, see the [Unstable Book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/unstable-book/).

Rust compilers, including `rustc`, will perform optimizations.
The reference does not specify what optimizations are allowed or disallowed.
Instead, think of the compiled program as a black box.
You can only probe by running it, feeding it input and observing its output.
Everything that happens that way must conform to what the reference says.

## How to use this book

This book does not assume you are reading this book sequentially.
Each chapter generally can be read standalone, but will cross-link to other chapters for facets of the language they refer to, but do not discuss.

There are two main ways to read this document.

The first is to answer a specific question.
If you know which chapter answers that question, you can jump to that chapter in the table of contents.
Otherwise, you can press `s` or click the magnifying glass on the top bar to search for keywords related to your question.
For example, say you wanted to know when a temporary value created in a let statement is dropped.
If you didn’t already know that the [lifetime of temporaries](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#temporaries) is defined in the [expressions chapter](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expressions), you could search “temporary let” and the first search result will take you to that section.

The second is to generally improve your knowledge of a facet of the language.
In that case, just browse the table of contents until you see something you want to know more about, and just start reading.
If a link looks interesting, click it, and read about that section.

That said, there is no wrong way to read this book. Read it however you feel helps you best.

### Conventions

Like all technical books, this book has certain conventions in how it displays information.
These conventions are documented here.

- Statements that define a term contain that term in _italics_.
Whenever that term is used outside of that chapter, it is usually a link to the section that has this definition.

An _example term_ is an example of a term being defined.

- The main text describes the latest stable edition. Differences to previous editions are separated in edition blocks:



> 2018 Edition differences
>
> Before the 2018 edition, the behavior was this. As of the 2018 edition, the behavior is that.

- Notes that contain useful information about the state of the book or point out useful, but mostly out of scope, information are in note blocks.



> Note
>
> This is an example note.

- Example blocks show an example that demonstrates some rule or points out some interesting aspect. Some examples may have hidden lines which can be viewed by clicking the eye icon that appears when hovering or tapping the example.



> Example
>
> This is a code example.
>
> ```rust
>
> #![allow(unused)]
> fn main() {
> println!("hello world");
> }
> ```

- Warnings that show unsound behavior in the language or possibly confusing interactions of language features are in a special warning box.



> Warning
>
> This is an example warning.

- Code snippets inline in the text are inside `<code>` tags.

Longer code examples are in a syntax highlighted box that has controls for copying, executing, and showing hidden lines in the top right corner.


```rust

// This is a hidden line.
fn main() {
      println!("This is a code example");
}
```


All examples are written for the latest edition unless otherwise stated.

- The grammar and lexical productions are described in the [Notation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#notation) chapter.


[\[example.rule.label\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-example.rule.label "example.rule.label")

- Rule identifiers appear before each language rule enclosed in square brackets. These identifiers provide a way to refer to and link to a specific rule in the language ( [e.g.](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-example.rule.label)). The rule identifier uses periods to separate sections from most general to most specific ( [destructors.scope.nesting.function-body](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.function-body) for example). On narrow screens, the rule name will collapse to display `[*]`.

The rule name can be clicked to link to that rule.



> Warning
>
> The organization of the rules is currently in flux. For the time being, these identifier names are not stable between releases, and links to these rules may fail if they are changed. We intend to stabilize these once the organization has settled so that links to the rule names will not break between releases.

- Rules that have associated tests will include a `Tests` link below them (on narrow screens, the link is `[T]`). Clicking the link will pop up a list of tests, which can be clicked to view the test. For example, see [input.encoding.utf8](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-input.encoding.utf8).

Linking rules to tests is an ongoing effort. See the [Test summary](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#test-summary) chapter for an overview.


## Contributing

We welcome contributions of all kinds.

You can contribute to this book by opening an issue or sending a pull request to [the Rust Reference repository](https://github.com/rust-lang/reference/).
If this book does not answer your question, and you think its answer is in scope of it, please do not hesitate to [file an issue](https://github.com/rust-lang/reference/issues) or ask about it in the `t-lang/doc` stream on [Zulip](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/237824-t-lang.2Fdoc).
Knowing what people use this book for the most helps direct our attention to making those sections the best that they can be.
And of course, if you see anything that is wrong or is non-normative but not specifically called out as such, please also [file an issue](https://github.com/rust-lang/reference/issues).

[\[notation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-notation "notation")

# Notation

[\[notation.grammar\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-notation.grammar "notation.grammar")

## Grammar

[\[notation.grammar.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-notation.grammar.syntax "notation.grammar.syntax")

The following notations are used by the _Lexer_ and _Syntax_ grammar snippets:

| Notation | Examples | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CAPITAL | KW\_IF, INTEGER\_LITERAL | A token produced by the lexer |
| _ItalicCamelCase_ | _LetStatement_, _Item_ | A syntactical production |
| `string` | `x`, `while`, `*` | The exact character(s) |
| x? | `pub`? | An optional item |
| x\* | _OuterAttribute_\* | 0 or more of x |
| x+ | _MacroMatch_ + | 1 or more of x |
| xa..b | HEX\_DIGIT1..6 | a to b repetitions of x, exclusive of b |
| xa..=b | HEX\_DIGIT1..=5 | a to b repetitions of x, inclusive of b |
| xn:a..=b | `#`n:1..=255 | a to b repetitions of x (inclusive of b), with the count bound to the name n |
| xn | `#`n | x repeated the number of times bound to n by a previous labeled repetition |
| Rule1 Rule2 | `fn` _Name_ _Parameters_ | Sequence of rules in order |
| \| | `u8` \| `u16`, Block \| Item | Either one or another |
| ! | !COMMENT | Matches if the expression does not follow, without consuming any input |
| \[ \] | \[`b``B`\] | Any of the characters listed |
| \[ \- \] | \[`a`-`z`\] | Any of the characters in the range |
| ~\[ \] | ~\[`b``B`\] | Any characters, except those listed |
| ~`string` | ~`\n`, ~`*/` | Any characters, except this sequence |
| ( ) | (`,` _Parameter_)? | Groups items |
| ^ | `b'` ^ ASCII\_FOR\_CHAR | The rest of the sequence must match or parsing fails unconditionally ( [hard cut operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-hard-cut-operator)) |
| U+xxxx..xxxxxx | U+0060 | A single Unicode character |
| <text> | <any ASCII char except CR> | An English description of what should be matched |
| Rule suffix | IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD _except `crate`_ | A modification to the previous rule |
| // Comment. | // Single line comment. | A comment extending to the end of the line. |

Sequences have a higher precedence than `|` alternation.

[\[notation.grammar.cut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-notation.grammar.cut "notation.grammar.cut")

### The hard cut operator

The grammar uses ordered alternation: the parser tries alternatives left to right and takes the first that matches. If an alternative fails partway through a sequence, the parser normally backtracks and tries the next alternative. The cut operator (`^`) prevents this. Once every expression to the left of `^` in a sequence has matched, the rest of the sequence must match or parsing fails unconditionally.

Mizushima et al. introduced [cut operators](https://kmizu.github.io/papers/paste513-mizushima.pdf) to parsing expression grammars. In the PEG literature, a _soft cut_ prevents backtracking only within the immediately enclosing ordered choice — outer choices can still recover. A _hard cut_ prevents all backtracking past the cut point; failure is definitive. The `^` used in this grammar is a hard cut.

The hard cut operator is necessary because some tokens in Rust begin with a prefix that is itself a valid token. For example, `c"` begins a C string literal, but `c` alone is a valid identifier. Without the cut, if `c"\0"` failed to lex as a C string literal (because null bytes are not allowed in C strings), the parser could backtrack and lex it as two tokens: the identifier `c` and the string literal `"\0"`. The [cut after `c"`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.syntax) prevents this — once the opening delimiter is recognized, the parser cannot go back. The same reasoning applies to [byte literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.byte.syntax), [byte string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.syntax), [raw string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str-raw.syntax), and other literals with prefixes that are themselves valid tokens.

[\[notation.grammar.string-tables\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-notation.grammar.string-tables "notation.grammar.string-tables")

### String table productions

Some rules in the grammar — notably [unary operators](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#borrow-operators), [binary\\
operators](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#arithmetic-and-logical-binary-operators), and [keywords](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#keywords) — are given in a simplified form: as a listing
of printable strings. These cases form a subset of the rules regarding the
[token](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tokens) rule, and are assumed to be the result of a lexical-analysis
phase feeding the parser, driven by a DFA, operating over the disjunction of all such string table
entries.

When such a string in `monospace` font occurs inside the grammar,
it is an implicit reference to a single member of such a string table
production. See [tokens](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tokens) for more information.

[\[notation.grammar.visualizations\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-notation.grammar.visualizations "notation.grammar.visualizations")

### Grammar visualizations

Below each grammar block is a button to toggle the display of a [syntax diagram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syntax_diagram). A square element is a non-terminal rule, and a rounded rectangle is a terminal.

[\[input.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-input.intro "input.intro")

This chapter describes how a source file is interpreted as a sequence of tokens.

See [Crates and source files](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#crates-and-source-files) for a description of how programs are organised into files.

[\[input.encoding\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-input.encoding "input.encoding")

## Source encoding

[\[input.encoding.utf8\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-input.encoding.utf8 "input.encoding.utf8")

Tests

## Byte order mark removal

If the first character in the sequence is `U+FEFF` ( [BYTE ORDER MARK](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Byte_order_mark#UTF-8)), it is removed.

[\[input.crlf\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-input.crlf "input.crlf")

Tests

Tests with this rule:

- [tests/ui/json/json-bom-plus-crlf-multifile.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/json/json-bom-plus-crlf-multifile.rs)
- [tests/ui/json/json-bom-plus-crlf.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/json/json-bom-plus-crlf.rs)
- [tests/ui/lexer/lexer-crlf-line-endings-string-literal-doc-comment.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/lexer/lexer-crlf-line-endings-string-literal-doc-comment.rs)

## CRLF normalization

Each pair of characters `U+000D` (CR) immediately followed by `U+000A` (LF) is replaced by a single `U+000A` (LF). This happens once, not repeatedly, so after the normalization, there can still exist `U+000D` (CR) immediately followed by `U+000A` (LF) in the input (e.g. if the raw input contained “CR CR LF LF”).

Other occurrences of the character `U+000D` (CR) are left in place (they are treated as [whitespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#whitespace)).

[\[input.shebang\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-input.shebang "input.shebang")

- [tests/ui/parser/shebang/shebang-comment.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/parser/shebang/shebang-comment.rs)

## Shebang removal

[\[input.shebang.removal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-input.shebang.removal "input.shebang.removal")

If a [shebang](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#shebang) is present, it is removed from the input sequence (and is therefore ignored).

[\[input.tokenization\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-input.tokenization "input.tokenization")

## Tokenization

The resulting sequence of characters is then converted into tokens as described in the remainder of this chapter.

> Note
>
> The standard library [`include!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.include.html) macro applies the following transformations to the file it reads:
>
> - Byte order mark removal.
> - CRLF normalization.
> - Shebang removal when invoked in an item context (as opposed to expression or statement contexts).
>
> The [`include_str!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.include_str.html) and [`include_bytes!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.include_bytes.html) macros do not apply these transformations.

[\[shebang\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-shebang "shebang")

# Shebang

[\[shebang.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-shebang.intro "shebang.intro")

A _[shebang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shebang_(Unix))_ is an optional line that is typically used in Unix-like systems to specify an interpreter for executing the file.

> Example
>
> ```rust
>
> #!/usr/bin/env rustx
>
> fn main() {
>     println!("Hello!");
> }
> ```

[\[shebang.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-shebang.syntax "shebang.syntax")

**Lexer**

[SHEBANG](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SHEBANG) →

#! !( ( [WHITESPACE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WHITESPACE) \| [LINE\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LINE_COMMENT) \| [BLOCK\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT) )\*\[ )\
\
    ~[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF)\* ( [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF) \| [EOF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EOF) )\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[SHEBANG](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SHEBANG)\
#!\
not followed by[WHITESPACE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WHITESPACE) [LINE\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LINE_COMMENT) [BLOCK\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT)\
\[\
⚠️ with the exception of [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF) [EOF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EOF)\
\
[\[shebang.syntax-description\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-shebang.syntax-description "shebang.syntax-description")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/parser/shebang/multiline-attrib.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/parser/shebang/multiline-attrib.rs)\
- [tests/ui/parser/shebang/regular-attrib.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/parser/shebang/regular-attrib.rs)\
- [tests/ui/parser/shebang/shebang-and-attrib.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/parser/shebang/shebang-and-attrib.rs)\
- [tests/ui/parser/shebang/shebang-doc-comment.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/parser/shebang/shebang-doc-comment.rs)\
- [tests/ui/parser/shebang/sneaky-attrib.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/parser/shebang/sneaky-attrib.rs)\
\
The shebang starts with the characters `#!` and extends through the first `U+000A` (LF) or through EOF if no LF is present. If the `#!` characters are followed by `[` (ignoring any intervening [comments](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#comments) or [whitespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#whitespace)), the line is not considered a shebang (to avoid ambiguity with an [inner attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)).\
\
[\[shebang.position\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-shebang.position "shebang.position")\
\
The shebang may appear immediately at the start of the file or after the optional [byte order mark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Byte_order_mark#UTF-8).\
\
[\[lex.keywords\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords "lex.keywords")\
\
# [Keywords](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#keywords)\
\
Rust divides keywords into three categories:\
\
- [strict](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#strict-keywords)\
- [reserved](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#reserved-keywords)\
- [weak](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#weak-keywords)\
\
[\[lex.keywords.strict\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.strict "lex.keywords.strict")\
\
## [Strict keywords](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#strict-keywords)\
\
[\[lex.keywords.strict.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.strict.intro "lex.keywords.strict.intro")\
\
These keywords can only be used in their correct contexts. They cannot be used as the names of:\
\
- [Items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items)\
- [Variables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variables) and function parameters\
- Fields and [variants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations)\
- [Type parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-parameters)\
- Lifetime parameters or [loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels)\
- [Macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros) or [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)\
- [Macro placeholders](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example)\
- [Crates](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#crates-and-source-files)\
\
[\[lex.keywords.strict.list\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.strict.list "lex.keywords.strict.list")\
\
The following keywords are in all editions:\
\
- `_`\
- `as`\
- `async`\
- `await`\
- `break`\
- `const`\
- `continue`\
- `crate`\
- `dyn`\
- `else`\
- `enum`\
- `extern`\
- `false`\
- `fn`\
- `for`\
- `if`\
- `impl`\
- `in`\
- `let`\
- `loop`\
- `match`\
- `mod`\
- `move`\
- `mut`\
- `pub`\
- `ref`\
- `return`\
- `self`\
- `Self`\
- `static`\
- `struct`\
- `super`\
- `trait`\
- `true`\
- `type`\
- `unsafe`\
- `use`\
- `where`\
- `while`\
\
[\[lex.keywords.strict.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.strict.edition2018 "lex.keywords.strict.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> The following keywords were added in the 2018 edition:\
>\
> - `async`\
> - `await`\
> - `dyn`\
\
[\[lex.keywords.reserved\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.reserved "lex.keywords.reserved")\
\
## [Reserved keywords](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reserved-keywords)\
\
[\[lex.keywords.reserved.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.reserved.intro "lex.keywords.reserved.intro")\
\
These keywords aren’t used yet, but they are reserved for future use. They have the same restrictions as strict keywords. The reasoning behind this is to make current programs forward compatible with future versions of Rust by forbidding them to use these keywords.\
\
[\[lex.keywords.reserved.list\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.reserved.list "lex.keywords.reserved.list")\
\
- `abstract`\
- `become`\
- `box`\
- `do`\
- `final`\
- `gen`\
- `macro`\
- `override`\
- `priv`\
- `try`\
- `typeof`\
- `unsized`\
- `virtual`\
- `yield`\
\
[\[lex.keywords.reserved.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.reserved.edition2018 "lex.keywords.reserved.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> The `try` keyword was added as a reserved keyword in the 2018 edition.\
\
[\[lex.keywords.reserved.edition2024\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.reserved.edition2024 "lex.keywords.reserved.edition2024")\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> The `gen` keyword was added as a reserved keyword in the 2024 edition.\
\
[\[lex.keywords.weak\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.weak "lex.keywords.weak")\
\
## [Weak keywords](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#weak-keywords)\
\
[\[lex.keywords.weak.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.weak.intro "lex.keywords.weak.intro")\
\
These keywords have special meaning only in certain contexts. For example, it is possible to declare a variable or method with the name `union`.\
\
- `'static`\
- `macro_rules`\
- `raw`\
- `safe`\
- `union`\
\
[\[lex.keywords.weak.macro\_rules\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.weak.macro_rules "lex.keywords.weak.macro_rules")\
\
- `macro_rules` is used to create custom [macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros).\
\
[\[lex.keywords.weak.union\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.weak.union "lex.keywords.weak.union")\
\
- `union` is used to declare a [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions) and is only a keyword when used in a union declaration.\
\
[\[lex.keywords.weak.lifetime-static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.weak.lifetime-static "lex.keywords.weak.lifetime-static")\
\
- `'static` is used for the static lifetime and cannot be used as a [generic lifetime parameter](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters) or [loop label](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels)\
\
\
```compile_fail\
\
// error[E0262]: invalid lifetime parameter name: `'static`\
fn invalid_lifetime_parameter<'static>(s: &'static str) -> &'static str { s }\
```\
\
\
[\[lex.keywords.weak.safe\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.weak.safe "lex.keywords.weak.safe")\
\
- `safe` is used for functions and statics, which has meaning in [external blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks).\
\
[\[lex.keywords.weak.raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.weak.raw "lex.keywords.weak.raw")\
\
- `raw` is used for [raw borrow operators](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-borrow-operators), and is only a keyword when matching a raw borrow operator form (such as `&raw const expr` or `&raw mut expr`).\
\
[\[lex.keywords.weak.dyn.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.weak.dyn.edition2018 "lex.keywords.weak.dyn.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, [`dyn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects) is a keyword when used in a type position followed by a path that does not start with `::` or `<`, a lifetime, a question mark, a `for` keyword or an opening parenthesis.\
>\
> Beginning in the 2018 edition, `dyn` has been promoted to a strict keyword.\
\
[\[ident\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident "ident")\
\
# [Identifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#identifiers)\
\
[\[ident.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.syntax "ident.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD) → ( [XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Start) \| \_ ) [XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Continue)\*\
\
[XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Start) → <`XID_Start` defined by Unicode>\
\
[XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Continue) → <`XID_Continue` defined by Unicode>\
\
[RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_IDENTIFIER) → r#[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)\
\
[NON\_KEYWORD\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-NON_KEYWORD_IDENTIFIER) → [IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)except a [strict](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.strict) or [reserved](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.keywords.reserved) keyword\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER) → [NON\_KEYWORD\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-NON_KEYWORD_IDENTIFIER) \| [RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_IDENTIFIER)\
\
[RESERVED\_RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_RAW_IDENTIFIER) →\
\
r# ( \_ \| crate \| self \| Self \| super ) ![XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Continue)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)[XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Start)\
\_[XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Continue)\
\
[XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Start)\
\`XID\_Start\` defined by Unicode\
\
[XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Continue)\
\`XID\_Continue\` defined by Unicode\
\
[RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_IDENTIFIER)\
r#[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)\
\
[NON\_KEYWORD\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-NON_KEYWORD_IDENTIFIER)\
except a strict or reserved keyword [IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[NON\_KEYWORD\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-NON_KEYWORD_IDENTIFIER) [RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_IDENTIFIER)\
\
[RESERVED\_RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_RAW_IDENTIFIER)\
r#\
\_\
crate\
self\
Self\
super\
not followed by [XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Continue)\
\
[\[ident.unicode\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.unicode "ident.unicode")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui-fulldeps/lexer/unicode-version.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui-fulldeps/lexer/unicode-version.rs)\
\
Identifiers follow the specification in [Unicode Standard Annex #31](https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr31/tr31-43.html) for Unicode version 17.0, with the additions described below. Some examples of identifiers:\
\
- `foo`\
- `_identifier`\
- `r#true`\
- `Москва`\
- `東京`\
\
[\[ident.profile\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.profile "ident.profile")\
\
The profile used from UAX #31 is:\
\
- Start := [`XID_Start`](http://unicode.org/cldr/utility/list-unicodeset.jsp?a=%5B%3AXID_Start%3A%5D&abb=on&g=&i=), plus the underscore character (U+005F)\
- Continue := [`XID_Continue`](http://unicode.org/cldr/utility/list-unicodeset.jsp?a=%5B%3AXID_Continue%3A%5D&abb=on&g=&i=)\
- Medial := empty\
\
> Note\
>\
> Identifiers starting with an underscore are typically used to indicate an identifier that is intentionally unused, and will silence the unused warning in `rustc`.\
\
[\[ident.keyword\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.keyword "ident.keyword")\
\
Identifiers may not be a [strict](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#strict-keywords) or [reserved](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#reserved-keywords) keyword without the `r#` prefix described below in [raw identifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-identifiers).\
\
[\[ident.zero-width-chars\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.zero-width-chars "ident.zero-width-chars")\
\
Zero width non-joiner (ZWNJ U+200C) and zero width joiner (ZWJ U+200D) characters are not allowed in identifiers.\
\
[\[ident.ascii-limitations\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.ascii-limitations "ident.ascii-limitations")\
\
Identifiers are restricted to the ASCII subset of [`XID_Start`](http://unicode.org/cldr/utility/list-unicodeset.jsp?a=%5B%3AXID_Start%3A%5D&abb=on&g=&i=) and [`XID_Continue`](http://unicode.org/cldr/utility/list-unicodeset.jsp?a=%5B%3AXID_Continue%3A%5D&abb=on&g=&i=) in the following situations:\
\
- [`extern crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-crate-declarations) declarations (except the [AsClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AsClause) identifier)\
- External crate names referenced in a [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths)\
- [Module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules) names loaded from the filesystem without a [`path` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-path-attribute)\
- [`no_mangle`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_mangle-attribute) attributed items\
- Item names in [external blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks)\
\
[\[ident.normalization\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.normalization "ident.normalization")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui-fulldeps/lexer/unicode-version.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui-fulldeps/lexer/unicode-version.rs)\
\
## [Normalization](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#normalization)\
\
Identifiers are normalized using Normalization Form C (NFC) as defined in [Unicode Standard Annex #15](https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr15/tr15-57.html). Two identifiers are equal if their NFC forms are equal.\
\
[Procedural](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#procedural-macros) and [declarative](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example) macros receive normalized identifiers in their input.\
\
[\[ident.raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.raw "ident.raw")\
\
## [Raw identifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#raw-identifiers)\
\
[\[ident.raw.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.raw.intro "ident.raw.intro")\
\
A raw identifier is like a normal identifier, but prefixed by `r#`. (Note that the `r#` prefix is not included as part of the actual identifier.)\
\
[\[ident.raw.allowed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.raw.allowed "ident.raw.allowed")\
\
Unlike a normal identifier, a raw identifier may be any strict or reserved keyword except the ones listed above for `RAW_IDENTIFIER`.\
\
[\[ident.raw.reserved\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-ident.raw.reserved "ident.raw.reserved")\
\
It is an error to use the [RESERVED\_RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_RAW_IDENTIFIER) token.\
\
[\[comments\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments "comments")\
\
# [Comments](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#comments)\
\
[\[comments.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.syntax "comments.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-COMMENT) →\
\
[LINE\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LINE_COMMENT)\
\
    \| [INNER\_LINE\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INNER_LINE_DOC)\
\
    \| [OUTER\_LINE\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OUTER_LINE_DOC)\
\
    \| [INNER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INNER_BLOCK_DOC)\
\
    \| [OUTER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OUTER_BLOCK_DOC)\
\
    \| [BLOCK\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT)\
\
[LINE\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LINE_COMMENT) →\
\
// ( ~\[/! [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF)\] \| // ) ~[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF)\*\
\
    \| //[EOF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EOF)\
\
    \| //immediately followed by LF\
\
[BLOCK\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT) →\
\
/\* ^\
\
      ( [BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC) \| ( !\*/[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CHAR) ) )\*\
\
\*/\
\
[INNER\_LINE\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-INNER_LINE_DOC) →\
\
//! ^ [LINE\_DOC\_COMMENT\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LINE_DOC_COMMENT_CONTENT) ( [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF) \| [EOF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EOF) )\
\
[LINE\_DOC\_COMMENT\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LINE_DOC_COMMENT_CONTENT) → ( ![CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) ~[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF) )\*\
\
[INNER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-INNER_BLOCK_DOC) →\
\
/\*! ^ ( [BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC) \| [BLOCK\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_CHAR) )\*\*/\
\
[OUTER\_LINE\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OUTER_LINE_DOC) →\
\
/// ^ [LINE\_DOC\_COMMENT\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LINE_DOC_COMMENT_CONTENT) ( [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF) \| [EOF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EOF) )\
\
[OUTER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OUTER_BLOCK_DOC) →\
\
/\*\* !\[\*/\]\
\
      ^\
\
      ( ~\* \| [BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC) )\
\
      ( [BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC) \| [BLOCK\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_CHAR) )\*\
\
\*/\
\
[BLOCK\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_CHAR) → ( !( \*/ \| [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) ) [CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CHAR) )\
\
[BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC) →\
\
[INNER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INNER_BLOCK_DOC)\
\
    \| [OUTER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OUTER_BLOCK_DOC)\
\
    \| [BLOCK\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-COMMENT)[LINE\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LINE_COMMENT)[INNER\_LINE\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-INNER_LINE_DOC)[OUTER\_LINE\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OUTER_LINE_DOC)[INNER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-INNER_BLOCK_DOC)[OUTER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OUTER_BLOCK_DOC)[BLOCK\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT)\
\
[LINE\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LINE_COMMENT)\
//\
⚠️ with the exception of\
/\
![LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
//\
⚠️ with the exception of [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
//[EOF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EOF)\
immediately followed by LF\
//\
\
[BLOCK\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT)\
/\*\
no backtracking[BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC)\
not followed by\
\*/[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
\*/\
\
[INNER\_LINE\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INNER_LINE_DOC)\
//!\
no backtracking[LINE\_DOC\_COMMENT\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LINE_DOC_COMMENT_CONTENT)[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF) [EOF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EOF)\
\
[LINE\_DOC\_COMMENT\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LINE_DOC_COMMENT_CONTENT)\
not followed by [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR)\
⚠️ with the exception of [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
\
[INNER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INNER_BLOCK_DOC)\
/\*!\
no backtracking[BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC) [BLOCK\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_CHAR)\
\*/\
\
[OUTER\_LINE\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OUTER_LINE_DOC)\
///\
no backtracking[LINE\_DOC\_COMMENT\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LINE_DOC_COMMENT_CONTENT)[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF) [EOF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EOF)\
\
[OUTER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OUTER_BLOCK_DOC)\
/\*\*\
not followed by\
\*\
/\
no backtracking\
⚠️ with the exception of\
\*[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)[BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC)[BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC) [BLOCK\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_CHAR)\
\*/\
\
[BLOCK\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_CHAR)\
not followed by\
\*/[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
\
[BLOCK\_COMMENT\_OR\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BLOCK_COMMENT_OR_DOC)[INNER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-INNER_BLOCK_DOC)[OUTER\_BLOCK\_DOC](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OUTER_BLOCK_DOC)[BLOCK\_COMMENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BLOCK_COMMENT)\
\
[\[comments.normal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.normal "comments.normal")\
\
## [Non-doc comments](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#non-doc-comments)\
\
Comments follow the general C++ style of line (`//`) and block (`/* ... */`) comment forms. Nested block comments are supported.\
\
[\[comments.normal.tokenization\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.normal.tokenization "comments.normal.tokenization")\
\
Non-doc comments are interpreted as a form of whitespace.\
\
[\[comments.doc\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.doc "comments.doc")\
\
## [Doc comments](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#doc-comments)\
\
[\[comments.doc.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.doc.syntax "comments.doc.syntax")\
\
Line doc comments beginning with exactly _three_ slashes (`///`), and block doc comments (`/** ... */`), both outer doc comments, are interpreted as a special syntax for [`doc` attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/the-doc-attribute.html).\
\
[\[comments.doc.attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.doc.attributes "comments.doc.attributes")\
\
That is, they are equivalent to writing `#[doc="..."]` around the body of the comment, i.e., `/// Foo` turns into `#[doc=" Foo"]` and `/** Bar */` turns into `#[doc=" Bar "]`. They must therefore appear before something that accepts an outer attribute.\
\
[\[comments.doc.inner-syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.doc.inner-syntax "comments.doc.inner-syntax")\
\
Line comments beginning with `//!` and block comments `/*! ... */` are doc comments that apply to the parent of the comment, rather than the item that follows.\
\
[\[comments.doc.inner-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.doc.inner-attributes "comments.doc.inner-attributes")\
\
That is, they are equivalent to writing `#![doc="..."]` around the body of the comment. `//!` comments are usually used to document modules that occupy a source file.\
\
[\[comments.doc.bare-crs\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-comments.doc.bare-crs "comments.doc.bare-crs")\
\
The character `U+000D` (CR) is not allowed in doc comments.\
\
> Note\
>\
> It is conventional for doc comments to contain Markdown, as expected by `rustdoc`. However, the comment syntax does not respect any internal Markdown. ``/** `glob = "*/*.rs";` */`` terminates the comment at the first `*/`, and the remaining code would cause a syntax error. This slightly limits the content of block doc comments compared to line doc comments.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The sequence `U+000D` (CR) immediately followed by `U+000A` (LF) would have been previously transformed into a single `U+000A` (LF).\
\
## [Examples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#examples)\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
//! A doc comment that applies to the implicit anonymous module of this crate\
\
pub mod outer_module {\
\
    //!  - Inner line doc\
    //!! - Still an inner line doc (but with a bang at the beginning)\
\
    /*!  - Inner block doc */\
    /*!! - Still an inner block doc (but with a bang at the beginning) */\
\
    //   - Only a comment\
    ///  - Outer line doc (exactly 3 slashes)\
    //// - Only a comment\
\
    /*   - Only a comment */\
    /**  - Outer block doc (exactly) 2 asterisks */\
    /*** - Only a comment */\
\
    pub mod inner_module {}\
\
    pub mod nested_comments {\
        /* In Rust /* we can /* nest comments */ */ */\
\
        // All three types of block comments can contain or be nested inside\
        // any other type:\
\
        /*   /* */  /** */  /*! */  */\
        /*!  /* */  /** */  /*! */  */\
        /**  /* */  /** */  /*! */  */\
        pub mod dummy_item {}\
    }\
\
    pub mod degenerate_cases {\
        // empty inner line doc\
        //!\
\
        // empty inner block doc\
        /*!*/\
\
        // empty line comment\
        //\
\
        // empty outer line doc\
        ///\
\
        // empty block comment\
        /**/\
\
        pub mod dummy_item {}\
\
        // empty 2-asterisk block isn't a doc block, it is a block comment\
        /***/\
\
    }\
\
    /* The next one isn't allowed because outer doc comments\
       require an item that will receive the doc */\
\
    /// Where is my item?\
  mod boo {}\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.whitespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.whitespace "lex.whitespace")\
\
# [Whitespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#whitespace)\
\
[\[whitespace.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-whitespace.syntax "whitespace.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[WHITESPACE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WHITESPACE) →\
\
      U+0009 // Horizontal tab, `'\t'`\
\
    \| U+000A // Line feed, `'\n'`\
\
    \| U+000B // Vertical tab\
\
    \| U+000C // Form feed\
\
    \| U+000D // Carriage return, `'\r'`\
\
    \| U+0020 // Space, `' '`\
\
    \| U+0085 // Next line\
\
    \| U+200E // Left-to-right mark\
\
    \| U+200F // Right-to-left mark\
\
    \| U+2028 // Line separator\
\
    \| U+2029 // Paragraph separator\
\
[TAB](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TAB) → U+0009 // Horizontal tab, `'\t'`\
\
[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF) → U+000A // Line feed, `'\n'`\
\
[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR) → U+000D // Carriage return, `'\r'`\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[WHITESPACE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WHITESPACE)\
U+0009\
U+000A\
U+000B\
U+000C\
U+000D\
U+0020\
U+0085\
U+200E\
U+200F\
U+2028\
U+2029\
\
[TAB](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TAB)\
U+0009\
\
[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF)\
U+000A\
\
[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR)\
U+000D\
\
[\[lex.whitespace.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.whitespace.intro "lex.whitespace.intro")\
\
Whitespace is any non-empty string containing only characters that have the [`Pattern_White_Space`](https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr31/) Unicode property.\
\
[\[lex.whitespace.token-sep\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.whitespace.token-sep "lex.whitespace.token-sep")\
\
Rust is a “free-form” language, meaning that all forms of whitespace serve only to separate _tokens_ in the grammar, and have no semantic significance.\
\
[\[lex.whitespace.replacement\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.whitespace.replacement "lex.whitespace.replacement")\
\
A Rust program has identical meaning if each whitespace element is replaced with any other legal whitespace element, such as a single space character.\
\
[\[lex.token\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token "lex.token")\
\
# [Tokens](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tokens)\
\
[\[lex.token.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.syntax "lex.token.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[Token](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Token) →\
\
[RESERVED\_TOKEN](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_TOKEN)\
\
    \| [RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_IDENTIFIER)\
\
    \| [CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CHAR_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [BYTE\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-C_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [FLOAT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FLOAT_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [INTEGER\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INTEGER_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [LIFETIME\_TOKEN](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LIFETIME_TOKEN)\
\
    \| [PUNCTUATION](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PUNCTUATION)\
\
    \| [IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Token](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Token)[RESERVED\_TOKEN](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN)[RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_IDENTIFIER)[CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR_LITERAL)[STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_LITERAL)[RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_LITERAL)[BYTE\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_LITERAL)[BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)[C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-C_STRING_LITERAL)[RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL)[FLOAT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FLOAT_LITERAL)[INTEGER\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-INTEGER_LITERAL)[LIFETIME\_TOKEN](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LIFETIME_TOKEN)[PUNCTUATION](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PUNCTUATION)[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)\
\
[\[lex.token.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.intro "lex.token.intro")\
\
Tokens are primitive productions in the grammar defined by regular (non-recursive) languages. Rust source input can be broken down into the following kinds of tokens:\
\
- [Keywords](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#keywords)\
- [Identifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#identifiers)\
- [Literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literals)\
- [Lifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lifetimes-and-loop-labels)\
- [Punctuation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#punctuation)\
- [Delimiters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#delimiters)\
\
Within this documentation’s grammar, “simple” tokens are given in [string table production](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-table-productions) form, and appear in `monospace` font.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal "lex.token.literal")\
\
## [Literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#literals)\
\
Literals are tokens used in [literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literal-expressions).\
\
### [Examples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#examples-1)\
\
#### [Characters and strings](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#characters-and-strings)\
\
|  | Example | `#` sets[1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-nsets) | Characters | Escapes |\
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\
| [Character](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#character-literals) | `'H'` | 0 | All Unicode | [Quote](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#quote-escapes) & [ASCII](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#ascii-escapes) & [Unicode](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unicode-escapes) |\
| [String](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-literals) | `"hello"` | 0 | All Unicode | [Quote](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#quote-escapes) & [ASCII](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#ascii-escapes) & [Unicode](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unicode-escapes) |\
| [Raw string](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-string-literals) | `r#"hello"#` | <256 | All Unicode | `N/A` |\
| [Byte](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#byte-literals) | `b'H'` | 0 | All ASCII | [Quote](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#quote-escapes) & [Byte](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#byte-escapes) |\
| [Byte string](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#byte-string-literals) | `b"hello"` | 0 | All ASCII | [Quote](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#quote-escapes) & [Byte](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#byte-escapes) |\
| [Raw byte string](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-byte-string-literals) | `br#"hello"#` | <256 | All ASCII | `N/A` |\
| [C string](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#c-string-literals) | `c"hello"` | 0 | All Unicode | [Quote](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#quote-escapes) & [Byte](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#byte-escapes) & [Unicode](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unicode-escapes) |\
| [Raw C string](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-c-string-literals) | `cr#"hello"#` | <256 | All Unicode | `N/A` |\
\
#### [ASCII escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#ascii-escapes)\
\
|  | Name |\
| --- | --- |\
| `\x41` | 7-bit character code (exactly 2 hex digits, up to 0x7F) |\
| `\n` | Newline |\
| `\r` | Carriage return |\
| `\t` | Tab |\
| `\\` | Backslash |\
| `\0` | Null |\
\
#### [Byte escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#byte-escapes)\
\
|  | Name |\
| --- | --- |\
| `\x7F` | 8-bit character code (exactly 2 hex digits) |\
| `\n` | Newline |\
| `\r` | Carriage return |\
| `\t` | Tab |\
| `\\` | Backslash |\
| `\0` | Null |\
\
#### [Unicode escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unicode-escapes)\
\
|  | Name |\
| --- | --- |\
| `\u{7FFF}` | 24-bit Unicode character code (up to 6 hex digits) |\
\
#### [Quote escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#quote-escapes)\
\
|  | Name |\
| --- | --- |\
| `\'` | Single quote |\
| `\"` | Double quote |\
\
#### [Numbers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#numbers)\
\
| [Number literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#number-literals) [2](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-nl) | Example | Exponentiation |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| Decimal integer | `98_222` | `N/A` |\
| Hex integer | `0xff` | `N/A` |\
| Octal integer | `0o77` | `N/A` |\
| Binary integer | `0b1111_0000` | `N/A` |\
| Floating-point | `123.0E+77` | `Optional` |\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.suffix "lex.token.literal.suffix")\
\
#### [Suffixes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#suffixes)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.literal.suffix.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.literal.suffix.intro "lex.token.literal.literal.suffix.intro")\
\
A suffix is a sequence of characters following (without intervening whitespace) the primary part of a literal of the same form as a non-raw identifier or keyword.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.suffix.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.suffix.syntax "lex.token.literal.suffix.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX) →\
\
\_ ^ [XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Continue)+\
\
    \| [XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Start)[XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Continue)\*\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)\
\_\
no backtracking[XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Continue)[XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Start)[XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Continue)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.suffix.validity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.suffix.validity "lex.token.literal.suffix.validity")\
\
Any kind of literal (string, integer, etc.) with any suffix is valid as a token.\
\
A literal token with any suffix can be passed to a macro without producing an error. The macro itself will decide how to interpret such a token and whether to produce an error or not. In particular, the `literal` fragment specifier for by-example macros matches literal tokens with arbitrary suffixes.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! blackhole { ($tt:tt) => () }\
macro_rules! blackhole_lit { ($l:literal) => () }\
\
blackhole!("string"suffix); // OK\
blackhole_lit!(1suffix); // OK\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.suffix.parse\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.suffix.parse "lex.token.literal.suffix.parse")\
\
However, suffixes on literal tokens which are interpreted as literal expressions or patterns are restricted. Any suffixes are rejected on non-numeric literal tokens, and numeric literal tokens are accepted only with suffixes from the list below.\
\
| Integer | Floating-point |\
| --- | --- |\
| `u8`, `i8`, `u16`, `i16`, `u32`, `i32`, `u64`, `i64`, `u128`, `i128`, `usize`, `isize` | `f32`, `f64` |\
\
### [Character and string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#character-and-string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char "lex.token.literal.char")\
\
#### [Character literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#character-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char.syntax "lex.token.literal.char.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR_LITERAL) →\
\
'\
\
        ( ~\['\ [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF) [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) [TAB](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TAB)\] \| [QUOTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QUOTE_ESCAPE) \| [ASCII\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_ESCAPE) \| [UNICODE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UNICODE_ESCAPE) )\
\
'[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
[QUOTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QUOTE_ESCAPE) → \\' \| \\"\
\
[ASCII\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_ESCAPE) →\
\
\\x[OCT\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OCT_DIGIT)[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_DIGIT)\
\
    \| \\n \| \\r \| \\t \| \\\ \| \\0\
\
[UNICODE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UNICODE_ESCAPE) →\
\
\\u{ ( [HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_DIGIT)\_\* )1..=6valid hex char value}​[3](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-valid-hex-char)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CHAR_LITERAL)\
'\
⚠️ with the exception of\
'\
\[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF) [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR) [TAB](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TAB)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)[QUOTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QUOTE_ESCAPE) [ASCII\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_ESCAPE) [UNICODE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UNICODE_ESCAPE)\
'[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\
[QUOTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QUOTE_ESCAPE)\
\\'\
\\"\
\
[ASCII\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_ESCAPE)\
\\x[OCT\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OCT_DIGIT) [HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_DIGIT)\
\\n\
\\r\
\\t\
\\\\
\\0\
\
[UNICODE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UNICODE_ESCAPE)\
\\u{\
valid hex char value\
at most 5 more times[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_DIGIT)\
\_\
}\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char.intro "lex.token.literal.char.intro")\
\
A _character literal_ is a single Unicode character enclosed within two `U+0027` (single-quote) characters, with the exception of `U+0027` itself, which must be _escaped_ by a preceding `U+005C` character (`\`).\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str "lex.token.literal.str")\
\
#### [String literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str.syntax "lex.token.literal.str.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_LITERAL) →\
\
" (\
\
        ~\["\ [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR)\]\
\
      \| [QUOTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QUOTE_ESCAPE)\
\
      \| [ASCII\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_ESCAPE)\
\
      \| [UNICODE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UNICODE_ESCAPE)\
\
      \| [STRING\_CONTINUE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_CONTINUE)\
\
    )\*"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
[STRING\_CONTINUE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_CONTINUE) → \[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL)\
"\
⚠️ with the exception of\
"\
\[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)[QUOTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QUOTE_ESCAPE)[ASCII\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_ESCAPE)[UNICODE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UNICODE_ESCAPE)[STRING\_CONTINUE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_CONTINUE)\
"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\
[STRING\_CONTINUE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_CONTINUE)\
\[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str.intro "lex.token.literal.str.intro")\
\
A _string literal_ is a sequence of any Unicode characters enclosed within two `U+0022` (double-quote) characters, with the exception of `U+0022` itself, which must be _escaped_ by a preceding `U+005C` character (`\`).\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str.linefeed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str.linefeed "lex.token.literal.str.linefeed")\
\
Line-breaks, represented by the character `U+000A` (LF), are allowed in string literals. The character `U+000D` (CR) may not appear in a string literal. When an unescaped `U+005C` character (`\`) occurs immediately before a line break, the line break does not appear in the string represented by the token. See [String continuation escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-continuation-escapes) for details.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char-escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char-escape "lex.token.literal.char-escape")\
\
#### [Character escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#character-escapes)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char-escape.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char-escape.intro "lex.token.literal.char-escape.intro")\
\
Some additional _escapes_ are available in either character or non-raw string literals. An escape starts with a `U+005C` (`\`) and continues with one of the following forms:\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char-escape.ascii\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char-escape.ascii "lex.token.literal.char-escape.ascii")\
\
- A _7-bit code point escape_ starts with `U+0078` (`x`) and is followed by exactly two _hex digits_ with value up to `0x7F`. It denotes the ASCII character with value equal to the provided hex value. Higher values are not permitted because it is ambiguous whether they mean Unicode code points or byte values.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char-escape.unicode\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char-escape.unicode "lex.token.literal.char-escape.unicode")\
\
- A _24-bit code point escape_ starts with `U+0075` (`u`) and is followed by up to six _hex digits_ surrounded by braces `U+007B` (`{`) and `U+007D` (`}`). It denotes the Unicode code point equal to the provided hex value. The value must be a valid Unicode scalar value.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char-escape.whitespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char-escape.whitespace "lex.token.literal.char-escape.whitespace")\
\
- A _whitespace escape_ is one of the characters `U+006E` (`n`), `U+0072` (`r`), or `U+0074` (`t`), denoting the Unicode values `U+000A` (LF), `U+000D` (CR) or `U+0009` (HT) respectively.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char-escape.null\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char-escape.null "lex.token.literal.char-escape.null")\
\
- The _null escape_ is the character `U+0030` (`0`) and denotes the Unicode value `U+0000` (NUL).\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.char-escape.slash\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char-escape.slash "lex.token.literal.char-escape.slash")\
\
- The _backslash escape_ is the character `U+005C` (`\`) which must be escaped in order to denote itself.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str-raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str-raw "lex.token.literal.str-raw")\
\
#### [Raw string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#raw-string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str-raw.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str-raw.syntax "lex.token.literal.str-raw.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_LITERAL) →\
\
r" ^ [RAW\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_CONTENT)"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
    \| r#n:1..=255 ^ "[RAW\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)"#n[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
[RAW\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_CONTENT) → ( !" ~[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) )\*\
\
[RAW\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED) → ( !( "#n ) ~[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) )\*\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_LITERAL)\
r\
"\
no backtracking[RAW\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_CONTENT)\
"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
r\
repeat count n\
at most 254 more times\
#\
no backtracking\
"[RAW\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)\
"\
repeat exactly n times\
#[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\
[RAW\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_CONTENT)\
not followed by\
"\
⚠️ with the exception of [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
\
[RAW\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)\
not followed by\
"\
repeat exactly n times\
#\
⚠️ with the exception of [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str-raw.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str-raw.intro "lex.token.literal.str-raw.intro")\
\
Raw string literals do not process any escapes. They start with the character `U+0072` (`r`), followed by fewer than 256 of the character `U+0023` (`#`) and a `U+0022` (double-quote) character.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str-raw.body\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str-raw.body "lex.token.literal.str-raw.body")\
\
The _raw string body_ can contain any sequence of Unicode characters other than `U+000D` (CR). It is terminated only by another `U+0022` (double-quote) character, followed by the same number of `U+0023` (`#`) characters that preceded the opening `U+0022` (double-quote) character.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str-raw.content\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str-raw.content "lex.token.literal.str-raw.content")\
\
All Unicode characters contained in the raw string body represent themselves, the characters `U+0022` (double-quote) (except when followed by at least as many `U+0023` (`#`) characters as were used to start the raw string literal) or `U+005C` (`\`) do not have any special meaning.\
\
Examples for string literals:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
"foo"; r"foo";                     // foo\
"\"foo\""; r#""foo""#;             // "foo"\
\
"foo #\"# bar";\
r##"foo #"# bar"##;                // foo #"# bar\
\
"\x52"; "R"; r"R";                 // R\
"\\x52"; r"\x52";                  // \x52\
}\
```\
\
### [Byte and byte string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#byte-and-byte-string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.byte\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.byte "lex.token.byte")\
\
#### [Byte literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#byte-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.byte.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.byte.syntax "lex.token.byte.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[BYTE\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_LITERAL) →\
\
b' ^ ( [ASCII\_FOR\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_FOR_CHAR) \| [BYTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_ESCAPE) ) '[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
[ASCII\_FOR\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_FOR_CHAR) → !\['\ [LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LF) [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) [TAB](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TAB)\] [ASCII](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII)\
\
[BYTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_ESCAPE) →\
\
\\x[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_DIGIT)[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_DIGIT)\
\
    \| \\n \| \\r \| \\t \| \\\ \| \\0 \| \\' \| \\"\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
