'[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\
[ASCII\_FOR\_CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_FOR_CHAR)\
not followed by\
'\
\[LF](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LF) [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR) [TAB](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TAB)[ASCII](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII)\
\
[BYTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_ESCAPE)\
\\x[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_DIGIT) [HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_DIGIT)\
\\n\
\\r\
\\t\
\\\\
\\0\
\\'\
\\"\
\
[\[lex.token.byte.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.byte.intro "lex.token.byte.intro")\
\
A _byte literal_ is a single ASCII character (in the `U+0000` to `U+007F` range) or a single _escape_ preceded by the characters `U+0062` (`b`) and `U+0027` (single-quote), and followed by the character `U+0027`. If the character `U+0027` is present within the literal, it must be _escaped_ by a preceding `U+005C` (`\`) character. It is equivalent to a `u8` unsigned 8-bit integer _number literal_.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte "lex.token.str-byte")\
\
#### [Byte string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#byte-string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.syntax "lex.token.str-byte.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL) →\
\
b" ^ ( [ASCII\_FOR\_STRING](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_FOR_STRING) \| [BYTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_ESCAPE) \| [STRING\_CONTINUE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_CONTINUE) )\*"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
[ASCII\_FOR\_STRING](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_FOR_STRING) → !\["\ [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR)\] [ASCII](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)\
b"\
no backtracking[ASCII\_FOR\_STRING](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_FOR_STRING) [BYTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_ESCAPE) [STRING\_CONTINUE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_CONTINUE)\
"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\
[ASCII\_FOR\_STRING](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_FOR_STRING)\
not followed by\
"\
\[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR)[ASCII](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.intro "lex.token.str-byte.intro")\
\
A non-raw _byte string literal_ is a sequence of ASCII characters and _escapes_, preceded by the characters `U+0062` (`b`) and `U+0022` (double-quote), and followed by the character `U+0022`. If the character `U+0022` is present within the literal, it must be _escaped_ by a preceding `U+005C` (`\`) character. Alternatively, a byte string literal can be a _raw byte string literal_, defined below.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte.linefeed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.linefeed "lex.token.str-byte.linefeed")\
\
Line-breaks, represented by the character `U+000A` (LF), are allowed in byte string literals. The character `U+000D` (CR) may not appear in a byte string literal. When an unescaped `U+005C` character (`\`) occurs immediately before a line break, the line break does not appear in the string represented by the token. See [String continuation escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-continuation-escapes) for details.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte.escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.escape "lex.token.str-byte.escape")\
\
Some additional _escapes_ are available in either byte or non-raw byte string literals. An escape starts with a `U+005C` (`\`) and continues with one of the following forms:\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte.escape-byte\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.escape-byte "lex.token.str-byte.escape-byte")\
\
- A _byte escape_ escape starts with `U+0078` (`x`) and is followed by exactly two _hex digits_. It denotes the byte equal to the provided hex value.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte.escape-whitespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.escape-whitespace "lex.token.str-byte.escape-whitespace")\
\
- A _whitespace escape_ is one of the characters `U+006E` (`n`), `U+0072` (`r`), or `U+0074` (`t`), denoting the bytes values `0x0A` (ASCII LF), `0x0D` (ASCII CR) or `0x09` (ASCII HT) respectively.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte.escape-null\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.escape-null "lex.token.str-byte.escape-null")\
\
- The _null escape_ is the character `U+0030` (`0`) and denotes the byte value `0x00` (ASCII NUL).\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte.escape-slash\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte.escape-slash "lex.token.str-byte.escape-slash")\
\
- The _backslash escape_ is the character `U+005C` (`\`) which must be escaped in order to denote its ASCII encoding `0x5C`.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte-raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte-raw "lex.token.str-byte-raw")\
\
#### [Raw byte string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#raw-byte-string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte-raw.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte-raw.syntax "lex.token.str-byte-raw.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL) →\
\
br" ^ [RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_CONTENT)"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
    \| br#n:1..=255 ^ "[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)"#n[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_BYTE_STRING_CONTENT) → ( !"[ASCII\_FOR\_RAW](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_FOR_RAW) )\*\
\
[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_BYTE_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED) → ( !( "#n ) [ASCII\_FOR\_RAW](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_FOR_RAW) )\*\
\
[ASCII\_FOR\_RAW](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_FOR_RAW) → ![CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR)[ASCII](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)\
br\
"\
no backtracking[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_BYTE_STRING_CONTENT)\
"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
br\
repeat count n\
at most 254 more times\
#\
no backtracking\
"[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_BYTE_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)\
"\
repeat exactly n times\
#[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\
[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_CONTENT)\
not followed by\
"[ASCII\_FOR\_RAW](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_FOR_RAW)\
\
[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)\
not followed by\
"\
repeat exactly n times\
#[ASCII\_FOR\_RAW](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII_FOR_RAW)\
\
[ASCII\_FOR\_RAW](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ASCII_FOR_RAW)\
not followed by [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR)[ASCII](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ASCII)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte-raw.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte-raw.intro "lex.token.str-byte-raw.intro")\
\
Raw byte string literals do not process any escapes. They start with the character `U+0062` (`b`), followed by `U+0072` (`r`), followed by fewer than 256 of the character `U+0023` (`#`), and a `U+0022` (double-quote) character.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-byte-raw.body\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-byte-raw.body "lex.token.str-byte-raw.body")\
\
The _raw string body_ can contain any sequence of ASCII characters other than `U+000D` (CR). It is terminated only by another `U+0022` (double-quote) character, followed by the same number of `U+0023` (`#`) characters that preceded the opening `U+0022` (double-quote) character. A raw byte string literal can not contain any non-ASCII byte.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.str-byte-raw.content\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.str-byte-raw.content "lex.token.literal.str-byte-raw.content")\
\
All characters contained in the raw string body represent their ASCII encoding, the characters `U+0022` (double-quote) (except when followed by at least as many `U+0023` (`#`) characters as were used to start the raw string literal) or `U+005C` (`\`) do not have any special meaning.\
\
Examples for byte string literals:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
b"foo"; br"foo";                     // foo\
b"\"foo\""; br#""foo""#;             // "foo"\
\
b"foo #\"# bar";\
br##"foo #"# bar"##;                 // foo #"# bar\
\
b"\x52"; b"R"; br"R";                // R\
b"\\x52"; br"\x52";                  // \x52\
}\
```\
\
### [C string and raw C string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#c-string-and-raw-c-string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c "lex.token.str-c")\
\
#### [C string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#c-string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.syntax "lex.token.str-c.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-C_STRING_LITERAL) →\
\
c" ^ (\
\
        ~\["\ [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) [NUL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-NUL)\]\
\
      \| [BYTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_ESCAPE)except `\0` or `\x00`\
\
      \| [UNICODE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UNICODE_ESCAPE)except `\u{0}`, `\u{00}`, …, `\u{000000}`\
\
      \| [STRING\_CONTINUE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_CONTINUE)\
\
    )\*"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-C_STRING_LITERAL)\
c"\
no backtracking\
⚠️ with the exception of\
"\
\[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR) [NUL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-NUL)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
except \`\\0\` or \`\\x00\` [BYTE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_ESCAPE)\
except \`\\u{0}\`, \`\\u{00}\`, …, \`\\u{000000}\` [UNICODE\_ESCAPE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UNICODE_ESCAPE)[STRING\_CONTINUE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_CONTINUE)\
"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.intro "lex.token.str-c.intro")\
\
A _C string literal_ is a sequence of Unicode characters and _escapes_, preceded by the characters `U+0063` (`c`) and `U+0022` (double-quote), and followed by the character `U+0022`. If the character `U+0022` is present within the literal, it must be _escaped_ by a preceding `U+005C` (`\`) character. Alternatively, a C string literal can be a _raw C string literal_, defined below.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.null\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.null "lex.token.str-c.null")\
\
C strings are implicitly terminated by byte `0x00`, so the C string literal `c""` is equivalent to manually constructing a `&CStr` from the byte string literal `b"\x00"`. Other than the implicit terminator, byte `0x00` is not permitted within a C string.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.linefeed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.linefeed "lex.token.str-c.linefeed")\
\
Line-breaks, represented by the character `U+000A` (LF), are allowed in C string literals. The character `U+000D` (CR) may not appear in a C string literal. When an unescaped `U+005C` character (`\`) occurs immediately before a line break, the line break does not appear in the string represented by the token. See [String continuation escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-continuation-escapes) for details.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.escape "lex.token.str-c.escape")\
\
Some additional _escapes_ are available in non-raw C string literals. An escape starts with a `U+005C` (`\`) and continues with one of the following forms:\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.escape-byte\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.escape-byte "lex.token.str-c.escape-byte")\
\
- A _byte escape_ escape starts with `U+0078` (`x`) and is followed by exactly two _hex digits_. It denotes the byte equal to the provided hex value.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.escape-unicode\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.escape-unicode "lex.token.str-c.escape-unicode")\
\
- A _24-bit code point escape_ starts with `U+0075` (`u`) and is followed by up to six _hex digits_ surrounded by braces `U+007B` (`{`) and `U+007D` (`}`). It denotes the Unicode code point equal to the provided hex value, encoded as UTF-8.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.escape-whitespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.escape-whitespace "lex.token.str-c.escape-whitespace")\
\
- A _whitespace escape_ is one of the characters `U+006E` (`n`), `U+0072` (`r`), or `U+0074` (`t`), denoting the bytes values `0x0A` (ASCII LF), `0x0D` (ASCII CR) or `0x09` (ASCII HT) respectively.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.escape-slash\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.escape-slash "lex.token.str-c.escape-slash")\
\
- The _backslash escape_ is the character `U+005C` (`\`) which must be escaped in order to denote its ASCII encoding `0x5C`.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.char-unicode\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.char-unicode "lex.token.str-c.char-unicode")\
\
A C string represents bytes with no defined encoding, but a C string literal may contain Unicode characters above `U+007F`. Such characters will be replaced with the bytes of that character’s UTF-8 representation.\
\
The following C string literals are equivalent:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
c"æ";        // LATIN SMALL LETTER AE (U+00E6)\
c"\u{00E6}";\
c"\xC3\xA6";\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c.edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c.edition2021 "lex.token.str-c.edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> C string literals are accepted in the 2021 edition or later. In earlier editions the token `c""` is lexed as `c ""`.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c-raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c-raw "lex.token.str-c-raw")\
\
#### [Raw C string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#raw-c-string-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c-raw.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c-raw.syntax "lex.token.str-c-raw.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL) →\
\
cr" ^ [RAW\_C\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_CONTENT)"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
    \| cr#n:1..=255 ^ "[RAW\_C\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)"#n[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
[RAW\_C\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_C_STRING_CONTENT) → ( !" ~\[ [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) [NUL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-NUL)\] )\*\
\
[RAW\_C\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_C_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED) → ( !( "#n ) ~\[ [CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CR) [NUL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-NUL)\] )\*\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL)\
cr\
"\
no backtracking[RAW\_C\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_C_STRING_CONTENT)\
"[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
cr\
repeat count n\
at most 254 more times\
#\
no backtracking\
"[RAW\_C\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_C_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)\
"\
repeat exactly n times\
#[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\
[RAW\_C\_STRING\_CONTENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_CONTENT)\
not followed by\
"\
⚠️ with the exception of[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR) [NUL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-NUL)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
\
[RAW\_C\_STRING\_CONTENT\_HASHED](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_CONTENT_HASHED)\
not followed by\
"\
repeat exactly n times\
#\
⚠️ with the exception of[CR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CR) [NUL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-NUL)[CHAR](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR)\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c-raw.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c-raw.intro "lex.token.str-c-raw.intro")\
\
Raw C string literals do not process any escapes. They start with the character `U+0063` (`c`), followed by `U+0072` (`r`), followed by fewer than 256 of the character `U+0023` (`#`), and a `U+0022` (double-quote) character.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c-raw.body\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c-raw.body "lex.token.str-c-raw.body")\
\
The _raw C string body_ can contain any sequence of Unicode characters other than `U+0000` (NUL) and `U+000D` (CR). It is terminated only by another `U+0022` (double-quote) character, followed by the same number of `U+0023` (`#`) characters that preceded the opening `U+0022` (double-quote) character.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c-raw.content\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c-raw.content "lex.token.str-c-raw.content")\
\
All characters contained in the raw C string body represent themselves in UTF-8 encoding. The characters `U+0022` (double-quote) (except when followed by at least as many `U+0023` (`#`) characters as were used to start the raw C string literal) or `U+005C` (`\`) do not have any special meaning.\
\
[\[lex.token.str-c-raw.edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.str-c-raw.edition2021 "lex.token.str-c-raw.edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> Raw C string literals are accepted in the 2021 edition or later. In earlier editions the token `cr""` is lexed as `cr ""`, and `cr#""#` is lexed as `cr #""#` (which is non-grammatical).\
\
#### [Examples for C string and raw C string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#examples-for-c-string-and-raw-c-string-literals)\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
c"foo"; cr"foo";                     // foo\
c"\"foo\""; cr#""foo""#;             // "foo"\
\
c"foo #\"# bar";\
cr##"foo #"# bar"##;                 // foo #"# bar\
\
c"\x52"; c"R"; cr"R";                // R\
c"\\x52"; cr"\x52";                  // \x52\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.num\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.num "lex.token.literal.num")\
\
### [Number literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#number-literals)\
\
A _number literal_ is either an _integer literal_ or a _floating-point literal_. The grammar for recognizing the two kinds of literals is mixed.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int "lex.token.literal.int")\
\
#### [Integer literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#integer-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.syntax "lex.token.literal.int.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[INTEGER\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-INTEGER_LITERAL) →\
\
    ( [BIN\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BIN_LITERAL) \| [OCT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OCT_LITERAL) \| [HEX\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_LITERAL) \| [DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_LITERAL) )\
\
    ^ ![RESERVED\_FLOAT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_FLOAT)[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_LITERAL) → [DEC\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_DIGIT) ( [DEC\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_DIGIT) \| \_ )\*\
\
[BIN\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BIN_LITERAL) → 0b ^ \_\*[BIN\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BIN_DIGIT) ( [BIN\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BIN_DIGIT) \| \_ )\* !\[eE2-9\]\
\
[OCT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OCT_LITERAL) → 0o ^ \_\*[OCT\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OCT_DIGIT) ( [OCT\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OCT_DIGIT) \| \_ )\* !\[eE8-9\]\
\
[HEX\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_LITERAL) → 0x ^ \_\*[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_DIGIT) ( [HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_DIGIT) \| \_ )\*\
\
[BIN\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BIN_DIGIT) → \[0-1\]\
\
[OCT\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OCT_DIGIT) → \[0-7\]\
\
[DEC\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_DIGIT) → \[0-9\]\
\
[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_DIGIT) → \[0-9a-fA-F\]\
\
[RESERVED\_FLOAT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_FLOAT) → . !( . \| \_ \| [XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Start) )\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[INTEGER\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INTEGER_LITERAL)[BIN\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BIN_LITERAL) [OCT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OCT_LITERAL) [HEX\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_LITERAL) [DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_LITERAL)\

not followed by [RESERVED\_FLOAT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_FLOAT)[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)\
\

\_\
\

\_[BIN\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BIN_DIGIT)[BIN\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BIN_DIGIT)\

e\
E\

\

no backtracking\
\_[OCT\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OCT_DIGIT)[OCT\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OCT_DIGIT)\
\_\
not followed by\
e\
E\
8-9\
\
[HEX\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_LITERAL)\
0x\
no backtracking\
\_[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_DIGIT)[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_DIGIT)\
\_\
\
[BIN\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BIN_DIGIT)\
0-1\
\
[OCT\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OCT_DIGIT)\
0-7\
\
[DEC\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_DIGIT)\
0-9\
\
[HEX\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_DIGIT)\
0-9\
a-f\
A-F\
\
[RESERVED\_FLOAT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_FLOAT)\
.\
not followed by\
.\
\_[XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Start)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.kind\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.kind "lex.token.literal.int.kind")\
\
An _integer literal_ has one of four forms:\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.kind-dec\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.kind-dec "lex.token.literal.int.kind-dec")\
\
- A _decimal literal_ starts with a _decimal digit_ and continues with any mixture of _decimal digits_ and _underscores_.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.kind-hex\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.kind-hex "lex.token.literal.int.kind-hex")\
\
- A _hex literal_ starts with the character sequence `U+0030``U+0078` (`0x`) and continues as any mixture (with at least one digit) of hex digits and underscores.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.kind-oct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.kind-oct "lex.token.literal.int.kind-oct")\
\
- An _octal literal_ starts with the character sequence `U+0030``U+006F` (`0o`) and continues as any mixture (with at least one digit) of octal digits and underscores.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.kind-bin\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.kind-bin "lex.token.literal.int.kind-bin")\
\
- A _binary literal_ starts with the character sequence `U+0030``U+0062` (`0b`) and continues as any mixture (with at least one digit) of binary digits and underscores.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.suffix "lex.token.literal.int.suffix")\
\
Like any literal, an integer literal may be followed (immediately, without any spaces) by a suffix as described above. The suffix may not begin with `e` or `E`, as that would be interpreted as the exponent of a floating-point literal. See [Integer literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#integer-literal-expressions) for the effect of these suffixes.\
\
Examples of integer literals which are accepted as literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#![allow(overflowing_literals)]\
123;\
123i32;\
123u32;\
123_u32;\
\
0xff;\
0xff_u8;\
0x01_f32; // integer 7986, not floating-point 1.0\
0x01_e3;  // integer 483, not floating-point 1000.0\
\
0o70;\
0o70_i16;\
\
0b1111_1111_1001_0000;\
0b1111_1111_1001_0000i64;\
0b________1;\
\
0usize;\
\
// These are too big for their type, but are accepted as literal expressions.\
128_i8;\
256_u8;\
\
// This is an integer literal, accepted as a floating-point literal expression.\
5f32;\
}\
```\
\
Note that `-1i8`, for example, is analyzed as two tokens: `-` followed by `1i8`.\
\
Examples of integer literals which are not accepted as literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[cfg(false)] {\
0invalidSuffix;\
123AFB43;\
0b010a;\
0xAB_CD_EF_GH;\
0b1111_f32;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.invalid\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.invalid "lex.token.literal.int.invalid")\
\
##### [Invalid integer literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#invalid-integer-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.invalid.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.invalid.intro "lex.token.literal.int.invalid.intro")\
\
Certain integer literal forms are invalid. To avoid ambiguity, the tokenizer rejects them rather than splitting them into separate tokens.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
0b0102;  // This is not `0b010` followed by `2`.\
0o1279;  // This is not `0o127` followed by `9`.\
0x80.0;  // This is not `0x80` followed by `.` and `0`.\
0b101e;  // This is not a suffixed literal or `0b101` followed by `e`.\
0b;      // This is not an integer literal or `0` followed by `b`.\
0b_;     // This is not an integer literal or `0` followed by `b_`.\
2em;     // This is not a suffixed literal or `2` followed by `em`.\
2.0em;   // This is not a suffixed literal or `2.0` followed by `em`.\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.out-of-range\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.out-of-range "lex.token.literal.int.out-of-range")\
\
It is an error to have an unsuffixed binary or octal literal followed without intervening whitespace by a decimal digit outside the range for its radix.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.period\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.period "lex.token.literal.int.period")\
\
It is an error to have an unsuffixed binary, octal, or hexadecimal literal followed without intervening whitespace by a period character (subject to the same restrictions on what may follow the period as in floating-point literals).\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.exp\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.exp "lex.token.literal.int.exp")\
\
It is an error to have an unsuffixed binary or octal literal followed without intervening whitespace by the character `e` or `E`.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.empty-with-radix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.empty-with-radix "lex.token.literal.int.empty-with-radix")\
\
It is an error for a radix prefix to not be followed, after any optional leading underscores, by at least one valid digit for its radix.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field "lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field")\
\
#### [Tuple index](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tuple-index)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.syntax "lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[TUPLE\_INDEX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TUPLE_INDEX) → [DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_LITERAL) \| [BIN\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BIN_LITERAL) \| [OCT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OCT_LITERAL) \| [HEX\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-HEX_LITERAL)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TUPLE\_INDEX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TUPLE_INDEX)[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_LITERAL) [BIN\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BIN_LITERAL) [OCT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OCT_LITERAL) [HEX\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-HEX_LITERAL)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.intro "lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.intro")\
\
A tuple index is used to refer to the fields of [tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types), [tuple structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), and [tuple enum variants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations).\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.eq\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.eq "lex.token.literal.int.tuple-field.eq")\
\
Tuple indices are compared with the literal token directly. Tuple indices start with `0` and each successive index increments the value by `1` as a decimal value. Thus, only decimal values will match, and the value must not have any extra `0` prefix characters.\
\
Tuple indices may not include any suffixes (such as `usize`).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let example = ("dog", "cat", "horse");\
let dog = example.0;\
let cat = example.1;\
// The following examples are invalid.\
let cat = example.01;  // ERROR no field named `01`\
let horse = example.0b10;  // ERROR no field named `0b10`\
let unicorn = example.0usize; // ERROR suffixes on a tuple index are invalid\
let underscore = example.0_0; // ERROR no field `0_0` on type `(&str, &str, &str)`\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.float\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.float "lex.token.literal.float")\
\
#### [Floating-point literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#floating-point-literals)\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.float.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.float.syntax "lex.token.literal.float.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[FLOAT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FLOAT_LITERAL) →\
\
[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_LITERAL) ( .[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_LITERAL) )?[FLOAT\_EXPONENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FLOAT_EXPONENT)[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
    \| [DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_LITERAL).[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_LITERAL)[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SUFFIX)?\
\
    \| [DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_LITERAL). !( . \| \_ \| [XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Start) )\
\
[FLOAT\_EXPONENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FLOAT_EXPONENT) →\
\
    ( e \| E ) ^ ( + \| - )?\_\*[DEC\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_DIGIT) ( [DEC\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DEC_DIGIT) \| \_ )\*\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[FLOAT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FLOAT_LITERAL)[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_LITERAL)\
.[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_LITERAL)[FLOAT\_EXPONENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FLOAT_EXPONENT)[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_LITERAL)\
.[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_LITERAL)[SUFFIX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SUFFIX)[DEC\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_LITERAL)\
.\
not followed by\
.\
\_[XID\_Start](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Start)\
\
[FLOAT\_EXPONENT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FLOAT_EXPONENT)\
e\
E\
no backtracking\
+\
-\
\_[DEC\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_DIGIT)[DEC\_DIGIT](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DEC_DIGIT)\
\_\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.float.form\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.float.form "lex.token.literal.float.form")\
\
A _floating-point literal_ has one of two forms:\
\
- A _decimal literal_ followed by a period character `U+002E` (`.`). This is optionally followed by another decimal literal, with an optional _exponent_.\
- A single _decimal literal_ followed by an _exponent_.\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.float.suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.float.suffix "lex.token.literal.float.suffix")\
\
Like integer literals, a floating-point literal may be followed by a suffix, so long as the pre-suffix part does not end with `U+002E` (`.`). The suffix may not begin with `e` or `E` if the literal does not include an exponent. See [Floating-point literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#floating-point-literal-expressions) for the effect of these suffixes.\
\
Examples of floating-point literals which are accepted as literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
123.0f64;\
0.1f64;\
0.1f32;\
12E+99_f64;\
let x: f64 = 2.;\
}\
```\
\
This last example is different because it is not possible to use the suffix syntax with a floating point literal ending in a period. `2.f64` would attempt to call a method named `f64` on `2`.\
\
Note that `-1.0`, for example, is analyzed as two tokens: `-` followed by `1.0`.\
\
Examples of floating-point literals which are not accepted as literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[cfg(false)] {\
2.0f80;\
2e5f80;\
2e5e6;\
2.0e5e6;\
1.3e10u64;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.token.literal.float.invalid-exponent\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.float.invalid-exponent "lex.token.literal.float.invalid-exponent")\
\
It is an error for a floating-point literal to have an exponent with no digits.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
2e;   // This is not a floating-point literal or `2` followed by `e`.\
2.0e; // This is not a floating-point literal or `2.0` followed by `e`.\
}\
```\
\
[\[lex.token.life\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.life "lex.token.life")\
\
## [Lifetimes and loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#lifetimes-and-loop-labels)\
\
[\[lex.token.life.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.life.syntax "lex.token.life.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[LIFETIME\_TOKEN](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LIFETIME_TOKEN) →\
\
[RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_LIFETIME)\
\
    \| '[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD) !'\
\
[LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LIFETIME_OR_LABEL) →\
\
[RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_LIFETIME)\
\
    \| '[NON\_KEYWORD\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-NON_KEYWORD_IDENTIFIER) !'\
\
[RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_LIFETIME) →\
\
'r# ^ [IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD) !'\
\
[RESERVED\_RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_RAW_LIFETIME) → 'r# ( \_ \| crate \| self \| Self \| super ) !( ' \| [XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-XID_Continue) )\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\

'[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)\
not followed by\
'\
\
[LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LIFETIME_OR_LABEL)[RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_LIFETIME)\
'[NON\_KEYWORD\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-NON_KEYWORD_IDENTIFIER)\
not followed by\
'\
\
[RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_LIFETIME)\
'r#\
no backtracking[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)\
not followed by\
'\
\
[RESERVED\_RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_RAW_LIFETIME)\
'r#\
\_\
crate\
self\
Self\
super\
not followed by\
'[XID\_Continue](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-XID_Continue)\
\
[\[lex.token.life.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.life.intro "lex.token.life.intro")\
\
Lifetime parameters and [loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels) use LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL tokens. Any LIFETIME\_TOKEN will be accepted by the lexer, and for example, can be used in macros.\
\
[\[lex.token.life.raw.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.life.raw.intro "lex.token.life.raw.intro")\
\
A raw lifetime is like a normal lifetime, but its identifier is prefixed by `r#`. (Note that the `r#` prefix is not included as part of the actual lifetime.)\
\
[\[lex.token.life.raw.allowed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.life.raw.allowed "lex.token.life.raw.allowed")\
\
Unlike a normal lifetime, a raw lifetime may be any strict or reserved keyword except the ones listed above for `RAW_LIFETIME`.\
\
[\[lex.token.life.raw.reserved\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.life.raw.reserved "lex.token.life.raw.reserved")\
\
It is an error to use the [RESERVED\_RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_RAW_LIFETIME) token.\
\
[\[lex.token.life.raw.edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.life.raw.edition2021 "lex.token.life.raw.edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> Raw lifetimes are accepted in the 2021 edition or later. In earlier editions the token `'r#lt` is lexed as `'r # lt`.\
\
[\[lex.token.punct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.punct "lex.token.punct")\
\
## [Punctuation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#punctuation)\
\
[\[lex.token.punct.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.punct.intro "lex.token.punct.intro")\
\
Punctuation tokens are used as operators, separators, and other parts of the grammar.\
\
[\[lex.token.punct.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.punct.syntax "lex.token.punct.syntax")\
\

\
[\[lex.token.delim\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.delim "lex.token.delim")\
\
## [Delimiters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#delimiters)\
\
Bracket punctuation is used in various parts of the grammar. An open bracket must always be paired with a close bracket. Brackets and the tokens within them are referred to as “token trees” in [macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example). The three types of brackets are:\
\
| Bracket | Type |\
| --- | --- |\
| `{``}` | Curly braces |\
| `[``]` | Square brackets |\
| `(``)` | Parentheses |\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved "lex.token.reserved")\
\
## [Reserved tokens](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reserved-tokens)\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved.intro "lex.token.reserved.intro")\
\
Several token forms are reserved for future use or to avoid confusion. It is an error for the source input to match one of these forms.\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved.syntax "lex.token.reserved.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[RESERVED\_TOKEN](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN) →\
\
[RESERVED\_GUARDED\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_GUARDED_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [RESERVED\_POUNDS](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_POUNDS)\
\
    \| [RESERVED\_RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_RAW_IDENTIFIER)\
\
    \| [RESERVED\_RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_RAW_LIFETIME)\
\
    \| [RESERVED\_TOKEN\_DOUBLE\_QUOTE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_TOKEN_DOUBLE_QUOTE)\
\
    \| [RESERVED\_TOKEN\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_TOKEN_LIFETIME)\
\
    \| [RESERVED\_TOKEN\_POUND](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_TOKEN_POUND)\
\
    \| [RESERVED\_TOKEN\_SINGLE\_QUOTE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_TOKEN_SINGLE_QUOTE)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[RESERVED\_TOKEN](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_TOKEN)[RESERVED\_GUARDED\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_GUARDED_STRING_LITERAL)[RESERVED\_POUNDS](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_POUNDS)[RESERVED\_RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_RAW_IDENTIFIER)[RESERVED\_RAW\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_RAW_LIFETIME)[RESERVED\_TOKEN\_DOUBLE\_QUOTE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN_DOUBLE_QUOTE)[RESERVED\_TOKEN\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN_LIFETIME)[RESERVED\_TOKEN\_POUND](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN_POUND)[RESERVED\_TOKEN\_SINGLE\_QUOTE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN_SINGLE_QUOTE)\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-prefix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-prefix "lex.token.reserved-prefix")\
\
## [Reserved prefixes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reserved-prefixes)\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-prefix.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-prefix.syntax "lex.token.reserved-prefix.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[RESERVED\_TOKEN\_DOUBLE\_QUOTE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN_DOUBLE_QUOTE) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)except `b` or `c` or `r` or `br` or `cr`"\
\
[RESERVED\_TOKEN\_SINGLE\_QUOTE](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN_SINGLE_QUOTE) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)except `b`'\
\
[RESERVED\_TOKEN\_POUND](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN_POUND) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)except `r` or `br` or `cr`#\
\
[RESERVED\_TOKEN\_LIFETIME](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_TOKEN_LIFETIME) →\
\
'[IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD)except `r`#\
\

\

\

\
[\[lex.token.reserved-prefix.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-prefix.intro "lex.token.reserved-prefix.intro")\
\
Some lexical forms known as _reserved prefixes_ are reserved for future use.\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-prefix.id\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-prefix.id "lex.token.reserved-prefix.id")\
\
Source input which would otherwise be lexically interpreted as a non-raw identifier (or a keyword) which is immediately followed by a `#`, `'`, or `"` character (without intervening whitespace) is identified as a reserved prefix.\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-prefix.raw-token\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-prefix.raw-token "lex.token.reserved-prefix.raw-token")\
\
Note that raw identifiers, raw string literals, and raw byte string literals may contain a `#` character but are not interpreted as containing a reserved prefix.\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-prefix.strings\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-prefix.strings "lex.token.reserved-prefix.strings")\
\
Similarly the `r`, `b`, `br`, `c`, and `cr` prefixes used in raw string literals, byte literals, byte string literals, raw byte string literals, C string literals, and raw C string literals are not interpreted as reserved prefixes.\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-prefix.life\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-prefix.life "lex.token.reserved-prefix.life")\
\
Source input which would otherwise be lexically interpreted as a non-raw lifetime (or a keyword) which is immediately followed by a `#` character (without intervening whitespace) is identified as a reserved lifetime prefix.\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-prefix.edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-prefix.edition2021 "lex.token.reserved-prefix.edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> Starting with the 2021 edition, reserved prefixes are reported as an error by the lexer (in particular, they cannot be passed to macros).\
>\
> Before the 2021 edition, reserved prefixes are accepted by the lexer and interpreted as multiple tokens (for example, one token for the identifier or keyword, followed by a `#` token).\
>\
> Examples accepted in all editions:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> macro_rules! lexes {($($_:tt)*) => {}}\
> lexes!{a #foo}\
> lexes!{continue 'foo}\
> lexes!{match "..." {}}\
> lexes!{r#let#foo}         // three tokens: r#let # foo\
> lexes!{'prefix #lt}\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> Examples accepted before the 2021 edition but rejected later:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> macro_rules! lexes {($($_:tt)*) => {}}\
> lexes!{a#foo}\
> lexes!{continue'foo}\
> lexes!{match"..." {}}\
> lexes!{'prefix#lt}\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-guards\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-guards "lex.token.reserved-guards")\
\
## [Reserved guards](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reserved-guards)\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-guards.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-guards.syntax "lex.token.reserved-guards.syntax")\
\
**Lexer**\
\
[RESERVED\_GUARDED\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_GUARDED_STRING_LITERAL) → #+[STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL)\
\
[RESERVED\_POUNDS](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RESERVED_POUNDS) → #2..\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[RESERVED\_GUARDED\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_GUARDED_STRING_LITERAL)\
#[STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_LITERAL)\
\
[RESERVED\_POUNDS](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RESERVED_POUNDS)\
#\
#\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-guards.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-guards.intro "lex.token.reserved-guards.intro")\
\
The reserved guards are syntax reserved for future use, and will generate a compile error if used.\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-guards.string-literal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-guards.string-literal "lex.token.reserved-guards.string-literal")\
\
The _reserved guarded string literal_ is a token of one or more `U+0023` (`#`) immediately followed by a [STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL).\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-guards.pounds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-guards.pounds "lex.token.reserved-guards.pounds")\
\
The _reserved pounds_ is a token of two or more `U+0023` (`#`).\
\
[\[lex.token.reserved-guards.edition2024\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.reserved-guards.edition2024 "lex.token.reserved-guards.edition2024")\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2024 edition, reserved guards are accepted by the lexer and interpreted as multiple tokens. For example, the `#"foo"#` form is interpreted as three tokens. `##` is interpreted as two tokens.\
\
* * *\
\
1. The number of `#`s on each side of the same literal must be equivalent. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-nsets-1)\
\
2. All number literals allow `_` as a visual separator: `1_234.0E+18f64` [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-nl-1)\
\
3. See [lex.token.literal.char-escape.unicode](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lex.token.literal.char-escape.unicode). [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-valid-hex-char-1)\
\
\
[\[macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro "macro")\
\
# [Macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#macros)\
\
[\[macro.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.intro "macro.intro")\
\
The functionality and syntax of Rust can be extended with custom definitions called macros. They are given names, and invoked through a consistent syntax: `some_extension!(...)`.\
\
There are two ways to define new macros:\
\
- [Macros by Example](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example) define new syntax in a higher-level, declarative way.\
- [Procedural Macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#procedural-macros) define function-like macros, custom derives, and custom attributes using functions that operate on input tokens.\
\
[\[macro.invocation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation "macro.invocation")\
\
## [Macro invocation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#macro-invocation)\
\
[\[macro.invocation.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.syntax "macro.invocation.syntax")\
\

\
[\[macro.invocation.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.intro "macro.invocation.intro")\
\
A macro invocation expands a macro at compile time and replaces the invocation with the result of the macro. Macros may be invoked in the following situations:\
\
[\[macro.invocation.expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.expr "macro.invocation.expr")\
\
- [Expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expressions) and [statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#statements)\
\
[\[macro.invocation.pattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.pattern "macro.invocation.pattern")\
\
- [Patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns)\
\
[\[macro.invocation.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.type "macro.invocation.type")\
\
- [Types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#types)\
\
[\[macro.invocation.item\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.item "macro.invocation.item")\
\
- [Items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items) including [associated items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-items)\
\
[\[macro.invocation.nested\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.nested "macro.invocation.nested")\
\
- [`macro_rules`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example) transcribers\
\
[\[macro.invocation.extern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.extern "macro.invocation.extern")\
\
- [External blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks)\
\
[\[macro.invocation.item-statement\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.item-statement "macro.invocation.item-statement")\
\
When used as an item or a statement, the [MacroInvocationSemi](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MacroInvocationSemi) form is used where a semicolon is required at the end when not using curly braces. [Visibility qualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#visibility-and-privacy) are never allowed before a macro invocation or [`macro_rules`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example) definition.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Used as an expression.\
let x = vec![1,2,3];\
\
// Used as a statement.\
println!("Hello!");\
\
// Used in a pattern.\
macro_rules! pat {\
    ($i:ident) => (Some($i))\
}\
\
if let pat!(x) = Some(1) {\
    assert_eq!(x, 1);\
}\
\
// Used in a type.\
macro_rules! Tuple {\
    { $A:ty, $B:ty } => { ($A, $B) };\
}\
\
type N2 = Tuple!(i32, i32);\
\
// Used as an item.\
use std::cell::RefCell;\
thread_local!(static FOO: RefCell<u32> = RefCell::new(1));\
\
// Used as an associated item.\
macro_rules! const_maker {\
    ($t:ty, $v:tt) => { const CONST: $t = $v; };\
}\
trait T {\
    const_maker!{i32, 7}\
}\
\
// Macro calls within macros.\
macro_rules! example {\
    () => { println!("Macro call in a macro!") };\
}\
// Outer macro `example` is expanded, then inner macro `println` is expanded.\
example!();\
}\
```\
\
[\[macro.invocation.name-resolution\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.name-resolution "macro.invocation.name-resolution")\
\
Macros invocations can be resolved via two kinds of scopes:\
\
- Textual Scope\
  - [Textual scope `macro_rules`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.textual)\
- Path-based scope\
  - [Path-based scope `macro_rules`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.path-based)\
  - [Procedural macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#procedural-macros)\
\
[\[macro.decl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl "macro.decl")\
\
# [Macros by example](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#macros-by-example)\
\
[\[macro.decl.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.syntax "macro.decl.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\

[MacroTranscriber](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MacroTranscriber)[DelimTokenTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DelimTokenTree)\
\
[\[macro.decl.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.intro "macro.decl.intro")\
\
`macro_rules` allows users to define syntax extension in a declarative way. We call such extensions “macros by example” or simply “macros”.\
\
Each macro by example has a name, and one or more _rules_. Each rule has two parts: a _matcher_, describing the syntax that it matches, and a _transcriber_, describing the syntax that will replace a successfully matched invocation. Both the matcher and the transcriber must be surrounded by delimiters. Macros can expand to expressions, statements, items (including traits, impls, and foreign items), types, or patterns.\
\
[\[macro.decl.transcription\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.transcription "macro.decl.transcription")\
\
## [Transcribing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#transcribing)\
\
[\[macro.decl.transcription.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.transcription.intro "macro.decl.transcription.intro")\
\
When a macro is invoked, the macro expander looks up macro invocations by name, and tries each macro rule in turn. It transcribes the first successful match; if this results in an error, then future matches are not tried.\
\
[\[macro.decl.transcription.lookahead\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.transcription.lookahead "macro.decl.transcription.lookahead")\
\
When matching, no lookahead is performed; if the compiler cannot unambiguously determine how to parse the macro invocation one token at a time, then it is an error. In the following example, the compiler does not look ahead past the identifier to see if the following token is a `)`, even though that would allow it to parse the invocation unambiguously:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! ambiguity {\
    ($($i:ident)* $j:ident) => { };\
}\
\
ambiguity!(error); // Error: local ambiguity\
}\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.transcription.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.transcription.syntax "macro.decl.transcription.syntax")\
\
In both the matcher and the transcriber, the `$` token is used to invoke special behaviours from the macro engine (described below in [Metavariables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#metavariables) and [Repetitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#repetitions)). Tokens that aren’t part of such an invocation are matched and transcribed literally, with one exception. The exception is that the outer delimiters for the matcher will match any pair of delimiters. Thus, for instance, the matcher `(())` will match `{()}` but not `{{}}`. The character `$` cannot be matched or transcribed literally.\
\
[\[macro.decl.transcription.fragment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.transcription.fragment "macro.decl.transcription.fragment")\
\
### [Forwarding a matched fragment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#forwarding-a-matched-fragment)\
\
When forwarding a matched fragment to another macro-by-example, matchers in the second macro will see an opaque AST of the fragment type. The second macro can’t use literal tokens to match the fragments in the matcher, only a fragment specifier of the same type. The `ident`, `lifetime`, and `tt` fragment types are an exception, and _can_ be matched by literal tokens. The following illustrates this restriction:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! foo {\
    ($l:expr) => { bar!($l); }\
// ERROR:               ^^ no rules expected this token in macro call\
}\
\
macro_rules! bar {\
    (3) => {}\
}\
\
foo!(3);\
}\
```\
\
The following illustrates how tokens can be directly matched after matching a `tt` fragment:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// compiles OK\
macro_rules! foo {\
    ($l:tt) => { bar!($l); }\
}\
\
macro_rules! bar {\
    (3) => {}\
}\
\
foo!(3);\
}\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.meta\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta "macro.decl.meta")\
\
## [Metavariables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#metavariables)\
\
[\[macro.decl.meta.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta.intro "macro.decl.meta.intro")\
\
In the matcher, `$` _name_`:` _fragment-specifier_ matches a Rust syntax fragment of the kind specified and binds it to the metavariable `$` _name_.\
\
[\[macro.decl.meta.specifier\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta.specifier "macro.decl.meta.specifier")\
\
Valid fragment specifiers are:\
\
- `block`: a [BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
- `expr`: an [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
- `expr_2021`: an [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) except [UnderscoreExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UnderscoreExpression) and [ConstBlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConstBlockExpression) (see [macro.decl.meta.edition2024](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta.edition2024))\
- `ident`: an [IDENTIFIER\_OR\_KEYWORD](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER_OR_KEYWORD) except `_`, [RAW\_IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_IDENTIFIER), or [`$crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.crate)\
- `item`: an [Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Item)\
- `lifetime`: a [LIFETIME\_TOKEN](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LIFETIME_TOKEN)\
- `literal`: matches `-`? [LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LiteralExpression)\
- `meta`: an [Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Attr), the contents of an attribute\
- `pat`: a [Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern) (see [macro.decl.meta.edition2021](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta.edition2021))\
- `pat_param`: a [PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternNoTopAlt)\
- `path`: a [TypePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePath) style path\
- `stmt`: a [Statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Statement) without the trailing semicolon (except for item statements that require semicolons)\
- `tt`: a [TokenTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TokenTree) (a single [token](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tokens) or tokens in matching delimiters `()`, `[]`, or `{}`)\
- `ty`: a [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)\
- `vis`: a possibly empty [Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Visibility) qualifier\
\
[\[macro.decl.meta.transcription\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta.transcription "macro.decl.meta.transcription")\
\
In the transcriber, metavariables are referred to simply by `$` _name_, since the fragment kind is specified in the matcher. Metavariables are replaced with the syntax element that matched them. Metavariables can be transcribed more than once or not at all.\
\
[\[macro.decl.meta.dollar-crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta.dollar-crate "macro.decl.meta.dollar-crate")\
\
The keyword metavariable [`$crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.crate) can be used to refer to the current crate.\
\
[\[macro.decl.meta.edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta.edition2021 "macro.decl.meta.edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> Starting with the 2021 edition, `pat` fragment-specifiers match top-level or-patterns (that is, they accept [Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern)).\
>\
> Before the 2021 edition, they match exactly the same fragments as `pat_param` (that is, they accept [PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternNoTopAlt)).\
>\
> The relevant edition is the one in effect for the `macro_rules!` definition.\
\
[\[macro.decl.meta.edition2024\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.meta.edition2024 "macro.decl.meta.edition2024")\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2024 edition, `expr` fragment specifiers do not match [UnderscoreExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UnderscoreExpression) or [ConstBlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConstBlockExpression) at the top level. They are allowed within subexpressions.\
>\
> The `expr_2021` fragment specifier exists to maintain backwards compatibility with editions before 2024.\
\
[\[macro.decl.repetition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.repetition "macro.decl.repetition")\
\
## [Repetitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#repetitions)\
\
[\[macro.decl.repetition.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.repetition.intro "macro.decl.repetition.intro")\
\
In both the matcher and transcriber, repetitions are indicated by placing the tokens to be repeated inside `$(`…`)`, followed by a repetition operator, optionally with a separator token between.\
\
[\[macro.decl.repetition.separator\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.repetition.separator "macro.decl.repetition.separator")\
\
The separator token can be any token other than a delimiter or one of the repetition operators, but `;` and `,` are the most common. For instance, `$( $i:ident ),*` represents any number of identifiers separated by commas. Nested repetitions are permitted.\
\
[\[macro.decl.repetition.operators\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.repetition.operators "macro.decl.repetition.operators")\
\
The repetition operators are:\
\
- `*` — indicates any number of repetitions.\
- `+` — indicates any number but at least one.\
- `?` — indicates an optional fragment with zero or one occurrence.\
\
[\[macro.decl.repetition.optional-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.repetition.optional-restriction "macro.decl.repetition.optional-restriction")\
\
Since `?` represents at most one occurrence, it cannot be used with a separator.\
\
[\[macro.decl.repetition.fragment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.repetition.fragment "macro.decl.repetition.fragment")\
\
The repeated fragment both matches and transcribes to the specified number of the fragment, separated by the separator token. Metavariables are matched to every repetition of their corresponding fragment. For instance, the `$( $i:ident ),*` example above matches `$i` to all of the identifiers in the list.\
\
During transcription, additional restrictions apply to repetitions so that the compiler knows how to expand them properly:\
\
1. A metavariable must appear in exactly the same number, kind, and nesting order of repetitions in the transcriber as it did in the matcher. So for the matcher `$( $i:ident ),*`, the transcribers `=> { $i }`, `=> { $( $( $i )* )* }`, and `=> { $( $i )+ }` are all illegal, but `=> { $( $i );* }` is correct and replaces a comma-separated list of identifiers with a semicolon-separated list.\
2. Each repetition in the transcriber must contain at least one metavariable to decide how many times to expand it. If multiple metavariables appear in the same repetition, they must be bound to the same number of fragments. For instance, `( $( $i:ident ),* ; $( $j:ident ),* ) => (( $( ($i,$j) ),* ))` must bind the same number of `$i` fragments as `$j` fragments. This means that invoking the macro with `(a, b, c; d, e, f)` is legal and expands to `((a,d), (b,e), (c,f))`, but `(a, b, c; d, e)` is illegal because it does not have the same number. This requirement applies to every layer of nested repetitions.\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope "macro.decl.scope")\
\
## [Scoping, exporting, and importing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#scoping-exporting-and-importing)\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.intro "macro.decl.scope.intro")\
\
For historical reasons, the scoping of macros by example does not work entirely like items. Macros have two forms of scope: textual scope, and path-based scope. Textual scope is based on the order that things appear in source files, or even across multiple files, and is the default scoping. It is explained further below. Path-based scope works exactly the same way that item scoping does. The scoping, exporting, and importing of macros is controlled largely by attributes.\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.unqualified\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.unqualified "macro.decl.scope.unqualified")\
\
When a macro is invoked by an unqualified identifier (not part of a multi-part path), it is first looked up in textual scoping. If this does not yield any results, then it is looked up in path-based scoping. If the macro’s name is qualified with a path, then it is only looked up in path-based scoping.\
\
```rust\
\
use lazy_static::lazy_static; // Path-based import.\
\
macro_rules! lazy_static { // Textual definition.\
    (lazy) => {};\
}\
\
lazy_static!{lazy} // Textual lookup finds our macro first.\
self::lazy_static!{} // Path-based lookup ignores our macro, finds imported one.\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.textual\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.textual "macro.decl.scope.textual")\
\
### [Textual scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#textual-scope)\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.textual.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.textual.intro "macro.decl.scope.textual.intro")\
\
Textual scope is based largely on the order that things appear in source files, and works similarly to the scope of local variables declared with `let` except it also applies at the module level. When `macro_rules!` is used to define a macro, the macro enters the scope after the definition (note that it can still be used recursively, since names are looked up from the invocation site), up until its surrounding scope, typically a module, is closed. This can enter child modules and even span across multiple files:\
\
```rust\
\
//// src/lib.rs\
mod has_macro {\
    // m!{} // Error: m is not in scope.\
\
    macro_rules! m {\
        () => {};\
    }\
    m!{} // OK: appears after declaration of m.\
\
    mod uses_macro;\
}\
\
// m!{} // Error: m is not in scope.\
\
//// src/has_macro/uses_macro.rs\
\
m!{} // OK: appears after declaration of m in src/lib.rs\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.textual.shadow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.textual.shadow "macro.decl.scope.textual.shadow")\
\
It is not an error to define a macro multiple times; the most recent declaration will shadow the previous one unless it has gone out of scope.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! m {\
    (1) => {};\
}\
\
m!(1);\
\
mod inner {\
    m!(1);\
\
    macro_rules! m {\
        (2) => {};\
    }\
    // m!(1); // Error: no rule matches '1'\
    m!(2);\
\
    macro_rules! m {\
        (3) => {};\
    }\
    m!(3);\
}\
\
m!(1);\
}\
```\
\
Macros can be declared and used locally inside functions as well, and work similarly:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn foo() {\
    // m!(); // Error: m is not in scope.\
    macro_rules! m {\
        () => {};\
    }\
    m!();\
}\
\
// m!(); // Error: m is not in scope.\
}\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.textual.shadow.path-based\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.textual.shadow.path-based "macro.decl.scope.textual.shadow.path-based")\
\
Textual scope name bindings for macros shadow path-based scope bindings to macros.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! m2 {\
    () => {\
        println!("m2");\
    };\
}\
\
// Resolves to path-based candidate from use declaration below.\
m!(); // prints "m2\n"\
\
// Introduce second candidate for `m` with textual scope.\
//\
// This shadows path-based candidate from below for the rest of this\
// example.\
macro_rules! m {\
    () => {\
        println!("m");\
    };\
}\
\
// Introduce `m2` macro as path-based candidate.\
//\
// This item is in scope for this entire example, not just below the\
// use declaration.\
use m2 as m;\
\
// Resolves to the textual macro candidate from above the use\
// declaration.\
m!(); // prints "m\n"\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> For areas where shadowing is not allowed, see [name resolution ambiguities](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity).\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.path-based\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.path-based "macro.decl.scope.path-based")\
\
### [Path-based scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#path-based-scope)\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.path-based.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.path-based.intro "macro.decl.scope.path-based.intro")\
\
By default, a macro has no path-based scope. Macros can gain path-based scope in two ways:\
\
- [Use declaration re-export](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-visibility)\
- [`macro_export`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_export-attribute)\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.path.reexport\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.path.reexport "macro.decl.scope.path.reexport")\
\
Macros can be re-exported to give them path-based scope from a module other than the crate root.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mac::m!(); // OK: Path-based lookup finds `m` in the mac module.\
\
mod mac {\
    // Introduce macro `m` with textual scope.\
    macro_rules! m {\
        () => {};\
    }\
\
    // Reexport with path-based scope from within `m`'s textual scope.\
    pub(crate) use m;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.path-based.visibility\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.path-based.visibility "macro.decl.scope.path-based.visibility")\
\
Macros have an implicit visibility of `pub(crate)`. `#[macro_export]` changes the implicit visibility to `pub`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Implicit visibility is `pub(crate)`.\
macro_rules! private_m {\
    () => {};\
}\
\
// Implicit visibility is `pub`.\
#[macro_export]\
macro_rules! pub_m {\
    () => {};\
}\
\
pub(crate) use private_m as private_macro; // OK.\
pub use pub_m as pub_macro; // OK.\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Implicit visibility is `pub(crate)`.\
macro_rules! private_m {\
    () => {};\
}\
\
// Implicit visibility is `pub`.\
#[macro_export]\
macro_rules! pub_m {\
    () => {};\
}\
\
pub(crate) use private_m as private_macro; // OK.\
pub use pub_m as pub_macro; // OK.\
\
pub use private_m; // ERROR: `private_m` is only public within\
                   // the crate and cannot be re-exported outside.\
}\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use "macro.decl.scope.macro_use")\
\
### [The `macro_use` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-macro_use-attribute)\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.intro "macro.decl.scope.macro_use.intro")\
\
The _`macro_use` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes)_ has two purposes: it may be used on modules to extend the scope of macros defined within them, and it may be used on [`extern crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate) to import macros from another crate into the [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro_use-prelude).\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> #[macro_use]\
> mod inner {\
>     macro_rules! m {\
>         () => {};\
>     }\
> }\
> m!();\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[macro_use]\
> extern crate log;\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.syntax "macro.decl.scope.macro_use.syntax")\
\
When used on modules, the `macro_use` attribute uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) syntax.\
\
