When used on `extern crate`, it uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) and [MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents) syntaxes. For more on how these syntaxes may be used, see [macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.prelude).\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.allowed-positions "macro.decl.scope.macro_use.allowed-positions")\
\
The `macro_use` attribute may be applied to modules or `extern crate`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` ignores use in other positions but lints against it. This may become an error in the future.\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.extern-crate-self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.extern-crate-self "macro.decl.scope.macro_use.extern-crate-self")\
\
The `macro_use` attribute may not be used on [`extern crate self`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.self).\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.duplicates "macro.decl.scope.macro_use.duplicates")\
\
The `macro_use` attribute may be used any number of times on a form.\
\
Multiple instances of `macro_use` in the [MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents) syntax may be specified. The union of all specified macros will be imported.\
\
> Note\
>\
> On modules, `rustc` lints against any [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord)`macro_use` attributes following the first.\
>\
> On `extern crate`, `rustc` lints against any `macro_use` attributes that have no effect due to not importing any macros not already imported by another `macro_use` attribute. If two or more [MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents)`macro_use` attributes import the same macro, the first is linted against. If any [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord)`macro_use` attributes are present, all [MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents)`macro_use` attributes are linted against. If two or more [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord)`macro_use` attributes are present, the ones following the first are linted against.\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.mod-decl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.mod-decl "macro.decl.scope.macro_use.mod-decl")\
\
When `macro_use` is used on a module, the module’s macro scope extends beyond the module’s lexical scope.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> #[macro_use]\
> mod inner {\
>     macro_rules! m {\
>         () => {};\
>     }\
> }\
> m!(); // OK\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.prelude "macro.decl.scope.macro_use.prelude")\
\
Specifying `macro_use` on an `extern crate` declaration in the crate root imports exported macros from that crate.\
\
Macros imported this way are imported into the [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro_use-prelude), not textually, which means that they can be shadowed by any other name. Macros imported by `macro_use` can be used before the import statement.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` currently prefers the last macro imported in case of conflict. Don’t rely on this. This behavior is unusual, as imports in Rust are generally order-independent. This behavior of `macro_use` may change in the future.\
>\
> For details, see [Rust issue #148025](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/148025).\
\
When using the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) syntax, all exported macros are imported. When using the [MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents) syntax, only the specified macros are imported.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[macro_use(lazy_static)] // Or `#[macro_use]` to import all macros.\
> extern crate lazy_static;\
>\
> lazy_static!{}\
> // self::lazy_static!{} // ERROR: lazy_static is not defined in `self`.\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_use.export\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use.export "macro.decl.scope.macro_use.export")\
\
Macros to be imported with `macro_use` must be exported with [`macro_export`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export).\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export "macro.decl.scope.macro_export")\
\
### [The `macro_export` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-macro_export-attribute)\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.intro "macro.decl.scope.macro_export.intro")\
\
The _`macro_export` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes)_ exports the macro from the crate and makes it available in the root of the crate for path-based resolution.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> self::m!();\
> //  ^^^^ OK: Path-based lookup finds `m` in the current module.\
> m!(); // As above.\
>\
> mod inner {\
>     super::m!();\
>     crate::m!();\
> }\
>\
> mod mac {\
>     #[macro_export]\
>     macro_rules! m {\
>         () => {};\
>     }\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.syntax "macro.decl.scope.macro_export.syntax")\
\
The `macro_export` attribute uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) and [MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents) syntaxes. With the [MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents) syntax, it accepts a single [`local_inner_macros`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.local_inner_macros) value.\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.allowed-positions "macro.decl.scope.macro_export.allowed-positions")\
\
The `macro_export` attribute may be applied to `macro_rules` definitions.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` ignores use in other positions but lints against it. This may become an error in the future.\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.duplicates "macro.decl.scope.macro_export.duplicates")\
\
Only the first use of `macro_export` on a macro has effect.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` lints against any use following the first.\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export.path-based\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.path-based "macro.decl.scope.macro_export.path-based")\
\
By default, macros only have [textual scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.textual) and cannot be resolved by path. When the `macro_export` attribute is used, the macro is made available in the crate root and can be referred to by its path.\
\
> Example\
>\
> Without `macro_export`, macros only have textual scope, so path-based resolution of the macro fails.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> macro_rules! m {\
>     () => {};\
> }\
> self::m!(); // ERROR\
> crate::m!(); // ERROR\
> fn main() {}\
> ```\
>\
> With `macro_export`, path-based resolution works.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[macro_export]\
> macro_rules! m {\
>     () => {};\
> }\
> self::m!(); // OK\
> crate::m!(); // OK\
> fn main() {}\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export.export\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.export "macro.decl.scope.macro_export.export")\
\
The `macro_export` attribute causes a macro to be exported from the crate root so that it can be referred to in other crates by path.\
\
> Example\
>\
> Given the following in a `log` crate:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> #[macro_export]\
> macro_rules! warn {\
>     ($message:expr) => { eprintln!("WARN: {}", $message) };\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> From another crate, you can refer to the macro by path:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> fn main() {\
>     log::warn!("example warning");\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export.macro\_use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.macro_use "macro.decl.scope.macro_export.macro_use")\
\
`macro_export` allows the use of [`macro_use`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_use) on an `extern crate` to import the macro into the [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro_use-prelude).\
\
> Example\
>\
> Given the following in a `log` crate:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> #[macro_export]\
> macro_rules! warn {\
>     ($message:expr) => { eprintln!("WARN: {}", $message) };\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> Using `macro_use` in a dependent crate allows you to use the macro from the prelude:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[macro_use]\
> extern crate log;\
>\
> pub mod util {\
>     pub fn do_thing() {\
>         // Resolved via macro prelude.\
>         warn!("example warning");\
>     }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.decl.scope.macro\_export.local\_inner\_macros\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.local_inner_macros "macro.decl.scope.macro_export.local_inner_macros")\
\
Adding `local_inner_macros` to the `macro_export` attribute causes all single-segment macro invocations in the macro definition to have an implicit `$crate::` prefix.\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is intended primarily as a tool to migrate code written before [`$crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.crate) was added to the language to work with Rust 2018’s path-based imports of macros. Its use is discouraged in new code.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> #[macro_export(local_inner_macros)]\
> macro_rules! helped {\
>     () => { helper!() } // Automatically converted to $crate::helper!().\
> }\
>\
> #[macro_export]\
> macro_rules! helper {\
>     () => { () }\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.decl.hygiene\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene "macro.decl.hygiene")\
\
## [Hygiene](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#hygiene)\
\
[\[macro.decl.hygiene.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.intro "macro.decl.hygiene.intro")\
\
Macros by example have _mixed-site hygiene_. This means that [loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels), [block labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels), and local variables are looked up at the macro definition site while other symbols are looked up at the macro invocation site. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = 1;\
fn func() {\
    unreachable!("this is never called")\
}\
\
macro_rules! check {\
    () => {\
        assert_eq!(x, 1); // Uses `x` from the definition site.\
        func();           // Uses `func` from the invocation site.\
    };\
}\
\
{\
    let x = 2;\
    fn func() { /* does not panic */ }\
    check!();\
}\
}\
```\
\
Labels and local variables defined in macro expansion are not shared between invocations, so this code doesn’t compile:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! m {\
    (define) => {\
        let x = 1;\
    };\
    (refer) => {\
        dbg!(x);\
    };\
}\
\
m!(define);\
m!(refer);\
}\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.hygiene.crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.crate "macro.decl.hygiene.crate")\
\
A special case is the `$crate` metavariable. It refers to the crate defining the macro, and can be used at the start of the path to look up items or macros which are not in scope at the invocation site.\
\
```rust\
\
//// Definitions in the `helper_macro` crate.\
#[macro_export]\
macro_rules! helped {\
    // () => { helper!() } // This might lead to an error due to 'helper' not being in scope.\
    () => { $crate::helper!() }\
}\
\
#[macro_export]\
macro_rules! helper {\
    () => { () }\
}\
\
//// Usage in another crate.\
// Note that `helper_macro::helper` is not imported!\
use helper_macro::helped;\
\
fn unit() {\
    helped!();\
}\
```\
\
Note that, because `$crate` refers to the current crate, it must be used with a fully qualified module path when referring to non-macro items:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
pub mod inner {\
    #[macro_export]\
    macro_rules! call_foo {\
        () => { $crate::inner::foo() };\
    }\
\
    pub fn foo() {}\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[macro.decl.hygiene.vis\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.vis "macro.decl.hygiene.vis")\
\
Additionally, even though `$crate` allows a macro to refer to items within its own crate when expanding, its use has no effect on visibility. An item or macro referred to must still be visible from the invocation site. In the following example, any attempt to invoke `call_foo!()` from outside its crate will fail because `foo()` is not public.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[macro_export]\
macro_rules! call_foo {\
    () => { $crate::foo() };\
}\
\
fn foo() {}\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Prior to Rust 1.30, `$crate` and [`local_inner_macros`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export.local_inner_macros) were unsupported. They were added alongside [path-based imports of macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.macro_export), to ensure that helper macros did not need to be manually imported by users of a macro-exporting crate. Crates written for earlier versions of Rust that use helper macros need to be modified to use `$crate` or `local_inner_macros` to work well with path-based imports.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set "macro.decl.follow-set")\
\
## [Follow-set ambiguity restrictions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#follow-set-ambiguity-restrictions)\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.intro "macro.decl.follow-set.intro")\
\
The parser used by the macro system is reasonably powerful, but it is limited in order to prevent ambiguity in current or future versions of the language.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.token-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.token-restriction "macro.decl.follow-set.token-restriction")\
\
In particular, in addition to the rule about ambiguous expansions, a nonterminal matched by a metavariable must be followed by a token which has been decided can be safely used after that kind of match.\
\
As an example, a macro matcher like `$i:expr [ , ]` could in theory be accepted in Rust today, since `[,]` cannot be part of a legal expression and therefore the parse would always be unambiguous. However, because `[` can start trailing expressions, `[` is not a character which can safely be ruled out as coming after an expression. If `[,]` were accepted in a later version of Rust, this matcher would become ambiguous or would misparse, breaking working code. Matchers like `$i:expr,` or `$i:expr;` would be legal, however, because `,` and `;` are legal expression separators. The specific rules are:\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.token-expr-stmt\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.token-expr-stmt "macro.decl.follow-set.token-expr-stmt")\
\
- `expr` and `stmt` may only be followed by one of: `=>`, `,`, or `;`.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.token-pat\_param\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.token-pat_param "macro.decl.follow-set.token-pat_param")\
\
- `pat_param` may only be followed by one of: `=>`, `,`, `=`, `|`, `if`, or `in`.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.token-pat\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.token-pat "macro.decl.follow-set.token-pat")\
\
- `pat` may only be followed by one of: `=>`, `,`, `=`, `if`, or `in`.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.token-path-ty\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.token-path-ty "macro.decl.follow-set.token-path-ty")\
\
- `path` and `ty` may only be followed by one of: `=>`, `,`, `=`, `|`, `;`, `:`, `>`, `>>`, `[`, `{`, `as`, `where`, or a macro variable of `block` fragment specifier.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.token-vis\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.token-vis "macro.decl.follow-set.token-vis")\
\
- `vis` may only be followed by one of: `,`, an identifier other than a non-raw `priv`, any token that can begin a type, or a metavariable with a `ident`, `ty`, or `path` fragment specifier.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.token-other\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.token-other "macro.decl.follow-set.token-other")\
\
- All other fragment specifiers have no restrictions.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.edition2021 "macro.decl.follow-set.edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2021 edition, `pat` may also be followed by `|`.\
\
[\[macro.decl.follow-set.repetition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.follow-set.repetition "macro.decl.follow-set.repetition")\
\
When repetitions are involved, then the rules apply to every possible number of expansions, taking separators into account. This means:\
\
- If the repetition includes a separator, that separator must be able to follow the contents of the repetition.\
- If the repetition can repeat multiple times (`*` or `+`), then the contents must be able to follow themselves.\
- The contents of the repetition must be able to follow whatever comes before, and whatever comes after must be able to follow the contents of the repetition.\
- If the repetition can match zero times (`*` or `?`), then whatever comes after must be able to follow whatever comes before.\
\
For more detail, see the [formal specification](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#appendix-macro-follow-set-ambiguity-formal-specification).\
\
[\[macro.proc\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc "macro.proc")\
\
# [Procedural macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#procedural-macros)\
\
[\[macro.proc.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.intro "macro.proc.intro")\
\
_Procedural macros_ allow creating syntax extensions as execution of a function. Procedural macros come in one of three flavors:\
\
- [Function-like macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-proc_macro-attribute) \- `custom!(...)`\
- [Derive macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive) \- `#[derive(CustomDerive)]`\
- [Attribute macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-proc_macro_attribute-attribute) \- `#[CustomAttribute]`\
\
Procedural macros allow you to run code at compile time that operates over Rust syntax, both consuming and producing Rust syntax. You can sort of think of procedural macros as functions from an AST to another AST.\
\
[\[macro.proc.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.def "macro.proc.def")\
\
Procedural macros must be defined in the root of a crate with the [crate type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#linkage) of `proc-macro`. The macros may not be used from the crate where they are defined, and can only be used when imported in another crate.\
\
> Note\
>\
> When using Cargo, Procedural macro crates are defined with the `proc-macro` key in your manifest:\
>\
> ```toml\
>\
> [lib]\
> proc-macro = true\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.proc.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.result "macro.proc.result")\
\
As functions, they must either return syntax, panic, or loop endlessly. Returned syntax either replaces or adds the syntax depending on the kind of procedural macro. Panics are caught by the compiler and are turned into a compiler error. Endless loops are not caught by the compiler which hangs the compiler.\
\
Procedural macros run during compilation, and thus have the same resources that the compiler has. For example, standard input, error, and output are the same that the compiler has access to. Similarly, file access is the same. Because of this, procedural macros have the same security concerns that [Cargo’s build scripts](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/build-scripts.html) have.\
\
[\[macro.proc.error\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.error "macro.proc.error")\
\
Procedural macros have two ways of reporting errors. The first is to panic. The second is to emit a [`compile_error`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.compile_error.html) macro invocation.\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro-crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro-crate "macro.proc.proc_macro-crate")\
\
## [The `proc_macro` crate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-proc_macro-crate)\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro-crate.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro-crate.intro "macro.proc.proc_macro-crate.intro")\
\
Procedural macro crates almost always will link to the compiler-provided [`proc_macro` crate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/index.html). The `proc_macro` crate provides types required for writing procedural macros and facilities to make it easier.\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro-crate.token-stream\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro-crate.token-stream "macro.proc.proc_macro-crate.token-stream")\
\
This crate primarily contains a [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) type. Procedural macros operate over _token streams_ instead of AST nodes, which is a far more stable interface over time for both the compiler and for procedural macros to target. A _token stream_ is roughly equivalent to `Vec<TokenTree>` where a `TokenTree` can roughly be thought of as lexical token. For example `foo` is an `Ident` token, `.` is a `Punct` token, and `1.2` is a `Literal` token. The `TokenStream` type, unlike `Vec<TokenTree>`, is cheap to clone.\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro-crate.span\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro-crate.span "macro.proc.proc_macro-crate.span")\
\
All tokens have an associated `Span`. A `Span` is an opaque value that cannot be modified but can be manufactured. `Span`s represent an extent of source code within a program and are primarily used for error reporting. While you cannot modify a `Span` itself, you can always change the `Span` _associated_ with any token, such as through getting a `Span` from another token.\
\
[\[macro.proc.hygiene\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.hygiene "macro.proc.hygiene")\
\
## [Procedural macro hygiene](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#procedural-macro-hygiene)\
\
Procedural macros are _unhygienic_. This means they behave as if the output token stream was simply written inline to the code it’s next to. This means that it’s affected by external items and also affects external imports.\
\
Macro authors need to be careful to ensure their macros work in as many contexts as possible given this limitation. This often includes using absolute paths to items in libraries (for example, `::std::option::Option` instead of `Option`) or by ensuring that generated functions have names that are unlikely to clash with other functions (like `__internal_foo` instead of `foo`).\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro "macro.proc.proc_macro")\
\
## [The `proc_macro` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-proc_macro-attribute)\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro.intro "macro.proc.proc_macro.intro")\
\
The _`proc_macro` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ defines a [function-like](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation) procedural macro.\
\
> Example\
>\
> This macro definition ignores its input and emits a function `answer` into its scope.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![crate_type = "proc-macro"]\
> extern crate proc_macro;\
> use proc_macro::TokenStream;\
>\
> #[proc_macro]\
> pub fn make_answer(_item: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {\
>     "fn answer() -> u32 { 42 }".parse().unwrap()\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> We can use it in a binary crate to print “42” to standard output.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> extern crate proc_macro_examples;\
> use proc_macro_examples::make_answer;\
>\
> make_answer!();\
>\
> fn main() {\
>     println!("{}", answer());\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro.syntax "macro.proc.proc_macro.syntax")\
\
The `proc_macro` attribute uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) syntax.\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro.allowed-positions "macro.proc.proc_macro.allowed-positions")\
\
The `proc_macro` attribute may only be applied to a `pub` function of type `fn(TokenStream) -> TokenStream` where [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) comes from the [`proc_macro` crate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/index.html). It must have the [“Rust” ABI](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern). No other function qualifiers are allowed. It must be located in the root of the crate.\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro.duplicates "macro.proc.proc_macro.duplicates")\
\
The `proc_macro` attribute may only be specified once on a function.\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro.namespace "macro.proc.proc_macro.namespace")\
\
The `proc_macro` attribute publicly defines the macro in the [macro namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) in the root of the crate with the same name as the function.\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro.behavior "macro.proc.proc_macro.behavior")\
\
A function-like macro invocation of a function-like procedural macro will pass what is inside the delimiters of the macro invocation as the input [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) argument and replace the entire macro invocation with the output [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) of the function.\
\
[\[macro.proc.proc\_macro.invocation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.proc_macro.invocation "macro.proc.proc_macro.invocation")\
\
Function-like procedural macros may be invoked in any macro invocation position, which includes:\
\
- [Statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#statements)\
- [Expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expressions)\
- [Patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns)\
- [Type expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-expressions)\
- [Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items) positions, including items in [`extern` blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks)\
- Inherent and trait [implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations)\
- [Trait definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits)\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive "macro.proc.derive")\
\
## [The `proc_macro_derive` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-proc_macro_derive-attribute)\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.intro "macro.proc.derive.intro")\
\
Applying the _`proc_macro_derive` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ to a function defines a _derive macro_ that can be invoked by the [`derive` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive). These macros are given the token stream of a [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), [enum](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations), or [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions) definition and can emit new [items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items) after it. They can also declare and use [derive macro helper attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive-macro-helper-attributes).\
\
> Example\
>\
> This derive macro ignores its input and appends tokens that define a function.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![crate_type = "proc-macro"]\
> extern crate proc_macro;\
> use proc_macro::TokenStream;\
>\
> #[proc_macro_derive(AnswerFn)]\
> pub fn derive_answer_fn(_item: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {\
>     "fn answer() -> u32 { 42 }".parse().unwrap()\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> To use it, we might write:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> extern crate proc_macro_examples;\
> use proc_macro_examples::AnswerFn;\
>\
> #[derive(AnswerFn)]\
> struct Struct;\
>\
> fn main() {\
>     assert_eq!(42, answer());\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.syntax "macro.proc.derive.syntax")\
\
The syntax for the `proc_macro_derive` attribute is:\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ProcMacroDeriveAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ProcMacroDeriveAttribute) →\
\
proc\_macro\_derive([DeriveMacroName](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DeriveMacroName) ( ,[DeriveMacroAttributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DeriveMacroAttributes) )?,?)\
\
[DeriveMacroName](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DeriveMacroName) → [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)\
\
[DeriveMacroAttributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DeriveMacroAttributes) →\
\
attributes( ( [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) ( ,[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) )\*,? )?)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ProcMacroDeriveAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ProcMacroDeriveAttribute)\
proc\_macro\_derive\
([DeriveMacroName](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DeriveMacroName)\
,[DeriveMacroAttributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DeriveMacroAttributes)\
,\
)\
\
[DeriveMacroName](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DeriveMacroName)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
\
[DeriveMacroAttributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DeriveMacroAttributes)\
attributes\
([IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
,[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
,\
)\
\
The name of the derive macro is given by [DeriveMacroName](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DeriveMacroName). The optional `attributes` argument is described in [macro.proc.derive.attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.attributes).\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.allowed-positions "macro.proc.derive.allowed-positions")\
\
The `proc_macro_derive` attribute may only be applied to a `pub` function with the [Rust ABI](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern) defined in the root of the crate with a type of `fn(TokenStream) -> TokenStream` where [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) comes from the [`proc_macro` crate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/index.html). The function may be `const` and may use `extern` to explicitly specify the Rust ABI, but it may not use any other [qualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FunctionQualifiers) (e.g. it may not be `async` or `unsafe`).\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.duplicates "macro.proc.derive.duplicates")\
\
The `proc_macro_derive` attribute may be used only once on a function.\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.namespace "macro.proc.derive.namespace")\
\
The `proc_macro_derive` attribute publicly defines the derive macro in the [macro namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) in the root of the crate.\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.output\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.output "macro.proc.derive.output")\
\
The input [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) is the token stream of the item to which the `derive` attribute is applied. The output [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) must be a (possibly empty) set of items. These items are appended following the input item within the same [module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules) or [block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions).\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.attributes "macro.proc.derive.attributes")\
\
### [Derive macro helper attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#derive-macro-helper-attributes)\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.attributes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.attributes.intro "macro.proc.derive.attributes.intro")\
\
Derive macros can declare _derive macro helper attributes_ to be used within the scope of the [item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items) to which the derive macro is applied. These [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) are [inert](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#active-and-inert-attributes). While their purpose is to be used by the macro that declared them, they can be seen by any macro.\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.attributes.decl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.attributes.decl "macro.proc.derive.attributes.decl")\
\
A helper attribute for a derive macro is declared by adding its identifier to the `attributes` list in the `proc_macro_derive` attribute.\
\
> Example\
>\
> This declares a helper attribute and then ignores it.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![crate_type="proc-macro"]\
> extern crate proc_macro;\
> use proc_macro::TokenStream;\
>\
> #[proc_macro_derive(WithHelperAttr, attributes(helper))]\
> pub fn derive_with_helper_attr(_item: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {\
>     TokenStream::new()\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> To use it, we might write:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[derive(WithHelperAttr)]\
> struct Struct {\
>     #[helper] field: (),\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.proc.derive.attributes.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.attributes.scope "macro.proc.derive.attributes.scope")\
\
When a derive macro invocation is applied to an item, the helper attributes introduced by that derive macro become in scope 1) for attributes that are applied to that item and are applied lexically after the derive macro invocation and 2) for attributes that are applied to fields and variants inside of the item.\
\
> Note\
>\
> rustc currently allows derive helpers to be used before the macro that introduces them. Such derive helpers used out of order may not shadow other attribute macros. This behavior is deprecated and slated for removal.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[helper] // Deprecated, hard error in the future.\
> #[derive(WithHelperAttr)]\
> struct Struct {\
>     field: (),\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> For more details, see [Rust issue #79202](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/79202).\
\
[\[macro.proc.attribute\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute "macro.proc.attribute")\
\
## [The `proc_macro_attribute` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-proc_macro_attribute-attribute)\
\
[\[macro.proc.attribute.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute.intro "macro.proc.attribute.intro")\
\
The _`proc_macro_attribute` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ defines an _attribute macro_ which can be used as an [outer attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1).\
\
> Example\
>\
> This attribute macro takes the input stream and emits it as-is, effectively being a no-op attribute.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![crate_type = "proc-macro"]\
> extern crate proc_macro;\
> use proc_macro::TokenStream;\
>\
> #[proc_macro_attribute]\
> pub fn return_as_is(_attr: TokenStream, item: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {\
>     item\
> }\
> ```\
\
> Example\
>\
> This shows, in the output of the compiler, the stringified [`TokenStream`s](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) that attribute macros see.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> // my-macro/src/lib.rs\
> extern crate proc_macro;\
> use proc_macro::TokenStream;\
> #[proc_macro_attribute]\
> pub fn show_streams(attr: TokenStream, item: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {\
>     println!("attr: \"{attr}\"");\
>     println!("item: \"{item}\"");\
>     item\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> // src/lib.rs\
> extern crate my_macro;\
>\
> use my_macro::show_streams;\
>\
> // Example: Basic function.\
> #[show_streams]\
> fn invoke1() {}\
> // out: attr: ""\
> // out: item: "fn invoke1() {}"\
>\
> // Example: Attribute with input.\
> #[show_streams(bar)]\
> fn invoke2() {}\
> // out: attr: "bar"\
> // out: item: "fn invoke2() {}"\
>\
> // Example: Multiple tokens in the input.\
> #[show_streams(multiple => tokens)]\
> fn invoke3() {}\
> // out: attr: "multiple => tokens"\
> // out: item: "fn invoke3() {}"\
>\
> // Example: Delimiters in the input.\
> #[show_streams { delimiters }]\
> fn invoke4() {}\
> // out: attr: "delimiters"\
> // out: item: "fn invoke4() {}"\
> ```\
\
[\[macro.proc.attribute.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute.syntax "macro.proc.attribute.syntax")\
\
The `proc_macro_attribute` attribute uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) syntax.\
\
[\[macro.proc.attribute.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute.allowed-positions "macro.proc.attribute.allowed-positions")\
\
The `proc_macro_attribute` attribute may only be applied to a `pub` function of type `fn(TokenStream, TokenStream) -> TokenStream` where [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) comes from the [`proc_macro` crate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/index.html). It must have the [“Rust” ABI](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern). No other function qualifiers are allowed. It must be located in the root of the crate.\
\
[\[macro.proc.attribute.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute.duplicates "macro.proc.attribute.duplicates")\
\
The `proc_macro_attribute` attribute may only be specified once on a function.\
\
[\[macro.proc.attribute.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute.namespace "macro.proc.attribute.namespace")\
\
The `proc_macro_attribute` attribute defines the attribute in the [macro namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) in the root of the crate with the same name as the function.\
\
[\[macro.proc.attribute.use-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute.use-positions "macro.proc.attribute.use-positions")\
\
Attribute macros can only be used on:\
\
- [Items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items)\
- Items in [`extern` blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks)\
- Inherent and trait [implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations)\
- [Trait definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits)\
\
[\[macro.proc.attribute.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute.behavior "macro.proc.attribute.behavior")\
\
The first [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) parameter is the delimited token tree following the attribute’s name but not including the outer delimiters. If the applied attribute contains only the attribute name or the attribute name followed by empty delimiters, the [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) is empty.\
\
The second [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html) is the rest of the [item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items), including other [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) on the [item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items).\
\
The item to which the attribute is applied is replaced by the zero or more items in the returned [`TokenStream`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.TokenStream.html).\
\
[\[macro.proc.token\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.token "macro.proc.token")\
\
## [Declarative macro tokens and procedural macro tokens](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#declarative-macro-tokens-and-procedural-macro-tokens)\
\
[\[macro.proc.token.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.token.intro "macro.proc.token.intro")\
\
Declarative `macro_rules` macros and procedural macros use similar, but different definitions for tokens (or rather [`TokenTree`s](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/enum.TokenTree.html).)\
\
[\[macro.proc.token.macro\_rules\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.token.macro_rules "macro.proc.token.macro_rules")\
\
Token trees in `macro_rules` (corresponding to `tt` matchers) are defined as\
\
- Delimited groups (`(...)`, `{...}`, etc)\
- All operators supported by the language, both single-character and multi-character ones (`+`, `+=`).\
\
  - Note that this set doesn’t include the single quote `'`.\
- Literals (`"string"`, `1`, etc)\
\
  - Note that negation (e.g. `-1`) is never a part of such literal tokens, but a separate operator token.\
- Identifiers, including keywords (`ident`, `r#ident`, `fn`)\
- Lifetimes (`'ident`)\
- Metavariable substitutions in `macro_rules` (e.g. `$my_expr` in `macro_rules! mac { ($my_expr: expr) => { $my_expr } }` after the `mac`’s expansion, which will be considered a single token tree regardless of the passed expression)\
\
[\[macro.proc.token.tree\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.token.tree "macro.proc.token.tree")\
\
Token trees in procedural macros are defined as\
\
- Delimited groups (`(...)`, `{...}`, etc)\
- All punctuation characters used in operators supported by the language (`+`, but not `+=`), and also the single quote `'` character (typically used in lifetimes, see below for lifetime splitting and joining behavior)\
- Literals (`"string"`, `1`, etc)\
\
  - Negation (e.g. `-1`) is supported as a part of integer and floating point literals.\
- Identifiers, including keywords (`ident`, `r#ident`, `fn`)\
\
[\[macro.proc.token.conversion.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.token.conversion.intro "macro.proc.token.conversion.intro")\
\
Mismatches between these two definitions are accounted for when token streams are passed to and from procedural macros. Note that the conversions below may happen lazily, so they might not happen if the tokens are not actually inspected.\
\
[\[macro.proc.token.conversion.to-proc\_macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.token.conversion.to-proc_macro "macro.proc.token.conversion.to-proc_macro")\
\
When passed to a proc-macro\
\
- All multi-character operators are broken into single characters.\
- Lifetimes are broken into a `'` character and an identifier.\
- The keyword metavariable [`$crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.crate) is passed as a single identifier.\
- All other metavariable substitutions are represented as their underlying token streams.\
  - Such token streams may be wrapped into delimited groups ( [`Group`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.Group.html)) with implicit delimiters ( [`Delimiter::None`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/enum.Delimiter.html#variant.None)) when it’s necessary for preserving parsing priorities.\
  - `tt` and `ident` substitutions are never wrapped into such groups and always represented as their underlying token trees.\
\
[\[macro.proc.token.conversion.from-proc\_macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.token.conversion.from-proc_macro "macro.proc.token.conversion.from-proc_macro")\
\
When emitted from a proc macro\
\
- Punctuation characters are glued into multi-character operators when applicable.\
- Single quotes `'` joined with identifiers are glued into lifetimes.\
- Negative literals are converted into two tokens (the `-` and the literal) possibly wrapped into a delimited group ( [`Group`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/struct.Group.html)) with implicit delimiters ( [`Delimiter::None`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/proc_macro/enum.Delimiter.html#variant.None)) when it’s necessary for preserving parsing priorities.\
\
[\[macro.proc.token.doc-comment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.token.doc-comment "macro.proc.token.doc-comment")\
\
Note that neither declarative nor procedural macros support doc comment tokens (e.g. `/// Doc`), so they are always converted to token streams representing their equivalent `#[doc = r"str"]` attributes when passed to macros.\
\
[\[crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate "crate")\
\
# [Crates and source files](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#crates-and-source-files)\
\
[\[crate.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.syntax "crate.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Crate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Crate) →\
\
[InnerAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InnerAttribute)\*\
\
[Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Item)\*\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Crate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Crate)[InnerAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-InnerAttribute)[Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Item)\
\
> Note\
>\
> Although Rust, like any other language, can be implemented by an interpreter as well as a compiler, the only existing implementation is a compiler, and the language has always been designed to be compiled. For these reasons, this section assumes a compiler.\
\
[\[crate.compile-time\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.compile-time "crate.compile-time")\
\
Rust’s semantics obey a _phase distinction_ between compile-time and run-time.[1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-phase-distinction) Semantic rules that have a _static interpretation_ govern the success or failure of compilation, while semantic rules that have a _dynamic interpretation_ govern the behavior of the program at run-time.\
\
[\[crate.unit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.unit "crate.unit")\
\
The compilation model centers on artifacts called _crates_. Each compilation processes a single crate in source form, and if successful, produces a single crate in binary form: either an executable or some sort of library.[2](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-cratesourcefile)\
\
[\[crate.module\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.module "crate.module")\
\
A _crate_ is a unit of compilation and linking, as well as versioning, distribution, and runtime loading. A crate contains a _tree_ of nested [module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules) scopes. The top level of this tree is a module that is anonymous (from the point of view of paths within the module) and any item within a crate has a canonical [module path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) denoting its location within the crate’s module tree.\
\
[\[crate.input-source\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.input-source "crate.input-source")\
\
The Rust compiler is always invoked with a single source file as input, and always produces a single output crate. The processing of that source file may result in other source files being loaded as modules. Source files have the extension `.rs`.\
\
[\[crate.module-def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.module-def "crate.module-def")\
\
A Rust source file describes a module, the name and location of which — in the module tree of the current crate — are defined from outside the source file: either by an explicit [Module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Module) item in a referencing source file, or by the name of the crate itself.\
\
[\[crate.inline-module\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.inline-module "crate.inline-module")\
\
Every source file is a module, but not every module needs its own source file: [module definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules) can be nested within one file.\
\
[\[crate.items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.items "crate.items")\
\
Each source file contains a sequence of zero or more [Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Item) definitions, and may optionally begin with any number of [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) that apply to the containing module, most of which influence the behavior of the compiler.\
\
[\[crate.attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.attributes "crate.attributes")\
\
The anonymous crate module can have additional attributes that apply to the crate as a whole.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The file’s contents may be preceded by a [shebang](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#shebang).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Specify the crate name.\
#![crate_name = "projx"]\
\
// Specify the type of output artifact.\
#![crate_type = "lib"]\
\
// Turn on a warning.\
// This can be done in any module, not just the anonymous crate module.\
#![warn(non_camel_case_types)]\
}\
```\
\
[\[crate.main\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.main "crate.main")\
\
## [Main functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#main-functions)\
\
[\[crate.main.executable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.main.executable "crate.main.executable")\
\
A crate that contains a `main` [function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions) can be compiled to an executable.\
\
[\[crate.main.restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.main.restriction "crate.main.restriction")\
\
If a `main` function is present, it must take no arguments, must not declare any [trait or lifetime bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-and-lifetime-bounds), must not have any [where clauses](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#where-clauses), and its return type must implement the [`Termination`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/process/trait.Termination.html) trait.\
\
```rust\
\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
fn main() -> ! {\
    std::process::exit(0);\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
fn main() -> impl std::process::Termination {\
    std::process::ExitCode::SUCCESS\
}\
```\
\
[\[crate.main.import\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.main.import "crate.main.import")\
\
The `main` function may be an import, e.g. from an external crate or from the current one.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod foo {\
    pub fn bar() {\
        println!("Hello, world!");\
    }\
}\
use foo::bar as main;\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Types with implementations of [`Termination`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/process/trait.Termination.html) in the standard library include:\
>\
> - `()`\
> - [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#never-type)\
> - [`Infallible`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/convert/enum.Infallible.html)\
> - [`ExitCode`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/process/struct.ExitCode.html)\
> - `Result<T, E> where T: Termination, E: Debug`\
\
[\[crate.uncaught-foreign-unwinding\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.uncaught-foreign-unwinding "crate.uncaught-foreign-unwinding")\
\
### [Uncaught foreign unwinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#uncaught-foreign-unwinding)\
\
When a “foreign” unwind (e.g. an exception thrown from C++ code, or a `panic!` in Rust code using a different panic handler) propagates beyond the `main` function, the process will be safely terminated. This may take the form of an abort, in which case it is not guaranteed that any `Drop` calls will be executed, and the error output may be less informative than if the runtime had been terminated by a “native” Rust `panic`.\
\
For more information, see the [panic documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unwinding-across-ffi-boundaries).\
\
[\[crate.no\_main\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.no_main "crate.no_main")\
\
### [The `no_main` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-no_main-attribute)\
\
The _`no_main` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ may be applied at the crate level to disable emitting the `main` symbol for an executable binary. This is useful when some other object being linked to defines `main`.\
\
[\[crate.crate\_name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.crate_name "crate.crate_name")\
\
## [The `crate_name` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-crate_name-attribute)\
\
[\[crate.crate\_name.general\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.crate_name.general "crate.crate_name.general")\
\
The _`crate_name` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ may be applied at the crate level to specify the name of the crate with the [MetaNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaNameValueStr) syntax.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
#![crate_name = "mycrate"]\
fn main() {\
}\
```\
\
[\[crate.crate\_name.restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-crate.crate_name.restriction "crate.crate_name.restriction")\
\
The crate name must not be empty, and must only contain [Unicode alphanumeric](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.char.html#method.is_alphanumeric) or `_` (U+005F) characters.\
\
* * *\
\
1. This distinction would also exist in an interpreter. Static checks like syntactic analysis, type checking, and lints should happen before the program is executed regardless of when it is executed. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-phase-distinction-1)\
\
2. A crate is somewhat analogous to an _assembly_ in the ECMA-335 CLI model, a _library_ in the SML/NJ Compilation Manager, a _unit_ in the Owens and Flatt module system, or a _configuration_ in Mesa. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-cratesourcefile-1)\
\
\
[\[cfg\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg "cfg")\
\
# [Conditional compilation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#conditional-compilation)\
\
[\[cfg.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.syntax "cfg.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicate) →\
\
[ConfigurationOption](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationOption)\
\
    \| [ConfigurationAll](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationAll)\
\
    \| [ConfigurationAny](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationAny)\
\
    \| [ConfigurationNot](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationNot)\
\
    \| true\
\
    \| false\
\
[ConfigurationOption](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationOption) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) ( = ( [STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL) \| [RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_LITERAL) ) )?\
\
[ConfigurationAll](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationAll) →\
\
all([ConfigurationPredicateList](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicateList)?)\
\
[ConfigurationAny](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationAny) →\
\
any([ConfigurationPredicateList](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicateList)?)\
\
[ConfigurationNot](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationNot) →\
\
not([ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicate))\
\
[ConfigurationPredicateList](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicateList) →\
\
[ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicate) ( ,[ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicate) )\*,?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicate)[ConfigurationOption](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationOption)[ConfigurationAll](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationAll)[ConfigurationAny](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationAny)[ConfigurationNot](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationNot)\
true\
false\
\
[ConfigurationOption](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationOption)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
=[STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_LITERAL) [RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
[ConfigurationAll](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationAll)\
all\
([ConfigurationPredicateList](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicateList)\
)\
\
[ConfigurationAny](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationAny)\
any\
([ConfigurationPredicateList](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicateList)\
)\
\
[ConfigurationNot](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationNot)\
not\
([ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicate)\
)\
\
[ConfigurationPredicateList](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicateList)[ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicate)\
,[ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicate)\
,\
\
[\[cfg.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.intro "cfg.intro")\
\
_Conditionally compiled source code_ is source code that is compiled only under certain conditions.\
\
[\[cfg.attributes-macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attributes-macro "cfg.attributes-macro")\
\
Source code can be made conditionally compiled using the [`cfg`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg-attribute) and [`cfg_attr`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg_attr-attribute) [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) and the built-in [`cfg!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg-macro) and [`cfg_select!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg_select-macro) [macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros).\
\
[\[cfg.conditional\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.conditional "cfg.conditional")\
\
Whether to compile can depend on the target architecture of the compiled crate, arbitrary values passed to the compiler, and other things further described below.\
\
[\[cfg.predicate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.predicate "cfg.predicate")\
\
Each form of conditional compilation takes a _configuration predicate_ that evaluates to true or false. The predicate is one of the following:\
\
[\[cfg.predicate.option\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.predicate.option "cfg.predicate.option")\
\
- A configuration option. The predicate is true if the option is set, and false if it is unset.\
\
[\[cfg.predicate.all\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.predicate.all "cfg.predicate.all")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfgs-on-items.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfgs-on-items.rs)\
\
- `all()` with a comma-separated list of configuration predicates. It is true if all of the given predicates are true, or if the list is empty.\
\
[\[cfg.predicate.any\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.predicate.any "cfg.predicate.any")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfgs-on-items.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfgs-on-items.rs)\
\
- `any()` with a comma-separated list of configuration predicates. It is true if at least one of the given predicates is true. If there are no predicates, it is false.\
\
[\[cfg.predicate.not\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.predicate.not "cfg.predicate.not")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfgs-on-items.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfgs-on-items.rs)\
\
- `not()` with a configuration predicate. It is true if its predicate is false and false if its predicate is true.\
\
[\[cfg.predicate.literal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.predicate.literal "cfg.predicate.literal")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-false-use-item.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-false-use-item.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/true-false.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/true-false.rs)\
\
- `true` or `false` literals, which are always true or false respectively.\
\
[\[cfg.option-spec\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.option-spec "cfg.option-spec")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-path-error.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-path-error.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-cfg-pred.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-cfg-pred.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-check-cfg-pred.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-check-cfg-pred.rs)\
\
_Configuration options_ are either names or key-value pairs, and are either set or unset.\
\
[\[cfg.option-name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.option-name "cfg.option-name")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-cfg-pred.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-cfg-pred.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-check-cfg-pred.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-check-cfg-pred.rs)\
\
Names are written as a single identifier, such as `unix`.\
\
[\[cfg.option-key-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.option-key-value "cfg.option-key-value")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-cfg-pred.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-cfg-pred.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-check-cfg-pred.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/invalid-cli-check-cfg-pred.rs)\
\
Key-value pairs are written as an identifier, `=`, and then a string, such as `target_arch = "x86_64"`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Whitespace around the `=` is ignored, so `foo="bar"` and `foo = "bar"` are equivalent.\
\
[\[cfg.option-key-uniqueness\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.option-key-uniqueness "cfg.option-key-uniqueness")\
\
Keys do not need to be unique. For example, both `feature = "std"` and `feature = "serde"` can be set at the same time.\
\
[\[cfg.options.set\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.options.set "cfg.options.set")\
\
## [Set configuration options](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#set-configuration-options)\
\
[\[cfg.options.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.options.intro "cfg.options.intro")\
\
Which configuration options are set is determined statically during the compilation of the crate.\
\
[\[cfg.options.target\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.options.target "cfg.options.target")\
\
Some options are _compiler-set_ based on data about the compilation.\
\
[\[cfg.options.other\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.options.other "cfg.options.other")\
\
Other options are _arbitrarily-set_ based on input passed to the compiler outside of the code.\
\
[\[cfg.options.crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.options.crate "cfg.options.crate")\
\
It is not possible to set a configuration option from within the source code of the crate being compiled.\
\
> Note\
>\
> For `rustc`, arbitrary-set configuration options are set using the [`--cfg`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/command-line-arguments.html#--cfg-configure-the-compilation-environment) flag. Configuration values for a specified target can be displayed with `rustc --print cfg --target $TARGET`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Configuration options with the key `feature` are a convention used by [Cargo](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/features.html) for specifying compile-time options and optional dependencies.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_arch\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_arch "cfg.target_arch")\
\
### [`target_arch`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_arch)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_arch.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_arch.def "cfg.target_arch.def")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/conditional-compile-arch.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/conditional-compile-arch.rs)\
\
Key-value option set once with the target’s CPU architecture. The value is similar to the first element of the platform’s target triple, but not identical.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_arch.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_arch.values "cfg.target_arch.values")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/conditional-compile-arch.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/conditional-compile-arch.rs)\
\
Example values:\
\
- `"x86"`\
- `"x86_64"`\
- `"mips"`\
- `"powerpc"`\
- `"powerpc64"`\
- `"arm"`\
- `"aarch64"`\
\
[\[cfg.target\_feature\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_feature "cfg.target_feature")\
\
### [`target_feature`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_feature)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_feature.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_feature.def "cfg.target_feature.def")\
\
Key-value option set for each platform feature available for the current compilation target.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_feature.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_feature.values "cfg.target_feature.values")\
\
Example values:\
\
- `"avx"`\
- `"avx2"`\
- `"crt-static"`\
- `"rdrand"`\
- `"sse"`\
- `"sse2"`\
- `"sse4.1"`\
\
See the [`target_feature` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-target_feature-attribute) for more details on the available features.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_feature.crt\_static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_feature.crt_static "cfg.target_feature.crt_static")\
\
An additional feature of `crt-static` is available to the `target_feature` option to indicate that a [static C runtime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-and-dynamic-c-runtimes) is available.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_os\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_os "cfg.target_os")\
\
### [`target_os`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_os)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_os.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_os.def "cfg.target_os.def")\
\
Key-value option set once with the target’s operating system. This value is similar to the second and third element of the platform’s target triple.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_os.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_os.values "cfg.target_os.values")\
\
Example values:\
\
- `"windows"`\
- `"macos"`\
- `"ios"`\
- `"linux"`\
- `"android"`\
- `"freebsd"`\
- `"dragonfly"`\
- `"openbsd"`\
- `"netbsd"`\
- `"none"` (typical for embedded targets)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_family\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_family "cfg.target_family")\
\
### [`target_family`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_family)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_family.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_family.def "cfg.target_family.def")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-family.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-family.rs)\
\
Key-value option providing a more generic description of a target, such as the family of the operating systems or architectures that the target generally falls into. Any number of `target_family` key-value pairs can be set.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_family.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_family.values "cfg.target_family.values")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-family.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-family.rs)\
\
Example values:\
\
- `"unix"`\
- `"windows"`\
- `"wasm"`\
- Both `"unix"` and `"wasm"`\
\
[\[cfg.target\_family.unix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_family.unix "cfg.target_family.unix")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-family.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-family.rs)\
\
### [`unix` and `windows`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unix-and-windows)\
\
`unix` is set if `target_family = "unix"` is set.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_family.windows\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_family.windows "cfg.target_family.windows")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-family.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-family.rs)\
\
`windows` is set if `target_family = "windows"` is set.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_env\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_env "cfg.target_env")\
\
### [`target_env`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_env)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_env.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_env.def "cfg.target_env.def")\
\
Key-value option set with further disambiguating information about the target platform with information about the ABI or `libc` used. For historical reasons, this value is only defined as not the empty-string when actually needed for disambiguation. Thus, for example, on many GNU platforms, this value will be empty. This value is similar to the fourth element of the platform’s target triple. One difference is that embedded ABIs such as `gnueabihf` will simply define `target_env` as `"gnu"`.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_env.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_env.values "cfg.target_env.values")\
\
Example values:\
\
- `""`\
- `"gnu"`\
- `"msvc"`\
- `"musl"`\
- `"sgx"`\
- `"sim"`\
- `"macabi"`\
\
[\[cfg.target\_abi\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_abi "cfg.target_abi")\
\
### [`target_abi`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_abi)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_abi.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_abi.def "cfg.target_abi.def")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-abi.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-abi.rs)\
\
Key-value option set to further disambiguate the target with information about the target ABI.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_abi.disambiguation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_abi.disambiguation "cfg.target_abi.disambiguation")\
\
For historical reasons, this value is only defined as not the empty-string when actually needed for disambiguation. Thus, for example, on many GNU platforms, this value will be empty.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_abi.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_abi.values "cfg.target_abi.values")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-abi.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-abi.rs)\
\
Example values:\
\
- `""`\
- `"llvm"`\
- `"eabihf"`\
- `"abi64"`\
\
[\[cfg.target\_endian\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_endian "cfg.target_endian")\
\
### [`target_endian`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_endian)\
\
Key-value option set once with either a value of “little” or “big” depending on the endianness of the target’s CPU.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_pointer\_width\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_pointer_width "cfg.target_pointer_width")\
\
### [`target_pointer_width`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_pointer_width)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_pointer\_width.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_pointer_width.def "cfg.target_pointer_width.def")\
\
Key-value option set once with the target’s pointer width in bits.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_pointer\_width.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_pointer_width.values "cfg.target_pointer_width.values")\
\
Example values:\
\
- `"16"`\
- `"32"`\
- `"64"`\
\
[\[cfg.target\_vendor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_vendor "cfg.target_vendor")\
\
### [`target_vendor`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_vendor)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_vendor.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_vendor.def "cfg.target_vendor.def")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-vendor.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-target-vendor.rs)\
\
Key-value option set once with the vendor of the target.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_vendor.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_vendor.values "cfg.target_vendor.values")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\

[\[cfg.target\_has\_atomic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_has_atomic "cfg.target_has_atomic")\
\
### [`target_has_atomic`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#target_has_atomic)\
\
[\[cfg.target\_has\_atomic.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_has_atomic.def "cfg.target_has_atomic.def")\
\
Key-value option set for each bit width that the target supports atomic loads, stores, and compare-and-swap operations.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_has\_atomic.stdlib\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_has_atomic.stdlib "cfg.target_has_atomic.stdlib")\
\
When this cfg is present, all of the stable [`core::sync::atomic`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/sync/atomic/index.html) APIs are available for the relevant atomic width.\
\
[\[cfg.target\_has\_atomic.values\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.target_has_atomic.values "cfg.target_has_atomic.values")\
\
Possible values:\
\
- `"8"`\
- `"16"`\
- `"32"`\
- `"64"`\
- `"128"`\
- `"ptr"`\
\
[\[cfg.test\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.test "cfg.test")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/test-attrs/test-vs-cfg-test.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/test-attrs/test-vs-cfg-test.rs)\
\
### [`test`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#test)\
\
Enabled when compiling the test harness. Done with `rustc` by using the [`--test`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/command-line-arguments.html#--test-build-a-test-harness) flag. See [Testing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#testing-attributes) for more on testing support.\
\
[\[cfg.debug\_assertions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.debug_assertions "cfg.debug_assertions")\
\
### [`debug_assertions`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#debug_assertions)\
\
Enabled by default when compiling without optimizations. This can be used to enable extra debugging code in development but not in production. For example, it controls the behavior of the standard library’s [`debug_assert!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.debug_assert.html) macro.\
\
[\[cfg.proc\_macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.proc_macro "cfg.proc_macro")\
\
### [`proc_macro`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#proc_macro)\
\
Set when the crate being compiled is being compiled with the `proc_macro` [crate type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#linkage).\
\
[\[cfg.panic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.panic "cfg.panic")\
\
### [`panic`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#panic)\
\
[\[cfg.panic.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.panic.def "cfg.panic.def")\
\

- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-panic.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-panic.rs)\
\
Key-value option set depending on the [panic strategy](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#panic-strategy). Note that more values may be added in the future.\
\
