## [Forms of conditional compilation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#forms-of-conditional-compilation)\
\
[\[cfg.attr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attr "cfg.attr")\
\
### [The `cfg` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-cfg-attribute)\
\
[\[cfg.attr.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attr.intro "cfg.attr.intro")\
\
The _`cfg` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ conditionally includes the form to which it is attached based on a configuration predicate.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> // The function is only included in the build when compiling for macOS\
> #[cfg(target_os = "macos")]\
> fn macos_only() {\
>   // ...\
> }\
>\
> // This function is only included when either foo or bar is defined\
> #[cfg(any(foo, bar))]\
> fn needs_foo_or_bar() {\
>   // ...\
> }\
>\
> // This function is only included when compiling for a unixish OS with a 32-bit\
> // architecture\
> #[cfg(all(unix, target_pointer_width = "32"))]\
> fn on_32bit_unix() {\
>   // ...\
> }\
>\
> // This function is only included when foo is not defined\
> #[cfg(not(foo))]\
> fn needs_not_foo() {\
>   // ...\
> }\
>\
> // This function is only included when the panic strategy is set to unwind\
> #[cfg(panic = "unwind")]\
> fn when_unwinding() {\
>   // ...\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[cfg.attr.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attr.syntax "cfg.attr.syntax")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/path-kw-as-cfg-pred.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/path-kw-as-cfg-pred.rs)\
\
The syntax for the `cfg` attribute is:\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[CfgAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgAttribute) → cfg([ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicate))\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[CfgAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgAttribute)\
cfg\
([ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicate)\
)\
\
[\[cfg.attr.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attr.allowed-positions "cfg.attr.allowed-positions")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg\_stmt\_expr.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg_stmt_expr.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/conditional-compile.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/conditional-compile.rs)\
\
The `cfg` attribute may be used anywhere attributes are allowed.\
\
[\[cfg.attr.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attr.duplicates "cfg.attr.duplicates")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/both-true-false.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/both-true-false.rs)\
\
The `cfg` attribute may be used any number of times on a form. The form to which the attributes are attached will not be included if any of the `cfg` predicates are false except as described in [cfg.attr.crate-level-attrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attr.crate-level-attrs).\
\
[\[cfg.attr.effect\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attr.effect "cfg.attr.effect")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg-false-use-item.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg-false-use-item.rs)\
\
If the predicates are true, the form is rewritten to not have the `cfg` attributes on it. If any predicate is false, the form is removed from the source code.\
\
[\[cfg.attr.crate-level-attrs\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.attr.crate-level-attrs "cfg.attr.crate-level-attrs")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg\_false\_no\_std-1.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg_false_no_std-1.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg\_false\_no\_std-2.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg_false_no_std-2.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg\_false\_no\_std.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg_false_no_std.rs)\
\
When a crate-level `cfg` has a false predicate, the crate itself still exists. Any crate attributes preceding the `cfg` are kept, and any crate attributes following the `cfg` are removed as well as removing all of the following crate contents.\
\
> Example\
>\
> The behavior of not removing the preceding attributes allows you to do things such as include `#![no_std]` to avoid linking `std` even if a `#![cfg(...)]` has otherwise removed the contents of the crate. For example:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> // This `no_std` attribute is kept even though the crate-level `cfg`\
> // attribute is false.\
> #![no_std]\
> #![cfg(false)]\
>\
> // This function is not included.\
> pub fn example() {}\
> ```\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_attr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_attr "cfg.cfg_attr")\
\
### [The `cfg_attr` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-cfg_attr-attribute)\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_attr.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_attr.intro "cfg.cfg_attr.intro")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg\_attr.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg_attr.rs)\
\
The _`cfg_attr` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ conditionally includes attributes based on a configuration predicate.\
\
> Example\
>\
> The following module will either be found at `linux.rs` or `windows.rs` based on the target.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[cfg_attr(target_os = "linux", path = "linux.rs")]\
> #[cfg_attr(windows, path = "windows.rs")]\
> mod os;\
> ```\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_attr.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_attr.syntax "cfg.cfg_attr.syntax")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/cfg\_attr.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/cfg_attr.rs)\
- [tests/ui/cfg/path-kw-as-cfg-pred.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/path-kw-as-cfg-pred.rs)\
\
The syntax for the `cfg_attr` attribute is:\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[CfgAttrAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgAttrAttribute) → cfg\_attr([ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicate),[CfgAttrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgAttrs)?)\
\
[CfgAttrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgAttrs) → [Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Attr) ( ,[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Attr) )\*,?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[CfgAttrAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgAttrAttribute)\
cfg\_attr\
([ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicate)\
,[CfgAttrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgAttrs)\
)\
\
[CfgAttrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgAttrs)[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Attr)\
,[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Attr)\
,\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_attr.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_attr.allowed-positions "cfg.cfg_attr.allowed-positions")\
\
The `cfg_attr` attribute may be used anywhere attributes are allowed.\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_attr.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_attr.duplicates "cfg.cfg_attr.duplicates")\
\
The `cfg_attr` attribute may be used any number of times on a form.\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_attr.attr-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_attr.attr-restriction "cfg.cfg_attr.attr-restriction")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/cfg/crate-attributes-using-cfg\_attr.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/cfg/crate-attributes-using-cfg_attr.rs)\
\
The [`crate_type`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#linkage) and [`crate_name`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-crate_name-attribute) attributes cannot be used with `cfg_attr`.\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_attr.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_attr.behavior "cfg.cfg_attr.behavior")\
\
When the configuration predicate is true, `cfg_attr` expands out to the attributes listed after the predicate.\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_attr.attribute-list\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_attr.attribute-list "cfg.cfg_attr.attribute-list")\
\
Zero, one, or more attributes may be listed. Multiple attributes will each be expanded into separate attributes.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[cfg_attr(feature = "magic", sparkles, crackles)]\
> fn bewitched() {}\
>\
> // When the `magic` feature flag is enabled, the above will expand to:\
> #[sparkles]\
> #[crackles]\
> fn bewitched() {}\
> ```\
\
> Note\
>\
> The `cfg_attr` can expand to another `cfg_attr`. For example, `#[cfg_attr(target_os = "linux", cfg_attr(feature = "multithreaded", some_other_attribute))]` is valid. This example would be equivalent to `#[cfg_attr(all(target_os = "linux", feature = "multithreaded"), some_other_attribute)]`.\
\
[\[cfg.macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.macro "cfg.macro")\
\
### [The `cfg` macro](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-cfg-macro)\
\
The built-in `cfg` macro takes in a single configuration predicate and evaluates to the `true` literal when the predicate is true and the `false` literal when it is false.\
\
For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let machine_kind = if cfg!(unix) {\
  "unix"\
} else if cfg!(windows) {\
  "windows"\
} else {\
  "unknown"\
};\
\
println!("I'm running on a {} machine!", machine_kind);\
}\
```\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_select\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_select "cfg.cfg_select")\
\
### [The `cfg_select` macro](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-cfg_select-macro)\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_select.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_select.intro "cfg.cfg_select.intro")\
\
The built-in [`cfg_select!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macros/macro.cfg_select.html) macro can be used to select code at compile-time based on multiple configuration predicates.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> cfg_select! {\
>     unix => {\
>         fn foo() { /* unix specific functionality */ }\
>     }\
>     target_pointer_width = "32" => {\
>         fn foo() { /* non-unix, 32-bit functionality */ }\
>     }\
>     _ => {\
>         fn foo() { /* fallback implementation */ }\
>     }\
> }\
>\
> let is_unix_str = cfg_select! {\
>     unix => "unix",\
>     _ => "not unix",\
> };\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_select.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_select.syntax "cfg.cfg_select.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[CfgSelect](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgSelect) → [CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgSelectArms)?\
\
[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgSelectArms) →\
\
[CfgSelectConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgSelectConfigurationPredicate)=>\
\
    (\
\
{ ^ [TokenTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TokenTree)},?[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgSelectArms)?\
\
      \| [ExpressionWithBlockNoAttrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExpressionWithBlockNoAttrs),?[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgSelectArms)?\
\
      \| [ExpressionWithoutBlockNoAttrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExpressionWithoutBlockNoAttrs) ( ,[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgSelectArms)? )?\
\
    )\
\
[CfgSelectConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgSelectConfigurationPredicate) →\
\
[ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConfigurationPredicate) \| \_\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[CfgSelect](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgSelect)[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgSelectArms)\
\
[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgSelectArms)[CfgSelectConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgSelectConfigurationPredicate)\
=>\
{\
no backtracking[TokenTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TokenTree)\
}\
,[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgSelectArms)[ExpressionWithBlockNoAttrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ExpressionWithBlockNoAttrs)\
,[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgSelectArms)[ExpressionWithoutBlockNoAttrs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ExpressionWithoutBlockNoAttrs)\
,[CfgSelectArms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CfgSelectArms)\
\
[CfgSelectConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CfgSelectConfigurationPredicate)[ConfigurationPredicate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConfigurationPredicate)\
\_\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_select.first-arm\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_select.first-arm "cfg.cfg_select.first-arm")\
\
`cfg_select` expands to the payload of the first arm whose configuration predicate evaluates to true.\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_select.braces\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_select.braces "cfg.cfg_select.braces")\
\
If the entire payload is wrapped in curly braces, the braces are removed during expansion.\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_select.wildcard\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_select.wildcard "cfg.cfg_select.wildcard")\
\
The configuration predicate `_` always evaluates to true.\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_select.fallthrough\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_select.fallthrough "cfg.cfg_select.fallthrough")\
\
It is a compile error if none of the predicates evaluate to true.\
\
[\[cfg.cfg\_select.well-formed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-cfg.cfg_select.well-formed "cfg.cfg_select.well-formed")\
\
Each right-hand side must be a syntactically valid expansion for the position in which the macro is invoked.\
\
[\[items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items "items")\
\
# [Items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#items)\
\
[\[items.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.syntax "items.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Item) →\
\
[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\* ( [VisItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-VisItem) \| [MacroItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MacroItem) )\
\
[VisItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-VisItem) →\
\
[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Visibility)?\
\
    (\
\
[Module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Module)\
\
      \| [ExternCrate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExternCrate)\
\
      \| [UseDeclaration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UseDeclaration)\
\
      \| [Function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Function)\
\
      \| [TypeAlias](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeAlias)\
\
      \| [Struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Struct)\
\
      \| [Enumeration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Enumeration)\
\
      \| [Union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Union)\
\
      \| [ConstantItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConstantItem)\
\
      \| [StaticItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StaticItem)\
\
      \| [Trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Trait)\
\
      \| [Implementation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Implementation)\
\
      \| [ExternBlock](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExternBlock)\
\
    )\
\
[MacroItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MacroItem) →\
\
[MacroInvocationSemi](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MacroInvocationSemi)\
\
    \| [MacroRulesDefinition](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MacroRulesDefinition)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Item)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[VisItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-VisItem) [MacroItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MacroItem)\
\
[VisItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-VisItem)[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Visibility)[Module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Module)[ExternCrate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ExternCrate)[UseDeclaration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UseDeclaration)[Function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Function)[TypeAlias](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeAlias)[Struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Struct)[Enumeration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Enumeration)[Union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Union)[ConstantItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConstantItem)[StaticItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StaticItem)[Trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Trait)[Implementation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Implementation)[ExternBlock](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ExternBlock)\
\
[MacroItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MacroItem)[MacroInvocationSemi](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MacroInvocationSemi)[MacroRulesDefinition](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MacroRulesDefinition)\
\
[\[items.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.intro "items.intro")\
\
An _item_ is a component of a crate. Items are organized within a crate by a nested set of [modules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules). Every crate has a single “outermost” anonymous module; all further items within the crate have [paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) within the module tree of the crate.\
\
[\[items.static-def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static-def "items.static-def")\
\
Items are entirely determined at compile-time, generally remain fixed during execution, and may reside in read-only memory.\
\
[\[items.kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.kinds "items.kinds")\
\
There are several kinds of items:\
\
- [modules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules)\
- [`extern crate` declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-crate-declarations)\
- [`use` declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations)\
- [function definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions)\
- [type alias definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-aliases)\
- [struct definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs)\
- [enumeration definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations)\
- [union definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions)\
- [constant items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items)\
- [static items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items)\
- [trait definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits)\
- [implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations)\
- [`extern` blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks)\
\
[\[items.locations\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.locations "items.locations")\
\
Items may be declared in the [root of the crate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#crates-and-source-files), a [module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules), or a [block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions).\
\
[\[items.associated-locations\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated-locations "items.associated-locations")\
\
A subset of items, called [associated items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-items), may be declared in [traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) and [implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations).\
\
[\[items.extern-locations\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-locations "items.extern-locations")\
\
A subset of items, called external items, may be declared in [`extern` blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks).\
\
[\[items.decl-order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.decl-order "items.decl-order")\
\
Items may be defined in any order, with the exception of [`macro_rules`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example) which has its own scoping behavior.\
\
[\[items.name-resolution\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.name-resolution "items.name-resolution")\
\
[Name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#name-resolution) of item names allows items to be defined before or after where the item is referred to in the module or block.\
\
See [item scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#item-scopes) for information on the scoping rules of items.\
\
[\[items.mod\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod "items.mod")\
\
# [Modules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#modules)\
\
[\[items.mod.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.syntax "items.mod.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Module) →\
\
unsafe?mod[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER);\
\
    \| unsafe?mod[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER){\
\
[InnerAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InnerAttribute)\*\
\
[Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Item)\*\
\
}\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Module)\
unsafe\
mod[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
;\
unsafe\
mod[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
{[InnerAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-InnerAttribute)[Item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Item)\
}\
\
[\[items.mod.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.intro "items.mod.intro")\
\
A module is a container for zero or more [items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items).\
\
[\[items.mod.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.def "items.mod.def")\
\
A _module item_ is a module, surrounded in braces, named, and prefixed with the keyword `mod`. A module item introduces a new, named module into the tree of modules making up a crate.\
\
[\[items.mod.nesting\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.nesting "items.mod.nesting")\
\
Modules can nest arbitrarily.\
\
An example of a module:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod math {\
    type Complex = (f64, f64);\
    fn sin(f: f64) -> f64 {\
        /* ... */\
      unimplemented!();\
    }\
    fn cos(f: f64) -> f64 {\
        /* ... */\
      unimplemented!();\
    }\
    fn tan(f: f64) -> f64 {\
        /* ... */\
      unimplemented!();\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.mod.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.namespace "items.mod.namespace")\
\
Modules are defined in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the module or block where they are located.\
\
[\[items.mod.multiple-items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.multiple-items "items.mod.multiple-items")\
\
It is an error to define multiple items with the same name in the same namespace within a module. See the [scopes chapter](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scopes) for more details on restrictions and shadowing behavior.\
\
[\[items.mod.unsafe\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.unsafe "items.mod.unsafe")\
\
The `unsafe` keyword is syntactically allowed to appear before the `mod` keyword, but it is rejected at a semantic level. This allows macros to consume the syntax and make use of the `unsafe` keyword, before removing it from the token stream.\
\
[\[items.mod.outlined\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.outlined "items.mod.outlined")\
\
## [Module source filenames](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#module-source-filenames)\
\
[\[items.mod.outlined.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.outlined.intro "items.mod.outlined.intro")\
\
A module without a body is loaded from an external file. When the module does not have a `path` attribute, the path to the file mirrors the logical [module path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths).\
\
[\[items.mod.outlined.search\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.outlined.search "items.mod.outlined.search")\
\
Ancestor module path components are directories, and the module’s contents are in a file with the name of the module plus the `.rs` extension. For example, the following module structure can have this corresponding filesystem structure:\
\
| Module Path | Filesystem Path | File Contents |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `crate` | `lib.rs` | `mod util;` |\
| `crate::util` | `util.rs` | `mod config;` |\
| `crate::util::config` | `util/config.rs` |  |\
\
[\[items.mod.outlined.search-mod\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.outlined.search-mod "items.mod.outlined.search-mod")\
\
Module filenames may also be the name of the module as a directory with the contents in a file named `mod.rs` within that directory. The above example can alternately be expressed with `crate::util`’s contents in a file named `util/mod.rs`. It is not allowed to have both `util.rs` and `util/mod.rs`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Prior to `rustc` 1.30, using `mod.rs` files was the way to load a module with nested children. It is encouraged to use the new naming convention as it is more consistent, and avoids having many files named `mod.rs` within a project.\
\
[\[items.mod.outlined.path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.outlined.path "items.mod.outlined.path")\
\
### [The `path` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-path-attribute)\
\
[\[items.mod.outlined.path.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.outlined.path.intro "items.mod.outlined.path.intro")\
\
The directories and files used for loading external file modules can be influenced with the `path` attribute.\
\
[\[items.mod.outlined.path.search\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.outlined.path.search "items.mod.outlined.path.search")\
\
For `path` attributes on modules not inside inline module blocks, the file path is relative to the directory the source file is located. For example, the following code snippet would use the paths shown based on where it is located:\
\
```rust\
\
#[path = "foo.rs"]\
mod c;\
```\
\
| Source File | `c`’s File Location | `c`’s Module Path |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `src/a/b.rs` | `src/a/foo.rs` | `crate::a::b::c` |\
| `src/a/mod.rs` | `src/a/foo.rs` | `crate::a::c` |\
\
[\[items.mod.outlined.path.search-nested\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.outlined.path.search-nested "items.mod.outlined.path.search-nested")\
\
For `path` attributes inside inline module blocks, the relative location of the file path depends on the kind of source file the `path` attribute is located in. “mod-rs” source files are root modules (such as `lib.rs` or `main.rs`) and modules with files named `mod.rs`. “non-mod-rs” source files are all other module files. Paths for `path` attributes inside inline module blocks in a mod-rs file are relative to the directory of the mod-rs file including the inline module components as directories. For non-mod-rs files, it is the same except the path starts with a directory with the name of the non-mod-rs module. For example, the following code snippet would use the paths shown based on where it is located:\
\
```rust\
\
mod inline {\
    #[path = "other.rs"]\
    mod inner;\
}\
```\
\
| Source File | `inner`’s File Location | `inner`’s Module Path |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `src/a/b.rs` | `src/a/b/inline/other.rs` | `crate::a::b::inline::inner` |\
| `src/a/mod.rs` | `src/a/inline/other.rs` | `crate::a::inline::inner` |\
\
An example of combining the above rules of `path` attributes on inline modules and nested modules within (applies to both mod-rs and non-mod-rs files):\
\
```rust\
\
#[path = "thread_files"]\
mod thread {\
    // Load the `local_data` module from `thread_files/tls.rs` relative to\
    // this source file's directory.\
    #[path = "tls.rs"]\
    mod local_data;\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.mod.attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.attributes "items.mod.attributes")\
\
## [Attributes on modules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-on-modules)\
\
[\[items.mod.attributes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.attributes.intro "items.mod.attributes.intro")\
\
Modules, like all items, accept outer attributes. They also accept inner attributes: either after `{` for a module with a body, or at the beginning of the source file, after the optional BOM and shebang.\
\
[\[items.mod.attributes.supported\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.mod.attributes.supported "items.mod.attributes.supported")\
\
The built-in attributes that have meaning on a module are [`cfg`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#conditional-compilation), [`deprecated`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-deprecated-attribute), [`doc`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/the-doc-attribute.html), [the lint check attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes), [`path`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-path-attribute), and [`no_implicit_prelude`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_implicit_prelude-attribute). Modules also accept macro attributes.\
\
[\[items.extern-crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate "items.extern-crate")\
\
# [Extern crate declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#extern-crate-declarations)\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.syntax "items.extern-crate.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ExternCrate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ExternCrate) → externcrate[CrateRef](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CrateRef)[AsClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AsClause)?;\
\
[CrateRef](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CrateRef) → [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| self\
\
[AsClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AsClause) → as ( [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| \_ )\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ExternCrate](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExternCrate)\
extern\
crate[CrateRef](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CrateRef)[AsClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AsClause)\
;\
\
[CrateRef](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CrateRef)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
self\
\
[AsClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AsClause)\
as[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
\_\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.intro "items.extern-crate.intro")\
\
An _`extern crate` declaration_ specifies a dependency on an external crate.\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.namespace "items.extern-crate.namespace")\
\
The external crate is then bound into the declaring scope as the given [identifier](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#identifiers) in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces).\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.extern-prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.extern-prelude "items.extern-crate.extern-prelude")\
\
Additionally, if the `extern crate` appears in the crate root, then the crate name is also added to the [extern prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude), making it automatically in scope in all modules.\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.as\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.as "items.extern-crate.as")\
\
The `as` clause can be used to bind the imported crate to a different name.\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.lookup\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.lookup "items.extern-crate.lookup")\
\
The external crate is resolved to a specific `soname` at compile time, and a runtime linkage requirement to that `soname` is passed to the linker for loading at runtime. The `soname` is resolved at compile time by scanning the compiler’s library path and matching the optional `crate_name` provided against the [`crate_name` attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-crate_name-attribute) that were declared on the external crate when it was compiled. If no `crate_name` is provided, a default `name` attribute is assumed, equal to the [identifier](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#identifiers) given in the `extern crate` declaration.\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.self "items.extern-crate.self")\
\
The `self` crate may be imported which creates a binding to the current crate. In this case the `as` clause must be used to specify the name to bind it to.\
\
Three examples of `extern crate` declarations:\
\
```rust\
\
extern crate pcre;\
\
extern crate std; // equivalent to: extern crate std as std;\
\
extern crate std as ruststd; // linking to 'std' under another name\
```\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.name-restrictions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.name-restrictions "items.extern-crate.name-restrictions")\
\
When naming Rust crates, hyphens are disallowed. However, Cargo packages may make use of them. In such case, when `Cargo.toml` doesn’t specify a crate name, Cargo will transparently replace `-` with `_` (Refer to [RFC 940](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/text/0940-hyphens-considered-harmful.md) for more details).\
\
Here is an example:\
\
```rust\
\
// Importing the Cargo package hello-world\
extern crate hello_world; // hyphen replaced with an underscore\
```\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.underscore\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.underscore "items.extern-crate.underscore")\
\
## [Underscore imports](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#underscore-imports)\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.underscore.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.underscore.intro "items.extern-crate.underscore.intro")\
\
An external crate dependency can be declared without binding its name in scope by using an underscore with the form `extern crate foo as _`. This may be useful for crates that only need to be linked, but are never referenced, and will avoid being reported as unused.\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.underscore.macro\_use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.underscore.macro_use "items.extern-crate.underscore.macro_use")\
\
The [`macro_use` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_use-attribute) works as usual and imports the macro names into the [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro_use-prelude).\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.no\_link\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.no_link "items.extern-crate.no_link")\
\
## [The `no_link` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-no_link-attribute)\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.no\_link.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.no_link.intro "items.extern-crate.no_link.intro")\
\
The _`no_link` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes)_ may be applied to an `extern crate` item to prevent linking the crate.\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is helpful, e.g., when only the macros of a crate are needed.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[no_link]\
> extern crate other_crate;\
>\
> other_crate::some_macro!();\
> ```\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.no\_link.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.no_link.syntax "items.extern-crate.no_link.syntax")\
\
The `no_link` attribute uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) syntax.\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.no\_link.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.no_link.allowed-positions "items.extern-crate.no_link.allowed-positions")\
\
The `no_link` attribute may only be applied to an `extern crate` declaration.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` ignores use in other positions but lints against it. This may become an error in the future.\
\
[\[items.extern-crate.no\_link.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern-crate.no_link.duplicates "items.extern-crate.no_link.duplicates")\
\
Only the first use of `no_link` on an `extern crate` declaration has effect.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` lints against any use following the first. This may become an error in the future.\
\
[\[items.use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use "items.use")\
\
# [Use declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#use-declarations)\
\
[\[items.use.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.syntax "items.use.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[UseDeclaration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UseDeclaration) → use[UseTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UseTree);\
\
[UseTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UseTree) →\
\
      ( [SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath)?:: )?\*\
\
    \| ( [SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath)?:: )?{ ( [UseTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UseTree) ( ,[UseTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UseTree) )\*,? )?}\
\
    \| [SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath) ( as ( [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| \_ ) )?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[UseDeclaration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UseDeclaration)\
use[UseTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UseTree)\
;\
\
[UseTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UseTree)[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)\
::\
\*[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)\
::\
{[UseTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UseTree)\
,[UseTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UseTree)\
,\
}[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)\
as[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
\_\
\
[\[items.use.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.intro "items.use.intro")\
\
A _use declaration_ creates one or more local name bindings synonymous with some other [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths). Usually a `use` declaration is used to shorten the path required to refer to a module item. These declarations may appear in [modules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules) and [blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions), usually at the top. A `use` declaration is also sometimes called an _import_, or, if it is public, a _re-export_.\
\
[\[items.use.forms\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.forms "items.use.forms")\
\
Use declarations support a number of convenient shortcuts:\
\
[\[items.use.forms.multiple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.forms.multiple "items.use.forms.multiple")\
\
- Simultaneously binding a list of paths with a common prefix, using the brace syntax `use a::b::{c, d, e::f, g::h::i};`\
\
[\[items.use.forms.self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.forms.self "items.use.forms.self")\
\
- Simultaneously binding a list of paths with a common prefix and their common parent module, using the `self` keyword, such as `use a::b::{self, c, d::e};`\
\
[\[items.use.forms.as\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.forms.as "items.use.forms.as")\
\
- Rebinding the target name as a new local name, using the syntax `use p::q::r as x;`. This can also be used with the last two features: `use a::b::{self as ab, c as abc}`.\
\
[\[items.use.forms.glob\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.forms.glob "items.use.forms.glob")\
\
- Binding all paths matching a given prefix, using the asterisk wildcard syntax `use a::b::*;`.\
\
[\[items.use.forms.nesting\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.forms.nesting "items.use.forms.nesting")\
\
- Nesting groups of the previous features multiple times, such as `use a::b::{self as ab, c, d::{*, e::f}};`\
\
An example of `use` declarations:\
\
```rust\
\
use std::collections::hash_map::{self, HashMap};\
\
fn foo<T>(_: T){}\
fn bar(map1: HashMap<String, usize>, map2: hash_map::HashMap<String, usize>){}\
\
fn main() {\
    // use declarations can also exist inside of functions\
    use std::option::Option::{Some, None};\
\
    // Equivalent to 'foo(vec![std::option::Option::Some(1.0f64),\
    // std::option::Option::None]);'\
    foo(vec![Some(1.0f64), None]);\
\
    // Both `hash_map` and `HashMap` are in scope.\
    let map1 = HashMap::new();\
    let map2 = hash_map::HashMap::new();\
    bar(map1, map2);\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.use.visibility\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.visibility "items.use.visibility")\
\
## [`use` Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#use-visibility)\
\
[\[items.use.visibility.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.visibility.intro "items.use.visibility.intro")\
\
Like items, `use` declarations are private to the containing module, by default. Also like items, a `use` declaration can be public, if qualified by the `pub` keyword. Such a `use` declaration serves to _re-export_ a name. A public `use` declaration can therefore _redirect_ some public name to a different target definition: even a definition with a private canonical path, inside a different module.\
\
[\[items.use.visibility.unambiguous\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.visibility.unambiguous "items.use.visibility.unambiguous")\
\
If a sequence of such redirections form a cycle or cannot be resolved unambiguously, they represent a compile-time error.\
\
An example of re-exporting:\
\
```rust\
\
mod quux {\
    pub use self::foo::{bar, baz};\
    pub mod foo {\
        pub fn bar() {}\
        pub fn baz() {}\
    }\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    quux::bar();\
    quux::baz();\
}\
```\
\
In this example, the module `quux` re-exports two public names defined in `foo`.\
\
[\[items.use.path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.path "items.use.path")\
\
## [`use` Paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#use-paths)\
\
[\[items.use.path.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.path.intro "items.use.path.intro")\
\
The [paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) that are allowed in a `use` item follow the [SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath) grammar and are similar to the paths that may be used in an expression. They may create bindings for:\
\
- Nameable [items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items)\
- [Enum variants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations)\
- [Built-in types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#types)\
- [Attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)\
- [Derive macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive)\
- [`macro_rules`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example)\
\
[\[items.use.path.disallowed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.path.disallowed "items.use.path.disallowed")\
\
They cannot import [associated items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-items), [generic parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters), [local variables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variables), paths with [`Self`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#self), or [tool attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-attributes). More restrictions are described below.\
\
[\[items.use.path.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.path.namespace "items.use.path.namespace")\
\
`use` will create bindings for all [namespaces](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) from the imported entities, with the exception that a `self` import will only import from the type namespace (as described below). For example, the following illustrates creating bindings for the same name in two namespaces:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod stuff {\
    pub struct Foo(pub i32);\
}\
\
// Imports the `Foo` type and the `Foo` constructor.\
use stuff::Foo;\
\
fn example() {\
    let ctor = Foo; // Uses `Foo` from the value namespace.\
    let x: Foo = ctor(123); // Uses `Foo` From the type namespace.\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.use.path.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.path.edition2018 "items.use.path.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, `use` paths are relative to the crate root. For example:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> mod foo {\
>     pub mod example { pub mod iter {} }\
>     pub mod baz { pub fn foobaz() {} }\
> }\
> mod bar {\
>     // Resolves `foo` from the crate root.\
>     use foo::example::iter;\
>     // The `::` prefix explicitly resolves `foo`\
>     // from the crate root.\
>     use ::foo::baz::foobaz;\
> }\
>\
> fn main() {}\
> ```\
>\
> The 2015 edition does not allow use declarations to reference the [extern prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude). Thus, [`extern crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-crate-declarations) declarations are still required in 2015 to reference an external crate in a `use` declaration. Beginning with the 2018 edition, `use` declarations can specify an external crate dependency the same way `extern crate` can.\
\
[\[items.use.as\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.as "items.use.as")\
\
## [`as` renames](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#as-renames)\
\
The `as` keyword can be used to change the name of an imported entity. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Creates a non-public alias `bar` for the function `foo`.\
use inner::foo as bar;\
\
mod inner {\
    pub fn foo() {}\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.use.multiple-syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.multiple-syntax "items.use.multiple-syntax")\
\
## [Brace syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#brace-syntax)\
\
[\[items.use.multiple-syntax.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.multiple-syntax.intro "items.use.multiple-syntax.intro")\
\
Braces can be used in the last segment of the path to import multiple entities from the previous segment, or, if there are no previous segments, from the current scope. Braces can be nested, creating a tree of paths, where each grouping of segments is logically combined with its parent to create a full path.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Creates bindings to:\
// - `std::collections::BTreeSet`\
// - `std::collections::hash_map`\
// - `std::collections::hash_map::HashMap`\
use std::collections::{BTreeSet, hash_map::{self, HashMap}};\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.use.multiple-syntax.empty\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.multiple-syntax.empty "items.use.multiple-syntax.empty")\
\
An empty brace does not import anything, though the leading path is validated that it is accessible.\
\
[\[items.use.multiple-syntax.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.multiple-syntax.edition2018 "items.use.multiple-syntax.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, paths are relative to the crate root, so an import such as `use {foo, bar};` will import the names `foo` and `bar` from the crate root, whereas starting in 2018, those names are relative to the current scope.\
\
[\[items.use.self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.self "items.use.self")\
\
## [`self` imports](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#self-imports)\
\
[\[items.use.self.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.self.intro "items.use.self.intro")\
\
The keyword `self` may be used within [brace syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#brace-syntax) to create a binding of the parent entity under its own name.\
\
```rust\
\
mod stuff {\
    pub fn foo() {}\
    pub fn bar() {}\
}\
mod example {\
    // Creates a binding for `stuff` and `foo`.\
    use crate::stuff::{self, foo};\
    pub fn baz() {\
        foo();\
        stuff::bar();\
    }\
}\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> `self` may also be used as the first segment of a path. The usage of `self` as the first segment and inside a `use` brace is logically the same; it means the current module of the parent segment, or the current module if there is no parent segment. See [`self`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#self) in the paths chapter for more information on the meaning of a leading `self`.\
\
[\[items.use.self.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.self.namespace "items.use.self.namespace")\
\
`self` only creates a binding from the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the parent entity. For example, in the following, only the `foo` mod is imported:\
\
```rust\
\
mod bar {\
    pub mod foo {}\
    pub fn foo() {}\
}\
\
// This only imports the module `foo`. The function `foo` lives in\
// the value namespace and is not imported.\
use bar::foo::{self};\
\
fn main() {\
    foo(); //~ ERROR `foo` is a module\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.use.glob\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob "items.use.glob")\
\
## [Glob imports](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#glob-imports)\
\
[\[items.use.glob.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob.intro "items.use.glob.intro")\
\
The `*` character may be used as the last segment of a `use` path to import all importable entities from the entity of the preceding segment. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Creates a non-public alias to `bar`.\
use foo::*;\
\
mod foo {\
    fn i_am_private() {}\
    enum Example {\
        V1,\
        V2,\
    }\
    pub fn bar() {\
        // Creates local aliases to `V1` and `V2`\
        // of the `Example` enum.\
        use Example::*;\
        let x = V1;\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.use.glob.shadowing\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob.shadowing "items.use.glob.shadowing")\
\
Items and named imports are allowed to shadow names from glob imports in the same [namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces). That is, if there is a name already defined by another item in the same namespace, the glob import will be shadowed. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// This creates a binding to the `clashing::Foo` tuple struct\
// constructor, but does not import its type because that would\
// conflict with the `Foo` struct defined here.\
//\
// Note that the order of definition here is unimportant.\
use clashing::*;\
struct Foo {\
    field: f32,\
}\
\
fn do_stuff() {\
    // Uses the constructor from `clashing::Foo`.\
    let f1 = Foo(123);\
    // The struct expression uses the type from\
    // the `Foo` struct defined above.\
    let f2 = Foo { field: 1.0 };\
    // `Bar` is also in scope due to the glob import.\
    let z = Bar {};\
}\
\
mod clashing {\
    pub struct Foo(pub i32);\
    pub struct Bar {}\
}\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> For areas where shadowing is not allowed, see [name resolution ambiguities](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity).\
\
[\[items.use.glob.last-segment-only\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob.last-segment-only "items.use.glob.last-segment-only")\
\
`*` cannot be used as the first or intermediate segments.\
\
[\[items.use.glob.self-import\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob.self-import "items.use.glob.self-import")\
\
`*` cannot be used to import a module’s contents into itself (such as `use self::*;`).\
\
[\[items.use.glob.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob.edition2018 "items.use.glob.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, paths are relative to the crate root, so an import such as `use *;` is valid, and it means to import everything from the crate root. This cannot be used in the crate root itself.\
\
[\[items.use.as-underscore\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.as-underscore "items.use.as-underscore")\
\
## [Underscore imports](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#underscore-imports-1)\
\
[\[items.use.as-underscore.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.as-underscore.intro "items.use.as-underscore.intro")\
\
Items can be imported without binding to a name by using an underscore with the form `use path as _`. This is particularly useful to import a trait so that its methods may be used without importing the trait’s symbol, for example if the trait’s symbol may conflict with another symbol. Another example is to link an external crate without importing its name.\
\
[\[items.use.as-underscore.glob\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.as-underscore.glob "items.use.as-underscore.glob")\
\
Asterisk glob imports will import items imported with `_` in their unnameable form.\
\
```rust\
\
mod foo {\
    pub trait Zoo {\
        fn zoo(&self) {}\
    }\
\
    impl<T> Zoo for T {}\
}\
\
use self::foo::Zoo as _;\
struct Zoo;  // Underscore import avoids name conflict with this item.\
\
fn main() {\
    let z = Zoo;\
    z.zoo();\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.use.as-underscore.macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.as-underscore.macro "items.use.as-underscore.macro")\
\
The unique, unnameable symbols are created after macro expansion so that macros may safely emit multiple references to `_` imports. For example, the following should not produce an error:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! m {\
    ($item: item) => { $item $item }\
}\
\
m!(use std as _;);\
// This expands to:\
// use std as _;\
// use std as _;\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.use.restrictions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.restrictions "items.use.restrictions")\
\
## [Restrictions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#restrictions)\
\
The following rules are restrictions for valid `use` declarations.\
\
[\[items.use.restrictions.crate-alias\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.restrictions.crate-alias "items.use.restrictions.crate-alias")\
\
When using `crate` to import the current crate, you must use `as` to define the binding name.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> use crate as root;\
> use crate::{self as root2};\
>\
> // Not allowed:\
> // use crate;\
> // use crate::{self};\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[items.use.restrictions.macro-crate-alias\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.restrictions.macro-crate-alias "items.use.restrictions.macro-crate-alias")\
\
When using [`$crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.macro-crate) in a macro transcriber to import the current crate, you must use `as` to define the binding name.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> macro_rules! import_crate_root {\
>     () => {\
>         use $crate as my_crate;\
>         use $crate::{self as my_crate2};\
>     };\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[items.use.restrictions.self-alias\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.restrictions.self-alias "items.use.restrictions.self-alias")\
\
When using `self` to import the current module, you must use `as` to define the binding name.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> use {self as this_module};\
> use self as this_module2;\
> use self::{self as this_module3};\
>\
> // Not allowed:\
> // use {self};\
> // use self;\
> // use self::{self};\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[items.use.restrictions.super-alias\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.restrictions.super-alias "items.use.restrictions.super-alias")\
\
When using `super` to import a parent module, you must use `as` to define the binding name.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> mod a {\
>     mod b {\
>         use super as parent;\
>         use super::{self as parent2};\
>         use self::super as parent3;\
>         use super::super as grandparent;\
>         use super::super::{self as grandparent2};\
>\
>         // Not allowed:\
>         // use super;\
>         // use super::{self};\
>         // use self::super;\
>         // use super::super;\
>         // use super::super::{self};\
>     }\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[items.use.restrictions.extern-prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.restrictions.extern-prelude "items.use.restrictions.extern-prelude")\
\
`::` as the [extern prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude) cannot be imported.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> use ::{self as root}; //~ Error\
> }\
> ```\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, the prefix `::` refers to the crate root, so `use ::{self as root};` is allowed because it is same as `use crate::{self as root};`. Starting with the 2018 edition the `::` prefix refers to the extern prelude, which cannot be directly imported.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> use ::{self as root}; //~ Ok\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[items.use.restrictions.duplicate-name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.restrictions.duplicate-name "items.use.restrictions.duplicate-name")\
\
As with any item definition, `use` imports cannot create duplicate bindings of the same name in the same namespace in a module or block.\
\
[\[items.use.restrictions.variant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.restrictions.variant "items.use.restrictions.variant")\
\
`use` paths cannot refer to enum variants through a [type alias](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-aliases).\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> enum MyEnum {\
>   MyVariant\
> }\
> type TypeAlias = MyEnum;\
>\
> use MyEnum::MyVariant; //~ OK\
> use TypeAlias::MyVariant; //~ ERROR\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[items.fn\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn "items.fn")\
\
# [Functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#functions)\
\
[\[items.fn.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.syntax "items.fn.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Function) →\
\
[FunctionQualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FunctionQualifiers)fn[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams)?\
\
([FunctionParameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FunctionParameters)?)\
\
[FunctionReturnType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FunctionReturnType)?[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClause)?\
\
        ( [BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression) \| ; )\
\
[FunctionQualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FunctionQualifiers) → const?async?​[1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-async-edition)[ItemSafety](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ItemSafety)?​[2](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-extern-qualifiers) ( extern[Abi](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Abi)? )?\
\
[ItemSafety](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ItemSafety) → safe​[3](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-extern-safe) \| unsafe\
\

\

\
[SelfParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SelfParam),?\

\
