\
[\[items.fn.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.intro "items.fn.intro")\
\
A _function_ consists of a [block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions) (that’s the _body_ of the function), along with a name, a set of parameters, and an output type. Other than a name, all these are optional.\
\
[\[items.fn.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.namespace "items.fn.namespace")\
\
Functions are declared with the keyword `fn` which defines the given name in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the module or block where it is located.\
\
[\[items.fn.signature\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.signature "items.fn.signature")\
\
Functions may declare a set of _input_ [_variables_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variables) as parameters, through which the caller passes arguments into the function, and the _output_ [_type_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-expressions) of the value the function will return to its caller on completion.\
\
[\[items.fn.implicit-return\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.implicit-return "items.fn.implicit-return")\
\
If the output type is not explicitly stated, it is the [unit type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types).\
\
[\[items.fn.fn-item-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.fn-item-type "items.fn.fn-item-type")\
\
When referred to, a _function_ yields a first-class _value_ of the corresponding zero-sized [_function item type_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-item-types), which when called evaluates to a direct call to the function.\
\
For example, this is a simple function:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn answer_to_life_the_universe_and_everything() -> i32 {\
    return 42;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.safety-qualifiers\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.safety-qualifiers "items.fn.safety-qualifiers")\
\
The `safe` function is semantically only allowed when used in an [`extern` block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks).\
\
[\[items.fn.params\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.params "items.fn.params")\
\
## [Function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#function-parameters)\
\
[\[items.fn.params.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.params.intro "items.fn.params.intro")\
\
Function parameters are irrefutable [patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns), so any pattern that is valid in an else-less `let` binding is also valid as a parameter:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn first((value, _): (i32, i32)) -> i32 { value }\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.params.self-pat\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.params.self-pat "items.fn.params.self-pat")\
\
If the first parameter is a [SelfParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SelfParam), this indicates that the function is a [method](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#methods).\
\
[\[items.fn.params.self-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.params.self-restriction "items.fn.params.self-restriction")\
\
Functions with a self parameter may only appear as an [associated function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-functions-and-methods) in a [trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) or [implementation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations).\
\
[\[items.fn.params.varargs\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.params.varargs "items.fn.params.varargs")\
\
A parameter with the `...` token indicates a [variadic function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variadic-functions), and may only be used as the last parameter of an [external block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks) function. The variadic parameter may have an optional identifier, such as `args: ...`.\
\
[\[items.fn.body\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.body "items.fn.body")\
\
## [Function body](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#function-body)\
\
[\[items.fn.body.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.body.intro "items.fn.body.intro")\
\
The body block of a function is conceptually wrapped in another block that first binds the argument patterns and then `return`s the value of the function’s body. This means that the tail expression of the block, if evaluated, ends up being returned to the caller. As usual, an explicit return expression within the body of the function will short-cut that implicit return, if reached.\
\
For example, the function above behaves as if it was written as:\
\
```rust\
\
// argument_0 is the actual first argument passed from the caller\
let (value, _) = argument_0;\
return {\
    value\
};\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.body.bodyless\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.body.bodyless "items.fn.body.bodyless")\
\
Functions without a body block are terminated with a semicolon. This form may only appear in a [trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) or [external block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks).\
\
[\[items.fn.generics\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.generics "items.fn.generics")\
\
## [Generic functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#generic-functions)\
\
[\[items.fn.generics.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.generics.intro "items.fn.generics.intro")\
\
A _generic function_ allows one or more _parameterized types_ to appear in its signature. Each type parameter must be explicitly declared in an angle-bracket-enclosed and comma-separated list, following the function name.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// foo is generic over A and B\
\
fn foo<A, B>(x: A, y: B) {\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.generics.param-names\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.generics.param-names "items.fn.generics.param-names")\
\
Inside the function signature and body, the name of the type parameter can be used as a type name.\
\
[\[items.fn.generics.param-bounds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.generics.param-bounds "items.fn.generics.param-bounds")\
\
[Trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) bounds can be specified for type parameters to allow methods with that trait to be called on values of that type. This is specified using the `where` syntax:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::fmt::Debug;\
fn foo<T>(x: T) where T: Debug {\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.generics.mono\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.generics.mono "items.fn.generics.mono")\
\
When a generic function is referenced, its type is instantiated based on the context of the reference. For example, calling the `foo` function here:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::fmt::Debug;\
\
fn foo<T>(x: &[T]) where T: Debug {\
    // details elided\
}\
\
foo(&[1, 2]);\
}\
```\
\
will instantiate type parameter `T` with `i32`.\
\
[\[items.fn.generics.explicit-arguments\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.generics.explicit-arguments "items.fn.generics.explicit-arguments")\
\
The type parameters can also be explicitly supplied in a trailing [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) component after the function name. This might be necessary if there is not sufficient context to determine the type parameters. For example, `mem::size_of::<u32>() == 4`.\
\
[\[items.fn.extern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern "items.fn.extern")\
\
## [Extern function qualifier](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#extern-function-qualifier)\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.intro "items.fn.extern.intro")\
\
The `extern` function qualifier allows providing function _definitions_ that can be called with a particular ABI:\
\
```rust\
\
extern "ABI" fn foo() { /* ... */ }\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.def "items.fn.extern.def")\
\
These are often used in combination with [external block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks) items which provide function _declarations_ that can be used to call functions without providing their _definition_:\
\
```rust\
\
unsafe extern "ABI" {\
  unsafe fn foo(); /* no body */\
  safe fn bar(); /* no body */\
}\
unsafe { foo() };\
bar();\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.default-abi\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.default-abi "items.fn.extern.default-abi")\
\
When `"extern" Abi?*` is omitted from `FunctionQualifiers` in function items, the ABI `"Rust"` is assigned. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn foo() {}\
}\
```\
\
is equivalent to:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
extern "Rust" fn foo() {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.foreign-call\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.foreign-call "items.fn.extern.foreign-call")\
\
Functions can be called by foreign code, and using an ABI that differs from Rust allows, for example, to provide functions that can be called from other programming languages like C:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Declares a function with the "C" ABI\
extern "C" fn new_i32() -> i32 { 0 }\
\
// Declares a function with the "stdcall" ABI\
#[cfg(any(windows, target_arch = "x86"))]\
extern "stdcall" fn new_i32_stdcall() -> i32 { 0 }\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.default-extern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.default-extern "items.fn.extern.default-extern")\
\
Just as with [external block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks), when the `extern` keyword is used and the `"ABI"` is omitted, the ABI used defaults to `"C"`. That is, this:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
extern fn new_i32() -> i32 { 0 }\
let fptr: extern fn() -> i32 = new_i32;\
}\
```\
\
is equivalent to:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
extern "C" fn new_i32() -> i32 { 0 }\
let fptr: extern "C" fn() -> i32 = new_i32;\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.unwind\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.unwind "items.fn.extern.unwind")\
\
### [Unwinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unwinding)\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.unwind.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.unwind.intro "items.fn.extern.unwind.intro")\
\
Most ABI strings come in two variants, one with an `-unwind` suffix and one without. The `Rust` ABI always permits unwinding, so there is no `Rust-unwind` ABI. The choice of ABI, together with the runtime [panic handler](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-panic_handler-attribute), determines the behavior when unwinding out of a function.\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.unwind.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.unwind.behavior "items.fn.extern.unwind.behavior")\
\
The table below indicates the behavior of an unwinding operation reaching each type of ABI boundary (function declaration or definition using the corresponding ABI string). Note that the Rust runtime is not affected by, and cannot have an effect on, any unwinding that occurs entirely within another language’s runtime, that is, unwinds that are thrown and caught without reaching a Rust ABI boundary.\
\
The `panic`-unwind column refers to [panicking](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#panic-1) via the `panic!` macro and similar standard library mechanisms, as well as to any other Rust operations that cause a panic, such as out-of-bounds array indexing or integer overflow.\
\
The “unwinding” ABI category refers to `"Rust"` (the implicit ABI of Rust functions not marked `extern`), `"C-unwind"`, and any other ABI with `-unwind` in its name. The “non-unwinding” ABI category refers to all other ABI strings, including `"C"` and `"stdcall"`.\
\
Native unwinding is defined per-target. On targets that support throwing and catching C++ exceptions, it refers to the mechanism used to implement this feature. Some platforms implement a form of unwinding referred to as [“forced unwinding”](https://rust-lang.github.io/rfcs/2945-c-unwind-abi.html#forced-unwinding); `longjmp` on Windows and `pthread_exit` in `glibc` are implemented this way. Forced unwinding is explicitly excluded from the “Native unwind” column in the table.\
\
| panic runtime | ABI | `panic`-unwind | Native unwind (unforced) |\
| --- | --- | --- | --- |\
| `panic=unwind` | unwinding | unwind | unwind |\
| `panic=unwind` | non-unwinding | abort (see notes below) | [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined) |\
| `panic=abort` | unwinding | `panic` aborts without unwinding | abort |\
| `panic=abort` | non-unwinding | `panic` aborts without unwinding | [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined) |\
\
[\[items.fn.extern.abort\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern.abort "items.fn.extern.abort")\
\
With `panic=unwind`, when a `panic` is turned into an abort by a non-unwinding ABI boundary, either no destructors (`Drop` calls) will run, or all destructors up until the ABI boundary will run. It is unspecified which of those two behaviors will happen.\
\
For other considerations and limitations regarding unwinding across FFI boundaries, see the [relevant section in the Panic documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unwinding-across-ffi-boundaries).\
\
[\[items.fn.const\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.const "items.fn.const")\
\
## [Const functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#const-functions)\
\
See [const functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#const-functions-1) for the definition of const functions.\
\
[\[items.fn.async\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async "items.fn.async")\
\
## [Async functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#async-functions)\
\
[\[items.fn.async.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.intro "items.fn.async.intro")\
\
Functions may be qualified as async, and this can also be combined with the `unsafe` qualifier:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
async fn regular_example() { }\
async unsafe fn unsafe_example() { }\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.async.future\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.future "items.fn.async.future")\
\
Async functions do no work when called: instead, they capture their arguments into a future. When polled, that future will execute the function’s body.\
\
[\[items.fn.async.desugar-brief\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.desugar-brief "items.fn.async.desugar-brief")\
\
An async function is roughly equivalent to a function that returns [`impl Future`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#impl-trait) and with an [`async move` block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-blocks) as its body:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Source\
async fn example(x: &str) -> usize {\
    x.len()\
}\
}\
```\
\
is roughly equivalent to:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::future::Future;\
// Desugared\
fn example<'a>(x: &'a str) -> impl Future<Output = usize> + 'a {\
    async move { x.len() }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.async.desugar\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.desugar "items.fn.async.desugar")\
\
The actual desugaring is more complex:\
\
[\[items.fn.async.lifetime-capture\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.lifetime-capture "items.fn.async.lifetime-capture")\
\
- The return type in the desugaring is assumed to capture all lifetime parameters from the `async fn` declaration. This can be seen in the desugared example above, which explicitly outlives, and hence captures, `'a`.\
\
[\[items.fn.async.param-capture\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.param-capture "items.fn.async.param-capture")\
\
- The [`async move` block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-blocks) in the body captures all function parameters, including those that are unused or bound to a `_` pattern. This ensures that function parameters are dropped in the same order as they would be if the function were not async, except that the drop occurs when the returned future has been fully awaited.\
\
For more information on the effect of async, see [`async` blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-blocks).\
\
[\[items.fn.async.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.edition2018 "items.fn.async.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> Async functions are only available beginning with Rust 2018.\
\
[\[items.fn.async.safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.safety "items.fn.async.safety")\
\
### [Combining `async` and `unsafe`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#combining-async-and-unsafe)\
\
[\[items.fn.async.safety.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async.safety.intro "items.fn.async.safety.intro")\
\
It is legal to declare a function that is both async and unsafe. The resulting function is unsafe to call and (like any async function) returns a future. This future is just an ordinary future and thus an `unsafe` context is not required to “await” it:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Returns a future that, when awaited, dereferences `x`.\
//\
// Soundness condition: `x` must be safe to dereference until\
// the resulting future is complete.\
async unsafe fn unsafe_example(x: *const i32) -> i32 {\
  *x\
}\
\
async fn safe_example() {\
    // An `unsafe` block is required to invoke the function initially:\
    let p = 22;\
    let future = unsafe { unsafe_example(&p) };\
\
    // But no `unsafe` block required here. This will\
    // read the value of `p`:\
    let q = future.await;\
}\
}\
```\
\
Note that this behavior is a consequence of the desugaring to a function that returns an `impl Future` – in this case, the function we desugar to is an `unsafe` function, but the return value remains the same.\
\
Unsafe is used on an async function in precisely the same way that it is used on other functions: it indicates that the function imposes some additional obligations on its caller to ensure soundness. As in any other unsafe function, these conditions may extend beyond the initial call itself – in the snippet above, for example, the `unsafe_example` function took a pointer `x` as argument, and then (when awaited) dereferenced that pointer. This implies that `x` would have to be valid until the future is finished executing, and it is the caller’s responsibility to ensure that.\
\
[\[items.fn.attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.attributes "items.fn.attributes")\
\
## [Attributes on functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-on-functions)\
\
[\[items.fn.attributes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.attributes.intro "items.fn.attributes.intro")\
\
[Outer attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) are allowed on functions. [Inner attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) are allowed directly after the `{` inside its body [block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions).\
\
This example shows an inner attribute on a function. The function is documented with just the word “Example”.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn documented() {\
    #![doc = "Example"]\
}\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Except for lints, it is idiomatic to only use outer attributes on function items.\
\
[\[items.fn.attributes.builtin-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.attributes.builtin-attributes "items.fn.attributes.builtin-attributes")\
\
The attributes that have meaning on a function are:\
\
- [`cfg_attr`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg_attr-attribute)\
- [`cfg`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg-attribute)\
- [`cold`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cold-attribute)\
- [`deprecated`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-deprecated-attribute)\
- [`doc`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/the-doc-attribute.html)\
- [`export_name`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-export_name-attribute)\
- [`inline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-inline-attribute)\
- [`link_section`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-link_section-attribute)\
- [`must_use`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-must_use-attribute)\
- [`no_mangle`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_mangle-attribute)\
- [Lint check attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes)\
- [Procedural macro attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.attribute)\
- [Testing attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#testing-attributes)\
\
[\[items.fn.param-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.param-attributes "items.fn.param-attributes")\
\
## [Attributes on function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-on-function-parameters)\
\
[\[items.fn.param-attributes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.param-attributes.intro "items.fn.param-attributes.intro")\
\
[Outer attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) are allowed on function parameters and the permitted [built-in attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#built-in-attributes-index) are restricted to `cfg`, `cfg_attr`, `allow`, `warn`, `deny`, and `forbid`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn len(\
    #[cfg(windows)] slice: &[u16],\
    #[cfg(not(windows))] slice: &[u8],\
) -> usize {\
    slice.len()\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.fn.param-attributes.parsed-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.param-attributes.parsed-attributes "items.fn.param-attributes.parsed-attributes")\
\
Inert helper attributes used by procedural macro attributes applied to items are also allowed but be careful to not include these inert attributes in your final `TokenStream`.\
\
For example, the following code defines an inert `some_inert_attribute` attribute that is not formally defined anywhere and the `some_proc_macro_attribute` procedural macro is responsible for detecting its presence and removing it from the output token stream.\
\
```rust\
\
#[some_proc_macro_attribute]\
fn foo_oof(#[some_inert_attribute] arg: u8) {\
}\
```\
\
* * *\
\
1. The `async` qualifier is not allowed in the 2015 edition. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-async-edition-1)\
\
2. _Relevant to editions earlier than Rust 2024_: Within `extern` blocks, the `safe` or `unsafe` function qualifier is only allowed when the `extern` is qualified as `unsafe`. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-extern-qualifiers-1)\
\
3. The `safe` function qualifier is only allowed semantically within `extern` blocks. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-extern-safe-1)\
\
4. Function parameters with only a type are only allowed in an associated function of a [trait item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) in the 2015 edition. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-fn-param-2015-1)\
\
\
[\[items.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.type "items.type")\
\
# [Type aliases](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#type-aliases)\
\
[\[items.type.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.type.syntax "items.type.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[TypeAlias](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeAlias) →\
\
type[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams)? ( :[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds) )?\
\
[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClause)?\
\
        ( =[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClause)? )?;\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TypeAlias](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeAlias)\
type[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParams)\
:[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeParamBounds)[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClause)\
=[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClause)\
;\
\
[\[items.type.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.type.intro "items.type.intro")\
\
A _type alias_ defines a new name for an existing [type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#types) in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the module or block where it is located. Type aliases are declared with the keyword `type`. Every value has a single, specific type, but may implement several different traits, and may be compatible with several different type constraints.\
\
For example, the following defines the type `Point` as a synonym for the type `(u8, u8)`, the type of pairs of unsigned 8 bit integers:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
type Point = (u8, u8);\
let p: Point = (41, 68);\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.type.constructor-alias\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.type.constructor-alias "items.type.constructor-alias")\
\
A type alias to a tuple-struct or unit-struct cannot be used to qualify that type’s constructor:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct MyStruct(u32);\
\
use MyStruct as UseAlias;\
type TypeAlias = MyStruct;\
\
let _ = UseAlias(5); // OK\
let _ = TypeAlias(5); // Doesn't work\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.type.associated-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.type.associated-type "items.type.associated-type")\
\
A type alias, when not used as an [associated type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types), must include a [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) and may not include [TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds).\
\
[\[items.type.associated-trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.type.associated-trait "items.type.associated-trait")\
\
A type alias, when used as an [associated type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types) in a [trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits), must not include a [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) specification but may include [TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds).\
\
[\[items.type.associated-impl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.type.associated-impl "items.type.associated-impl")\
\
A type alias, when used as an [associated type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types) in a [trait impl](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-implementations), must include a [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) specification and may not include [TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds).\
\
[\[items.type.deprecated\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.type.deprecated "items.type.deprecated")\
\
Where clauses before the equals sign on a type alias in a [trait impl](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-implementations) (like `type TypeAlias<T> where T: Foo = Bar<T>`) are deprecated. Where clauses after the equals sign (like `type TypeAlias<T> = Bar<T> where T: Foo`) are preferred.\
\
[\[items.struct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct "items.struct")\
\
# [Structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#structs)\
\
[\[items.struct.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.syntax "items.struct.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Struct) →\
\
[StructStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructStruct)\
\
    \| [TupleStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleStruct)\
\
[StructStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructStruct) →\
\
struct[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams)?[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClause)? ( {[StructFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructFields)?} \| ; )\
\
[TupleStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleStruct) →\
\
struct[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams)?([TupleFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleFields)?)[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClause)?;\
\
[StructFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructFields) → [StructField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructField) ( ,[StructField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructField) )\*,?\
\
[StructField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructField) → [OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\*[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Visibility)?[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER):[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)\
\
[TupleFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleFields) → [TupleField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleField) ( ,[TupleField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleField) )\*,?\
\
[TupleField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleField) → [OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\*[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Visibility)?[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Struct)[StructStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructStruct)[TupleStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleStruct)\
\
[StructStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructStruct)\
struct[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParams)[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClause)\
{[StructFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructFields)\
}\
;\
\
[TupleStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleStruct)\

([TupleFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleFields)\
)[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClause)\
;\
\
[StructFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructFields)[StructField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructField)\
,[StructField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructField)\
,\
\
[StructField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructField)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Visibility)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
:[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
\
[TupleFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleFields)[TupleField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleField)\
,[TupleField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleField)\
,\
\
[TupleField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleField)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Visibility)[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
\
[\[items.struct.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.intro "items.struct.intro")\
\
A _struct_ is a nominal [struct type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-types) defined with the keyword `struct`.\
\
[\[items.struct.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.namespace "items.struct.namespace")\
\
A struct declaration defines the given name in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the module or block where it is located.\
\
An example of a `struct` item and its use:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Point {x: i32, y: i32}\
let p = Point {x: 10, y: 11};\
let px: i32 = p.x;\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.struct.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.tuple "items.struct.tuple")\
\
A _tuple struct_ is a nominal [tuple type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types), and is also defined with the keyword `struct`. In addition to defining a type, it also defines a constructor of the same name in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces). The constructor is a function which can be called to create a new instance of the struct. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Point(i32, i32);\
let p = Point(10, 11);\
let px: i32 = match p { Point(x, _) => x };\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.struct.unit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.unit "items.struct.unit")\
\
A _unit-like struct_ is a struct without any fields, defined by leaving off the list of fields entirely. Such a struct implicitly defines a [constant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items) of its type with the same name. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Cookie;\
let c = [Cookie, Cookie {}, Cookie, Cookie {}];\
}\
```\
\
is equivalent to\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Cookie {}\
const Cookie: Cookie = Cookie {};\
let c = [Cookie, Cookie {}, Cookie, Cookie {}];\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.struct.layout\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.layout "items.struct.layout")\
\
The precise memory layout of a struct is not specified. One can specify a particular layout using the [`repr` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#representations).\
\
[\[items.enum\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum "items.enum")\
\
# [Enumerations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#enumerations)\
\
[\[items.enum.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.syntax "items.enum.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Enumeration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Enumeration) →\
\
enum[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams)?[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClause)?{[EnumVariants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariants)?}\
\
[EnumVariants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariants) → [EnumVariant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariant) ( ,[EnumVariant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariant) )\*,?\
\
[EnumVariant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariant) →\
\
[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\*[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Visibility)?\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) ( [EnumVariantTuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariantTuple) \| [EnumVariantStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariantStruct) )?[EnumVariantDiscriminant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariantDiscriminant)?\
\
[EnumVariantTuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariantTuple) → ([TupleFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleFields)?)\
\
[EnumVariantStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariantStruct) → {[StructFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructFields)?}\
\
[EnumVariantDiscriminant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariantDiscriminant) → =[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Enumeration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Enumeration)\
enum[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParams)[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClause)\
{[EnumVariants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariants)\
}\
\
[EnumVariants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariants)[EnumVariant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariant)\
,[EnumVariant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariant)\
,\
\
[EnumVariant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariant)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Visibility)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)[EnumVariantTuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariantTuple) [EnumVariantStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariantStruct)[EnumVariantDiscriminant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-EnumVariantDiscriminant)\
\
[EnumVariantTuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariantTuple)\
([TupleFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleFields)\
)\
\
[EnumVariantStruct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariantStruct)\
{[StructFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructFields)\
}\
\
[EnumVariantDiscriminant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-EnumVariantDiscriminant)\
=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[items.enum.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.intro "items.enum.intro")\
\
An _enumeration_, also referred to as an _enum_, is a simultaneous definition of a nominal [enumerated type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerated-types) as well as a set of _constructors_, that can be used to create or pattern-match values of the corresponding enumerated type.\
\
[\[items.enum.decl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.decl "items.enum.decl")\
\
Enumerations are declared with the keyword `enum`.\
\
[\[items.enum.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.namespace "items.enum.namespace")\
\
The `enum` declaration defines the enumeration type in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the module or block where it is located.\
\
An example of an `enum` item and its use:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Animal {\
    Dog,\
    Cat,\
}\
\
let mut a: Animal = Animal::Dog;\
a = Animal::Cat;\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.constructor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.constructor "items.enum.constructor")\
\
Enum constructors can have either named or unnamed fields:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Animal {\
    Dog(String, f64),\
    Cat { name: String, weight: f64 },\
}\
\
let mut a: Animal = Animal::Dog("Cocoa".to_string(), 37.2);\
a = Animal::Cat { name: "Spotty".to_string(), weight: 2.7 };\
}\
```\
\
In this example, `Cat` is a _struct-like enum variant_, whereas `Dog` is simply called an enum variant.\
\
[\[items.enum.fieldless\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.fieldless "items.enum.fieldless")\
\
An enum where no constructors contain fields is called a _field-less enum_. For example, this is a fieldless enum:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Fieldless {\
    Tuple(),\
    Struct{},\
    Unit,\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.unit-only\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.unit-only "items.enum.unit-only")\
\
If a field-less enum only contains unit variants, the enum is called an _unit-only enum_. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Enum {\
    Foo = 3,\
    Bar = 2,\
    Baz = 1,\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.constructor-names\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.constructor-names "items.enum.constructor-names")\
\
Variant constructors are similar to [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs) definitions, and can be referenced by a path from the enumeration name, including in [use declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations).\
\
[\[items.enum.constructor-namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.constructor-namespace "items.enum.constructor-namespace")\
\
Each variant defines its type in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces), though that type cannot be used as a type specifier. Tuple-like and unit-like variants also define a constructor in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces).\
\
[\[items.enum.struct-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.struct-expr "items.enum.struct-expr")\
\
A struct-like variant can be instantiated with a [struct expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-expressions).\
\
[\[items.enum.tuple-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.tuple-expr "items.enum.tuple-expr")\
\
A tuple-like variant can be instantiated with a [call expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#call-expressions) or a [struct expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-expressions).\
\
[\[items.enum.path-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.path-expr "items.enum.path-expr")\
\
A unit-like variant can be instantiated with a [path expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-expressions) or a [struct expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-expressions). For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Examples {\
    UnitLike,\
    TupleLike(i32),\
    StructLike { value: i32 },\
}\
\
use Examples::*; // Creates aliases to all variants.\
let x = UnitLike; // Path expression of the const item.\
let x = UnitLike {}; // Struct expression.\
let y = TupleLike(123); // Call expression.\
let y = TupleLike { 0: 123 }; // Struct expression using integer field names.\
let z = StructLike { value: 123 }; // Struct expression.\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant "items.enum.discriminant")\
\
## [Discriminants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#discriminants)\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.intro "items.enum.discriminant.intro")\
\
Each enum instance has a _discriminant_: an integer logically associated to it that is used to determine which variant it holds.\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.repr-rust\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.repr-rust "items.enum.discriminant.repr-rust")\
\
Under the [`Rust` representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-rust-representation), the discriminant is interpreted as an `isize` value. However, the compiler is allowed to use a smaller type (or another means of distinguishing variants) in its actual memory layout.\
\
### [Assigning discriminant values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#assigning-discriminant-values)\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.explicit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.explicit "items.enum.discriminant.explicit")\
\
#### [Explicit discriminants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#explicit-discriminants)\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.explicit.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.explicit.intro "items.enum.discriminant.explicit.intro")\
\
In two circumstances, the discriminant of a variant may be explicitly set by following the variant name with `=` and a [constant expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-expressions):\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.explicit.unit-only\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.explicit.unit-only "items.enum.discriminant.explicit.unit-only")\
\
1. if the enumeration is “ [unit-only](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unit-only-enum)”.\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.explicit.primitive-repr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.explicit.primitive-repr "items.enum.discriminant.explicit.primitive-repr")\
\
2. if a [primitive representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#primitive-representations) is used. For example:\
\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(u8)]\
enum Enum {\
       Unit = 3,\
       Tuple(u16),\
       Struct {\
           a: u8,\
           b: u16,\
       } = 1,\
}\
}\
```\
\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.implicit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.implicit "items.enum.discriminant.implicit")\
\
#### [Implicit discriminants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#implicit-discriminants)\
\
If a discriminant for a variant is not specified, then it is set to one higher than the discriminant of the previous variant in the declaration. If the discriminant of the first variant in the declaration is unspecified, then it is set to zero.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Foo {\
    Bar,            // 0\
    Baz = 123,      // 123\
    Quux,           // 124\
}\
\
let baz_discriminant = Foo::Baz as u32;\
assert_eq!(baz_discriminant, 123);\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.restrictions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.restrictions "items.enum.discriminant.restrictions")\
\
#### [Restrictions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#restrictions-1)\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.same-discriminant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.same-discriminant "items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.same-discriminant")\
\
It is an error when two variants share the same discriminant.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum SharedDiscriminantError {\
    SharedA = 1,\
    SharedB = 1\
}\
\
enum SharedDiscriminantError2 {\
    Zero,       // 0\
    One,        // 1\
    OneToo = 1  // 1 (collision with previous!)\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.above-max-discriminant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.above-max-discriminant "items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.above-max-discriminant")\
\
It is also an error to have an unspecified discriminant where the previous discriminant is the maximum value for the size of the discriminant.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(u8)]\
enum OverflowingDiscriminantError {\
    Max = 255,\
    MaxPlusOne // Would be 256, but that overflows the enum.\
}\
\
#[repr(u8)]\
enum OverflowingDiscriminantError2 {\
    MaxMinusOne = 254, // 254\
    Max,               // 255\
    MaxPlusOne         // Would be 256, but that overflows the enum.\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.generics\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.generics "items.enum.discriminant.restrictions.generics")\
\
Explicit enum discriminant initializers may not use generic parameters from the enclosing enum.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(u32)]\
enum E<'a, T, const N: u32> {\
    Lifetime(&'a T) = {\
        let a: &'a (); // ERROR.\
        1\
    },\
    Type(T) = {\
        let x: T; // ERROR.\
        2\
    },\
    Const = N, // ERROR.\
}\
}\
```\
\
### [Accessing discriminant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#accessing-discriminant)\
\
#### [Via `mem::discriminant`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#via-memdiscriminant)\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.access-opaque\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.access-opaque "items.enum.discriminant.access-opaque")\
\
[`std::mem::discriminant`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/fn.discriminant.html) returns an opaque reference to the discriminant of an enum value which can be compared. This cannot be used to get the value of the discriminant.\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.coercion\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.coercion "items.enum.discriminant.coercion")\
\
#### [Casting](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#casting)\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.coercion.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.coercion.intro "items.enum.discriminant.coercion.intro")\
\
If an enumeration is [unit-only](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unit-only-enum) (with no tuple and struct variants), then its discriminant can be directly accessed with a [numeric cast](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#semantics); e.g.:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Enum {\
    Foo,\
    Bar,\
    Baz,\
}\
\
assert_eq!(0, Enum::Foo as isize);\
assert_eq!(1, Enum::Bar as isize);\
assert_eq!(2, Enum::Baz as isize);\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.coercion.fieldless\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.coercion.fieldless "items.enum.discriminant.coercion.fieldless")\
\
[Field-less enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-less-enum) can be cast if they do not have explicit discriminants, or where only unit variants are explicit.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Fieldless {\
    Tuple(),\
    Struct{},\
    Unit,\
}\
\
assert_eq!(0, Fieldless::Tuple() as isize);\
assert_eq!(1, Fieldless::Struct{} as isize);\
assert_eq!(2, Fieldless::Unit as isize);\
\
#[repr(u8)]\
enum FieldlessWithDiscriminants {\
    First = 10,\
    Tuple(),\
    Second = 20,\
    Struct{},\
    Unit,\
}\
\
assert_eq!(10, FieldlessWithDiscriminants::First as u8);\
assert_eq!(11, FieldlessWithDiscriminants::Tuple() as u8);\
assert_eq!(20, FieldlessWithDiscriminants::Second as u8);\
assert_eq!(21, FieldlessWithDiscriminants::Struct{} as u8);\
assert_eq!(22, FieldlessWithDiscriminants::Unit as u8);\
}\
```\
\
#### [Pointer casting](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pointer-casting)\
\
[\[items.enum.discriminant.access-memory\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.discriminant.access-memory "items.enum.discriminant.access-memory")\
\
If the enumeration specifies a [primitive representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#primitive-representations), then the discriminant may be reliably accessed via unsafe pointer casting:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(u8)]\
enum Enum {\
    Unit,\
    Tuple(bool),\
    Struct{a: bool},\
}\
\
impl Enum {\
    fn discriminant(&self) -> u8 {\
        unsafe { *(self as *const Self as *const u8) }\
    }\
}\
\
let unit_like = Enum::Unit;\
let tuple_like = Enum::Tuple(true);\
let struct_like = Enum::Struct{a: false};\
\
assert_eq!(0, unit_like.discriminant());\
assert_eq!(1, tuple_like.discriminant());\
assert_eq!(2, struct_like.discriminant());\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.empty\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.empty "items.enum.empty")\
\
## [Zero-variant enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#zero-variant-enums)\
\
[\[items.enum.empty.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.empty.intro "items.enum.empty.intro")\
\
Enums with zero variants are known as _zero-variant enums_. As they have no valid values, they cannot be instantiated.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum ZeroVariants {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.empty.uninhabited\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.empty.uninhabited "items.enum.empty.uninhabited")\
\
Zero-variant enums are equivalent to the [never type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#never-type), but they cannot be coerced into other types.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum ZeroVariants {}\
let x: ZeroVariants = panic!();\
let y: u32 = x; // mismatched type error\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.enum.variant-visibility\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.variant-visibility "items.enum.variant-visibility")\
\
## [Variant visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#variant-visibility)\
\
Enum variants syntactically allow a [Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Visibility) annotation, but this is rejected when the enum is validated. This allows items to be parsed with a unified syntax across different contexts where they are used.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! mac_variant {\
    ($vis:vis $name:ident) => {\
        enum $name {\
            $vis Unit,\
\
            $vis Tuple(u8, u16),\
\
            $vis Struct { f: u8 },\
        }\
    }\
}\
\
// Empty `vis` is allowed.\
mac_variant! { E }\
\
// This is allowed, since it is removed before being validated.\
#[cfg(false)]\
enum E {\
    pub U,\
    pub(crate) T(u8),\
    pub(super) T { f: String }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.union\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union "items.union")\
\
# [Unions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unions)\
\
[\[items.union.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.syntax "items.union.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Union) →\
\
union[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams)?[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClause)?{[StructFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructFields)?}\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Union)\
union[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParams)[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClause)\
{[StructFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructFields)\
}\
\
[\[items.union.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.intro "items.union.intro")\
\
A union declaration uses the same syntax as a struct declaration, except with `union` in place of `struct`.\
\
[\[items.union.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.namespace "items.union.namespace")\
\
A union declaration defines the given name in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the module or block where it is located.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(C)]\
union MyUnion {\
    f1: u32,\
    f2: f32,\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.union.common-storage\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.common-storage "items.union.common-storage")\
\
The key property of unions is that all fields of a union share common storage. As a result, writes to one field of a union can overwrite its other fields, and size of a union is determined by the size of its largest field.\
\
[\[items.union.field-restrictions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.field-restrictions "items.union.field-restrictions")\
\
Union field types are restricted to the following subset of types:\
\
[\[items.union.field-copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.field-copy "items.union.field-copy")\
\
- `Copy` types\
\
[\[items.union.field-references\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.field-references "items.union.field-references")\
\
- References (`&T` and `&mut T` for arbitrary `T`)\
\
[\[items.union.field-manually-drop\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.field-manually-drop "items.union.field-manually-drop")\
\
- `ManuallyDrop<T>` (for arbitrary `T`)\
\
[\[items.union.field-tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.field-tuple "items.union.field-tuple")\
\
- Tuples and arrays containing only allowed union field types\
\
[\[items.union.drop\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.drop "items.union.drop")\
\
This restriction ensures, in particular, that union fields never need to be dropped. Like for structs and enums, it is possible to `impl Drop` for a union to manually define what happens when it gets dropped.\
\
[\[items.union.fieldless\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.fieldless "items.union.fieldless")\
\
Unions without any fields are not accepted by the compiler, but can be accepted by macros.\
\
[\[items.union.init\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.init "items.union.init")\
\
## [Initialization of a union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#initialization-of-a-union)\
\
[\[items.union.init.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.init.intro "items.union.init.intro")\
\
A value of a union type can be created using the same syntax that is used for struct types, except that it must specify exactly one field:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
union MyUnion { f1: u32, f2: f32 }\
\
let u = MyUnion { f1: 1 };\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.union.init.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.init.result "items.union.init.result")\
\
The expression above creates a value of type `MyUnion` and initializes the storage using field `f1`. The union can be accessed using the same syntax as struct fields:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
union MyUnion { f1: u32, f2: f32 }\
\
let u = MyUnion { f1: 1 };\
let f = unsafe { u.f1 };\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.union.fields\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.fields "items.union.fields")\
\
## [Reading and writing union fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reading-and-writing-union-fields)\
\
[\[items.union.fields.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.fields.intro "items.union.fields.intro")\
\
Unions have no notion of an “active field”. Instead, every union access just interprets the storage as the type of the field used for the access.\
\
[\[items.union.fields.read\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.fields.read "items.union.fields.read")\
\
Reading a union field reads the bits of the union at the field’s type.\
\
[\[items.union.fields.offset\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.fields.offset "items.union.fields.offset")\
\
Fields might have a non-zero offset (except when [the C representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#reprc-unions) is used); in that case the bits starting at the offset of the fields are read\
\
[\[items.union.fields.validity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.fields.validity "items.union.fields.validity")\
\
It is the programmer’s responsibility to make sure that the data is valid at the field’s type. Failing to do so results in [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined). For example, reading the value `3` from a field of the [boolean type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type) is undefined behavior. Effectively, writing to and then reading from a union with [the C representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#reprc-unions) is analogous to a [`transmute`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/intrinsics/fn.transmute.html) from the type used for writing to the type used for reading.\
\
[\[items.union.fields.read-safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.fields.read-safety "items.union.fields.read-safety")\
\
Consequently, all reads of union fields have to be placed in `unsafe` blocks:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
union MyUnion { f1: u32, f2: f32 }\
let u = MyUnion { f1: 1 };\
\
unsafe {\
    let f = u.f1;\
}\
}\
```\
\
Commonly, code using unions will provide safe wrappers around unsafe union field accesses.\
\
[\[items.union.fields.write-safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.fields.write-safety "items.union.fields.write-safety")\
\
In contrast, writes to union fields are safe, since they just overwrite arbitrary data, but cannot cause undefined behavior. (Note that union field types can never have drop glue, so a union field write will never implicitly drop anything.)\
\
[\[items.union.pattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.pattern "items.union.pattern")\
\
## [Pattern matching on unions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pattern-matching-on-unions)\
\
[\[items.union.pattern.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.pattern.intro "items.union.pattern.intro")\
\
Another way to access union fields is to use pattern matching.\
\
[\[items.union.pattern.one-field\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.pattern.one-field "items.union.pattern.one-field")\
\
Pattern matching on union fields uses the same syntax as struct patterns, except that the pattern must specify exactly one field.\
\
[\[items.union.pattern.safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.pattern.safety "items.union.pattern.safety")\
\
Since pattern matching is like reading the union with a particular field, it has to be placed in `unsafe` blocks as well.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
union MyUnion { f1: u32, f2: f32 }\
\
fn f(u: MyUnion) {\
    unsafe {\
        match u {\
            MyUnion { f1: 10 } => { println!("ten"); }\
            MyUnion { f2 } => { println!("{}", f2); }\
        }\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.union.pattern.subpattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.pattern.subpattern "items.union.pattern.subpattern")\
\
Pattern matching may match a union as a field of a larger structure. In particular, when using a Rust union to implement a C tagged union via FFI, this allows matching on the tag and the corresponding field simultaneously:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(u32)]\
enum Tag { I, F }\
\
#[repr(C)]\
union U {\
    i: i32,\
    f: f32,\
}\
\
#[repr(C)]\
struct Value {\
    tag: Tag,\
    u: U,\
}\
\
fn is_zero(v: Value) -> bool {\
    unsafe {\
        match v {\
            Value { tag: Tag::I, u: U { i: 0 } } => true,\
            Value { tag: Tag::F, u: U { f: num } } if num == 0.0 => true,\
            _ => false,\
        }\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.union.ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.ref "items.union.ref")\
\
## [References to union fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#references-to-union-fields)\
\
[\[items.union.ref.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.ref.intro "items.union.ref.intro")\
\
Since union fields share common storage, gaining write access to one field of a union can give write access to all its remaining fields.\
\
[\[items.union.ref.borrow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.ref.borrow "items.union.ref.borrow")\
\
Borrow checking rules have to be adjusted to account for this fact. As a result, if one field of a union is borrowed, all its remaining fields are borrowed as well for the same lifetime.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
union MyUnion { f1: u32, f2: f32 }\
// ERROR: cannot borrow `u` (via `u.f2`) as mutable more than once at a time\
fn test() {\
    let mut u = MyUnion { f1: 1 };\
    unsafe {\
        let b1 = &mut u.f1;\
//                    ---- first mutable borrow occurs here (via `u.f1`)\
        let b2 = &mut u.f2;\
//                    ^^^^ second mutable borrow occurs here (via `u.f2`)\
        *b1 = 5;\
    }\
//  - first borrow ends here\
    assert_eq!(unsafe { u.f1 }, 5);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.union.ref.usage\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.union.ref.usage "items.union.ref.usage")\
\
As you could see, in many aspects (except for layouts, safety, and ownership) unions behave exactly like structs, largely as a consequence of inheriting their syntactic shape from structs. This is also true for many unmentioned aspects of Rust language (such as privacy, name resolution, type inference, generics, trait implementations, inherent implementations, coherence, pattern checking, etc etc etc).\
\
[\[items.const\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const "items.const")\
\
# [Constant items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#constant-items)\
\
[\[items.const.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.syntax "items.const.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ConstantItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConstantItem) →\
\
const ( [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| \_ ) :[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) ( =[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) )?;\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ConstantItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConstantItem)\
const[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
\_\
:[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
;\
\
[\[items.const.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.intro "items.const.intro")\
\
A _constant item_ is an optionally named _[constant value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-expressions)_ which is not associated with a specific memory location in the program.\
\
[\[items.const.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.behavior "items.const.behavior")\
\
Constants are essentially inlined wherever they are used, meaning that they are copied directly into the relevant context when used. This includes usage of constants from external crates, and non- [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy) types. References to the same constant are not necessarily guaranteed to refer to the same memory address.\
\
[\[items.const.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.namespace "items.const.namespace")\
\
The constant declaration defines the constant value in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the module or block where it is located.\
\
[\[items.const.static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.static "items.const.static")\
\
Constants must be explicitly typed. The type must have a `'static` lifetime: any references in the initializer must have `'static` lifetimes. References in the type of a constant default to `'static` lifetime; see [static lifetime elision](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#const-and-static-elision).\
\
[\[items.const.static-temporary\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.static-temporary "items.const.static-temporary")\
\
A reference to a constant will have `'static` lifetime if the constant value is eligible for [promotion](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.const-promotion); otherwise, a temporary will be created.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
const BIT1: u32 = 1 << 0;\
const BIT2: u32 = 1 << 1;\
\
const BITS: [u32; 2] = [BIT1, BIT2];\
const STRING: &'static str = "bitstring";\
\
struct BitsNStrings<'a> {\
    mybits: [u32; 2],\
    mystring: &'a str,\
}\
\
const BITS_N_STRINGS: BitsNStrings<'static> = BitsNStrings {\
    mybits: BITS,\
    mystring: STRING,\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.const.expr-omission\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.expr-omission "items.const.expr-omission")\
\
The constant expression may only be omitted in a [trait definition](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits).\
\
[\[items.const.destructor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.destructor "items.const.destructor")\
\
## [Constants with destructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#constants-with-destructors)\
\
Constants can contain destructors. Destructors are run when the value goes out of scope.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct TypeWithDestructor(i32);\
\
impl Drop for TypeWithDestructor {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("Dropped. Held {}.", self.0);\
    }\
}\
\
const ZERO_WITH_DESTRUCTOR: TypeWithDestructor = TypeWithDestructor(0);\
\
fn create_and_drop_zero_with_destructor() {\
    let x = ZERO_WITH_DESTRUCTOR;\
    // x gets dropped at end of function, calling drop.\
    // prints "Dropped. Held 0.".\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.const.unnamed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.unnamed "items.const.unnamed")\
\
## [Unnamed constant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unnamed-constant)\
\
[\[items.const.unnamed.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.unnamed.intro "items.const.unnamed.intro")\
\
Unlike an [associated constant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-constants), a [free](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#free-item) constant may be unnamed by using an underscore instead of the name. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
const _: () =  { struct _SameNameTwice; };\
\
// OK although it is the same name as above:\
const _: () =  { struct _SameNameTwice; };\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.const.unnamed.repetition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.unnamed.repetition "items.const.unnamed.repetition")\
\
As with [underscore imports](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#underscore-imports-1), macros may safely emit the same unnamed constant in the same scope more than once. For example, the following should not produce an error:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! m {\
    ($item: item) => { $item $item }\
}\
\
m!(const _: () = (););\
// This expands to:\
// const _: () = ();\
// const _: () = ();\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.const.eval\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.const.eval "items.const.eval")\
\
## [Evaluation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#evaluation)\
\
[Free](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#free-item) constants are always [evaluated](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-evaluation) at compile-time to surface panics. This happens even within an unused function:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Compile-time panic\
const PANIC: () = std::unimplemented!();\
\
fn unused_generic_function<T>() {\
    // A failing compile-time assertion\
    const _: () = assert!(usize::BITS == 0);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static "items.static")\
\
# [Static items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#static-items)\
\
[\[items.static.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.syntax "items.static.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[StaticItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StaticItem) →\
\
[ItemSafety](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ItemSafety)?​[1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-extern-safety)staticmut?[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER):[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) ( =[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) )?;\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[StaticItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StaticItem)[ItemSafety](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ItemSafety)\
static\
mut[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
:[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
;\
\
[\[items.static.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.intro "items.static.intro")\
\
A _static item_ is similar to a [constant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items), except that it represents an allocation in the program that is initialized with the initializer expression. All references and raw pointers to the static refer to the same allocation.\
\
[\[items.static.lifetime\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.lifetime "items.static.lifetime")\
\
Static items have the `static` lifetime, which outlives all other lifetimes in a Rust program. Static items do not call [`drop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#destructors) at the end of the program.\
\
[\[items.static.storage-disjointness\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.storage-disjointness "items.static.storage-disjointness")\
\
If the `static` has a size of at least 1 byte, this allocation is disjoint from all other such `static` allocations as well as heap allocations and stack-allocated variables. However, the storage of immutable `static` items can overlap with allocations that do not themselves have a unique address, such as [promoteds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-promotion) and [`const` items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items).\
\
[\[items.static.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.namespace "items.static.namespace")\
\
The static declaration defines a static value in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of the module or block where it is located.\
\
[\[items.static.init\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.init "items.static.init")\
\
The static initializer is a [constant expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-expressions) evaluated at compile time. Static initializers may refer to and read from other statics. When reading from mutable statics, they read the initial value of that static.\
\
[\[items.static.read-only\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.read-only "items.static.read-only")\
\
Non-`mut` static items that contain a type that is not [interior mutable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#interior-mutability) may be placed in read-only memory.\
\
[\[items.static.safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.safety "items.static.safety")\
\
All access to a static is safe, but there are a number of restrictions on statics:\
\
[\[items.static.sync\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.sync "items.static.sync")\
\
- The type must have the [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Sync.html) trait bound to allow thread-safe access.\
\
[\[items.static.init.omission\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.init.omission "items.static.init.omission")\
\
The initializer expression must be omitted in an [external block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks), and must be provided for free static items.\
\
[\[items.static.safety-qualifiers\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.safety-qualifiers "items.static.safety-qualifiers")\
\
The `safe` and `unsafe` qualifiers are semantically only allowed when used in an [external block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks).\
\
[\[items.static.generics\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.static.generics "items.static.generics")\
\
## [Statics & generics](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#statics--generics)\
\
A static item defined in a generic scope (for example in a blanket or default implementation) will result in exactly one static item being defined, as if the static definition was pulled out of the current scope into the module. There will _not_ be one item per monomorphization.\
\
This code:\
\
```rust\
\
use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicUsize, Ordering};\
\
trait Tr {\
    fn default_impl() {\
        static COUNTER: AtomicUsize = AtomicUsize::new(0);\
        println!("default_impl: counter was {}", COUNTER.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed));\
    }\
\
    fn blanket_impl();\
}\
\
struct Ty1 {}\
struct Ty2 {}\
\
impl<T> Tr for T {\
    fn blanket_impl() {\
        static COUNTER: AtomicUsize = AtomicUsize::new(0);\
        println!("blanket_impl: counter was {}", COUNTER.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed));\
    }\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    <Ty1 as Tr>::default_impl();\
    <Ty2 as Tr>::default_impl();\
    <Ty1 as Tr>::blanket_impl();\
    <Ty2 as Tr>::blanket_impl();\
}\
```\
\
prints\
\
```text\
\
default_impl: counter was 0\
default_impl: counter was 1\
