[\[items.extern.attributes.link\_ordinal.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern.attributes.link_ordinal.intro "items.extern.attributes.link_ordinal.intro")\
\
The _`link_ordinal` attribute_ can be applied on declarations inside an `extern` block to indicate the numeric ordinal to use when generating the import library to link against. An ordinal is a unique number per symbol exported by a dynamic library on Windows and can be used when the library is being loaded to find that symbol rather than having to look it up by name.\
\
> Warning\
>\
> `link_ordinal` should only be used in cases where the ordinal of the symbol is known to be stable: if the ordinal of a symbol is not explicitly set when its containing binary is built then one will be automatically assigned to it, and that assigned ordinal may change between builds of the binary.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[cfg(all(windows, target_arch = "x86"))]\
#[link(name = "exporter", kind = "raw-dylib")]\
unsafe extern "stdcall" {\
    #[link_ordinal(15)]\
    safe fn imported_function_stdcall(i: i32);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.extern.attributes.link\_ordinal.allowed-kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern.attributes.link_ordinal.allowed-kinds "items.extern.attributes.link_ordinal.allowed-kinds")\
\
This attribute is only used with the `raw-dylib` linking kind. Using any other kind will result in a compiler error.\
\
[\[items.extern.attributes.link\_ordinal.exclusive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern.attributes.link_ordinal.exclusive "items.extern.attributes.link_ordinal.exclusive")\
\
Using this attribute with the `link_name` attribute will result in a compiler error.\
\
[\[items.extern.attributes.fn-parameters\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern.attributes.fn-parameters "items.extern.attributes.fn-parameters")\
\
### [Attributes on function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-on-function-parameters-1)\
\
Attributes on extern function parameters follow the same rules and restrictions as [regular function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-on-function-parameters).\
\
* * *\
\
1. Starting with the 2024 Edition, the `unsafe` keyword is required semantically. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-unsafe-2024-1)\
\
\
[\[items.generics\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics "items.generics")\
\
# [Generic parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#generic-parameters)\
\
[\[items.generics.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.syntax "items.generics.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParams) → < ( [GenericParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParam) ( ,[GenericParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParam) )\*,? )?>\
\
[GenericParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParam) → [OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\* ( [LifetimeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LifetimeParam) \| [TypeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParam) \| [ConstParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConstParam) )\
\
[LifetimeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LifetimeParam) → [Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Lifetime) ( :[LifetimeBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LifetimeBounds) )?\
\
[TypeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeParam) → [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) ( :[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds)? )? ( =[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) )?\
\
[ConstParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConstParam) →\
\
const[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER):[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)\
\
    ( = ( [BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression) \| [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| -?[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LiteralExpression) ) )?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams)\
<[GenericParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParam)\
,[GenericParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParam)\
,\
>\
\
[GenericParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParam)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[LifetimeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LifetimeParam) [TypeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeParam) [ConstParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConstParam)\
\
[LifetimeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LifetimeParam)[Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Lifetime)\
:[LifetimeBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LifetimeBounds)\
\
[TypeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParam)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
:[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeParamBounds)\
=[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
\
[ConstParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ConstParam)\
const[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
:[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
=[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression) [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
-[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LiteralExpression)\
\
[\[items.generics.syntax.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.syntax.intro "items.generics.syntax.intro")\
\
[Functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions), [type aliases](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-aliases), [structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), [enumerations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations), [unions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions), [traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits), and [implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations) may be _parameterized_ by types, constants, and lifetimes. These parameters are listed in angle brackets (`<...>`), usually immediately after the name of the item and before its definition. For implementations, which don’t have a name, they come directly after `impl`.\
\
[\[items.generics.syntax.decl-order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.syntax.decl-order "items.generics.syntax.decl-order")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/const-generics/argument\_order.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/const-generics/argument_order.rs)\
\
The order of generic parameters is restricted to lifetime parameters and then type and const parameters intermixed.\
\
[\[items.generics.syntax.duplicate-params\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.syntax.duplicate-params "items.generics.syntax.duplicate-params")\
\
The same parameter name may not be declared more than once in a [GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams) list.\
\
Some examples of items with type, const, and lifetime parameters:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn foo<'a, T>() {}\
trait A<U> {}\
struct Ref<'a, T> where T: 'a { r: &'a T }\
struct InnerArray<T, const N: usize>([T; N]);\
struct EitherOrderWorks<const N: bool, U>(U);\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.generics.syntax.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.syntax.scope "items.generics.syntax.scope")\
\
Generic parameters are in scope within the item definition where they are declared. They are not in scope for items declared within the body of a function as described in [item declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#item-declarations). See [generic parameter scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameter-scopes) for more details.\
\
[\[items.generics.builtin-generic-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.builtin-generic-types "items.generics.builtin-generic-types")\
\
[References](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#shared-references-), [raw pointers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-pointers-const-and-mut), [arrays](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types), [slices](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-types), [tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types), and [function pointers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-pointer-types) have lifetime or type parameters as well, but are not referred to with path syntax.\
\
[\[items.generics.invalid-lifetimes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.invalid-lifetimes "items.generics.invalid-lifetimes")\
\
`'_` and `'static` are not valid lifetime parameter names.\
\
[\[items.generics.const\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const "items.generics.const")\
\
### [Const generics](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#const-generics)\
\
[\[items.generics.const.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.intro "items.generics.const.intro")\
\
_Const generic parameters_ allow items to be generic over constant values.\
\
[\[items.generics.const.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.namespace "items.generics.const.namespace")\
\
The const identifier introduces a name in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) for the constant parameter, and all instances of the item must be instantiated with a value of the given type.\
\
[\[items.generics.const.allowed-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.allowed-types "items.generics.const.allowed-types")\
\
The only allowed types of const parameters are `u8`, `u16`, `u32`, `u64`, `u128`, `usize`, `i8`, `i16`, `i32`, `i64`, `i128`, `isize`, `char` and `bool`.\
\
[\[items.generics.const.usage\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.usage "items.generics.const.usage")\
\
Const parameters can be used anywhere a [const item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items) can be used, with the exception that when used in a [type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#types) or [array repeat expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-array-index-expressions), it must be standalone (as described below). That is, they are allowed in the following places:\
\
1. As an applied const to any type which forms a part of the signature of the item in question.\
2. As part of a const expression used to define an [associated const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-constants), or as a parameter to an [associated type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types).\
3. As a value in any runtime expression in the body of any functions in the item.\
4. As a parameter to any type used in the body of any functions in the item.\
5. As a part of the type of any fields in the item.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Examples where const generic parameters can be used.\
\
// Used in the signature of the item itself.\
fn foo<const N: usize>(arr: [i32; N]) {\
    // Used as a type within a function body.\
    let x: [i32; N];\
    // Used as an expression.\
    println!("{}", N * 2);\
}\
\
// Used as a field of a struct.\
struct Foo<const N: usize>([i32; N]);\
\
impl<const N: usize> Foo<N> {\
    // Used as an associated constant.\
    const CONST: usize = N * 4;\
}\
\
trait Trait {\
    type Output;\
}\
\
impl<const N: usize> Trait for Foo<N> {\
    // Used as an associated type.\
    type Output = [i32; N];\
}\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Examples where const generic parameters cannot be used.\
fn foo<const N: usize>() {\
    // Cannot use in item definitions within a function body.\
    const BAD_CONST: [usize; N] = [1; N];\
    static BAD_STATIC: [usize; N] = [1; N];\
    fn inner(bad_arg: [usize; N]) {\
        let bad_value = N * 2;\
    }\
    type BadAlias = [usize; N];\
    struct BadStruct([usize; N]);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.generics.const.standalone\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.standalone "items.generics.const.standalone")\
\
As a further restriction, const parameters may only appear as a standalone argument inside of a [type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#types) or [array repeat expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-array-index-expressions). In those contexts, they may only be used as a single segment [path expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-expressions), possibly inside a [block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions) (such as `N` or `{N}`). That is, they cannot be combined with other expressions.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Examples where const parameters may not be used.\
\
// Not allowed to combine in other expressions in types, such as the\
// arithmetic expression in the return type here.\
fn bad_function<const N: usize>() -> [u8; {N + 1}] {\
    // Similarly not allowed for array repeat expressions.\
    [1; {N + 1}]\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.generics.const.argument\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.argument "items.generics.const.argument")\
\
A const argument in a [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) specifies the const value to use for that item.\
\
[\[items.generics.const.argument.const-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.argument.const-expr "items.generics.const.argument.const-expr")\
\
The argument must either be an [inferred const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred) or be a [const expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-expressions) of the type ascribed to the const parameter. The const expression must be a [block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions) (surrounded with braces) unless it is a single path segment (an [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)) or a [literal](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literal-expressions) (with a possibly leading `-` token).\
\
> Note\
>\
> This syntactic restriction is necessary to avoid requiring infinite lookahead when parsing an expression inside of a type.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S<const N: i64>;\
const C: i64 = 1;\
fn f<const N: i64>() -> S<N> { S }\
\
let _ = f::<1>(); // Literal.\
let _ = f::<-1>(); // Negative literal.\
let _ = f::<{ 1 + 2 }>(); // Constant expression.\
let _ = f::<C>(); // Single segment path.\
let _ = f::<{ C + 1 }>(); // Constant expression.\
let _: S<1> = f::<_>(); // Inferred const.\
let _: S<1> = f::<(((_)))>(); // Inferred const.\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> In a generic argument list, an [inferred const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred) is parsed as an [inferred type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InferredType) but then semantically treated as a separate kind of [const generic argument](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.argument).\
\
[\[items.generics.const.inferred\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred "items.generics.const.inferred")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/const-generics/generic\_arg\_infer/paren\_infer.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/const-generics/generic_arg_infer/paren_infer.rs)\
\
Where a const argument is expected, an `_` (optionally surrounded by any number of matching parentheses), called the _inferred const_ ( [path rules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.expr.complex-const-params), [array expression rules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.length-restriction)), can be used instead. This asks the compiler to infer the const argument if possible based on surrounding information.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn make_buf<const N: usize>() -> [u8; N] {\
    [0; _]\
    //  ^ Infers `N`.\
}\
let _: [u8; 1024] = make_buf::<_>();\
//                             ^ Infers `1024`.\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> An [inferred const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred) is not semantically an [expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) and so is not accepted within braces.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn f<const N: usize>() -> [u8; N] { [0; _] }\
> let _: [_; 1] = f::<{ _ }>();\
> //                    ^ ERROR `_` not allowed here\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[items.generics.const.inferred.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred.constraint "items.generics.const.inferred.constraint")\
\
The inferred const cannot be used in item signatures.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn f<const N: usize>(x: [u8; N]) -> [u8; _] { x }\
//                                       ^ ERROR not allowed\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.generics.const.type-ambiguity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.type-ambiguity "items.generics.const.type-ambiguity")\
\
When there is ambiguity if a generic argument could be resolved as either a type or const argument, it is always resolved as a type. Placing the argument in a block expression can force it to be interpreted as a const argument.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
type N = u32;\
struct Foo<const N: usize>;\
// The following is an error, because `N` is interpreted as the type alias `N`.\
fn foo<const N: usize>() -> Foo<N> { todo!() } // ERROR\
// Can be fixed by wrapping in braces to force it to be interpreted as the `N`\
// const parameter:\
fn bar<const N: usize>() -> Foo<{ N }> { todo!() } // ok\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.generics.const.variance\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.variance "items.generics.const.variance")\
\
Unlike type and lifetime parameters, const parameters can be declared without being used inside of a parameterized item, with the exception of implementations as described in [generic implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-implementations):\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// ok\
struct Foo<const N: usize>;\
enum Bar<const M: usize> { A, B }\
\
// ERROR: unused parameter\
struct Baz<T>;\
struct Biz<'a>;\
struct Unconstrained;\
impl<const N: usize> Unconstrained {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.generics.const.exhaustiveness\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.exhaustiveness "items.generics.const.exhaustiveness")\
\
When resolving a trait bound obligation, the exhaustiveness of all implementations of const parameters is not considered when determining if the bound is satisfied. For example, in the following, even though all possible const values for the `bool` type are implemented, it is still an error that the trait bound is not satisfied:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Foo<const B: bool>;\
trait Bar {}\
impl Bar for Foo<true> {}\
impl Bar for Foo<false> {}\
\
fn needs_bar(_: impl Bar) {}\
fn generic<const B: bool>() {\
    let v = Foo::<B>;\
    needs_bar(v); // ERROR: trait bound `Foo<B>: Bar` is not satisfied\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.generics.where\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.where "items.generics.where")\
\
## [Where clauses](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#where-clauses)\
\
[\[items.generics.where.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.where.syntax "items.generics.where.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClause) → where ( [WhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClauseItem), )\*[WhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClauseItem)?\
\
[WhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClauseItem) →\
\
[LifetimeWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LifetimeWhereClauseItem)\
\
    \| [TypeBoundWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeBoundWhereClauseItem)\
\
[LifetimeWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LifetimeWhereClauseItem) → [Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Lifetime):[LifetimeBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LifetimeBounds)\
\
[TypeBoundWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeBoundWhereClauseItem) → [ForLifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ForLifetimes)?[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type):[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds)?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[WhereClause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClause)\
where[WhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClauseItem)\
,[WhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WhereClauseItem)\
\
[WhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WhereClauseItem)[LifetimeWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LifetimeWhereClauseItem)[TypeBoundWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeBoundWhereClauseItem)\
\
[LifetimeWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LifetimeWhereClauseItem)[Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Lifetime)\
:[LifetimeBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LifetimeBounds)\
\
[TypeBoundWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeBoundWhereClauseItem)[ForLifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ForLifetimes)[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
:[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeParamBounds)\
\
[\[items.generics.where.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.where.intro "items.generics.where.intro")\
\
_Where clauses_ provide another way to specify bounds on type and lifetime parameters as well as a way to specify bounds on types that aren’t type parameters.\
\
[\[items.generics.where.higher-ranked-lifetimes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.where.higher-ranked-lifetimes "items.generics.where.higher-ranked-lifetimes")\
\
The `for` keyword can be used to introduce [higher-ranked lifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#higher-ranked-trait-bounds). It only allows [LifetimeParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LifetimeParam) parameters.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct A<T>\
where\
    T: Iterator,            // Could use A<T: Iterator> instead\
    T::Item: Copy,          // Bound on an associated type\
    String: PartialEq<T>,   // Bound on `String`, using the type parameter\
    i32: Default,           // Allowed, but not useful\
{\
    f: T,\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.generics.attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.attributes "items.generics.attributes")\
\
## [Attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes)\
\
Generic lifetime and type parameters allow [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) on them. There are no built-in attributes that do anything in this position, although custom derive attributes may give meaning to it.\
\
This example shows using a custom derive attribute to modify the meaning of a generic parameter.\
\
```rust\
\
// Assume that the derive for MyFlexibleClone declared `my_flexible_clone` as\
// an attribute it understands.\
#[derive(MyFlexibleClone)]\
struct Foo<#[my_flexible_clone(unbounded)] H> {\
    a: *const H\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.associated\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated "items.associated")\
\
# [Associated items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#associated-items)\
\
[\[items.associated.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.syntax "items.associated.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[AssociatedItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AssociatedItem) →\
\

\
    )\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[AssociatedItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AssociatedItem)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[MacroInvocationSemi](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MacroInvocationSemi)[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Visibility)[TypeAlias](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeAlias) [ConstantItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ConstantItem) [Function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Function)\
\
[\[items.associated.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.intro "items.associated.intro")\
\
_Associated Items_ are the items declared in [traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) or defined in [implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations). They are called this because they are defined on an associate type — the type in the implementation.\
\
[\[items.associated.kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.kinds "items.associated.kinds")\
\
They are a subset of the kinds of items you can declare in a module. Specifically, there are [associated functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-functions-and-methods) (including methods), [associated types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types), and [associated constants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-constants).\
\
[\[items.associated.related\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.related "items.associated.related")\
\
Associated items are useful when the associated item is logically related to the associating item. For example, the `is_some` method on `Option` is intrinsically related to Options, so should be associated.\
\
[\[items.associated.decl-def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.decl-def "items.associated.decl-def")\
\
Every associated item kind comes in two varieties: definitions that contain the actual implementation and declarations that declare signatures for definitions.\
\
[\[items.associated.trait-items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.trait-items "items.associated.trait-items")\
\
It is the declarations that make up the contract of traits and what is available on generic types.\
\
[\[items.associated.fn\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn "items.associated.fn")\
\
## [Associated functions and methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#associated-functions-and-methods)\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.intro "items.associated.fn.intro")\
\
_Associated functions_ are [functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions) associated with a type.\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.decl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.decl "items.associated.fn.decl")\
\
An _associated function declaration_ declares a signature for an associated function definition. It is written as a function item, except the function body is replaced with a `;`.\
\
[\[items.associated.name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.name "items.associated.name")\
\
The identifier is the name of the function.\
\
[\[items.associated.same-signature\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.same-signature "items.associated.same-signature")\
\
The generics, parameter list, return type, and where clause of the associated function must be the same as the associated function declarations’s.\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.def "items.associated.fn.def")\
\
An _associated function definition_ defines a function associated with another type. It is written the same as a [function item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-item-types).\
\
> Note\
>\
> A common example is an associated function named `new` that returns a value of the type with which it is associated.\
\
```rust\
\
struct Struct {\
    field: i32\
}\
\
impl Struct {\
    fn new() -> Struct {\
        Struct {\
            field: 0i32\
        }\
    }\
}\
\
fn main () {\
    let _struct = Struct::new();\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.qualified-self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.qualified-self "items.associated.fn.qualified-self")\
\
When the associated function is declared on a trait, the function can also be called with a [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) that is a path to the trait appended by the name of the trait. When this happens, it is substituted for `<_ as Trait>::function_name`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Num {\
    fn from_i32(n: i32) -> Self;\
}\
\
impl Num for f64 {\
    fn from_i32(n: i32) -> f64 { n as f64 }\
}\
\
// These 4 are all equivalent in this case.\
let _: f64 = Num::from_i32(42);\
let _: f64 = <_ as Num>::from_i32(42);\
let _: f64 = <f64 as Num>::from_i32(42);\
let _: f64 = f64::from_i32(42);\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.method\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.method "items.associated.fn.method")\
\
### [Methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#methods)\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.method.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.method.intro "items.associated.fn.method.intro")\
\
Associated functions whose first parameter is named `self` are called _methods_ and may be invoked using the [method call operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#method-call-expressions), for example, `x.foo()`, as well as the usual function call notation.\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.method.self-ty\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.method.self-ty "items.associated.fn.method.self-ty")\
\
If the type of the `self` parameter is specified, it is limited to types resolving to one generated by the following grammar (where `'lt` denotes some arbitrary lifetime):\
\
```text\
\
P = &'lt S | &'lt mut S | Box<S> | Rc<S> | Arc<S> | Pin<P>\
S = Self | P\
```\
\
The `Self` terminal in this grammar denotes a type resolving to the implementing type. This can also include the contextual type alias `Self`, other type aliases, or associated type projections resolving to the implementing type.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::rc::Rc;\
use std::sync::Arc;\
use std::pin::Pin;\
// Examples of methods implemented on struct `Example`.\
struct Example;\
type Alias = Example;\
trait Trait { type Output; }\
impl Trait for Example { type Output = Example; }\
impl Example {\
    fn by_value(self: Self) {}\
    fn by_ref(self: &Self) {}\
    fn by_ref_mut(self: &mut Self) {}\
    fn by_box(self: Box<Self>) {}\
    fn by_rc(self: Rc<Self>) {}\
    fn by_arc(self: Arc<Self>) {}\
    fn by_pin(self: Pin<&Self>) {}\
    fn explicit_type(self: Arc<Example>) {}\
    fn with_lifetime<'a>(self: &'a Self) {}\
    fn nested<'a>(self: &mut &'a Arc<Rc<Box<Alias>>>) {}\
    fn via_projection(self: <Example as Trait>::Output) {}\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[associated.fn.method.self-pat-shorthands\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-associated.fn.method.self-pat-shorthands "associated.fn.method.self-pat-shorthands")\
\
Shorthand syntax can be used without specifying a type, which have the following equivalents:\
\
| Shorthand | Equivalent |\
| --- | --- |\
| `self` | `self: Self` |\
| `&'lifetime self` | `self: &'lifetime Self` |\
| `&'lifetime mut self` | `self: &'lifetime mut Self` |\
\
> Note\
>\
> Lifetimes can be, and usually are, elided with this shorthand.\
\
[\[associated.fn.method.self-pat-mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-associated.fn.method.self-pat-mut "associated.fn.method.self-pat-mut")\
\
If the `self` parameter is prefixed with `mut`, it becomes a mutable variable, similar to regular parameters using a `mut` [identifier pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#identifier-patterns). For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Changer: Sized {\
    fn change(mut self) {}\
    fn modify(mut self: Box<Self>) {}\
}\
}\
```\
\
As an example of methods on a trait, consider the following:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
type Surface = i32;\
type BoundingBox = i32;\
trait Shape {\
    fn draw(&self, surface: Surface);\
    fn bounding_box(&self) -> BoundingBox;\
}\
}\
```\
\
This defines a trait with two methods. All values that have [implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations) of this trait while the trait is in scope can have their `draw` and `bounding_box` methods called.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
type Surface = i32;\
type BoundingBox = i32;\
trait Shape {\
    fn draw(&self, surface: Surface);\
    fn bounding_box(&self) -> BoundingBox;\
}\
\
struct Circle {\
    // ...\
}\
\
impl Shape for Circle {\
    // ...\
  fn draw(&self, _: Surface) {}\
  fn bounding_box(&self) -> BoundingBox { 0i32 }\
}\
\
impl Circle {\
    fn new() -> Circle { Circle{} }\
}\
\
let circle_shape = Circle::new();\
let bounding_box = circle_shape.bounding_box();\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.params.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.params.edition2018 "items.associated.fn.params.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, it is possible to declare trait methods with anonymous parameters (e.g. `fn foo(u8)`). This is deprecated and an error as of the 2018 edition. All parameters must have an argument name.\
\
[\[items.associated.fn.param-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.fn.param-attributes "items.associated.fn.param-attributes")\
\
#### [Attributes on method parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-on-method-parameters)\
\
Attributes on method parameters follow the same rules and restrictions as [regular function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-on-function-parameters).\
\
[\[items.associated.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type "items.associated.type")\
\
## [Associated types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#associated-types)\
\
[\[items.associated.type.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.intro "items.associated.type.intro")\
\
_Associated types_ are [type aliases](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-aliases) associated with another type.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.restrictions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.restrictions "items.associated.type.restrictions")\
\
Associated types cannot be defined in [inherent implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#inherent-implementations) nor can they be given a default implementation in traits.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.decl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.decl "items.associated.type.decl")\
\
An _associated type declaration_ declares a signature for associated type definitions. It is written in one of the following forms, where `Assoc` is the name of the associated type, `Params` is a comma-separated list of type, lifetime or const parameters, `Bounds` is a plus-separated list of trait bounds that the associated type must meet, and `WhereBounds` is a comma-separated list of bounds that the parameters must meet:\
\
```rust\
\
type Assoc;\
type Assoc: Bounds;\
type Assoc<Params>;\
type Assoc<Params>: Bounds;\
type Assoc<Params> where WhereBounds;\
type Assoc<Params>: Bounds where WhereBounds;\
```\
\
[\[items.associated.type.name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.name "items.associated.type.name")\
\
The identifier is the name of the declared type alias.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.impl-fulfillment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.impl-fulfillment "items.associated.type.impl-fulfillment")\
\
The optional trait bounds must be fulfilled by the implementations of the type alias.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.sized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.sized "items.associated.type.sized")\
\
There is an implicit [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sized-1) bound on associated types that can be relaxed using the special `?Sized` bound.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.def "items.associated.type.def")\
\
An _associated type definition_ defines a type alias for the implementation of a trait on a type.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.def.restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.def.restriction "items.associated.type.def.restriction")\
\
They are written similarly to an _associated type declaration_, but cannot contain `Bounds`, but instead must contain a `Type`:\
\
```rust\
\
type Assoc = Type;\
type Assoc<Params> = Type; // the type `Type` here may reference `Params`\
type Assoc<Params> = Type where WhereBounds;\
type Assoc<Params> where WhereBounds = Type; // deprecated, prefer the form above\
```\
\
[\[items.associated.type.alias\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.alias "items.associated.type.alias")\
\
If a type `Item` has an associated type `Assoc` from a trait `Trait`, then `<Item as Trait>::Assoc` is a type that is an alias of the type specified in the associated type definition.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.param\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.param "items.associated.type.param")\
\
Furthermore, if `Item` is a type parameter, then `Item::Assoc` can be used in type parameters.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.generic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.generic "items.associated.type.generic")\
\
Associated types may include [generic parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters) and [where clauses](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#where-clauses); these are often referred to as _generic associated types_, or _GATs_. If the type `Thing` has an associated type `Item` from a trait `Trait` with the generics `<'a>` , the type can be named like `<Thing as Trait>::Item<'x>`, where `'x` is some lifetime in scope. In this case, `'x` will be used wherever `'a` appears in the associated type definitions on impls.\
\
```rust\
\
trait AssociatedType {\
    // Associated type declaration\
    type Assoc;\
}\
\
struct Struct;\
\
struct OtherStruct;\
\
impl AssociatedType for Struct {\
    // Associated type definition\
    type Assoc = OtherStruct;\
}\
\
impl OtherStruct {\
    fn new() -> OtherStruct {\
        OtherStruct\
    }\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    // Usage of the associated type to refer to OtherStruct as <Struct as AssociatedType>::Assoc\
    let _other_struct: OtherStruct = <Struct as AssociatedType>::Assoc::new();\
}\
```\
\
An example of associated types with generics and where clauses:\
\
```rust\
\
struct ArrayLender<'a, T>(&'a mut [T; 16]);\
\
trait Lend {\
    // Generic associated type declaration\
    type Lender<'a> where Self: 'a;\
    fn lend<'a>(&'a mut self) -> Self::Lender<'a>;\
}\
\
impl<T> Lend for [T; 16] {\
    // Generic associated type definition\
    type Lender<'a> = ArrayLender<'a, T> where Self: 'a;\
\
    fn lend<'a>(&'a mut self) -> Self::Lender<'a> {\
        ArrayLender(self)\
    }\
}\
\
fn borrow<'a, T: Lend>(array: &'a mut T) -> <T as Lend>::Lender<'a> {\
    array.lend()\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    let mut array = [0usize; 16];\
    let lender = borrow(&mut array);\
}\
```\
\
### [Associated types container example](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#associated-types-container-example)\
\
Consider the following example of a `Container` trait. Notice that the type is available for use in the method signatures:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Container {\
    type E;\
    fn empty() -> Self;\
    fn insert(&mut self, elem: Self::E);\
}\
}\
```\
\
In order for a type to implement this trait, it must not only provide implementations for every method, but it must specify the type `E`. Here’s an implementation of `Container` for the standard library type `Vec`:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Container {\
    type E;\
    fn empty() -> Self;\
    fn insert(&mut self, elem: Self::E);\
}\
impl<T> Container for Vec<T> {\
    type E = T;\
    fn empty() -> Vec<T> { Vec::new() }\
    fn insert(&mut self, x: T) { self.push(x); }\
}\
}\
```\
\
### [Relationship between `Bounds` and `WhereBounds`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#relationship-between-bounds-and-wherebounds)\
\
In this example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::fmt::Debug;\
trait Example {\
    type Output<T>: Ord where T: Debug;\
}\
}\
```\
\
Given a reference to the associated type like `<X as Example>::Output<Y>`, the associated type itself must be `Ord`, and the type `Y` must be `Debug`.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.generic-where-clause\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.generic-where-clause "items.associated.type.generic-where-clause")\
\
### [Required where clauses on generic associated types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#required-where-clauses-on-generic-associated-types)\
\
[\[items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.intro "items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.intro")\
\
Generic associated type declarations on traits currently may require a list of where clauses, dependent on functions in the trait and how the GAT is used. These rules may be loosened in the future; updates can be found [on the generic associated types initiative repository](https://rust-lang.github.io/generic-associated-types-initiative/explainer/required_bounds.html).\
\
[\[items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.valid-fn\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.valid-fn "items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.valid-fn")\
\
In a few words, these where clauses are required in order to maximize the allowed definitions of the associated type in impls. To do this, any clauses that _can be proven to hold_ on functions (using the parameters of the function or trait) where a GAT appears as an input or output must also be written on the GAT itself.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait LendingIterator {\
    type Item<'x> where Self: 'x;\
    fn next<'a>(&'a mut self) -> Self::Item<'a>;\
}\
}\
```\
\
In the above, on the `next` function, we can prove that `Self: 'a`, because of the implied bounds from `&'a mut self`; therefore, we must write the equivalent bound on the GAT itself: `where Self: 'x`.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.intersection\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.intersection "items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.intersection")\
\
When there are multiple functions in a trait that use the GAT, then the _intersection_ of the bounds from the different functions are used, rather than the union.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Check<T> {\
    type Checker<'x>;\
    fn create_checker<'a>(item: &'a T) -> Self::Checker<'a>;\
    fn do_check(checker: Self::Checker<'_>);\
}\
}\
```\
\
In this example, no bounds are required on the `type Checker<'a>;`. While we know that `T: 'a` on `create_checker`, we do not know that on `do_check`. However, if `do_check` was commented out, then the `where T: 'x` bound would be required on `Checker`.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.forward\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.forward "items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.forward")\
\
The bounds on associated types also propagate required where clauses.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Iterable {\
    type Item<'a> where Self: 'a;\
    type Iterator<'a>: Iterator<Item = Self::Item<'a>> where Self: 'a;\
    fn iter<'a>(&'a self) -> Self::Iterator<'a>;\
}\
}\
```\
\
Here, `where Self: 'a` is required on `Item` because of `iter`. However, `Item` is used in the bounds of `Iterator`, the `where Self: 'a` clause is also required there.\
\
[\[items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.static "items.associated.type.generic-where-clause.static")\
\
Finally, any explicit uses of `'static` on GATs in the trait do not count towards the required bounds.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait StaticReturn {\
    type Y<'a>;\
    fn foo(&self) -> Self::Y<'static>;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[items.associated.const\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.const "items.associated.const")\
\
## [Associated constants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#associated-constants)\
\
[\[items.associated.const.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.const.intro "items.associated.const.intro")\
\
_Associated constants_ are [constants](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items) associated with a type.\
\
[\[items.associated.const.decl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.const.decl "items.associated.const.decl")\
\
An _associated constant declaration_ declares a signature for associated constant definitions. It is written as `const`, then an identifier, then `:`, then a type, finished by a `;`.\
\
[\[items.associated.const.name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.const.name "items.associated.const.name")\
\
The identifier is the name of the constant used in the path. The type is the type that the definition has to implement.\
\
[\[items.associated.const.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.const.def "items.associated.const.def")\
\
An _associated constant definition_ defines a constant associated with a type. It is written the same as a [constant item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items).\
\
[\[items.associated.const.eval\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.associated.const.eval "items.associated.const.eval")\
\
Associated constant definitions undergo [constant evaluation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-evaluation) only when referenced. Further, definitions that include [generic parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters) are evaluated after monomorphization.\
\
```rust\
\
struct Struct;\
struct GenericStruct<const ID: i32>;\
\
impl Struct {\
    // Definition not immediately evaluated\
    const PANIC: () = panic!("compile-time panic");\
}\
\
impl<const ID: i32> GenericStruct<ID> {\
    // Definition not immediately evaluated\
    const NON_ZERO: () = if ID == 0 {\
        panic!("contradiction")\
    };\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    // Referencing Struct::PANIC causes compilation error\
    let _ = Struct::PANIC;\
\
    // Fine, ID is not 0\
    let _ = GenericStruct::<1>::NON_ZERO;\
\
    // Compilation error from evaluating NON_ZERO with ID=0\
    let _ = GenericStruct::<0>::NON_ZERO;\
}\
```\
\
### [Associated constants examples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#associated-constants-examples)\
\
A basic example:\
\
```rust\
\
trait ConstantId {\
    const ID: i32;\
}\
\
struct Struct;\
\
impl ConstantId for Struct {\
    const ID: i32 = 1;\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    assert_eq!(1, Struct::ID);\
}\
```\
\
Using default values:\
\
```rust\
\
trait ConstantIdDefault {\
    const ID: i32 = 1;\
}\
\
struct Struct;\
struct OtherStruct;\
\
impl ConstantIdDefault for Struct {}\
\
impl ConstantIdDefault for OtherStruct {\
    const ID: i32 = 5;\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    assert_eq!(1, Struct::ID);\
    assert_eq!(5, OtherStruct::ID);\
}\
```\
\
[\[attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes "attributes")\
\
# [Attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-1)\
\
[\[attributes.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.syntax "attributes.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[InnerAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-InnerAttribute) → #!\[[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Attr)\]\
\
[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute) → #\[[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Attr)\]\
\
[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Attr) →\
\
[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath)[AttrInput](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AttrInput)?\
\
    \| unsafe([SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath)[AttrInput](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AttrInput)?)\
\
[AttrInput](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AttrInput) →\
\
[DelimTokenTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-DelimTokenTree)\
\
    \| =[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[InnerAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InnerAttribute)\
#\
!\
\[[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Attr)\
\]\
\
[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\
#\
\[[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Attr)\
\]\
\
[Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Attr)[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)[AttrInput](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AttrInput)\
unsafe\
([SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)[AttrInput](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AttrInput)\
)\
\
[AttrInput](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AttrInput)[DelimTokenTree](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-DelimTokenTree)\
=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[attributes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.intro "attributes.intro")\
\
An _attribute_ is a general, free-form metadatum that is interpreted according to name, convention, language, and compiler version. Attributes are modeled on Attributes in [ECMA-335](https://www.ecma-international.org/publications-and-standards/standards/ecma-335/), with the syntax coming from [ECMA-334](https://www.ecma-international.org/publications-and-standards/standards/ecma-334/) (C#).\
\
[\[attributes.inner\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.inner "attributes.inner")\
\
_Inner attributes_, written with a bang (`!`) after the hash (`#`), apply to the form that the attribute is declared within.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> // General metadata applied to the enclosing module or crate.\
> #![crate_type = "lib"]\
>\
> // Inner attribute applies to the entire function.\
> fn some_unused_variables() {\
>   #![allow(unused_variables)]\
>\
>   let x = ();\
>   let y = ();\
>   let z = ();\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[attributes.outer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.outer "attributes.outer")\
\
_Outer attributes_, written without the bang after the hash, apply to the form that follows the attribute.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> // A function marked as a unit test\
> #[test]\
> fn test_foo() {\
>     /* ... */\
> }\
>\
> // A conditionally-compiled module\
> #[cfg(target_os = "linux")]\
> mod bar {\
>     /* ... */\
> }\
>\
> // A lint attribute used to suppress a warning/error\
> #[allow(non_camel_case_types)]\
> type int8_t = i8;\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[attributes.input\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.input "attributes.input")\
\
The attribute consists of a path to the attribute, followed by an optional delimited token tree whose interpretation is defined by the attribute. Attributes other than macro attributes also allow the input to be an equals sign (`=`) followed by an expression. See the [meta item syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#meta-item-attribute-syntax) below for more details.\
\
[\[attributes.safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.safety "attributes.safety")\
\
An attribute may be unsafe to apply. To avoid undefined behavior when using these attributes, certain obligations that cannot be checked by the compiler must be met. To assert these have been, the attribute is wrapped in `unsafe(..)`, e.g. `#[unsafe(no_mangle)]`.\
\
The following attributes are unsafe:\
\
- [`export_name`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-export_name-attribute)\
- [`link_section`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-link_section-attribute)\
- [`naked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-naked-attribute)\
- [`no_mangle`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_mangle-attribute)\
\
[\[attributes.kind\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.kind "attributes.kind")\
\
Attributes can be classified into the following kinds:\
\
- [Built-in attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#built-in-attributes-index)\
- [Proc macro attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-proc_macro_attribute-attribute)\
- [Derive macro helper attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive-macro-helper-attributes)\
- [Tool attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-attributes)\
\
[\[attributes.allowed-position\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.allowed-position "attributes.allowed-position")\
\
Attributes may be applied to many forms in the language:\
\
- All [item declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items) accept outer attributes while [external blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks), [functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions), [implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations), and [modules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules) accept inner attributes.\
- Most [statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#statements) accept outer attributes (see [Expression Attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expression-attributes) for limitations on expression statements).\
- [Block expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions) accept outer and inner attributes, but only when they are the outer expression of an [expression statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expression-statements) or the final expression of another block expression.\
- [Enum](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations) variants and [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs) and [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions) fields accept outer attributes.\
- [Match expression arms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions) accept outer attributes.\
- [Generic lifetime or type parameter](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters) accept outer attributes.\
- Expressions accept outer attributes in limited situations, see [Expression Attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expression-attributes) for details.\
- [Function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions), [closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-expressions) and [function pointer](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-pointer-types) parameters accept outer attributes. This includes attributes on variadic parameters denoted with `...` in function pointers and [external blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variadic-functions).\
- [Inline assembly](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#inline-assembly) template strings and operands accept outer attributes. Only certain attributes are accepted semantically; for details, see [asm.attributes.supported-attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-asm.attributes.supported-attributes).\
\
[\[attributes.meta\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.meta "attributes.meta")\
\
## [Meta item attribute syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#meta-item-attribute-syntax)\
\
[\[attributes.meta.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.meta.intro "attributes.meta.intro")\
\
A “meta item” is the syntax used for the [Attr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Attr) rule by most [built-in attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#built-in-attributes-index). It has the following grammar:\
\
[\[attributes.meta.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.meta.syntax "attributes.meta.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[MetaItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaItem) →\
\
[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath)\
\
    \| [SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath)=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
    \| [SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath)([MetaSeq](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaSeq)?)\
\
[MetaSeq](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaSeq) →\
\
[MetaItemInner](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaItemInner) ( ,[MetaItemInner](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaItemInner) )\*,?\
\
[MetaItemInner](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaItemInner) →\
\
[MetaItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaItem)\
\
    \| [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[MetaItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaItem)[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)\
=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)\
([MetaSeq](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaSeq)\
)\
\
[MetaSeq](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaSeq)[MetaItemInner](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaItemInner)\
,[MetaItemInner](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaItemInner)\
,\
\
[MetaItemInner](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaItemInner)[MetaItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaItem)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[attributes.meta.literal-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.meta.literal-expr "attributes.meta.literal-expr")\
\
Expressions in meta items must macro-expand to literal expressions, which must not include integer or float type suffixes. Expressions which are not literal expressions will be syntactically accepted (and can be passed to proc-macros), but will be rejected after parsing.\
\
[\[attributes.meta.order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.meta.order "attributes.meta.order")\
\
Note that if the attribute appears within another macro, it will be expanded after that outer macro. For example, the following code will expand the `Serialize` proc-macro first, which must preserve the `include_str!` call in order for it to be expanded:\
\
```rust\
\
#[derive(Serialize)]\
struct Foo {\
    #[doc = include_str!("x.md")]\
    x: u32\
}\
```\
\
[\[attributes.meta.order-macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.meta.order-macro "attributes.meta.order-macro")\
\
Additionally, macros in attributes will be expanded only after all other attributes applied to the item:\
\
```rust\
\
#[macro_attr1] // expanded first\
#[doc = mac!()] // `mac!` is expanded fourth.\
#[macro_attr2] // expanded second\
#[derive(MacroDerive1, MacroDerive2)] // expanded third\
fn foo() {}\
```\
\
[\[attributes.meta.builtin\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.meta.builtin "attributes.meta.builtin")\
\
Various built-in attributes use different subsets of the meta item syntax to specify their inputs. The following grammar rules show some commonly used forms:\
\
[\[attributes.meta.builtin.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.meta.builtin.syntax "attributes.meta.builtin.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaWord) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)\
\
[MetaNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaNameValueStr) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)= ( [STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL) \| [RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_LITERAL) )\
\
[MetaListPaths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaListPaths) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)( ( [SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath) ( ,[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath) )\*,? )?)\
\
[MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaListIdents) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)( ( [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) ( ,[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) )\*,? )?)\
\
[MetaListNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaListNameValueStr) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)( ( [MetaNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaNameValueStr) ( ,[MetaNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaNameValueStr) )\*,? )?)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
\
[MetaNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaNameValueStr)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
=[STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_LITERAL) [RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
[MetaListPaths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListPaths)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
([SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)\
,[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)\
,\
)\
\
[MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
([IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
,[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
,\
)\
\
[MetaListNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListNameValueStr)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
([MetaNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaNameValueStr)\
,[MetaNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MetaNameValueStr)\
,\
)\
\
Some examples of meta items are:\
\
| Style | Example |\
| --- | --- |\
| [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) | `no_std` |\
| [MetaNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaNameValueStr) | `doc = "example"` |\
| [MetaListPaths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListPaths) | `allow(unused, clippy::inline_always)` |\
| [MetaListIdents](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListIdents) | `macro_use(foo, bar)` |\
| [MetaListNameValueStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaListNameValueStr) | `link(name = "CoreFoundation", kind = "framework")` |\
\
[\[attributes.activity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.activity "attributes.activity")\
\
## [Active and inert attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#active-and-inert-attributes)\
\
[\[attributes.activity.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.activity.intro "attributes.activity.intro")\
\
An attribute is either active or inert. During attribute processing, _active attributes_ remove themselves from the form they are on while _inert attributes_ stay on.\
\
The [`cfg`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg-attribute) and [`cfg_attr`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg_attr-attribute) attributes are active. [Attribute macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-proc_macro_attribute-attribute) are active. All other attributes are inert.\
\
[\[attributes.tool\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.tool "attributes.tool")\
\
## [Tool attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tool-attributes)\
\
[\[attributes.tool.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.tool.intro "attributes.tool.intro")\
\
The compiler may allow attributes for external tools where each tool resides in its own module in the [tool prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-prelude). The first segment of the attribute path is the name of the tool, with one or more additional segments whose interpretation is up to the tool.\
\
[\[attributes.tool.ignored\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.tool.ignored "attributes.tool.ignored")\
\
When a tool is not in use, the tool’s attributes are accepted without a warning. When the tool is in use, the tool is responsible for processing and interpretation of its attributes.\
\
[\[attributes.tool.prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.tool.prelude "attributes.tool.prelude")\
\
Tool attributes are not available if the [`no_implicit_prelude`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_implicit_prelude-attribute) attribute is used.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Tells the rustfmt tool to not format the following element.\
#[rustfmt::skip]\
struct S {\
}\
\
// Controls the "cyclomatic complexity" threshold for the clippy tool.\
#[clippy::cyclomatic_complexity = "100"]\
pub fn f() {}\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` currently recognizes the tools “clippy”, “rustfmt”, “diagnostic”, “miri”, and “rust\_analyzer”.\
\
[\[attributes.builtin\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.builtin "attributes.builtin")\
\
## [Built-in attributes index](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#built-in-attributes-index)\
\
The following is an index of all built-in attributes.\
\
- Conditional compilation\
  - [`cfg`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg-attribute) — Controls conditional compilation.\
  - [`cfg_attr`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg_attr-attribute) — Conditionally includes attributes.\
- Testing\
  - [`test`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-test-attribute) — Marks a function as a test.\
  - [`ignore`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-ignore-attribute) — Disables a test function.\
  - [`should_panic`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-should_panic-attribute) — Indicates a test should generate a panic.\
- Derive\
  - [`derive`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive) — Automatic trait implementations.\
  - [`automatically_derived`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-automatically_derived-attribute) — Marker for implementations created by `derive`.\
- Macros\
  - [`macro_export`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_export-attribute) — Exports a `macro_rules` macro for cross-crate usage.\
  - [`macro_use`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_use-attribute) — Expands macro visibility, or imports macros from other crates.\
  - [`proc_macro`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-proc_macro-attribute) — Defines a function-like macro.\
  - [`proc_macro_derive`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive) — Defines a derive macro.\
  - [`proc_macro_attribute`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-proc_macro_attribute-attribute) — Defines an attribute macro.\
- Diagnostics\
  - [`allow`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes), [`expect`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes), [`warn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes), [`deny`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes), [`forbid`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes) — Alters the default lint level.\
  - [`deprecated`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-deprecated-attribute) — Generates deprecation notices.\
  - [`must_use`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-must_use-attribute) — Generates a lint for unused values.\
  - [`diagnostic::on_unimplemented`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-diagnosticon_unimplemented-attribute) — Hints the compiler to emit a certain error message if a trait is not implemented.\
  - [`diagnostic::do_not_recommend`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-diagnosticdo_not_recommend-attribute) — Hints the compiler to not show a certain trait impl in error messages.\
- ABI, linking, symbols, and FFI\
  - [`link`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-link-attribute) — Specifies a native library to link with an `extern` block.\
  - [`link_name`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-link_name-attribute) — Specifies the name of the symbol for functions or statics in an `extern` block.\
  - [`link_ordinal`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-link_ordinal-attribute) — Specifies the ordinal of the symbol for functions or statics in an `extern` block.\
  - [`no_link`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_link-attribute) — Prevents linking an extern crate.\
  - [`repr`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#representations) — Controls type layout.\
  - [`crate_type`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#linkage) — Specifies the type of crate (library, executable, etc.).\
  - [`no_main`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_main-attribute) — Disables emitting the `main` symbol.\
  - [`export_name`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-export_name-attribute) — Specifies the exported symbol name for a function or static.\
  - [`link_section`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-link_section-attribute) — Specifies the section of an object file to use for a function or static.\
  - [`no_mangle`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_mangle-attribute) — Disables symbol name encoding.\
  - [`used`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-used-attribute) — Forces the compiler to keep a static item in the output object file.\
  - [`crate_name`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-crate_name-attribute) — Specifies the crate name.\
- Code generation\
  - [`inline`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-inline-attribute) — Hint to inline code.\
  - [`cold`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cold-attribute) — Hint that a function is unlikely to be called.\
  - [`naked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-naked-attribute) — Prevent the compiler from emitting a function prologue and epilogue.\
  - [`no_builtins`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_builtins-attribute) — Disables use of certain built-in functions.\
  - [`target_feature`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-target_feature-attribute) — Configure platform-specific code generation.\
  - [`track_caller`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-track_caller-attribute) — Pass the parent call location to `std::panic::Location::caller()`.\
  - [`instruction_set`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-instruction_set-attribute) — Specify the instruction set used to generate a function’s code.\
- Documentation\
  - `doc` — Specifies documentation. See [The Rustdoc Book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/the-doc-attribute.html) for more information. [Doc comments](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#doc-comments) are transformed into `doc` attributes.\
- Preludes\
  - [`no_std`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_std-attribute) — Removes std from the prelude.\
  - [`no_implicit_prelude`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_implicit_prelude-attribute) — Disables prelude lookups within a module.\
- Modules\
  - [`path`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-path-attribute) — Specifies the filename for a module.\
- Limits\
  - [`recursion_limit`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-recursion_limit-attribute) — Sets the maximum recursion limit for certain compile-time operations.\
  - [`type_length_limit`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-type_length_limit-attribute) — Sets the maximum size of a polymorphic type.\
- Runtime\
  - [`panic_handler`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-panic_handler-attribute) — Sets the function to handle panics.\
  - [`global_allocator`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-global_allocator-attribute) — Sets the global memory allocator.\
  - [`windows_subsystem`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-windows_subsystem-attribute) — Specifies the windows subsystem to link with.\
- Features\
  - `feature` — Used to enable unstable or experimental compiler features. See [The Unstable Book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/unstable-book/index.html) for features implemented in `rustc`.\
- Type System\
  - [`non_exhaustive`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-non_exhaustive-attribute) — Indicate that a type will have more fields/variants added in future.\
- Debugger\
  - [`debugger_visualizer`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-debugger_visualizer-attribute) — Embeds a file that specifies debugger output for a type.\
  - [`collapse_debuginfo`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-collapse_debuginfo-attribute) — Controls how macro invocations are encoded in debuginfo.\
\
[\[attributes.testing\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.testing "attributes.testing")\
\
# [Testing attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#testing-attributes)\
\
The following [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) are used for specifying functions for performing\
tests. Compiling a crate in “test” mode enables building the test functions\
along with a test harness for executing the tests. Enabling the test mode also\
enables the [`test` conditional compilation option](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#test).\
\
[\[attributes.testing.test\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.testing.test "attributes.testing.test")\
\
## [The `test` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-test-attribute)\
\
[\[attributes.testing.test.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.testing.test.intro "attributes.testing.test.intro")\
\
The _`test` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ marks a function to be executed as a test.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> pub fn add(left: u64, right: u64) -> u64 { left + right }\
> #[test]\
> fn it_works() {\
>     let result = add(2, 2);\
>     assert_eq!(result, 4);\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[attributes.testing.test.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.testing.test.syntax "attributes.testing.test.syntax")\
\
The `test` attribute uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) syntax.\
\
[\[attributes.testing.test.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.testing.test.allowed-positions "attributes.testing.test.allowed-positions")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
