| [Function calls](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call), [array indexing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index) |  |\
| [`?`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.try) |  |\
| Unary [`-`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.negate) [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.negate) [`*`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.deref) [borrow](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.operator.borrow) |  |\
| [`as`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.as) | left to right |\
| [`*`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) [`/`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) [`%`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) | left to right |\
| [`+`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) [`-`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) | left to right |\
| [`<<`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) [`>>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) | left to right |\
| [`&`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) | left to right |\
| [`^`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) | left to right |\
| [`|`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic) | left to right |\
| [`==`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.cmp) [`!=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.cmp) [`<`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.cmp) [`>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.cmp) [`<=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.cmp) [`>=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.cmp) | Require parentheses |\
| [`&&`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.bool-logic) | left to right |\
| [`||`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.bool-logic) | left to right |\
| [`..`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.range) [`..=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.range) | Require parentheses |\
| [`=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.assign) [`+=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) [`-=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) [`*=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) [`/=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) [`%=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign)<br>[`&=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) [`|=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) [`^=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) [`<<=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) [`>>=`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign) | right to left |\
| [`return`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.return) [`break`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break) [closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure) |  |\
\
[\[expr.operand-order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.operand-order "expr.operand-order")\
\
## [Evaluation order of operands](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#evaluation-order-of-operands)\
\
[\[expr.operand-order.default\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.operand-order.default "expr.operand-order.default")\
\
The following list of expressions all evaluate their operands the same way, as described after the list. Other expressions either don’t take operands or evaluate them conditionally as described on their respective pages.\
\
- Dereference expression\
- Error propagation expression\
- Negation expression\
- Arithmetic and logical binary operators\
- Comparison operators\
- Type cast expression\
- Grouped expression\
- Array expression\
- Await expression\
- Index expression\
- Tuple expression\
- Tuple index expression\
- Struct expression\
- Call expression\
- Method call expression\
- Field expression\
- Break expression\
- Range expression\
- Return expression\
\
[\[expr.operand-order.operands-before-primary\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.operand-order.operands-before-primary "expr.operand-order.operands-before-primary")\
\
The operands of these expressions are evaluated prior to applying the effects of the expression. Expressions taking multiple operands are evaluated left to right as written in the source code.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Which subexpressions are the operands of an expression is determined by expression precedence as per the previous section.\
\
For example, the two `next` method calls will always be called in the same order:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Using vec instead of array to avoid references\
// since there is no stable owned array iterator\
// at the time this example was written.\
let mut one_two = vec![1, 2].into_iter();\
assert_eq!(\
    (1, 2),\
    (one_two.next().unwrap(), one_two.next().unwrap())\
);\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Since this is applied recursively, these expressions are also evaluated from innermost to outermost, ignoring siblings until there are no inner subexpressions.\
\
[\[expr.place-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value "expr.place-value")\
\
## [Place expressions and value expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#place-expressions-and-value-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.place-value.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.intro "expr.place-value.intro")\
\
Expressions are divided into two main categories: place expressions and value expressions; there is also a third, minor category of expressions called assignee expressions. Within each expression, operands may likewise occur in either place context or value context. The evaluation of an expression depends both on its own category and the context it occurs within.\
\
[\[expr.place-value.place-memory-location\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.place-memory-location "expr.place-value.place-memory-location")\
\
A _place expression_ is an expression that represents a memory location.\
\
[\[expr.place-value.place-expr-kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.place-expr-kinds "expr.place-value.place-expr-kinds")\
\
These expressions are [paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-expressions) which refer to local variables, [static variables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items), [dereferences](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator) (`*expr`), [array indexing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions) expressions (`expr[expr]`), [field](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-access-expressions) references (`expr.f`) and parenthesized place expressions.\
\
[\[expr.place-value.value-expr-kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.value-expr-kinds "expr.place-value.value-expr-kinds")\
\
All other expressions are value expressions.\
\
[\[expr.place-value.value-result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.value-result "expr.place-value.value-result")\
\
A _value expression_ is an expression that represents an actual value.\
\
[\[expr.place-value.place-context\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.place-context "expr.place-value.place-context")\
\
The following contexts are _place expression_ contexts:\
\
- The left operand of a [compound assignment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#compound-assignment-expressions) expression.\
- The operand of a unary [borrow](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#borrow-operators), [raw borrow](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-borrow-operators) or [dereference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator) operator.\
- The operand of a [field expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field).\
- The indexed operand of an [array indexing expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index).\
- The tuple operand of a [tuple indexing expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple-index).\
- The operand of any [implicit borrow](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implicit-borrows).\
- The initializer of a [let statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#let-statements).\
- The [scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scrutinee) of an [`if let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-let-patterns), [`match`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions), or [`while let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#while-let-patterns) expression.\
- The base of a [functional update](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functional-update-syntax) struct expression.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Historically, place expressions were called _lvalues_ and value expressions were called _rvalues_.\
\
[\[expr.place-value.assignee\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.assignee "expr.place-value.assignee")\
\
An _assignee expression_ is an expression that appears in the left operand of an [assignment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#assignment-expressions) expression. Explicitly, the assignee expressions are:\
\
- Place expressions.\
- [Underscores](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#_-expressions).\
- [Tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-and-tuple-indexing-expressions) of assignee expressions.\
- [Slices](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index) of assignee expressions.\
- [Tuple structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.tuple) of assignee expressions.\
- [Structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct) of assignee expressions (with optionally named fields).\
- [Unit structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.unit)\
\
[\[expr.place-value.parenthesis\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.parenthesis "expr.place-value.parenthesis")\
\
Arbitrary parenthesisation is permitted inside assignee expressions.\
\
[\[expr.move\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.move "expr.move")\
\
### [Moved and copied types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#moved-and-copied-types)\
\
[\[expr.move.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.move.intro "expr.move.intro")\
\
When a place expression is evaluated in a value expression context, or is bound by value in a pattern, it denotes the value held _in_ that memory location.\
\
[\[expr.move.copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.move.copy "expr.move.copy")\
\
If the type of that value implements [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy), then the value will be copied.\
\
[\[expr.move.requires-sized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.move.requires-sized "expr.move.requires-sized")\
\
In the remaining situations, if that type is [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sized-1), then it may be possible to move the value.\
\
[\[expr.move.movable-place\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.move.movable-place "expr.move.movable-place")\
\
Only the following place expressions may be moved out of:\
\
- [Variables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variables) which are not currently borrowed.\
- [Temporary values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#temporaries).\
- [Fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-access-expressions) of a place expression which can be moved out of and don’t implement [`Drop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#drop).\
- The result of [dereferencing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator) an expression with type [`Box<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boxt) and that can also be moved out of.\
\
[\[expr.move.deinitialization\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.move.deinitialization "expr.move.deinitialization")\
\
After moving out of a place expression that evaluates to a local variable, the location is deinitialized and cannot be read from again until it is reinitialized.\
\
[\[expr.move.place-invalid\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.move.place-invalid "expr.move.place-invalid")\
\
In all other cases, trying to use a place expression in a value expression context is an error.\
\
[\[expr.mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.mut "expr.mut")\
\
### [Mutability](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#mutability)\
\
[\[expr.mut.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.mut.intro "expr.mut.intro")\
\
For a place expression to be [assigned](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#assignment-expressions) to, mutably [borrowed](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#borrow-operators), [implicitly mutably borrowed](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implicit-borrows), or bound to a pattern containing `ref mut`, it must be _mutable_. We call these _mutable place expressions_. In contrast, other place expressions are called _immutable place expressions_.\
\
[\[expr.mut.valid-places\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.mut.valid-places "expr.mut.valid-places")\
\
The following expressions can be mutable place expression contexts:\
\
- Mutable [variables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variables) which are not currently borrowed.\
- [Mutable `static` items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#mutable-statics).\
- [Temporary values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#temporaries).\
- [Fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-access-expressions): this evaluates the subexpression in a mutable place expression context.\
- [Dereferences](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator) of a `*mut T` pointer.\
- Dereference of a variable, or field of a variable, with type `&mut T`. Note: This is an exception to the requirement of the next rule.\
- Dereferences of a type that implements `DerefMut`: this then requires that the value being dereferenced is evaluated in a mutable place expression context.\
- [Array indexing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions) of a type that implements `IndexMut`: this then evaluates the value being indexed, but not the index, in mutable place expression context.\
\
[\[expr.temporary\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.temporary "expr.temporary")\
\
### [Temporaries](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#temporaries)\
\
When using a value expression in most place expression contexts, a temporary unnamed memory location is created and initialized to that value. The expression evaluates to that location instead, except if [promoted](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-promotion) to a `static`. The [drop scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#drop-scopes) of the temporary is usually the end of the enclosing statement.\
\
[\[expr.super-macros\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros "expr.super-macros")\
\
### [Super macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#super-macros)\
\
[\[expr.super-macros.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros.intro "expr.super-macros.intro")\
\
Certain built-in macros may create [temporaries](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.temporary) whose [scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary) may be [extended](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension). These temporaries are _super temporaries_ and these macros are _super macros_. [Invocations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation) of these macros are _super macro call expressions_. Arguments to these macros may be _super operands_.\
\
> Note\
>\
> When a super macro call expression is an [extending expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs), its super operands are [extending expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs) and the [scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary) of the super temporaries are [extended](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension). See [destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs).\
\
[\[expr.super-macros.format\_args\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros.format_args "expr.super-macros.format_args")\
\
#### [`format_args!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#format_args)\
\
[\[expr.super-macros.format\_args.super-operands\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros.format_args.super-operands "expr.super-macros.format_args.super-operands")\
\
Except for the format string argument, all arguments passed to [`format_args!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.format_args.html) are _super operands_.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() -> String { String::from("") }\
// Due to the call being an extending expression and the argument\
// being a super operand, the inner block is an extending expression,\
// so the scope of the temporary created in its trailing expression\
// is extended.\
let _ = format_args!("{}", { &temp() }); // OK\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.super-macros.format\_args.super-temporaries\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros.format_args.super-temporaries "expr.super-macros.format_args.super-temporaries")\
\
The super operands of [`format_args!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.format_args.html) are [implicitly borrowed](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.implicit-borrow) and are therefore [place expression contexts](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value). When a [value expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value) is passed as an argument, it creates a _super temporary_.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() -> String { String::from("") }\
let x = format_args!("{}", temp());\
x; // <-- The temporary is extended, allowing use here.\
}\
```\
\
The expansion of a call to [`format_args!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.format_args.html) sometimes creates other internal _super temporaries_.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = {\
    // This call creates an internal temporary.\
    let x = format_args!("{:?}", 0);\
    x // <-- The temporary is extended, allowing its use here.\
}; // <-- The temporary is dropped here.\
x; // ERROR\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// This call doesn't create an internal temporary.\
let x = { let x = format_args!("{}", 0); x };\
x; // OK\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> The details of when [`format_args!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.format_args.html) does or does not create internal temporaries are currently unspecified.\
\
[\[expr.super-macros.pin\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros.pin "expr.super-macros.pin")\
\
#### [`pin!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pin)\
\
[\[expr.super-macros.pin.super-operands\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros.pin.super-operands "expr.super-macros.pin.super-operands")\
\
The argument to [`pin!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/pin/macro.pin.html) is a _super operand_.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use core::pin::pin;\
fn temp() {}\
// As above for `format_args!`.\
let _ = pin!({ &temp() }); // OK\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.super-macros.pin.super-temporaries\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros.pin.super-temporaries "expr.super-macros.pin.super-temporaries")\
\
The argument to [`pin!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/pin/macro.pin.html) is a [value expression context](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value) and creates a _super temporary_.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use core::pin::pin;\
fn temp() {}\
// The argument is evaluated into a super temporary.\
let x = pin!(temp());\
// The temporary is extended, allowing its use here.\
x; // OK\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.implicit-borrow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.implicit-borrow "expr.implicit-borrow")\
\
### [Implicit borrows](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#implicit-borrows)\
\
[\[expr.implicit-borrow-intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.implicit-borrow-intro "expr.implicit-borrow-intro")\
\
Certain expressions will treat an expression as a place expression by implicitly borrowing it. For example, it is possible to compare two unsized [slices](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-types) for equality directly, because the `==` operator implicitly borrows its operands:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let c = [1, 2, 3];\
let d = vec![1, 2, 3];\
let a: &[i32];\
let b: &[i32];\
a = &c;\
b = &d;\
// ...\
*a == *b;\
// Equivalent form:\
::std::cmp::PartialEq::eq(&*a, &*b);\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.implicit-borrow.application\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.implicit-borrow.application "expr.implicit-borrow.application")\
\
Implicit borrows may be taken in the following expressions:\
\
- Left operand in [method-call](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#method-call-expressions) expressions.\
- Left operand in [field](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-access-expressions) expressions.\
- Left operand in [call expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#call-expressions).\
- Left operand in [array indexing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions) expressions.\
- Operand of the [dereference operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator) (`*`).\
- Operands of [comparison](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#comparison-operators).\
- Left operands of the [compound assignment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#compound-assignment-expressions).\
- Arguments to [`format_args!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.format_args.html) except the format string.\
\
[\[expr.overload\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.overload "expr.overload")\
\
## [Overloading traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#overloading-traits)\
\
Many of the following operators and expressions can also be overloaded for other types using traits in `std::ops` or `std::cmp`. These traits also exist in `core::ops` and `core::cmp` with the same names.\
\
[\[expr.attr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.attr "expr.attr")\
\
## [Expression attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#expression-attributes)\
\
[\[expr.attr.restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.attr.restriction "expr.attr.restriction")\
\
[Outer attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) before an expression are allowed only in a few specific cases:\
\
- Before an expression used as a [statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#statements).\
- Elements of [array expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-array-index-expressions), [tuple expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-and-tuple-indexing-expressions), [call expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#call-expressions), and tuple-style [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-expressions) expressions.\
- The tail expression of [block expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.attr.never-before\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.attr.never-before "expr.attr.never-before")\
\
They are never allowed before:\
\
- [Range](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#range-expressions) expressions.\
- Binary operator expressions ( [ArithmeticOrLogicalExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ArithmeticOrLogicalExpression), [ComparisonExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ComparisonExpression), [LazyBooleanExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LazyBooleanExpression), [TypeCastExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeCastExpression), [AssignmentExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AssignmentExpression), [CompoundAssignmentExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CompoundAssignmentExpression)).\
\
[\[expr.literal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal "expr.literal")\
\
# [Literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.syntax "expr.literal.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LiteralExpression) →\
\
[CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CHAR_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [BYTE\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-C_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [INTEGER\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INTEGER_LITERAL)\
\
    \| [FLOAT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FLOAT_LITERAL)\
\
    \| true\
\
    \| false\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LiteralExpression)[CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CHAR_LITERAL)[STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-STRING_LITERAL)[RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_STRING_LITERAL)[BYTE\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_LITERAL)[BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)[RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL)[C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-C_STRING_LITERAL)[RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL)[INTEGER\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-INTEGER_LITERAL)[FLOAT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FLOAT_LITERAL)\
true\
false\
\
[\[expr.literal.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.intro "expr.literal.intro")\
\
A _literal expression_ is an expression consisting of a single token, rather than a sequence of tokens, that immediately and directly denotes the value it evaluates to, rather than referring to it by name or some other evaluation rule.\
\
[\[expr.literal.const-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.const-expr "expr.literal.const-expr")\
\
A literal is a form of [constant expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-expressions), so is evaluated (primarily) at compile time.\
\
[\[expr.literal.literal-token\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.literal-token "expr.literal.literal-token")\
\
Each of the lexical [literal](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literals) forms described earlier can make up a literal expression, as can the keywords `true` and `false`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
"hello";   // string type\
'5';       // character type\
5;         // integer type\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.literal.string-representation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string-representation "expr.literal.string-representation")\
\
In the descriptions below, the _string representation_ of a token is the sequence of characters from the input which matched the token’s production in a _Lexer_ grammar snippet.\
\
> Note\
>\
> This string representation never includes a character `U+000D` (CR) immediately followed by `U+000A` (LF): this pair would have been previously transformed into a single `U+000A` (LF).\
\
[\[expr.literal.escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.escape "expr.literal.escape")\
\
## [Escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#escapes)\
\
[\[expr.literal.escape.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.escape.intro "expr.literal.escape.intro")\
\
The descriptions of textual literal expressions below make use of several forms of _escape_.\
\
[\[expr.literal.escape.sequence\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.escape.sequence "expr.literal.escape.sequence")\
\
Each form of escape is characterised by:\
\
- an _escape sequence_: a sequence of characters, which always begins with `U+005C` (`\`)\
- an _escaped value_: either a single character or an empty sequence of characters\
\
In the definitions of escapes below:\
\
- An _octal digit_ is any of the characters in the range \[`0`-`7`\].\
- A _hexadecimal digit_ is any of the characters in the ranges \[`0`-`9`\], \[`a`-`f`\], or \[`A`-`F`\].\
\
[\[expr.literal.escape.simple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.escape.simple "expr.literal.escape.simple")\
\
### [Simple escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#simple-escapes)\
\
Each sequence of characters occurring in the first column of the following table is an escape sequence.\
\
In each case, the escaped value is the character given in the corresponding entry in the second column.\
\
| Escape sequence | Escaped value |\
| --- | --- |\
| `\0` | U+0000 (NUL) |\
| `\t` | U+0009 (HT) |\
| `\n` | U+000A (LF) |\
| `\r` | U+000D (CR) |\
| `\"` | U+0022 (QUOTATION MARK) |\
| `\'` | U+0027 (APOSTROPHE) |\
| `\\` | U+005C (REVERSE SOLIDUS) |\
\
[\[expr.literal.escape.hex-octet\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.escape.hex-octet "expr.literal.escape.hex-octet")\
\
### [8-bit escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#8-bit-escapes)\
\
The escape sequence consists of `\x` followed by two hexadecimal digits.\
\
The escaped value is the character whose [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value) is the result of interpreting the final two characters in the escape sequence as a hexadecimal integer, as if by [`u8::from_str_radix`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u8.html#method.from_str_radix) with radix 16.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The escaped value therefore has a [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value) in the range of [`u8`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types).\
\
[\[expr.literal.escape.hex-ascii\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.escape.hex-ascii "expr.literal.escape.hex-ascii")\
\
### [7-bit escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#7-bit-escapes)\
\
The escape sequence consists of `\x` followed by an octal digit then a hexadecimal digit.\
\
The escaped value is the character whose [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value) is the result of interpreting the final two characters in the escape sequence as a hexadecimal integer, as if by [`u8::from_str_radix`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u8.html#method.from_str_radix) with radix 16.\
\
[\[expr.literal.escape.unicode\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.escape.unicode "expr.literal.escape.unicode")\
\
### [Unicode escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unicode-escapes-1)\
\
The escape sequence consists of `\u{`, followed by a sequence of characters each of which is a hexadecimal digit or `_`, followed by `}`.\
\
The escaped value is the character whose [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value) is the result of interpreting the hexadecimal digits contained in the escape sequence as a hexadecimal integer, as if by [`u32::from_str_radix`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u32.html#method.from_str_radix) with radix 16.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The permitted forms of a [CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CHAR_LITERAL) or [STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL) token ensure that there is such a character.\
\
[\[expr.literal.continuation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.continuation "expr.literal.continuation")\
\
### [String continuation escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#string-continuation-escapes)\
\
The escape sequence consists of `\` followed immediately by `U+000A` (LF), and all following whitespace characters before the next non-whitespace character. For this purpose, the whitespace characters are `U+0009` (HT), `U+000A` (LF), `U+000D` (CR), and `U+0020` (SPACE).\
\
The escaped value is an empty sequence of characters.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The effect of this form of escape is that a string continuation skips following whitespace, including additional newlines. Thus `a`, `b` and `c` are equal:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> let a = "foobar";\
> let b = "foo\\
>          bar";\
> let c = "foo\\
>\
>      bar";\
>\
> assert_eq!(a, b);\
> assert_eq!(b, c);\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> Skipping additional newlines (as in example c) is potentially confusing and unexpected. This behavior may be adjusted in the future. Until a decision is made, it is recommended to avoid relying on skipping multiple newlines with line continuations. See [this issue](https://github.com/rust-lang/reference/pull/1042) for more information.\
\
[\[expr.literal.char\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char "expr.literal.char")\
\
## [Character literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#character-literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.char.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char.intro "expr.literal.char.intro")\
\
A character literal expression consists of a single [CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CHAR_LITERAL) token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.char.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char.type "expr.literal.char.type")\
\
The expression’s type is the primitive [`char`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#character-type) type.\
\
[\[expr.literal.char.no-suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char.no-suffix "expr.literal.char.no-suffix")\
\
The token must not have a suffix.\
\
[\[expr.literal.char.literal-content\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char.literal-content "expr.literal.char.literal-content")\
\
The token’s _literal content_ is the sequence of characters following the first `U+0027` (`'`) and preceding the last `U+0027` (`'`) in the string representation of the token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.char.represented\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char.represented "expr.literal.char.represented")\
\
The literal expression’s _represented character_ is derived from the literal content as follows:\
\
[\[expr.literal.char.escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char.escape "expr.literal.char.escape")\
\
- If the literal content is one of the following forms of escape sequence, the represented character is the escape sequence’s escaped value:\
  - [Simple escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#simple-escapes)\
  - [7-bit escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#7-bit-escapes)\
  - [Unicode escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unicode-escapes-1)\
\
[\[expr.literal.char.single\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char.single "expr.literal.char.single")\
\
- Otherwise the represented character is the single character that makes up the literal content.\
\
[\[expr.literal.char.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.char.result "expr.literal.char.result")\
\
The expression’s value is the [`char`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#character-type) corresponding to the represented character’s [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value).\
\
> Note\
>\
> The permitted forms of a [CHAR\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CHAR_LITERAL) token ensure that these rules always produce a single character.\
\
Examples of character literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
'R';                               // R\
'\'';                              // '\
'\x52';                            // R\
'\u{00E6}';                        // LATIN SMALL LETTER AE (U+00E6)\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.literal.string\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string "expr.literal.string")\
\
## [String literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#string-literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.string.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string.intro "expr.literal.string.intro")\
\
A string literal expression consists of a single [STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL) or [RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_LITERAL) token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.string.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string.type "expr.literal.string.type")\
\
The expression’s type is a shared reference (with `static` lifetime) to the primitive [`str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-slice-type) type. That is, the type is `&'static str`.\
\
[\[expr.literal.string.no-suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string.no-suffix "expr.literal.string.no-suffix")\
\
The token must not have a suffix.\
\
[\[expr.literal.string.literal-content\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string.literal-content "expr.literal.string.literal-content")\
\
The token’s _literal content_ is the sequence of characters following the first `U+0022` (`"`) and preceding the last `U+0022` (`"`) in the string representation of the token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.string.represented\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string.represented "expr.literal.string.represented")\
\
The literal expression’s _represented string_ is a sequence of characters derived from the literal content as follows:\
\
[\[expr.literal.string.escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string.escape "expr.literal.string.escape")\
\
- If the token is a [STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-STRING_LITERAL), each escape sequence of any of the following forms occurring in the literal content is replaced by the escape sequence’s escaped value.\
\
\
  - [Simple escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#simple-escapes)\
  - [7-bit escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#7-bit-escapes)\
  - [Unicode escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unicode-escapes-1)\
  - [String continuation escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-continuation-escapes)\
\
These replacements take place in left-to-right order. For example, the token `"\\x41"` is converted to the characters `\``x``4``1`.\
\
[\[expr.literal.string.raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string.raw "expr.literal.string.raw")\
\
- If the token is a [RAW\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_STRING_LITERAL), the represented string is identical to the literal content.\
\
[\[expr.literal.string.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.string.result "expr.literal.string.result")\
\
The expression’s value is a reference to a statically allocated [`str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-slice-type) containing the UTF-8 encoding of the represented string.\
\
Examples of string literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
"foo"; r"foo";                     // foo\
"\"foo\""; r#""foo""#;             // "foo"\
\
"foo #\"# bar";\
r##"foo #"# bar"##;                // foo #"# bar\
\
"\x52"; "R"; r"R";                 // R\
"\\x52"; r"\x52";                  // \x52\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char "expr.literal.byte-char")\
\
## [Byte literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#byte-literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char.intro "expr.literal.byte-char.intro")\
\
A byte literal expression consists of a single [BYTE\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_LITERAL) token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char.literal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char.literal "expr.literal.byte-char.literal")\
\
The expression’s type is the primitive [`u8`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types) type.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char.no-suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char.no-suffix "expr.literal.byte-char.no-suffix")\
\
The token must not have a suffix.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char.literal-content\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char.literal-content "expr.literal.byte-char.literal-content")\
\
The token’s _literal content_ is the sequence of characters following the first `U+0027` (`'`) and preceding the last `U+0027` (`'`) in the string representation of the token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char.represented\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char.represented "expr.literal.byte-char.represented")\
\
The literal expression’s _represented character_ is derived from the literal content as follows:\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char.escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char.escape "expr.literal.byte-char.escape")\
\
- If the literal content is one of the following forms of escape sequence, the represented character is the escape sequence’s escaped value:\
  - [Simple escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#simple-escapes)\
  - [8-bit escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#8-bit-escapes)\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char.single\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char.single "expr.literal.byte-char.single")\
\
- Otherwise the represented character is the single character that makes up the literal content.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-char.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-char.result "expr.literal.byte-char.result")\
\
The expression’s value is the represented character’s [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value).\
\
> Note\
>\
> The permitted forms of a [BYTE\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_LITERAL) token ensure that these rules always produce a single character, whose Unicode scalar value is in the range of [`u8`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types).\
\
Examples of byte literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
b'R';                              // 82\
b'\'';                             // 39\
b'\x52';                           // 82\
b'\xA0';                           // 160\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string "expr.literal.byte-string")\
\
## [Byte string literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#byte-string-literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string.intro "expr.literal.byte-string.intro")\
\
A byte string literal expression consists of a single [BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL) or [RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL) token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string.type "expr.literal.byte-string.type")\
\
The expression’s type is a shared reference (with `static` lifetime) to an array whose element type is [`u8`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types). That is, the type is `&'static [u8; N]`, where `N` is the number of bytes in the represented string described below.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string.no-suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string.no-suffix "expr.literal.byte-string.no-suffix")\
\
The token must not have a suffix.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string.literal-content\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string.literal-content "expr.literal.byte-string.literal-content")\
\
The token’s _literal content_ is the sequence of characters following the first `U+0022` (`"`) and preceding the last `U+0022` (`"`) in the string representation of the token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string.represented\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string.represented "expr.literal.byte-string.represented")\
\
The literal expression’s _represented string_ is a sequence of characters derived from the literal content as follows:\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string.escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string.escape "expr.literal.byte-string.escape")\
\
- If the token is a [BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL), each escape sequence of any of the following forms occurring in the literal content is replaced by the escape sequence’s escaped value.\
\
\
  - [Simple escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#simple-escapes)\
  - [8-bit escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#8-bit-escapes)\
  - [String continuation escapes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-continuation-escapes)\
\
These replacements take place in left-to-right order. For example, the token `b"\\x41"` is converted to the characters `\``x``4``1`.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string.raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string.raw "expr.literal.byte-string.raw")\
\
- If the token is a [RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL), the represented string is identical to the literal content.\
\
[\[expr.literal.byte-string.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.byte-string.result "expr.literal.byte-string.result")\
\
The expression’s value is a reference to a statically allocated array containing the [Unicode scalar values](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value) of the characters in the represented string, in the same order.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The permitted forms of [BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BYTE_STRING_LITERAL) and [RAW\_BYTE\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_BYTE_STRING_LITERAL) tokens ensure that these rules always produce array element values in the range of [`u8`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types).\
\
Examples of byte string literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
b"foo"; br"foo";                     // foo\
b"\"foo\""; br#""foo""#;             // "foo"\
\
b"foo #\"# bar";\
br##"foo #"# bar"##;                 // foo #"# bar\
\
b"\x52"; b"R"; br"R";                // R\
b"\\x52"; br"\x52";                  // \x52\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string "expr.literal.c-string")\
\
## [C string literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#c-string-literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string.intro "expr.literal.c-string.intro")\
\
A C string literal expression consists of a single [C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-C_STRING_LITERAL) or [RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL) token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string.type "expr.literal.c-string.type")\
\
The expression’s type is a shared reference (with `static` lifetime) to the standard library [CStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ffi/c_str/struct.CStr.html) type. That is, the type is `&'static core::ffi::CStr`.\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string.no-suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string.no-suffix "expr.literal.c-string.no-suffix")\
\
The token must not have a suffix.\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string.literal-content\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string.literal-content "expr.literal.c-string.literal-content")\
\
The token’s _literal content_ is the sequence of characters following the first `"` and preceding the last `"` in the string representation of the token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string.represented\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string.represented "expr.literal.c-string.represented")\
\
The literal expression’s _represented bytes_ are a sequence of bytes derived from the literal content as follows:\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string.escape\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string.escape "expr.literal.c-string.escape")\
\
- If the token is a [C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-C_STRING_LITERAL), the literal content is treated as a sequence of items, each of which is either a single Unicode character other than `\` or an [escape](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#escapes). The sequence of items is converted to a sequence of bytes as follows:\
\
  - Each single Unicode character contributes its UTF-8 representation.\
  - Each [simple escape](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#simple-escapes) contributes the [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value) of its escaped value.\
  - Each [8-bit escape](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#8-bit-escapes) contributes a single byte containing the [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value) of its escaped value.\
  - Each [unicode escape](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unicode-escapes-1) contributes the UTF-8 representation of its escaped value.\
  - Each [string continuation escape](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-continuation-escapes) contributes no bytes.\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string.raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string.raw "expr.literal.c-string.raw")\
\
- If the token is a [RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL), the represented bytes are the UTF-8 encoding of the literal content.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The permitted forms of [C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-C_STRING_LITERAL) and [RAW\_C\_STRING\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RAW_C_STRING_LITERAL) tokens ensure that the represented bytes never include a null byte.\
\
[\[expr.literal.c-string.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.c-string.result "expr.literal.c-string.result")\
\
The expression’s value is a reference to a statically allocated [CStr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ffi/c_str/struct.CStr.html) whose array of bytes contains the represented bytes followed by a null byte.\
\
Examples of C string literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
c"foo"; cr"foo";                     // foo\
c"\"foo\""; cr#""foo""#;             // "foo"\
\
c"foo #\"# bar";\
cr##"foo #"# bar"##;                 // foo #"# bar\
\
c"\x52"; c"R"; cr"R";                // R\
c"\\x52"; cr"\x52";                  // \x52\
\
c"æ";                                // LATIN SMALL LETTER AE (U+00E6)\
c"\u{00E6}";                         // LATIN SMALL LETTER AE (U+00E6)\
c"\xC3\xA6";                         // LATIN SMALL LETTER AE (U+00E6)\
\
c"\xE6".to_bytes();                  // [230]\
c"\u{00E6}".to_bytes();              // [195, 166]\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.literal.int\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int "expr.literal.int")\
\
## [Integer literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#integer-literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.intro "expr.literal.int.intro")\
\
An integer literal expression consists of a single [INTEGER\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INTEGER_LITERAL) token.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.suffix "expr.literal.int.suffix")\
\
If the token has a [suffix](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#suffixes), the suffix must be the name of one of the [primitive integer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types): `u8`, `i8`, `u16`, `i16`, `u32`, `i32`, `u64`, `i64`, `u128`, `i128`, `usize`, or `isize`, and the expression has that type.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.infer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.infer "expr.literal.int.infer")\
\
If the token has no suffix, the expression’s type is determined by type inference:\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.inference-unique-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.inference-unique-type "expr.literal.int.inference-unique-type")\
\
- If an integer type can be _uniquely_ determined from the surrounding program context, the expression has that type.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.inference-default\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.inference-default "expr.literal.int.inference-default")\
\
- If the program context under-constrains the type, it defaults to the signed 32-bit integer `i32`.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.inference-error\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.inference-error "expr.literal.int.inference-error")\
\
- If the program context over-constrains the type, it is considered a static type error.\
\
Examples of integer literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
123;                               // type i32\
123i32;                            // type i32\
123u32;                            // type u32\
123_u32;                           // type u32\
let a: u64 = 123;                  // type u64\
\
0xff;                              // type i32\
0xff_u8;                           // type u8\
\
0o70;                              // type i32\
0o70_i16;                          // type i16\
\
0b1111_1111_1001_0000;             // type i32\
0b1111_1111_1001_0000i64;          // type i64\
\
0usize;                            // type usize\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.representation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.representation "expr.literal.int.representation")\
\
The value of the expression is determined from the string representation of the token as follows:\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.radix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.radix "expr.literal.int.radix")\
\
- An integer radix is chosen by inspecting the first two characters of the string, as follows:\
  - `0b` indicates radix 2\
  - `0o` indicates radix 8\
  - `0x` indicates radix 16\
  - otherwise the radix is 10.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.radix-prefix-stripped\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.radix-prefix-stripped "expr.literal.int.radix-prefix-stripped")\
\
- If the radix is not 10, the first two characters are removed from the string.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.type-suffix-stripped\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.type-suffix-stripped "expr.literal.int.type-suffix-stripped")\
\
- Any suffix is removed from the string.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.separators-stripped\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.separators-stripped "expr.literal.int.separators-stripped")\
\
- Any underscores are removed from the string.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.u128-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.u128-value "expr.literal.int.u128-value")\
\
- The string is converted to a `u128` value as if by [`u128::from_str_radix`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u128.html#method.from_str_radix) with the chosen radix. If the value does not fit in `u128`, it is a compiler error.\
\
[\[expr.literal.int.cast\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.int.cast "expr.literal.int.cast")\
\
- The `u128` value is converted to the expression’s type via a [numeric cast](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-cast).\
\
> Note\
>\
> The final cast will truncate the value of the literal if it does not fit in the expression’s type. `rustc` includes a [lint check](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes) named `overflowing_literals`, defaulting to `deny`, which rejects expressions where this occurs.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `-1i8`, for example, is an application of the [negation operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#negation-operators) to the literal expression `1i8`, not a single integer literal expression. See [Overflow](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#overflow) for notes on representing the most negative value for a signed type.\
\
[\[expr.literal.float\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float "expr.literal.float")\
\
## [Floating-point literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#floating-point-literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.intro "expr.literal.float.intro")\
\
A floating-point literal expression has one of two forms:\
\
- a single [FLOAT\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FLOAT_LITERAL) token\
- a single [INTEGER\_LITERAL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-INTEGER_LITERAL) token which has a suffix and no radix indicator\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.suffix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.suffix "expr.literal.float.suffix")\
\
If the token has a [suffix](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#suffixes), the suffix must be the name of one of the [primitive floating-point types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#floating-point-types): `f32` or `f64`, and the expression has that type.\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.infer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.infer "expr.literal.float.infer")\
\
If the token has no suffix, the expression’s type is determined by type inference:\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.inference-unique-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.inference-unique-type "expr.literal.float.inference-unique-type")\
\
- If a floating-point type can be _uniquely_ determined from the surrounding program context, the expression has that type.\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.inference-default\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.inference-default "expr.literal.float.inference-default")\
\
- If the program context under-constrains the type, it defaults to `f64`.\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.inference-error\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.inference-error "expr.literal.float.inference-error")\
\
- If the program context over-constrains the type, it is considered a static type error.\
\
Examples of floating-point literal expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
123.0f64;        // type f64\
0.1f64;          // type f64\
0.1f32;          // type f32\
12E+99_f64;      // type f64\
5f32;            // type f32\
let x: f64 = 2.; // type f64\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.result "expr.literal.float.result")\
\
The value of the expression is determined from the string representation of the token as follows:\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.type-suffix-stripped\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.type-suffix-stripped "expr.literal.float.type-suffix-stripped")\
\
- Any suffix is removed from the string.\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.separators-stripped\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.separators-stripped "expr.literal.float.separators-stripped")\
\
- Any underscores are removed from the string.\
\
[\[expr.literal.float.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.float.value "expr.literal.float.value")\
\
- The string is converted to the expression’s type as if by [`f32::from_str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/primitive.f32.html#method.from_str) or [`f64::from_str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/primitive.f64.html#method.from_str).\
\
> Note\
>\
> `-1.0`, for example, is an application of the [negation operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#negation-operators) to the literal expression `1.0`, not a single floating-point literal expression.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `inf` and `NaN` are not literal tokens. The [`f32::INFINITY`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.f32.html#associatedconstant.INFINITY), [`f64::INFINITY`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.f64.html#associatedconstant.INFINITY), [`f32::NAN`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.f32.html#associatedconstant.NAN), and [`f64::NAN`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.f64.html#associatedconstant.NAN) constants can be used instead of literal expressions. In `rustc`, a literal large enough to be evaluated as infinite will trigger the `overflowing_literals` lint check.\
\
[\[expr.literal.bool\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.bool "expr.literal.bool")\
\
## [Boolean literal expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#boolean-literal-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.literal.bool.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.bool.intro "expr.literal.bool.intro")\
\
A boolean literal expression consists of one of the keywords `true` or `false`.\
\
[\[expr.literal.bool.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.literal.bool.result "expr.literal.bool.result")\
\
The expression’s type is the primitive [boolean type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type), and its value is:\
\
- true if the keyword is `true`\
- false if the keyword is `false`\
\
[\[expr.path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.path "expr.path")\
\
# [Path expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#path-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.path.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.path.syntax "expr.path.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[PathExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathExpression) →\
\
[PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathInExpression)\
\
    \| [QualifiedPathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QualifiedPathInExpression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[PathExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathExpression)[PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathInExpression)[QualifiedPathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QualifiedPathInExpression)\
\
[\[expr.path.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.path.intro "expr.path.intro")\
\
A [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) used as an expression context denotes either a local variable or an item.\
\
[\[expr.path.place\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.path.place "expr.path.place")\
\
Path expressions that resolve to local or static variables are [place expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions); other paths are [value expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.path.safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.path.safety "expr.path.safety")\
\
Using a [`static mut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#mutable-statics) variable requires an [`unsafe` block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unsafe-blocks).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod globals {\
    pub static STATIC_VAR: i32 = 5;\
    pub static mut STATIC_MUT_VAR: i32 = 7;\
}\
let local_var = 3;\
local_var;\
globals::STATIC_VAR;\
unsafe { globals::STATIC_MUT_VAR };\
let some_constructor = Some::<i32>;\
let push_integer = Vec::<i32>::push;\
let slice_reverse = <[i32]>::reverse;\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.path.const\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.path.const "expr.path.const")\
\
Evaluation of associated constants is handled the same way as [`const` blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#const-blocks).\
\
[\[expr.block\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block "expr.block")\
\
# [Block expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#block-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.block.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.syntax "expr.block.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression) →\
\
{\
\
[InnerAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InnerAttribute)\*\
\
[Statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Statements)?\
\
}\
\
[Statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Statements) →\
\
[Statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Statement)+\
\
    \| [Statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Statement)+[ExpressionWithoutBlock](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExpressionWithoutBlock)\
\
    \| [ExpressionWithoutBlock](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExpressionWithoutBlock)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
{[InnerAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-InnerAttribute)[Statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Statements)\
}\
\
[Statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Statements)[Statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Statement)[Statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Statement)[ExpressionWithoutBlock](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ExpressionWithoutBlock)[ExpressionWithoutBlock](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ExpressionWithoutBlock)\
\
[\[expr.block.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.intro "expr.block.intro")\
\
A _block expression_, or _block_, is a control flow expression and anonymous namespace scope for items and variable declarations.\
\
[\[expr.block.sequential-evaluation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.sequential-evaluation "expr.block.sequential-evaluation")\
\
As a control flow expression, a block sequentially executes its component non-item declaration statements and then its final optional expression.\
\
[\[expr.block.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.namespace "expr.block.namespace")\
\
As an anonymous namespace scope, item declarations are only in scope inside the block itself and variables declared by `let` statements are in scope from the next statement until the end of the block. See the [scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scopes) chapter for more details.\
\
[\[expr.block.inner-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.inner-attributes "expr.block.inner-attributes")\
\
The syntax for a block is `{`, then any [inner attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1), then any number of [statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#statements), then an optional expression, called the final operand, and finally a `}`.\
\
[\[expr.block.statements\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.statements "expr.block.statements")\
\
Statements are usually required to be followed by a semicolon, with two exceptions:\
\
1. Item declaration statements do not need to be followed by a semicolon.\
2. Expression statements usually require a following semicolon except if its outer expression is a flow control expression.\
\
[\[expr.block.null-statement\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.null-statement "expr.block.null-statement")\
\
Furthermore, extra semicolons between statements are allowed, but these semicolons do not affect semantics.\
\
[\[expr.block.evaluation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.evaluation "expr.block.evaluation")\
\
When evaluating a block expression, each statement, except for item declaration statements, is executed sequentially.\
\
[\[expr.block.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.result "expr.block.result")\
\
Then the final operand is executed, if given.\
\
[\[expr.block.value-trailing-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.value-trailing-expr "expr.block.value-trailing-expr")\
\
When a block contains a [final operand](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.inner-attributes), the block has the type and value of that final operand.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x: u8 = { 0u8 }; // `0u8` is the final operand.\
assert_eq!(x, 0);\
let x: u8 = { (); 0u8 }; // As above.\
assert_eq!(x, 0);\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.block.value-no-trailing-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.value-no-trailing-expr "expr.block.value-no-trailing-expr")\
\
When a block does not contain a [final operand](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.inner-attributes) and the block does not diverge, the block has [unit type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.unit) and [unit value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.unit).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x: () = {}; // Has no final operand.\
assert_eq!(x, ());\
let x: () = { 0u8; }; // As above.\
assert_eq!(x, ());\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.block.value-diverges-no-trailing-expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.value-diverges-no-trailing-expr "expr.block.value-diverges-no-trailing-expr")\
\
When a block does not contain a [final operand](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.inner-attributes) and the block [diverges](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.diverging), the block has the [never type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never) and has no final value (because its type is [uninhabited](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-glossary.uninhabited)).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn f() -> ! { loop {}; } // Diverges and has no final operand.\
//          ^^^^^^^^^^^^\
// The body of a function is a block expression.\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Observe that a block having no final operand is distinct from having an explicit final operand with unit type. E.g., even though this block diverges, the type of the block is [unit](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.unit) rather than [never](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never).\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn f() -> ! { loop {}; () } // ERROR: Mismatched types.\
> //          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ This block has unit type.\
> }\
> ```\
\
> Note\
>\
> As a control flow expression, if a block expression is the outer expression of an expression statement, the expected type is `()` unless it is followed immediately by a semicolon.\
\
[\[expr.block.diverging\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.diverging "expr.block.diverging")\
\
A block is considered to be [diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence) if all reachable control flow paths contain a diverging expression, unless that expression is a [place expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.place-value.place-memory-location) that is not read from.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#![ feature(never_type) ]\
fn no_control_flow() -> ! {\
    // There are no conditional statements, so this entire function body is diverging.\
    loop {}\
}\
\
fn control_flow_diverging() -> ! {\
    // All paths are diverging, so this entire function body is diverging.\
    if true {\
        loop {}\
    } else {\
        loop {}\
    }\
}\
\
fn control_flow_not_diverging() -> () {\
    // Some paths are not diverging, so this entire block is not diverging.\
    if true {\
        ()\
    } else {\
        loop {}\
    }\
}\
\
// Note: This makes use of the unstable never type which is only available on\
// Rust's nightly channel. This is done for illustration purposes. It is\
// possible to encounter this scenario in stable Rust, but requires a more\
// convoluted example.\
struct Foo {\
    x: !,\
}\
\
fn make<T>() -> T { loop {} }\
\
fn diverging_place_read() -> ! {\
    let foo = Foo { x: make() };\
    // A read of a place expression produces a diverging block.\
    let _x = foo.x;\
}\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#![ feature(never_type) ]\
fn make<T>() -> T { loop {} }\
struct Foo {\
    x: !,\
}\
fn diverging_place_not_read() -> ! {\
    let foo = Foo { x: make() };\
    // Assignment to `_` means the place is not read.\
    let _ = foo.x;\
} // ERROR: Mismatched types.\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.block.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.value "expr.block.value")\
\
Blocks are always [value expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions) and evaluate the last operand in value expression context.\
\
> Note\
>\
> This can be used to force moving a value if really needed. For example, the following example fails on the call to `consume_self` because the struct was moved out of `s` in the block expression.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> struct Struct;\
>\
> impl Struct {\
>     fn consume_self(self) {}\
>     fn borrow_self(&self) {}\
> }\
>\
> fn move_by_block_expression() {\
>     let s = Struct;\
>\
>     // Move the value out of `s` in the block expression.\
>     (&{ s }).borrow_self();\
>\
>     // Fails to execute because `s` is moved out of.\
>     s.consume_self();\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.block.async\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async "expr.block.async")\
\
## [`async` blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#async-blocks)\
\
[\[expr.block.async.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async.syntax "expr.block.async.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[AsyncBlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AsyncBlockExpression) → asyncmove?[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[AsyncBlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AsyncBlockExpression)\
async\
move[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression)\
\
[\[expr.block.async.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async.intro "expr.block.async.intro")\
\
An _async block_ is a variant of a block expression which evaluates to a future.\
\
[\[expr.block.async.future-result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async.future-result "expr.block.async.future-result")\
\
The final expression of the block, if present, determines the result value of the future.\
\
[\[expr.block.async.anonymous-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async.anonymous-type "expr.block.async.anonymous-type")\
\
Executing an async block is similar to executing a closure expression: its immediate effect is to produce and return an anonymous type.\
\
[\[expr.block.async.future\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async.future "expr.block.async.future")\
\
Whereas closures return a type that implements one or more of the [`std::ops::Fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.Fn.html) traits, however, the type returned for an async block implements the [`std::future::Future`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/future/future/trait.Future.html) trait.\
\
[\[expr.block.async.layout-unspecified\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async.layout-unspecified "expr.block.async.layout-unspecified")\
\
The actual data format for this type is unspecified.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The future type that rustc generates is roughly equivalent to an enum with one variant per `await` point, where each variant stores the data needed to resume from its corresponding point.\
\
[\[expr.block.async.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async.edition2018 "expr.block.async.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
