    \| [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)-=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
    \| [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\*=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\

\

\

\

\

\

\

\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[CompoundAssignmentExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CompoundAssignmentExpression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
+=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
-=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\

<<=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\

\
[\[expr.compound-assign.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign.intro "expr.compound-assign.intro")\
\
_Compound assignment expressions_ combine arithmetic and logical binary operators with assignment expressions.\
\
For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut x = 5;\
x += 1;\
assert!(x == 6);\
}\
```\
\
The syntax of compound assignment is a [mutable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#mutability) [place expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions), the _assigned operand_, then one of the operators followed by an `=` as a single token (no whitespace), and then a [value expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions), the _modifying operand_.\
\
[\[expr.compound-assign.place\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign.place "expr.compound-assign.place")\
\
Unlike other place operands, the assigned place operand must be a place expression.\
\
[\[expr.compound-assign.no-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign.no-value "expr.compound-assign.no-value")\
\
Attempting to use a value expression is a compiler error rather than promoting it to a temporary.\
\
[\[expr.compound-assign.operand-order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign.operand-order "expr.compound-assign.operand-order")\
\
Evaluation of compound assignment expressions depends on the types of the operands.\
\
[\[expr.compound-assign.primitives\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign.primitives "expr.compound-assign.primitives")\
\
If the types of both operands are known, prior to monomorphization, to be primitive, the right hand side is evaluated first, the left hand side is evaluated next, and the place given by the evaluation of the left hand side is mutated by applying the operator to the values of both sides.\
\
```rust\
\
use core::{num::Wrapping, ops::AddAssign};\
\
trait Equate {}\
impl<T> Equate for (T, T) {}\
\
fn f1(x: (u8,)) {\
    let mut order = vec![];\
    // The RHS is evaluated first as both operands are of primitive\
    // type.\
    { order.push(2); x }.0 += { order.push(1); x }.0;\
    assert!(order.is_sorted());\
}\
\
fn f2(x: (Wrapping<u8>,)) {\
    let mut order = vec![];\
    // The LHS is evaluated first as `Wrapping<_>` is not a primitive\
    // type.\
    { order.push(1); x }.0 += { order.push(2); (0u8,) }.0;\
    assert!(order.is_sorted());\
}\
\
fn f3<T: AddAssign<u8> + Copy>(x: (T,)) where (T, u8): Equate {\
    let mut order = vec![];\
    // The LHS is evaluated first as one of the operands is a generic\
    // parameter, even though that generic parameter can be unified\
    // with a primitive type due to the where clause bound.\
    { order.push(1); x }.0 += { order.push(2); (0u8,) }.0;\
    assert!(order.is_sorted());\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    f1((0u8,));\
    f2((Wrapping(0u8),));\
    // We supply a primitive type as the generic argument, but this\
    // does not affect the evaluation order in `f3` when\
    // monomorphized.\
    f3::<u8>((0u8,));\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is unusual. Elsewhere left to right evaluation is the norm.\
>\
> See the [eval order test](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.58.0/src/test/ui/expr/compound-assignment/eval-order.rs) for more examples.\
\
[\[expr.compound-assign.trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign.trait "expr.compound-assign.trait")\
\
Otherwise, this expression is syntactic sugar for using the corresponding trait for the operator (see [expr.arith-logic.behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.arith-logic.behavior)) and calling its method with the left hand side as the [receiver](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.intro) and the right hand side as the next argument.\
\
For example, the following two statements are equivalent:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::ops::AddAssign;\
fn f<T: AddAssign + Copy>(mut x: T, y: T) {\
    x += y; // Statement 1.\
    x.add_assign(y); // Statement 2.\
}\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Surprisingly, desugaring this further to a fully qualified method call is not equivalent, as there is special borrow checker behavior when the mutable reference to the first operand is taken via [autoref](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.candidate-receivers-refs).\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> use std::ops::AddAssign;\
> fn f<T: AddAssign + Copy>(mut x: T) {\
>     // Here we used `x` as both the LHS and the RHS. Because the\
>     // mutable borrow of the LHS needed to call the trait method\
>     // is taken implicitly by autoref, this is OK.\
>     x += x; //~ OK\
>     x.add_assign(x); //~ OK\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> use std::ops::AddAssign;\
> fn f<T: AddAssign + Copy>(mut x: T) {\
>     // We can't desugar the above to the below, as once we take the\
>     // mutable borrow of `x` to pass the first argument, we can't\
>     // pass `x` by value in the second argument because the mutable\
>     // reference is still live.\
>     <T as AddAssign>::add_assign(&mut x, x);\
>     //~^ ERROR cannot use `x` because it was mutably borrowed\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> use std::ops::AddAssign;\
> fn f<T: AddAssign + Copy>(mut x: T) {\
>     // As above.\
>     (&mut x).add_assign(x);\
>     //~^ ERROR cannot use `x` because it was mutably borrowed\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.compound-assign.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.compound-assign.result "expr.compound-assign.result")\
\
As with normal assignment expressions, compound assignment expressions always produce [the unit value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types).\
\
> Warning\
>\
> Avoid writing code that depends on the evaluation order of operands in compound assignments as it can be unusual and surprising.\
\
* * *\
\
1. Only when `m₁` is `mut` or `m₂` is `const`. Casting `mut` reference/pointer to `const` pointer is allowed. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-lessmut-1) [↩2](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-lessmut-2)\
\
2. Only closures that do not capture (close over) any local variables can be cast to function pointers. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-no-capture-1)\
\
\
[\[expr.paren\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.paren "expr.paren")\
\
# [Grouped expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#grouped-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.paren.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.paren.syntax "expr.paren.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[GroupedExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GroupedExpression) → ([Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression))\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[GroupedExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GroupedExpression)\
([Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
)\
\
[\[expr.paren.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.paren.intro "expr.paren.intro")\
\
A _parenthesized expression_ wraps a single expression, evaluating to that expression. The syntax for a parenthesized expression is a `(`, then an expression, called the _enclosed operand_, and then a `)`.\
\
[\[expr.paren.evaluation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.paren.evaluation "expr.paren.evaluation")\
\
Parenthesized expressions evaluate to the value of the enclosed operand.\
\
[\[expr.paren.place-or-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.paren.place-or-value "expr.paren.place-or-value")\
\
A parenthesized expression is a [place expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions) if the enclosed operand is a place expression, and is a value expression if the enclosed operand is a value expression.\
\
[\[expr.paren.override-precedence\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.paren.override-precedence "expr.paren.override-precedence")\
\
Parentheses can be used to explicitly modify the precedence order of subexpressions within an expression.\
\
An example of a parenthesized expression:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x: i32 = 2 + 3 * 4; // not parenthesized\
let y: i32 = (2 + 3) * 4; // parenthesized\
assert_eq!(x, 14);\
assert_eq!(y, 20);\
}\
```\
\
An example of a necessary use of parentheses is when calling a function pointer that is a member of a struct:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct A {\
   f: fn() -> &'static str\
}\
impl A {\
   fn f(&self) -> &'static str {\
       "The method f"\
   }\
}\
let a = A{f: || "The field f"};\
\
assert_eq!( a.f (), "The method f");\
assert_eq!((a.f)(), "The field f");\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.array\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array "expr.array")\
\
# [Array and array index expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#array-and-array-index-expressions)\
\
## [Array expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#array-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.array.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.syntax "expr.array.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ArrayExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ArrayExpression) → \[[ArrayElements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ArrayElements)?\]\
\
[ArrayElements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ArrayElements) →\
\
[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) ( ,[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) )\*,?\
\
    \| [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression);[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ArrayExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ArrayExpression)\
\[[ArrayElements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ArrayElements)\
\]\
\
[ArrayElements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ArrayElements)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
,[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
,[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
;[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[expr.array.constructor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.constructor "expr.array.constructor")\
\
_Array expressions_ construct [arrays](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types). Array expressions come in two forms.\
\
[\[expr.array.array\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.array "expr.array.array")\
\
The first form lists out every value in the array.\
\
[\[expr.array.array-syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.array-syntax "expr.array.array-syntax")\
\
The syntax for this form is a comma-separated list of expressions of uniform type enclosed in square brackets.\
\
[\[expr.array.array-behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.array-behavior "expr.array.array-behavior")\
\
This produces an array containing each of these values in the order they are written.\
\
[\[expr.array.repeat\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.repeat "expr.array.repeat")\
\
The syntax for the second form is two expressions separated by a semicolon (`;`) enclosed in square brackets.\
\
[\[expr.array.repeat-operand\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.repeat-operand "expr.array.repeat-operand")\
\
The expression before the `;` is called the _repeat operand_.\
\
[\[expr.array.length-operand\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.length-operand "expr.array.length-operand")\
\
The expression after the `;` is called the _length operand_.\
\
[\[expr.array.length-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.length-restriction "expr.array.length-restriction")\
\
The length operand must either be an [inferred const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred) or be a [constant expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-expressions) of type `usize` (e.g. a [literal](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literals) or a [constant item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items)).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
const C: usize = 1;\
let _: [u8; C] = [0; 1]; // Literal.\
let _: [u8; C] = [0; C]; // Constant item.\
let _: [u8; C] = [0; _]; // Inferred const.\
let _: [u8; C] = [0; (((_)))]; // Inferred const.\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> In an array expression, an [inferred const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred) is parsed as an [expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) but then semantically treated as a separate kind of [const generic argument](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.argument).\
\
[\[expr.array.repeat-behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.repeat-behavior "expr.array.repeat-behavior")\
\
An array expression of this form creates an array with the length of the value of the length operand with each element being a copy of the repeat operand. That is, `[a; b]` creates an array containing `b` copies of the value of `a`.\
\
[\[expr.array.repeat-copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.repeat-copy "expr.array.repeat-copy")\
\
If the length operand has a value greater than 1 then this requires the repeat operand to have a type that implements [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy), to be a [const block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.const), or to be a [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-expressions) to a constant item.\
\
[\[expr.array.repeat-const-item\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.repeat-const-item "expr.array.repeat-const-item")\
\
When the repeat operand is a const block or a path to a constant item, it is evaluated the number of times specified in the length operand.\
\
[\[expr.array.repeat-evaluation-zero\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.repeat-evaluation-zero "expr.array.repeat-evaluation-zero")\
\
If that value is `0`, then the const block or constant item is not evaluated at all.\
\
[\[expr.array.repeat-non-const\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.repeat-non-const "expr.array.repeat-non-const")\
\
For expressions that are neither a const block nor a path to a constant item, it is evaluated exactly once, and then the result is copied the length operand’s value times.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
[1, 2, 3, 4];\
["a", "b", "c", "d"];\
[0; 128];              // array with 128 zeros\
[0u8, 0u8, 0u8, 0u8,];\
[[1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 1]]; // 2D array\
const EMPTY: Vec<i32> = Vec::new();\
[EMPTY; 2];\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.array.index\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index "expr.array.index")\
\
## [Array and slice indexing expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.array.index.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index.syntax "expr.array.index.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[IndexExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IndexExpression) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\[[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\]\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[IndexExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IndexExpression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\[[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\]\
\
[\[expr.array.index.array\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index.array "expr.array.index.array")\
\
[Array](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types) and [slice](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-types)-typed values can be indexed by writing a square-bracket-enclosed expression of type `usize` (the index) after them. When the array is mutable, the resulting [memory location](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions) can be assigned to.\
\
[\[expr.array.index.trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index.trait "expr.array.index.trait")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/binop/operator-overloading.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/binop/operator-overloading.rs)\
\
For other types an index expression `a[b]` is equivalent to `*std::ops::Index::index(&a, b)`, or `*std::ops::IndexMut::index_mut(&mut a, b)` in a mutable place expression context, except that when the index expression undergoes [temporary lifetime extension](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension), the indexed expression `a` also has its [temporary scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary) extended. Just as with methods, Rust will also insert dereference operations on `a` repeatedly to find an implementation.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// The temporary holding the result of `vec![()]` is extended to\
// live to the end of the block, so `x` may be used in subsequent\
// statements.\
let x = &vec![()][0];\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// The temporary holding the result of `vec![()]` is dropped at the\
// end of the statement, so it's an error to use `y` after.\
let y = &*std::ops::Index::index(&vec![()], 0); // ERROR\
y;\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.array.index.zero-index\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index.zero-index "expr.array.index.zero-index")\
\
Indices are zero-based for arrays and slices.\
\
[\[expr.array.index.const\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index.const "expr.array.index.const")\
\
Array access is a [constant expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-expressions), so bounds can be checked at compile-time with a constant index value. Otherwise a check will be performed at run-time that will put the thread in a [_panicked state_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#panic-1) if it fails.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// lint is deny by default.\
#![warn(unconditional_panic)]\
\
([1, 2, 3, 4])[2];        // Evaluates to 3\
\
let b = [[1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 1]];\
b[1][2];                  // multidimensional array indexing\
\
let x = (["a", "b"])[10]; // warning: index out of bounds\
\
let n = 10;\
let y = (["a", "b"])[n];  // panics\
\
let arr = ["a", "b"];\
arr[10];                  // warning: index out of bounds\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.array.index.trait-impl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.index.trait-impl "expr.array.index.trait-impl")\
\
The array index expression can be implemented for types other than arrays and slices by implementing the [Index](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/index/trait.Index.html) and [IndexMut](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/index/trait.IndexMut.html) traits.\
\
[\[expr.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple "expr.tuple")\
\
# [Tuple and tuple indexing expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tuple-and-tuple-indexing-expressions)\
\
## [Tuple expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tuple-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.tuple.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple.syntax "expr.tuple.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[TupleExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleExpression) → ([TupleElements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleElements)?)\
\
[TupleElements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleElements) → ( [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression), )+[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TupleExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleExpression)\
([TupleElements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleElements)\
)\
\
[TupleElements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleElements)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
,[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[expr.tuple.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple.result "expr.tuple.result")\
\
A _tuple expression_ constructs [tuple values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types).\
\
[\[expr.tuple.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple.intro "expr.tuple.intro")\
\
The syntax for tuple expressions is a parenthesized, comma separated list of expressions, called the _tuple initializer operands_.\
\
[\[expr.tuple.unary-tuple-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple.unary-tuple-restriction "expr.tuple.unary-tuple-restriction")\
\
1-ary tuple expressions require a comma after their tuple initializer operand to be disambiguated with a [parenthetical expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grouped-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.tuple.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple.value "expr.tuple.value")\
\
Tuple expressions are a [value expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions) that evaluate into a newly constructed value of a tuple type.\
\
[\[expr.tuple.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple.type "expr.tuple.type")\
\
The number of tuple initializer operands is the arity of the constructed tuple.\
\
[\[expr.tuple.unit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple.unit "expr.tuple.unit")\
\
Tuple expressions without any tuple initializer operands produce the unit tuple.\
\
[\[expr.tuple.fields\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple.fields "expr.tuple.fields")\
\
For other tuple expressions, the first written tuple initializer operand initializes the field `0` and subsequent operands initializes the next highest field. For example, in the tuple expression `('a', 'b', 'c')`, `'a'` initializes the value of the field `0`, `'b'` field `1`, and `'c'` field `2`.\
\
Examples of tuple expressions and their types:\
\
| Expression | Type |\
| --- | --- |\
| `()` | `()` (unit) |\
| `(0.0, 4.5)` | `(f64, f64)` |\
| `("x".to_string(), )` | `(String, )` |\
| `("a", 4usize, true)` | `(&'static str, usize, bool)` |\
\
[\[expr.tuple-index\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple-index "expr.tuple-index")\
\
## [Tuple indexing expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tuple-indexing-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.tuple-index.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple-index.syntax "expr.tuple-index.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[TupleIndexingExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleIndexingExpression) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression).[TUPLE\_INDEX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TUPLE_INDEX)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TupleIndexingExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleIndexingExpression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
.[TUPLE\_INDEX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TUPLE_INDEX)\
\
[\[expr.tuple-index.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple-index.intro "expr.tuple-index.intro")\
\
A _tuple indexing expression_ accesses fields of [tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types) and [tuple structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-types).\
\
The syntax for a tuple index expression is an expression, called the _tuple operand_, then a `.`, then finally a tuple index.\
\
[\[expr.tuple-index.index-syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple-index.index-syntax "expr.tuple-index.index-syntax")\
\
The syntax for the _tuple index_ is a [decimal literal](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#integer-literals) with no leading zeros, underscores, or suffix. For example `0` and `2` are valid tuple indices but not `01`, `0_`, nor `0i32`.\
\
[\[expr.tuple-index.required-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple-index.required-type "expr.tuple-index.required-type")\
\
The type of the tuple operand must be a [tuple type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types) or a [tuple struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-types).\
\
[\[expr.tuple-index.index-name-operand\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple-index.index-name-operand "expr.tuple-index.index-name-operand")\
\
The tuple index must be a name of a field of the type of the tuple operand.\
\
[\[expr.tuple-index.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.tuple-index.result "expr.tuple-index.result")\
\
Evaluation of tuple index expressions has no side effects beyond evaluation of its tuple operand. As a [place expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions), it evaluates to the location of the field of the tuple operand with the same name as the tuple index.\
\
Examples of tuple indexing expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Indexing a tuple\
let pair = ("a string", 2);\
assert_eq!(pair.1, 2);\
\
// Indexing a tuple struct\
struct Point(f32, f32);\
let point = Point(1.0, 0.0);\
assert_eq!(point.0, 1.0);\
assert_eq!(point.1, 0.0);\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Unlike field access expressions, tuple index expressions can be the function operand of a [call expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#call-expressions) as it cannot be confused with a method call since method names cannot be numbers.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Although arrays and slices also have elements, you must use an [array or slice indexing expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions) or a [slice pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-patterns) to access their elements.\
\
[\[expr.struct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct "expr.struct")\
\
# [Struct expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#struct-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.struct.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.syntax "expr.struct.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[StructExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructExpression) →\
\
[PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathInExpression){ ( [StructExprFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExprFields) \| [StructBase](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructBase) )?}\
\
[StructExprFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructExprFields) →\
\
[StructExprField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExprField) ( ,[StructExprField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExprField) )\* ( ,[StructBase](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructBase) \| ,? )\
\
[StructExprField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructExprField) →\
\
[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\*\
\
    (\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)\
\
      \| ( [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| [TUPLE\_INDEX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TUPLE_INDEX) ) :[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
    )\
\
[StructBase](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructBase) → ..[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[StructExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExpression)[PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathInExpression)\
{[StructExprFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructExprFields) [StructBase](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructBase)\
}\
\
[StructExprFields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExprFields)[StructExprField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructExprField)\
,[StructExprField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructExprField)\
,[StructBase](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructBase)\
,\
\
[StructExprField](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExprField)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER) [TUPLE\_INDEX](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TUPLE_INDEX)\
:[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[StructBase](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructBase)\
..[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[expr.struct.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.intro "expr.struct.intro")\
\
A _struct expression_ creates a struct, enum, or union value. It consists of a path to a [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), [enum variant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations), or [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions) item followed by the values for the fields of the item.\
\
The following are examples of struct expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Point { x: f64, y: f64 }\
struct NothingInMe { }\
mod game { pub struct User<'a> { pub name: &'a str, pub age: u32, pub score: usize } }\
enum Enum { Variant {} }\
Point {x: 10.0, y: 20.0};\
NothingInMe {};\
let u = game::User {name: "Joe", age: 35, score: 100_000};\
Enum::Variant {};\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Tuple structs and tuple enum variants are typically instantiated using a [call expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call) referring to the [constructor in the value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.tuple). These are distinct from a struct expression using curly braces referring to the constructor in the type namespace.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> struct Position(i32, i32, i32);\
> Position(0, 0, 0);  // Typical way of creating a tuple struct.\
> let c = Position;  // `c` is a function that takes 3 arguments.\
> let pos = c(8, 6, 7);  // Creates a `Position` value.\
>\
> enum Version { Triple(i32, i32, i32) };\
> Version::Triple(0, 0, 0);\
> let f = Version::Triple;\
> let ver = f(8, 6, 7);\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> The last segment of the call path cannot refer to a type alias:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> trait Tr { type T; }\
> impl<T> Tr for T { type T = T; }\
>\
> struct Tuple();\
> enum Enum { Tuple() }\
>\
> // <Unit as Tr>::T(); // causes an error -- `::T` is a type, not a value\
> <Enum as Tr>::T::Tuple(); // OK\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> * * *\
>\
> Unit structs and unit enum variants are typically instantiated using a [path expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.path) referring to the [constant in the value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.struct.unit).\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> struct Gamma;\
> // Gamma unit value, referring to the const in the value namespace.\
> let a = Gamma;\
> // Exact same value as `a`, but constructed using a struct expression\
> // referring to the type namespace.\
> let b = Gamma {};\
>\
> enum ColorSpace { Oklch }\
> let c = ColorSpace::Oklch;\
> let d = ColorSpace::Oklch {};\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.struct.field\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.field "expr.struct.field")\
\
## [Field struct expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#field-struct-expression)\
\
[\[expr.struct.field.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.field.intro "expr.struct.field.intro")\
\
A struct expression with fields enclosed in curly braces allows you to specify the value for each individual field in any order. The field name is separated from its value with a colon.\
\
[\[expr.struct.field.union-constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.field.union-constraint "expr.struct.field.union-constraint")\
\
A value of a [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions) type can only be created using this syntax, and it must specify exactly one field.\
\
[\[expr.struct.update\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.update "expr.struct.update")\
\
## [Functional update syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#functional-update-syntax)\
\
[\[expr.struct.update.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.update.intro "expr.struct.update.intro")\
\
A struct expression that constructs a value of a struct type can terminate with the syntax `..` followed by an expression to denote a functional update.\
\
[\[expr.struct.update.base-same-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.update.base-same-type "expr.struct.update.base-same-type")\
\
The expression following `..` (the base) must have the same struct type as the new struct type being formed.\
\
[\[expr.struct.update.fields\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.update.fields "expr.struct.update.fields")\
\
The entire expression uses the given values for the fields that were specified and moves or copies the remaining fields from the base expression.\
\
[\[expr.struct.update.visibility-constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.update.visibility-constraint "expr.struct.update.visibility-constraint")\
\
As with all struct expressions, all of the fields of the struct must be [visible](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#visibility-and-privacy), even those not explicitly named.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Point3d { x: i32, y: i32, z: i32 }\
let mut base = Point3d {x: 1, y: 2, z: 3};\
let y_ref = &mut base.y;\
Point3d {y: 0, z: 10, .. base}; // OK, only base.x is accessed\
drop(y_ref);\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.struct.brace-restricted-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.brace-restricted-positions "expr.struct.brace-restricted-positions")\
\
Struct expressions can’t be used directly in a [loop](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loops-and-other-breakable-expressions) or [if](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-expressions) expression’s head, or in the [scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scrutinee) of an [if let](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-let-patterns) or [match](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions) expression. However, struct expressions can be used in these situations if they are within another expression, for example inside [parentheses](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grouped-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.struct.tuple-field\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.tuple-field "expr.struct.tuple-field")\
\
The field names can be decimal integer values to specify indices for constructing tuple structs. This can be used with base structs to fill out the remaining indices not specified:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Color(u8, u8, u8);\
let c1 = Color(0, 0, 0);  // Typical way of creating a tuple struct.\
let c2 = Color{0: 255, 1: 127, 2: 0};  // Specifying fields by index.\
let c3 = Color{1: 0, ..c2};  // Fill out all other fields using a base struct.\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.struct.field.named\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.struct.field.named "expr.struct.field.named")\
\
### [Struct field init shorthand](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#struct-field-init-shorthand)\
\
When initializing a data structure (struct, enum, union) with named (but not numbered) fields, it is allowed to write `fieldname` as a shorthand for `fieldname: fieldname`. This allows a compact syntax with less duplication. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Point3d { x: i32, y: i32, z: i32 }\
let x = 0;\
let y_value = 0;\
let z = 0;\
Point3d { x: x, y: y_value, z: z };\
Point3d { x, y: y_value, z };\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.call\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call "expr.call")\
\
# [Call expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#call-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.call.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.syntax "expr.call.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[CallExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CallExpression) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)([CallParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CallParams)?)\
\
[CallParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CallParams) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) ( ,[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) )\*,?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[CallExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CallExpression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
([CallParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CallParams)\
)\
\
[CallParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CallParams)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
,[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
,\
\
[\[expr.call.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.intro "expr.call.intro")\
\
A _call expression_ calls a function. The syntax of a call expression is an expression, called the _function operand_, followed by a parenthesized comma-separated list of expression, called the _argument operands_.\
\
[\[expr.call.convergence\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.convergence "expr.call.convergence")\
\
If the function eventually returns, then the expression completes.\
\
[\[expr.call.trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.trait "expr.call.trait")\
\
For [non-function types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-item-types), the expression `f(...)` uses the method on one of the following traits based on the function operand:\
\
- [`Fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.Fn.html) or [`AsyncFn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/async_function/trait.AsyncFn.html) — shared reference.\
- [`FnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnMut.html) or [`AsyncFnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/async_function/trait.AsyncFnMut.html) — mutable reference.\
- [`FnOnce`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html) or [`AsyncFnOnce`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/async_function/trait.AsyncFnOnce.html) — value.\
\
[\[expr.call.autoref-deref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.autoref-deref "expr.call.autoref-deref")\
\
An automatic borrow will be taken if needed. The function operand will also be [automatically dereferenced](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#automatic-dereferencing) as required.\
\
Some examples of call expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn add(x: i32, y: i32) -> i32 { 0 }\
let three: i32 = add(1i32, 2i32);\
let name: &'static str = (|| "Rust")();\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.call.desugar\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.desugar "expr.call.desugar")\
\
## [Disambiguating function calls](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#disambiguating-function-calls)\
\
[\[expr.call.desugar.fully-qualified\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.desugar.fully-qualified "expr.call.desugar.fully-qualified")\
\
All function calls are sugar for a more explicit [fully-qualified syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#qualified-paths).\
\
[\[expr.call.desugar.ambiguity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.desugar.ambiguity "expr.call.desugar.ambiguity")\
\
Function calls may need to be fully qualified, depending on the ambiguity of a call in light of in-scope items.\
\
> Note\
>\
> In the past, the terms “Unambiguous Function Call Syntax”, “Universal Function Call Syntax”, or “UFCS”, have been used in documentation, issues, RFCs, and other community writings. However, these terms lack descriptive power and potentially confuse the issue at hand. We mention them here for searchability’s sake.\
\
[\[expr.call.desugar.limits\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.desugar.limits "expr.call.desugar.limits")\
\
Several situations often occur which result in ambiguities about the receiver or referent of method or associated function calls. These situations may include:\
\
- Multiple in-scope traits define methods with the same name for the same types\
- Auto-`deref` is undesirable; for example, distinguishing between methods on a smart pointer itself and the pointer’s referent\
- Methods which take no arguments, like [`default()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/default/trait.Default.html#tymethod.default), and return properties of a type, like [`size_of()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/fn.size_of.html)\
\
[\[expr.call.desugar.explicit-path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.call.desugar.explicit-path "expr.call.desugar.explicit-path")\
\
To resolve the ambiguity, the programmer may refer to their desired method or function using more specific paths, types, or traits.\
\
For example,\
\
```rust\
\
trait Pretty {\
    fn print(&self);\
}\
\
trait Ugly {\
    fn print(&self);\
}\
\
struct Foo;\
impl Pretty for Foo {\
    fn print(&self) {}\
}\
\
struct Bar;\
impl Pretty for Bar {\
    fn print(&self) {}\
}\
impl Ugly for Bar {\
    fn print(&self) {}\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    let f = Foo;\
    let b = Bar;\
\
    // we can do this because we only have one item called `print` for `Foo`s\
    f.print();\
    // more explicit, and, in the case of `Foo`, not necessary\
    Foo::print(&f);\
    // if you're not into the whole brevity thing\
    <Foo as Pretty>::print(&f);\
\
    // b.print(); // Error: multiple 'print' found\
    // Bar::print(&b); // Still an error: multiple `print` found\
\
    // necessary because of in-scope items defining `print`\
    <Bar as Pretty>::print(&b);\
}\
```\
\
Refer to [RFC 132](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/text/0132-ufcs.md) for further details and motivations.\
\
[\[expr.method\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method "expr.method")\
\
# [Method-call expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#method-call-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.method.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.syntax "expr.method.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[MethodCallExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MethodCallExpression) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression).[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathExprSegment)([CallParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-CallParams)?)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[MethodCallExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MethodCallExpression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
.[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathExprSegment)\
([CallParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CallParams)\
)\
\
[\[expr.method.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.intro "expr.method.intro")\
\
A _method call_ consists of an expression (the _receiver_) followed by a single dot, an expression path segment, and a parenthesized expression-list.\
\
[\[expr.method.target\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.target "expr.method.target")\
\
Method calls are resolved to associated [methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#methods) on specific traits, either statically dispatching to a method if the exact `self`-type of the left-hand-side is known, or dynamically dispatching if the left-hand-side expression is an indirect [trait object](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let pi: Result<f32, _> = "3.14".parse();\
let log_pi = pi.unwrap_or(1.0).log(2.72);\
assert!(1.14 < log_pi && log_pi < 1.15)\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.method.autoref-deref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.autoref-deref "expr.method.autoref-deref")\
\
When looking up a method call, the receiver may be automatically dereferenced or borrowed in order to call a method. This requires a more complex lookup process than for other functions, since there may be a number of possible methods to call. The following procedure is used:\
\
[\[expr.method.candidate-receivers\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.candidate-receivers "expr.method.candidate-receivers")\
\
The first step is to build a list of candidate receiver types. Obtain these by repeatedly [dereferencing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator) the receiver expression’s type, adding each type encountered to the list, then finally attempting an array [unsized coercion](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unsized-coercions) at the end, and adding the result type if that is successful.\
\
[\[expr.method.candidate-receivers-refs\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.candidate-receivers-refs "expr.method.candidate-receivers-refs")\
\
Then, for each candidate `T`, add `&T` and `&mut T` to the list immediately after `T`.\
\
For instance, if the receiver has type `Box<[i32;2]>`, then the candidate types will be `Box<[i32;2]>`, `&Box<[i32;2]>`, `&mut Box<[i32;2]>`, `[i32; 2]` (by dereferencing), `&[i32; 2]`, `&mut [i32; 2]`, `[i32]` (by unsized coercion), `&[i32]`, and finally `&mut [i32]`.\
\
[\[expr.method.candidate-search\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.candidate-search "expr.method.candidate-search")\
\
Then, for each candidate type `T`, search for a [visible](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#visibility-and-privacy) method with a receiver of that type in the following places:\
\
1. `T`’s inherent methods (methods implemented directly on `T`).\
2. Any of the methods provided by a [visible](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#visibility-and-privacy) trait implemented by `T`. If `T` is a type parameter, methods provided by trait bounds on `T` are looked up first. Then all remaining methods in scope are looked up.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The lookup is done for each type in order, which can occasionally lead to surprising results. The below code will print “In trait impl!”, because `&self` methods are looked up first, the trait method is found before the struct’s `&mut self` method is found.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> struct Foo {}\
>\
> trait Bar {\
>   fn bar(&self);\
> }\
>\
> impl Foo {\
>   fn bar(&mut self) {\
>     println!("In struct impl!")\
>   }\
> }\
>\
> impl Bar for Foo {\
>   fn bar(&self) {\
>     println!("In trait impl!")\
>   }\
> }\
>\
> fn main() {\
>   let mut f = Foo{};\
>   f.bar();\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.method.ambiguous-target\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.ambiguous-target "expr.method.ambiguous-target")\
\
If this results in multiple possible candidates, then it is an error, and the receiver must be [converted](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#disambiguating-function-calls) to an appropriate receiver type to make the method call.\
\
[\[expr.method.receiver-constraints\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.receiver-constraints "expr.method.receiver-constraints")\
\
This process does not take into account the mutability or lifetime of the receiver, or whether a method is `unsafe`. Once a method is looked up, if it can’t be called for one (or more) of those reasons, the result is a compiler error.\
\
[\[expr.method.ambiguous-search\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.ambiguous-search "expr.method.ambiguous-search")\
\
If a step is reached where there is more than one possible method, such as where generic methods or traits are considered the same, then it is a compiler error. These cases require a [disambiguating function call syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#disambiguating-function-calls) for method and function invocation.\
\
[\[expr.method.edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.method.edition2021 "expr.method.edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2021 edition, during the search for visible methods, if the candidate receiver type is an [array type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types), methods provided by the standard library [`IntoIterator`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/iter/traits/collect/trait.IntoIterator.html) trait are ignored.\
>\
> The edition used for this purpose is determined by the token representing the method name.\
>\
> This special case may be removed in the future.\
\
> Warning\
>\
> For [trait objects](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects), if there is an inherent method of the same name as a trait method, it will give a compiler error when trying to call the method in a method call expression. Instead, you can call the method using [disambiguating function call syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#disambiguating-function-calls), in which case it calls the trait method, not the inherent method. There is no way to call the inherent method. Just don’t define inherent methods on trait objects with the same name as a trait method and you’ll be fine.\
\
[\[expr.field\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field "expr.field")\
\
# [Field access expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#field-access-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.field.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field.syntax "expr.field.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[FieldExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FieldExpression) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression).[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[FieldExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FieldExpression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
.[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
\
[\[expr.field.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field.intro "expr.field.intro")\
\
A _field expression_ is a [place expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions) that evaluates to the location of a field of a [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs) or [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions).\
\
[\[expr.field.mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field.mut "expr.field.mut")\
\
When the operand is [mutable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#mutability), the field expression is also mutable.\
\
[\[expr.field.form\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field.form "expr.field.form")\
\
The syntax for a field expression is an expression, called the _container operand_, then a `.`, and finally an [identifier](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#identifiers).\
\
[\[expr.field.not-method-call\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field.not-method-call "expr.field.not-method-call")\
\
Field expressions cannot be followed by a parenthetical comma-separated list of expressions, as that is instead parsed as a [method call expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#method-call-expressions). That is, they cannot be the function operand of a [call expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#call-expressions).\
\
> Note\
>\
> Wrap the field expression in a [parenthesized expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grouped-expressions) to use it in a call expression.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> struct HoldsCallable<F: Fn()> { callable: F }\
> let holds_callable = HoldsCallable { callable: || () };\
>\
> // Invalid: Parsed as calling the method "callable"\
> // holds_callable.callable();\
>\
> // Valid\
> (holds_callable.callable)();\
> }\
> ```\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
mystruct.myfield;\
foo().x;\
(Struct {a: 10, b: 20}).a;\
(mystruct.function_field)() // Call expression containing a field expression\
```\
\
[\[expr.field.autoref-deref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field.autoref-deref "expr.field.autoref-deref")\
\
## [Automatic dereferencing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#automatic-dereferencing)\
\
If the type of the container operand implements [`Deref`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#deref-and-derefmut) or [`DerefMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#deref-and-derefmut) depending on whether the operand is [mutable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#mutability), it is _automatically dereferenced_ as many times as necessary to make the field access possible. This process is also called _autoderef_ for short.\
\
[\[expr.field.borrow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.field.borrow "expr.field.borrow")\
\
## [Borrowing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#borrowing)\
\
The fields of a struct or a reference to a struct are treated as separate entities when borrowing. If the struct does not implement [`Drop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#drop) and is stored in a local variable, this also applies to moving out of each of its fields. This also does not apply if automatic dereferencing is done through user-defined types other than [`Box`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boxt).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct A { f1: String, f2: String, f3: String }\
let mut x: A;\
x = A {\
    f1: "f1".to_string(),\
    f2: "f2".to_string(),\
    f3: "f3".to_string()\
};\
let a: &mut String = &mut x.f1; // x.f1 borrowed mutably\
let b: &String = &x.f2;         // x.f2 borrowed immutably\
let c: &String = &x.f2;         // Can borrow again\
let d: String = x.f3;           // Move out of x.f3\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.closure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure "expr.closure")\
\
# [Closure expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#closure-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.closure.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.syntax "expr.closure.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ClosureExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ClosureExpression) →\
\
async?​[1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-cl-async-edition)\
\
move?\
\
    ( \|\| \| \|[ClosureParameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ClosureParameters)?\| )\
\
    ( [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) \| ->[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeNoBounds)[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression) )\
\
[ClosureParameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ClosureParameters) → [ClosureParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ClosureParam) ( ,[ClosureParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ClosureParam) )\*,?\
\
[ClosureParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ClosureParam) → [OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\*[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternNoTopAlt) ( :[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) )?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ClosureExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ClosureExpression)\
async\
move\
\|\|\
\|[ClosureParameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ClosureParameters)\
\|[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
->[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeNoBounds) [BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression)\
\
[ClosureParameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ClosureParameters)[ClosureParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ClosureParam)\
,[ClosureParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ClosureParam)\
,\
\
[ClosureParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ClosureParam)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PatternNoTopAlt)\
:[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
\
[\[expr.closure.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.intro "expr.closure.intro")\
\
A _closure expression_, also known as a lambda expression or a lambda, defines a [closure type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types) and evaluates to a value of that type. The syntax for a closure expression is an optional `async` keyword, an optional `move` keyword, then a pipe-symbol-delimited (`|`) comma-separated list of [patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns), called the _closure parameters_ each optionally followed by a `:` and a type, then an optional `->` and type, called the _return type_, and then an expression, called the _closure body operand_.\
\
[\[expr.closure.param-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.param-type "expr.closure.param-type")\
\
The optional type after each pattern is a type annotation for the pattern.\
\
[\[expr.closure.explicit-type-body\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.explicit-type-body "expr.closure.explicit-type-body")\
\
If there is a return type, the closure body must be a [block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.closure.parameter-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.parameter-restriction "expr.closure.parameter-restriction")\
\
A closure expression denotes a function that maps a list of parameters onto the expression that follows the parameters. Just like a [`let` binding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#let-statements), the closure parameters are irrefutable [patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns), whose type annotation is optional and will be inferred from context if not given.\
\
[\[expr.closure.unique-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.unique-type "expr.closure.unique-type")\
\
Each closure expression has a unique, anonymous type.\
\
[\[expr.closure.captures\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.captures "expr.closure.captures")\
\
Significantly, closure expressions _capture their environment_, which regular [function definitions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions) do not.\
\
[\[expr.closure.capture-inference\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.capture-inference "expr.closure.capture-inference")\
\
Without the `move` keyword, the closure expression [infers how it captures each variable from its environment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#capture-modes-1), preferring to capture by shared reference, effectively borrowing all outer variables mentioned inside the closure’s body.\
\
[\[expr.closure.capture-mut-ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.capture-mut-ref "expr.closure.capture-mut-ref")\
\
If needed the compiler will infer that instead mutable references should be taken, or that the values should be moved or copied (depending on their type) from the environment.\
\
[\[expr.closure.capture-move\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.capture-move "expr.closure.capture-move")\
\
A closure can be forced to capture its environment by copying or moving values by prefixing it with the `move` keyword. This is often used to ensure that the closure’s lifetime is `'static`.\
\
[\[expr.closure.trait-impl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.trait-impl "expr.closure.trait-impl")\
\
## [Closure trait implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#closure-trait-implementations)\
\
Which traits the closure type implement depends on how variables are captured, the types of the captured variables, and the presence of `async`. See the [call traits and coercions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#call-traits-and-coercions) chapter for how and when a closure implements `Fn`, `FnMut`, and `FnOnce`. The closure type implements [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#send) and [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sync) if the type of every captured variable also implements the trait.\
\
[\[expr.closure.async\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.async "expr.closure.async")\
\
## [Async closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#async-closures)\
\
[\[expr.closure.async.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.async.intro "expr.closure.async.intro")\
\
Closures marked with the `async` keyword indicate that they are asynchronous in an analogous way to an [async function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.async).\
\
[\[expr.closure.async.future\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.async.future "expr.closure.async.future")\
\
Calling the async closure does not perform any work, but instead evaluates to a value that implements [`Future`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/future/future/trait.Future.html) that corresponds to the computation of the body of the closure.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
async fn takes_async_callback(f: impl AsyncFn(u64)) {\
    f(0).await;\
    f(1).await;\
}\
\
async fn example() {\
    takes_async_callback(async |i| {\
        core::future::ready(i).await;\
        println!("done with {i}.");\
    }).await;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.closure.async.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.async.edition2018 "expr.closure.async.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> Async closures are only available beginning with Rust 2018.\
\
## [Example](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#example)\
\
In this example, we define a function `ten_times` that takes a higher-order function argument, and we then call it with a closure expression as an argument, followed by a closure expression that moves values from its environment.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn ten_times<F>(f: F) where F: Fn(i32) {\
    for index in 0..10 {\
        f(index);\
    }\
}\
\
ten_times(|j| println!("hello, {}", j));\
// With type annotations\
ten_times(|j: i32| -> () { println!("hello, {}", j) });\
\
let word = "konnichiwa".to_owned();\
ten_times(move |j| println!("{}, {}", word, j));\
}\
```\
\
## [Attributes on closure parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-on-closure-parameters)\
\
[\[expr.closure.param-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.closure.param-attributes "expr.closure.param-attributes")\
\
Attributes on closure parameters follow the same rules and restrictions as [regular function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-on-function-parameters).\
\
* * *\
\
1. The `async` qualifier is not allowed in the 2015 edition. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-cl-async-edition-1)\
\
\
[\[expr.loop\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop "expr.loop")\
\
# [Loops and other breakable expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#loops-and-other-breakable-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.loop.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.syntax "expr.loop.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[LoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LoopExpression) →\
\
[LoopLabel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LoopLabel)? (\
\
[InfiniteLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InfiniteLoopExpression)\
\
      \| [PredicateLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PredicateLoopExpression)\
\
      \| [IteratorLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IteratorLoopExpression)\
\
      \| [LabelBlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LabelBlockExpression)\
\
    )\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[LoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LoopExpression)[LoopLabel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LoopLabel)[InfiniteLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-InfiniteLoopExpression)[PredicateLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PredicateLoopExpression)[IteratorLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IteratorLoopExpression)[LabelBlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LabelBlockExpression)\
\
[\[expr.loop.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.intro "expr.loop.intro")\
\
Rust supports four loop expressions:\
\
- A [`loop` expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#infinite-loops) denotes an infinite loop.\
- A [`while` expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#predicate-loops) loops until a predicate is false.\
- A [`for` expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#iterator-loops) extracts values from an iterator, looping until the iterator is empty.\
- A [labeled block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels) runs a loop exactly once, but allows exiting the loop early with `break`.\
\
[\[expr.loop.break-label\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-label "expr.loop.break-label")\
\
All four types of loop support [`break` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#break-expressions), and [labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels).\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue-label\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue-label "expr.loop.continue-label")\
\
All except labeled block expressions support [`continue` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#continue-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.loop.explicit-result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.explicit-result "expr.loop.explicit-result")\
\
Only `loop` and labeled block expressions support [evaluation to non-trivial values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#break-and-loop-values).\
\
[\[expr.loop.infinite\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.infinite "expr.loop.infinite")\
\
## [Infinite loops](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#infinite-loops)\
\
[\[expr.loop.infinite.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.infinite.syntax "expr.loop.infinite.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[InfiniteLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-InfiniteLoopExpression) → loop[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[InfiniteLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InfiniteLoopExpression)\
loop[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression)\
\
[\[expr.loop.infinite.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.infinite.intro "expr.loop.infinite.intro")\
\
A `loop` expression repeats execution of its body continuously: `loop { println!("I live."); }`.\
\
[\[expr.loop.infinite.diverging\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.infinite.diverging "expr.loop.infinite.diverging")\
\
A `loop` expression without an associated `break` expression is [diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence) and has type [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never).\
\
[\[expr.loop.infinite.break\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.infinite.break "expr.loop.infinite.break")\
\
A `loop` expression containing associated [`break` expression(s)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#break-expressions) may terminate, and must have type compatible with the value of the `break` expression(s).\
\
[\[expr.loop.while\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while "expr.loop.while")\
\
## [Predicate loops](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#predicate-loops)\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.syntax "expr.loop.while.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[PredicateLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PredicateLoopExpression) → while[Conditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Conditions)[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[PredicateLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PredicateLoopExpression)\
while[Conditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Conditions) [BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression)\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.intro "expr.loop.while.intro")\
\
A `while` loop expression allows repeating the evaluation of a block while a set of conditions remain true.\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.condition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.condition "expr.loop.while.condition")\
\
Condition operands must be either an [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) with a [boolean type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type) or a conditional `let` match. If all of the condition operands evaluate to `true` and all of the `let` patterns successfully match their [scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scrutinee) s, then the loop body block executes.\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.repeat\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.repeat "expr.loop.while.repeat")\
\
After the loop body successfully executes, the condition operands are re-evaluated to determine if the body should be executed again.\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.exit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.exit "expr.loop.while.exit")\
\
If any condition operand evaluates to `false` or any `let` pattern does not match its scrutinee, the body is not executed and execution continues after the `while` expression.\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.eval\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.eval "expr.loop.while.eval")\
\
A `while` expression evaluates to `()`.\
\
An example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut i = 0;\
\
while i < 10 {\
    println!("hello");\
    i = i + 1;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.let "expr.loop.while.let")\
\
### [`while let` patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#while-let-patterns)\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.let.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.let.intro "expr.loop.while.let.intro")\
\
`let` patterns in a `while` condition allow binding new variables into scope when the pattern matches successfully. The following examples illustrate bindings using `let` patterns:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut x = vec![1, 2, 3];\
\
while let Some(y) = x.pop() {\
    println!("y = {}", y);\
}\
\
while let _ = 5 {\
    println!("Irrefutable patterns are always true");\
    break;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.let.desugar\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.let.desugar "expr.loop.while.let.desugar")\
\
A `while let` loop is equivalent to a `loop` expression containing a [`match` expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions) as follows.\
\
```rust\
\
'label: while let PATS = EXPR {\
    /* loop body */\
}\
```\
\
is equivalent to\
\
```rust\
\
'label: loop {\
    match EXPR {\
        PATS => { /* loop body */ },\
        _ => break,\
    }\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.let.or-pattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.let.or-pattern "expr.loop.while.let.or-pattern")\
\
Multiple patterns may be specified with the `|` operator. This has the same semantics as with `|` in `match` expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut vals = vec![2, 3, 1, 2, 2];\
while let Some(v @ 1) | Some(v @ 2) = vals.pop() {\
    // Prints 2, 2, then 1\
    println!("{}", v);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.chains\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.chains "expr.loop.while.chains")\
\
### [`while` condition chains](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#while-condition-chains)\
\
[\[expr.loop.while.chains.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.while.chains.intro "expr.loop.while.chains.intro")\
\
Multiple condition operands can be separated with `&&`. These have the same semantics and restrictions as [`if` condition chains](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#chains-of-conditions).\
\
The following is an example of chaining multiple expressions, mixing `let` bindings and boolean expressions, and with expressions able to reference pattern bindings from previous expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
fn main() {\
    let outer_opt = Some(Some(1i32));\
\
    while let Some(inner_opt) = outer_opt\
        && let Some(number) = inner_opt\
        && number == 1\
    {\
        println!("Peek a boo");\
        break;\
    }\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.for\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.for "expr.loop.for")\
\
## [Iterator loops](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#iterator-loops)\
\
[\[expr.loop.for.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.for.syntax "expr.loop.for.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[IteratorLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IteratorLoopExpression) →\
\
for[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern)in[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)except [StructExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExpression)[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[IteratorLoopExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IteratorLoopExpression)\
for[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
in\
except StructExpression [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression)\
\
[\[expr.loop.for.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.for.intro "expr.loop.for.intro")\
\
A `for` expression is a syntactic construct for looping over elements provided by an implementation of `std::iter::IntoIterator`.\
\
[\[expr.loop.for.condition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.for.condition "expr.loop.for.condition")\
\
If the iterator yields a value, that value is matched against the irrefutable pattern, the body of the loop is executed, and then control returns to the head of the `for` loop. If the iterator is empty, the `for` expression completes.\
\
An example of a `for` loop over the contents of an array:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let v = &["apples", "cake", "coffee"];\
\
for text in v {\
    println!("I like {}.", text);\
}\
}\
```\
\
An example of a for loop over a series of integers:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut sum = 0;\
for n in 1..11 {\
    sum += n;\
}\
assert_eq!(sum, 55);\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.for.desugar\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.for.desugar "expr.loop.for.desugar")\
\
