A `for` loop is equivalent to a `loop` expression containing a [`match` expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions) as follows:\
\
```rust\
\
'label: for PATTERN in iter_expr {\
    /* loop body */\
}\
```\
\
is equivalent to\
\
```rust\
\
{\
    let result = match IntoIterator::into_iter(iter_expr) {\
        mut iter => 'label: loop {\
            let mut next;\
            match Iterator::next(&mut iter) {\
                Option::Some(val) => next = val,\
                Option::None => break,\
            };\
            let PATTERN = next;\
            let () = { /* loop body */ };\
        },\
    };\
    result\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.for.lang-items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.for.lang-items "expr.loop.for.lang-items")\
\
`IntoIterator`, `Iterator`, and `Option` are always the standard library items here, not whatever those names resolve to in the current scope.\
\
The variable names `next`, `iter`, and `val` are for exposition only, they do not actually have names the user can type.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The outer `match` is used to ensure that any [temporary values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#temporaries) in `iter_expr` don’t get dropped before the loop is finished. `next` is declared before being assigned because it results in types being inferred correctly more often.\
\
[\[expr.loop.label\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.label "expr.loop.label")\
\
## [Loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#loop-labels)\
\
[\[expr.loop.label.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.label.syntax "expr.loop.label.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[LoopLabel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LoopLabel) → [LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LIFETIME_OR_LABEL):\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[LoopLabel](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LoopLabel)[LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LIFETIME_OR_LABEL)\
:\
\
[\[expr.loop.label.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.label.intro "expr.loop.label.intro")\
\
A loop expression may optionally have a _label_. The label is written as a lifetime preceding the loop expression, as in `'foo: loop { break 'foo; }`, `'bar: while false {}`, `'humbug: for _ in 0..0 {}`.\
\
[\[expr.loop.label.control-flow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.label.control-flow "expr.loop.label.control-flow")\
\
If a label is present, then labeled `break` and `continue` expressions nested within this loop may exit out of this loop or return control to its head. See [break expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#break-expressions) and [continue expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#continue-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.loop.label.ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.label.ref "expr.loop.label.ref")\
\
Labels follow the hygiene and shadowing rules of local variables. For example, this code will print “outer loop”:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
'a: loop {\
    'a: loop {\
        break 'a;\
    }\
    print!("outer loop");\
    break 'a;\
}\
}\
```\
\
`'_` is not a valid loop label.\
\
[\[expr.loop.break\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break "expr.loop.break")\
\
## [`break` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#break-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.loop.break.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break.syntax "expr.loop.break.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[BreakExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BreakExpression) → break[LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LIFETIME_OR_LABEL)?[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[BreakExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BreakExpression)\
break[LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LIFETIME_OR_LABEL)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[expr.loop.break.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break.intro "expr.loop.break.intro")\
\
When `break` is encountered, execution of the associated loop body is immediately terminated, for example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut last = 0;\
for x in 1..100 {\
    if x > 12 {\
        break;\
    }\
    last = x;\
}\
assert_eq!(last, 12);\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.break.diverging\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break.diverging "expr.loop.break.diverging")\
\
A `break` expression is [diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence) and has a type of [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never).\
\
[\[expr.loop.break.label\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break.label "expr.loop.break.label")\
\
A `break` expression is normally associated with the innermost `loop`, `for` or `while` loop enclosing the `break` expression, but a [label](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels) can be used to specify which enclosing loop is affected. Example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
'outer: loop {\
    while true {\
        break 'outer;\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.break.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break.value "expr.loop.break.value")\
\
A `break` expression is only permitted in the body of a loop, and has one of the forms `break`, `break 'label` or ( [see below](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#break-and-loop-values)) `break EXPR` or `break 'label EXPR`.\
\
[\[expr.loop.break-value.implicit-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-value.implicit-value "expr.loop.break-value.implicit-value")\
\
In a [`loop` with break expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-value) or a [labeled block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels), a `break` without an expression is equivalent to `break ()`.\
\
[\[expr.loop.block-labels\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels "expr.loop.block-labels")\
\
## [Labeled block expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#labeled-block-expressions-1)\
\
[\[expr.loop.block-labels.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels.syntax "expr.loop.block-labels.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[LabelBlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LabelBlockExpression) → [BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[LabelBlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LabelBlockExpression)[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression)\
\
[\[expr.loop.block-labels.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels.intro "expr.loop.block-labels.intro")\
\
Labeled block expressions are exactly like block expressions, except that they allow using `break` expressions within the block.\
\
[\[expr.loop.block-labels.break\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels.break "expr.loop.block-labels.break")\
\
Unlike loops, `break` expressions within a labeled block expression _must_ have a label (i.e. the label is not optional).\
\
[\[expr.loop.block-labels.label-required\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels.label-required "expr.loop.block-labels.label-required")\
\
Similarly, labeled block expressions _must_ begin with a label.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn do_thing() {}\
fn condition_not_met() -> bool { true }\
fn do_next_thing() {}\
fn do_last_thing() {}\
let result = 'block: {\
    do_thing();\
    if condition_not_met() {\
        break 'block 1;\
    }\
    do_next_thing();\
    if condition_not_met() {\
        break 'block 2;\
    }\
    do_last_thing();\
    3\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.block-labels.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels.type "expr.loop.block-labels.type")\
\
The type of a labeled block expression is the [least upper bound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound) of all of the break operands and the final operand. If the final operand is omitted, the type of the final operand defaults to the [unit type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.unit), unless the block [diverges](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.diverging), in which case it is the [never type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never).\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn example(condition: bool) {\
>     let s = String::from("owned");\
>\
>     let _: &str = 'block: {\
>         if condition {\
>             break 'block &s;  // &String coerced to &str via Deref\
>         }\
>         break 'block "literal";  // &'static str coerced to &str\
>     };\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue "expr.loop.continue")\
\
## [`continue` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#continue-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue.syntax "expr.loop.continue.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ContinueExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ContinueExpression) → continue[LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LIFETIME_OR_LABEL)?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ContinueExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ContinueExpression)\
continue[LIFETIME\_OR\_LABEL](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LIFETIME_OR_LABEL)\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue.intro "expr.loop.continue.intro")\
\
When `continue` is encountered, the current iteration of the associated loop body is immediately terminated, returning control to the loop _head_.\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue.diverging\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue.diverging "expr.loop.continue.diverging")\
\
A `continue` expression is [diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence) and has a type of [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never).\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue.while\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue.while "expr.loop.continue.while")\
\
In the case of a `while` loop, the head is the conditional operands controlling the loop.\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue.for\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue.for "expr.loop.continue.for")\
\
In the case of a `for` loop, the head is the call-expression controlling the loop.\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue.label\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue.label "expr.loop.continue.label")\
\
Like `break`, `continue` is normally associated with the innermost enclosing loop, but `continue 'label` may be used to specify the loop affected.\
\
[\[expr.loop.continue.in-loop-only\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue.in-loop-only "expr.loop.continue.in-loop-only")\
\
A `continue` expression is only permitted in the body of a loop.\
\
[\[expr.loop.break-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-value "expr.loop.break-value")\
\
## [`break` and loop values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#break-and-loop-values)\
\
[\[expr.loop.break-value.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-value.intro "expr.loop.break-value.intro")\
\
When associated with a `loop`, a break expression may be used to return a value from that loop, via one of the forms `break EXPR` or `break 'label EXPR`, where `EXPR` is an expression whose result is returned from the `loop`. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let (mut a, mut b) = (1, 1);\
let result = loop {\
    if b > 10 {\
        break b;\
    }\
    let c = a + b;\
    a = b;\
    b = c;\
};\
// first number in Fibonacci sequence over 10:\
assert_eq!(result, 13);\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.loop.break-value.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-value.type "expr.loop.break-value.type")\
\
The type of a `loop` with associated `break` expressions is the [least upper bound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound) of all of the break operands.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn example(condition: bool) {\
>     let s = String::from("owned");\
>\
>     let _: &str = loop {\
>         if condition {\
>             break &s; // &String coerced to &str via Deref\
>         }\
>         break "literal"; // &'static str coerced to &str\
>     };\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.loop.break-value.diverging\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-value.diverging "expr.loop.break-value.diverging")\
\
A `loop` with associated `break` expressions does not [diverge](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence) if any of the break operands do not diverge. If all of the `break` operands diverge, then the `loop` expression also diverges.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn diverging_loop_with_break(condition: bool) -> ! {\
>     // This loop is diverging because all `break` operands are diverging.\
>     loop {\
>         if condition {\
>             break loop {};\
>         } else {\
>             break panic!();\
>         }\
>     }\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn loop_with_non_diverging_break(condition: bool) -> ! {\
>     // The type of this loop is i32 even though one of the breaks is\
>     // diverging.\
>     loop {\
>         if condition {\
>             break loop {};\
>         } else {\
>             break 123i32;\
>         }\
>     } // ERROR: expected `!`, found `i32`\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.range\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.range "expr.range")\
\
# [Range expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#range-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.range.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.range.syntax "expr.range.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[RangeExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeExpression) →\
\
[RangeExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeExpr)\
\
    \| [RangeFromExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFromExpr)\
\
    \| [RangeToExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeToExpr)\
\
    \| [RangeFullExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFullExpr)\
\
    \| [RangeInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeInclusiveExpr)\
\
    \| [RangeToInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeToInclusiveExpr)\
\
[RangeExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeExpr) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)..[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
[RangeFromExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeFromExpr) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)..\
\
[RangeToExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeToExpr) → ..[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
[RangeFullExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeFullExpr) → ..\
\
[RangeInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeInclusiveExpr) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)..=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
[RangeToInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeToInclusiveExpr) → ..=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[RangeExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeExpression)[RangeExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeExpr)[RangeFromExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeFromExpr)[RangeToExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeToExpr)[RangeFullExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeFullExpr)[RangeInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeInclusiveExpr)[RangeToInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeToInclusiveExpr)\
\
[RangeExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeExpr)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
..[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[RangeFromExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFromExpr)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
..\
\
[RangeToExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeToExpr)\
..[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[RangeFullExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFullExpr)\
..\
\
[RangeInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeInclusiveExpr)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
..=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[RangeToInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeToInclusiveExpr)\
..=[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[expr.range.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.range.behavior "expr.range.behavior")\
\
The `..` and `..=` operators will construct an object of one of the `std::ops::Range` (or `core::ops::Range`) variants, according to the following table:\
\
| Production | Syntax | Type | Range |\
| --- | --- | --- | --- |\
| [RangeExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeExpr) | start`..`end | [std::ops::Range](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/range/struct.Range.html) | start ≤ x < end |\
| [RangeFromExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFromExpr) | start`..` | [std::ops::RangeFrom](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/range/struct.RangeFrom.html) | start ≤ x |\
| [RangeToExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeToExpr) | `..`end | [std::ops::RangeTo](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/range/struct.RangeTo.html) | x < end |\
| [RangeFullExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFullExpr) | `..` | [std::ops::RangeFull](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/range/struct.RangeFull.html) | - |\
| [RangeInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeInclusiveExpr) | start`..=`end | [std::ops::RangeInclusive](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/range/struct.RangeInclusive.html) | start ≤ x ≤ end |\
| [RangeToInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeToInclusiveExpr) | `..=`end | [std::ops::RangeToInclusive](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/range/struct.RangeToInclusive.html) | x ≤ end |\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
1..2;   // std::ops::Range\
3..;    // std::ops::RangeFrom\
..4;    // std::ops::RangeTo\
..;     // std::ops::RangeFull\
5..=6;  // std::ops::RangeInclusive\
..=7;   // std::ops::RangeToInclusive\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.range.equivalence\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.range.equivalence "expr.range.equivalence")\
\
The following expressions are equivalent.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = std::ops::Range {start: 0, end: 10};\
let y = 0..10;\
\
assert_eq!(x, y);\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.range.for\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.range.for "expr.range.for")\
\
Ranges can be used in `for` loops:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
for i in 1..11 {\
    println!("{}", i);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.if\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if "expr.if")\
\
# [`if` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#if-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.if.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.syntax "expr.if.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[IfExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IfExpression) →\
\
if[Conditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Conditions)[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
\
    ( else ( [BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression) \| [IfExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IfExpression) ) )?\
\
[Conditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Conditions) →\
\
[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)except [StructExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExpression)\
\
    \| [LetChain](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LetChain)\
\

\
[LetChainCondition](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LetChainCondition) →\
\
[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)except [ExcludedConditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExcludedConditions)\
\
    \| [OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\*let[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern)=[Scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Scrutinee)except [ExcludedConditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExcludedConditions)\
\
[ExcludedConditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ExcludedConditions) →\
\
[StructExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructExpression)\
\

\

\
    \| [RangeFromExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFromExpr)\
\
    \| [RangeInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeInclusiveExpr)\
\
    \| [AssignmentExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AssignmentExpression)\
\

\

except ExcludedConditions [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)\
let[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
=\
except ExcludedConditions [Scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Scrutinee)\
\
[ExcludedConditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ExcludedConditions)[StructExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructExpression)[LazyBooleanExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LazyBooleanExpression)[RangeExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeExpr)[RangeFromExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeFromExpr)[RangeInclusiveExpr](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeInclusiveExpr)[AssignmentExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AssignmentExpression)[CompoundAssignmentExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-CompoundAssignmentExpression)\
\
[\[expr.if.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.intro "expr.if.intro")\
\
The syntax of an `if` expression is a sequence of one or more condition operands separated by `&&`, followed by a consequent block, any number of `else if` conditions and blocks, and an optional trailing `else` block.\
\
[\[expr.if.condition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.condition "expr.if.condition")\
\
Condition operands must be either an [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) with a [boolean type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type) or a conditional `let` match.\
\
[\[expr.if.condition-true\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.condition-true "expr.if.condition-true")\
\
If all of the condition operands evaluate to `true` and all of the `let` patterns successfully match their [scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scrutinee) s, the consequent block is executed and any subsequent `else if` or `else` block is skipped.\
\
[\[expr.if.else-if\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.else-if "expr.if.else-if")\
\
If any condition operand evaluates to `false` or any `let` pattern does not match its scrutinee, the consequent block is skipped and any subsequent `else if` condition is evaluated.\
\
[\[expr.if.else\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.else "expr.if.else")\
\
If all `if` and `else if` conditions evaluate to `false` then any `else` block is executed.\
\
[\[expr.if.result\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.result "expr.if.result")\
\
An `if` expression evaluates to the same value as the executed block, or `()` if no block is evaluated.\
\
[\[expr.if.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.type "expr.if.type")\
\
An `if` expression must have the same type in all situations.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = 3;\
if x == 4 {\
    println!("x is four");\
} else if x == 3 {\
    println!("x is three");\
} else {\
    println!("x is something else");\
}\
\
// `if` can be used as an expression.\
let y = if 12 * 15 > 150 {\
    "Bigger"\
} else {\
    "Smaller"\
};\
assert_eq!(y, "Bigger");\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.if.diverging\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.diverging "expr.if.diverging")\
\
An `if` expression [diverges](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence) if either the condition expression diverges or if all arms diverge.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn diverging_condition() -> ! {\
    // Diverges because the condition expression diverges\
    if loop {} {\
        ()\
    } else {\
        ()\
    };\
    // The semicolon above is important: The type of the `if` expression is\
    // `()`, despite being diverging. When the final body expression is\
    // elided, the type of the body is inferred to ! because the function body\
    // diverges. Without the semicolon, the `if` would be the tail expression\
    // with type `()`, which would fail to match the return type `!`.\
}\
\
fn diverging_arms() -> ! {\
    // Diverges because all arms diverge\
    if true {\
        loop {}\
    } else {\
        loop {}\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.if.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.let "expr.if.let")\
\
## [`if let` patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#if-let-patterns)\
\
[\[expr.if.let.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.let.intro "expr.if.let.intro")\
\
`let` patterns in an `if` condition allow binding new variables into scope when the pattern matches successfully.\
\
The following examples illustrate bindings using `let` patterns:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let dish = ("Ham", "Eggs");\
\
// This body will be skipped because the pattern is refuted.\
if let ("Bacon", b) = dish {\
    println!("Bacon is served with {}", b);\
} else {\
    // This block is evaluated instead.\
    println!("No bacon will be served");\
}\
\
// This body will execute.\
if let ("Ham", b) = dish {\
    println!("Ham is served with {}", b);\
}\
\
if let _ = 5 {\
    println!("Irrefutable patterns are always true");\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.if.let.or-pattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.let.or-pattern "expr.if.let.or-pattern")\
\
Multiple patterns may be specified with the `|` operator. This has the same semantics as with `|` in [`match` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions):\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum E {\
    X(u8),\
    Y(u8),\
    Z(u8),\
}\
let v = E::Y(12);\
if let E::X(n) | E::Y(n) = v {\
    assert_eq!(n, 12);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.if.chains\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.chains "expr.if.chains")\
\
## [Chains of conditions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#chains-of-conditions)\
\
[\[expr.if.chains.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.chains.intro "expr.if.chains.intro")\
\
Multiple condition operands can be separated with `&&`.\
\
[\[expr.if.chains.order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.chains.order "expr.if.chains.order")\
\
Similar to a `&&` [LazyBooleanExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LazyBooleanExpression), each operand is evaluated from left-to-right until an operand evaluates as `false` or a `let` match fails, in which case the subsequent operands are not evaluated.\
\
[\[expr.if.chains.bindings\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.chains.bindings "expr.if.chains.bindings")\
\
The bindings of each pattern are put into scope to be available for the next condition operand and the consequent block.\
\
The following is an example of chaining multiple expressions, mixing `let` bindings and boolean expressions, and with expressions able to reference pattern bindings from previous expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn single() {\
    let outer_opt = Some(Some(1i32));\
\
    if let Some(inner_opt) = outer_opt\
        && let Some(number) = inner_opt\
        && number == 1\
    {\
        println!("Peek a boo");\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
The above is equivalent to the following without using chains of conditions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn nested() {\
    let outer_opt = Some(Some(1i32));\
\
    if let Some(inner_opt) = outer_opt {\
        if let Some(number) = inner_opt {\
            if number == 1 {\
                println!("Peek a boo");\
            }\
        }\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.if.chains.or\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.chains.or "expr.if.chains.or")\
\
If any condition operand is a `let` pattern, then none of the condition operands can be a `||` [lazy boolean operator expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.bool-logic) due to ambiguity and precedence with the `let` scrutinee. If a `||` expression is needed, then parentheses can be used. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let foo = Some(123);\
let condition1 = true;\
let condition2 = false;\
// Parentheses are required here.\
if let Some(x) = foo && (condition1 || condition2) { /*...*/ }\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.if.edition2024\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.edition2024 "expr.if.edition2024")\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2024 edition, let chains are not supported. That is, the [LetChain](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LetChain) grammar is not allowed in an `if` expression.\
\
[\[expr.match\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match "expr.match")\
\

\
