\
[\[expr.match.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.intro "expr.match.intro")\
\
A _`match` expression_ branches on a pattern. The exact form of matching that occurs depends on the [pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns).\
\
[\[expr.match.scrutinee\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.scrutinee "expr.match.scrutinee")\
\
A `match` expression has a _[scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scrutinee) expression_, which is the value to compare to the patterns.\
\
[\[expr.match.scrutinee-constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.scrutinee-constraint "expr.match.scrutinee-constraint")\
\
The scrutinee expression and the patterns must have the same type.\
\
[\[expr.match.scrutinee-behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.scrutinee-behavior "expr.match.scrutinee-behavior")\
\
A `match` behaves differently depending on whether or not the scrutinee expression is a [place expression or value expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.match.scrutinee-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.scrutinee-value "expr.match.scrutinee-value")\
\
If the scrutinee expression is a [value expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions), it is first evaluated into a temporary location, and the resulting value is sequentially compared to the patterns in the arms until a match is found. The first arm with a matching pattern is chosen as the branch target of the `match`, any variables bound by the pattern are assigned to local variables in the arm’s block, and control enters the block.\
\
[\[expr.match.scrutinee-place\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.scrutinee-place "expr.match.scrutinee-place")\
\
When the scrutinee expression is a [place expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions), the match does not allocate a temporary location; however, a by-value binding may copy or move from the memory location. When possible, it is preferable to match on place expressions, as the lifetime of these matches inherits the lifetime of the place expression rather than being restricted to the inside of the match.\
\
An example of a `match` expression:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = 1;\
\
match x {\
    1 => println!("one"),\
    2 => println!("two"),\
    3 => println!("three"),\
    4 => println!("four"),\
    5 => println!("five"),\
    _ => println!("something else"),\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.match.pattern-vars\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.pattern-vars "expr.match.pattern-vars")\
\
Variables bound within the pattern are scoped to the match guard and the arm’s expression.\
\
[\[expr.match.pattern-var-binding\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.pattern-var-binding "expr.match.pattern-var-binding")\
\
The [binding mode](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#binding-modes) (move, copy, or reference) depends on the pattern.\
\
[\[expr.match.or-pattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.or-pattern "expr.match.or-pattern")\
\
Multiple match patterns may be joined with the `|` operator. Each pattern will be tested in left-to-right sequence until a successful match is found.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = 9;\
let message = match x {\
    0 | 1  => "not many",\
    2 ..= 9 => "a few",\
    _      => "lots"\
};\
\
assert_eq!(message, "a few");\
\
// Demonstration of pattern match order.\
struct S(i32, i32);\
\
match S(1, 2) {\
    S(z @ 1, _) | S(_, z @ 2) => assert_eq!(z, 1),\
    _ => panic!(),\
}\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> The `2..=9` is a [Range Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#range-patterns), not a [Range Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#range-expressions). Thus, only those types of ranges supported by range patterns can be used in match arms.\
\
[\[expr.match.or-patterns-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.or-patterns-restriction "expr.match.or-patterns-restriction")\
\
Every binding in each `|` separated pattern must appear in all of the patterns in the arm.\
\
[\[expr.match.binding-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.binding-restriction "expr.match.binding-restriction")\
\
Every binding of the same name must have the same type, and have the same binding mode.\
\
[\[expr.match.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.type "expr.match.type")\
\
The type of the overall `match` expression is the [least upper bound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound) of the individual match arms.\
\
[\[expr.match.empty\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.empty "expr.match.empty")\
\
If there are no match arms, then the `match` expression is [diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence) and the type is [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never).\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn make<T>() -> T { loop {} }\
> enum Empty {}\
>\
> fn diverging_match_no_arms() -> ! {\
>     let e: Empty = make();\
>     match e {}\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.match.diverging\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.diverging "expr.match.diverging")\
\
If either the scrutinee expression or all of the match arms diverge, then the entire `match` expression also diverges.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard "expr.match.guard")\
\
## [Match guards](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#match-guards)\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.intro "expr.match.guard.intro")\
\
Match arms can accept _match guards_ to further refine the criteria for matching a case.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.condition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.condition "expr.match.guard.condition")\
\
Pattern guards appear after the pattern following the `if` keyword and consist of an [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression) with a [boolean type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool) or a conditional `let` match.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.behavior "expr.match.guard.behavior")\
\
When the pattern matches successfully, the pattern guard is executed. If all of the guard condition operands evaluate to `true` and all of the `let` patterns successfully match their [scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scrutinee) s, the match arm is successfully matched against and the arm body is executed.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.next\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.next "expr.match.guard.next")\
\
Otherwise, the next pattern, including other matches with the `|` operator in the same arm, is tested.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let maybe_digit = Some(0);\
fn process_digit(i: i32) { }\
fn process_other(i: i32) { }\
let message = match maybe_digit {\
    Some(x) if x < 10 => process_digit(x),\
    Some(x) => process_other(x),\
    None => panic!(),\
};\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Multiple matches using the `|` operator can cause the pattern guard and the side effects it has to execute multiple times. For example:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> use std::cell::Cell;\
> let i : Cell<i32> = Cell::new(0);\
> match 1 {\
>     1 | _ if { i.set(i.get() + 1); false } => {}\
>     _ => {}\
> }\
> assert_eq!(i.get(), 2);\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.bound-variables\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.bound-variables "expr.match.guard.bound-variables")\
\
A pattern guard may refer to the variables bound within the pattern they follow.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.shared-ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.shared-ref "expr.match.guard.shared-ref")\
\
Before evaluating the guard, a shared reference is taken to the part of the scrutinee the variable matches on. While evaluating the guard, this shared reference is then used when accessing the variable.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.value "expr.match.guard.value")\
\
Only when the guard evaluates successfully is the value moved, or copied, from the scrutinee into the variable. This allows shared borrows to be used inside guards without moving out of the scrutinee in case guard fails to match.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.no-mutation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.no-mutation "expr.match.guard.no-mutation")\
\
Moreover, by holding a shared reference while evaluating the guard, mutation inside guards is also prevented.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.let "expr.match.guard.let")\
\
Guards can use `let` patterns to conditionally match a scrutinee and to bind new variables into scope when the pattern matches successfully.\
\
> Example\
>\
> In this example, the guard condition `let Some(first_char) = name.chars().next()` is evaluated. If the `let` pattern successfully matches (i.e. the string has at least one character), the arm’s body is executed. Otherwise, pattern matching continues to the next arm.\
>\
> The `let` pattern creates a new binding (`first_char`), which can be used alongside the original pattern bindings (`name`) in the arm’s body.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> enum Command {\
>     Run(String),\
>     Stop,\
> }\
> let cmd = Command::Run("example".to_string());\
>\
> match cmd {\
>     Command::Run(name) if let Some(first_char) = name.chars().next() => {\
>         // Both `name` and `first_char` are available here\
>         println!("Running: {name} (starts with '{first_char}')");\
>     }\
>     Command::Run(name) => {\
>         println!("{name} is empty");\
>     }\
>     _ => {}\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.chains\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.chains "expr.match.guard.chains")\
\
## [Match guard chains](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#match-guard-chains)\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.chains.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.chains.intro "expr.match.guard.chains.intro")\
\
Multiple guard condition operands can be separated with `&&`.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> let foo = Some([123]);\
> let already_checked = false;\
> match foo {\
>     Some(xs) if let [single] = xs && !already_checked => { dbg!(single); }\
>     _ => {}\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.chains.order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.chains.order "expr.match.guard.chains.order")\
\
Similar to a `&&` [LazyBooleanExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LazyBooleanExpression), each operand is evaluated from left-to-right until an operand evaluates as `false` or a `let` match fails, in which case the subsequent operands are not evaluated.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.chains.bindings\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.chains.bindings "expr.match.guard.chains.bindings")\
\
The bindings of each `let` pattern are put into scope to be available for the next condition operand and the match arm body.\
\
[\[expr.match.guard.chains.or\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.chains.or "expr.match.guard.chains.or")\
\
If any guard condition operand is a `let` pattern, then none of the condition operands can be a `||` [lazy boolean operator expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.bool-logic) due to ambiguity and precedence with the `let` scrutinee.\
\
> Example\
>\
> If a `||` expression is needed, then parentheses can be used. For example:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> let foo = Some([123]);\
> match foo {\
>     // Parentheses are required here.\
>     Some(xs) if let [x] = xs && (x < -100 || x > 20) => {}\
>     _ => {}\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[expr.match.attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.attributes "expr.match.attributes")\
\
## [Attributes on match arms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-on-match-arms)\
\
[\[expr.match.attributes.outer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.attributes.outer "expr.match.attributes.outer")\
\
Outer attributes are allowed on match arms. The only attributes that have meaning on match arms are [`cfg`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#conditional-compilation) and the [lint check attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes).\
\
[\[expr.match.attributes.inner\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.attributes.inner "expr.match.attributes.inner")\
\
[Inner attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) are allowed directly after the opening brace of the match expression in the same expression contexts as [attributes on block expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-on-block-expressions).\
\
[\[expr.return\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.return "expr.return")\
\
# [`return` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#return-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.return.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.return.syntax "expr.return.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ReturnExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ReturnExpression) → return[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ReturnExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ReturnExpression)\
return[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\
[\[expr.return.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.return.intro "expr.return.intro")\
\
Return expressions are denoted with the keyword `return`.\
\
[\[expr.return.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.return.behavior "expr.return.behavior")\
\
Evaluating a `return` expression moves its argument into the designated output location for the current function call, destroys the current function activation frame, and transfers control to the caller frame.\
\
[\[expr.return.diverging\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.return.diverging "expr.return.diverging")\
\
A `return` expression is [diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence) and has a type of [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never).\
\
An example of a `return` expression:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn max(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {\
    if a > b {\
        return a;\
    }\
    return b;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[expr.await\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await "expr.await")\
\
# [Await expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#await-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.await.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await.syntax "expr.await.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[AwaitExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-AwaitExpression) → [Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression).await\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[AwaitExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-AwaitExpression)[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
.\
await\
\
[\[expr.await.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await.intro "expr.await.intro")\
\
An `await` expression is a syntactic construct for suspending a computation provided by an implementation of `std::future::IntoFuture` until the given future is ready to produce a value.\
\
[\[expr.await.construct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await.construct "expr.await.construct")\
\
The syntax for an await expression is an expression with a type that implements the [`IntoFuture`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/future/into_future/trait.IntoFuture.html) trait, called the _future operand_, then the token `.`, and then the `await` keyword.\
\
[\[expr.await.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await.allowed-positions "expr.await.allowed-positions")\
\
Await expressions are legal only within an [async context](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-context), like an [`async fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-functions), [`async` closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-closures), or [`async` block](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-blocks).\
\
[\[expr.await.effects\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await.effects "expr.await.effects")\
\
More specifically, an await expression has the following effect.\
\
1. Create a future by calling [`IntoFuture::into_future`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/future/into_future/trait.IntoFuture.html#tymethod.into_future) on the future operand.\
2. Evaluate the future to a [future](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/future/future/trait.Future.html)`tmp`;\
3. Pin `tmp` using [`Pin::new_unchecked`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/pin/struct.Pin.html#method.new_unchecked);\
4. This pinned future is then polled by calling the [`Future::poll`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/future/future/trait.Future.html#tymethod.poll) method and passing it the current [task context](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#task-context);\
5. If the call to `poll` returns [`Poll::Pending`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/task/poll/enum.Poll.html#variant.Pending), then the future returns `Poll::Pending`, suspending its state so that, when the surrounding async context is re-polled, execution returns to step 3;\
6. Otherwise the call to `poll` must have returned [`Poll::Ready`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/task/poll/enum.Poll.html#variant.Ready), in which case the value contained in the [`Poll::Ready`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/task/poll/enum.Poll.html#variant.Ready) variant is used as the result of the `await` expression itself.\
\
[\[expr.await.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await.edition2018 "expr.await.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> Await expressions are only available beginning with Rust 2018.\
\
[\[expr.await.task\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await.task "expr.await.task")\
\
## [Task context](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#task-context)\
\
The task context refers to the [`Context`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/task/wake/struct.Context.html) which was supplied to the current [async context](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-context) when the async context itself was polled. Because `await` expressions are only legal in an async context, there must be some task context available.\
\
[\[expr.await.desugar\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.await.desugar "expr.await.desugar")\
\
## [Approximate desugaring](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#approximate-desugaring)\
\
Effectively, an await expression is roughly equivalent to the following non-normative desugaring:\
\
```rust\
\
match operand.into_future() {\
    mut pinned => loop {\
        let mut pin = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&mut pinned) };\
        match Pin::future::poll(Pin::borrow(&mut pin), &mut current_context) {\
            Poll::Ready(r) => break r,\
            Poll::Pending => yield Poll::Pending,\
        }\
    }\
}\
```\
\
where the `yield` pseudo-code returns `Poll::Pending` and, when re-invoked, resumes execution from that point. The variable `current_context` refers to the context taken from the async environment.\
\
[\[expr.placeholder\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.placeholder "expr.placeholder")\
\
# [`_` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#_-expressions)\
\
[\[expr.placeholder.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.placeholder.syntax "expr.placeholder.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[UnderscoreExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-UnderscoreExpression) → \_\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[UnderscoreExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UnderscoreExpression)\
\_\
\
[\[expr.placeholder.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.placeholder.intro "expr.placeholder.intro")\
\
Underscore expressions, denoted with the symbol `_`, are used to signify a placeholder in a destructuring assignment.\
\
[\[expr.placeholder.lhs-assignment-only\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.placeholder.lhs-assignment-only "expr.placeholder.lhs-assignment-only")\
\
They may only appear in the left-hand side of an assignment.\
\
[\[expr.placeholder.pattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.placeholder.pattern "expr.placeholder.pattern")\
\
Note that this is distinct from the [wildcard pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#wildcard-pattern).\
\
Examples of `_` expressions:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let p = (1, 2);\
let mut a = 0;\
(_, a) = p;\
\
struct Position {\
    x: u32,\
    y: u32,\
}\
\
Position { x: a, y: _ } = Position{ x: 2, y: 3 };\
\
// unused result, assignment to `_` used to declare intent and remove a warning\
_ = 2 + 2;\
// triggers unused_must_use warning\
// 2 + 2;\
\
// equivalent technique using a wildcard pattern in a let-binding\
let _ = 2 + 2;\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns "patterns")\
\
# [Patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.syntax "patterns.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern) → \|?[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternNoTopAlt) ( \|[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternNoTopAlt) )\*\
\
[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PatternNoTopAlt) →\
\
[PatternWithoutRange](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternWithoutRange)\
\
    \| [RangePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePattern)\
\
[PatternWithoutRange](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PatternWithoutRange) →\
\
[LiteralPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LiteralPattern)\
\
    \| [IdentifierPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IdentifierPattern)\
\
    \| [WildcardPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WildcardPattern)\
\
    \| [RestPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RestPattern)\
\
    \| [ReferencePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ReferencePattern)\
\
    \| [StructPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructPattern)\
\
    \| [TupleStructPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleStructPattern)\
\
    \| [TuplePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TuplePattern)\
\
    \| [GroupedPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GroupedPattern)\
\
    \| [SlicePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SlicePattern)\
\
    \| [PathPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathPattern)\
\
    \| [MacroInvocation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MacroInvocation)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern)\
\|[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PatternNoTopAlt)\
\|[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PatternNoTopAlt)\
\
[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternNoTopAlt)[PatternWithoutRange](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PatternWithoutRange)[RangePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangePattern)\
\
[PatternWithoutRange](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternWithoutRange)[LiteralPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LiteralPattern)[IdentifierPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IdentifierPattern)[WildcardPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WildcardPattern)[RestPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RestPattern)[ReferencePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ReferencePattern)[StructPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-StructPattern)[TupleStructPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleStructPattern)[TuplePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TuplePattern)[GroupedPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GroupedPattern)[SlicePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SlicePattern)[PathPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathPattern)[MacroInvocation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MacroInvocation)\
\
[\[patterns.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.intro "patterns.intro")\
\
Patterns are used to match values against structures and to, optionally, bind variables to values inside these structures. They are also used in variable declarations and parameters for functions and closures.\
\
The pattern in the following example does four things:\
\
- Tests if `person` has the `car` field filled with something.\
- Tests if the person’s `age` field is between 13 and 19, and binds its value to the `person_age` variable.\
- Binds a reference to the `name` field to the variable `person_name`.\
- Ignores the rest of the fields of `person`. The remaining fields can have any value and are not bound to any variables.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Car;\
struct Computer;\
struct Person {\
    name: String,\
    car: Option<Car>,\
    computer: Option<Computer>,\
    age: u8,\
}\
let person = Person {\
    name: String::from("John"),\
    car: Some(Car),\
    computer: None,\
    age: 15,\
};\
if let\
    Person {\
        car: Some(_),\
        age: person_age @ 13..=19,\
        name: ref person_name,\
        ..\
    } = person\
{\
    println!("{} has a car and is {} years old.", person_name, person_age);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.usage\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.usage "patterns.usage")\
\
Patterns are used in:\
\
[\[patterns.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.let "patterns.let")\
\
- [`let` declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#let-statements)\
\
[\[patterns.param\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.param "patterns.param")\
\
- [Function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions) and [closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-expressions) parameters\
\
[\[patterns.match\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.match "patterns.match")\
\
- [`match` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions)\
\
[\[patterns.if-let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.if-let "patterns.if-let")\
\
- [`if let` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-expressions)\
\
[\[patterns.while-let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.while-let "patterns.while-let")\
\
- [`while let` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#while-let-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.for\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.for "patterns.for")\
\
- [`for` expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#iterator-loops)\
\
[\[patterns.destructure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.destructure "patterns.destructure")\
\
## [Destructuring](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#destructuring)\
\
[\[patterns.destructure.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.destructure.intro "patterns.destructure.intro")\
\
Patterns can be used to _destructure_ [structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), [enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations), and [tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types). Destructuring breaks up a value into its component pieces. The syntax used is almost the same as when creating such values.\
\
[\[patterns.destructure.wildcard\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.destructure.wildcard "patterns.destructure.wildcard")\
\
In a pattern whose [scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scrutinee) expression has a `struct`, `enum` or `tuple` type, a [wildcard pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#wildcard-pattern) (`_`) stands in for a _single_ data field, whereas an [et cetera](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructPatternEtCetera) or [rest pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.rest) (`..`) stands in for _all_ the remaining fields of a particular variant.\
\
[\[patterns.destructure.named-field-shorthand\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.destructure.named-field-shorthand "patterns.destructure.named-field-shorthand")\
\
When destructuring a data structure with named (but not numbered) fields, it is allowed to write `fieldname` as a shorthand for `fieldname: fieldname`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Message {\
    Quit,\
    WriteString(String),\
    Move { x: i32, y: i32 },\
    ChangeColor(u8, u8, u8),\
}\
let message = Message::Quit;\
match message {\
    Message::Quit => println!("Quit"),\
    Message::WriteString(write) => println!("{}", &write),\
    Message::Move{ x, y: 0 } => println!("move {} horizontally", x),\
    Message::Move{ .. } => println!("other move"),\
    Message::ChangeColor { 0: red, 1: green, 2: _ } => {\
        println!("color change, red: {}, green: {}", red, green);\
    }\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.refutable "patterns.refutable")\
\
## [Refutability](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#refutability)\
\
A pattern is said to be _refutable_ when it has the possibility of not being matched by the value it is being matched against. _Irrefutable_ patterns, on the other hand, always match the value they are being matched against. Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let (x, y) = (1, 2);               // "(x, y)" is an irrefutable pattern\
\
if let (a, 3) = (1, 2) {           // "(a, 3)" is refutable, and will not match\
    panic!("Shouldn't reach here");\
} else if let (a, 4) = (3, 4) {    // "(a, 4)" is refutable, and will match\
    println!("Matched ({}, 4)", a);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.literal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.literal "patterns.literal")\
\
## [Literal patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#literal-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.literal.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.literal.syntax "patterns.literal.syntax")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/binop/binary-minus-without-space.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/binop/binary-minus-without-space.rs)\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[LiteralPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LiteralPattern) → -?[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LiteralExpression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[LiteralPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LiteralPattern)\
-[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LiteralExpression)\
\
[\[patterns.literal.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.literal.intro "patterns.literal.intro")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/binop/binary-minus-without-space.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/binop/binary-minus-without-space.rs)\
\
_Literal patterns_ match exactly the same value as what is created by the literal. Since negative numbers are not [literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literal-expressions), literals in patterns may be prefixed by an optional minus sign, which acts like the negation operator.\
\
> Warning\
>\
> C string and raw C string literals are accepted in literal patterns, but `&CStr` doesn’t implement structural equality (`#[derive(Eq, PartialEq)]`) and therefore any such `match` on a `&CStr` will be rejected with a type error.\
\
[\[patterns.literal.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.literal.refutable "patterns.literal.refutable")\
\
Literal patterns are always refutable.\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
for i in -2..5 {\
    match i {\
        -1 => println!("It's minus one"),\
        1 => println!("It's a one"),\
        2|4 => println!("It's either a two or a four"),\
        _ => println!("Matched none of the arms"),\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.ident\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident "patterns.ident")\
\
## [Identifier patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#identifier-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.ident.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.syntax "patterns.ident.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[IdentifierPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IdentifierPattern) → ref?mut?[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) ( @[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternNoTopAlt) )?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[IdentifierPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IdentifierPattern)\
ref\
mut[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
@[PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PatternNoTopAlt)\
\
[\[patterns.ident.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.intro "patterns.ident.intro")\
\
Identifier patterns bind the value they match to a variable in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.kinds).\
\
[\[patterns.ident.unique\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.unique "patterns.ident.unique")\
\
The identifier must be unique within the pattern.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.scope "patterns.ident.scope")\
\
The variable will shadow any variables of the same name in scope. The [scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scopes) of the new binding depends on the context of where the pattern is used (such as a `let` binding or a `match` arm).\
\
[\[patterns.ident.bare\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.bare "patterns.ident.bare")\
\
Patterns that consist of only an identifier, possibly with a `mut`, match any value and bind it to that identifier. This is the most commonly used pattern in variable declarations and parameters for functions and closures.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut variable = 10;\
fn sum(x: i32, y: i32) -> i32 {\
   x + y\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.ident.scrutinized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.scrutinized "patterns.ident.scrutinized")\
\
To bind the matched value of a pattern to a variable, use the syntax `variable @ subpattern`. For example, the following binds the value 2 to `e` (not the entire range: the range here is a range subpattern).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = 2;\
\
match x {\
    e @ 1 ..= 5 => println!("got a range element {}", e),\
    _ => println!("anything"),\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.ident.move\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.move "patterns.ident.move")\
\
By default, identifier patterns bind a variable to a copy of or move from the matched value depending on whether the matched value implements [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy).\
\
[\[patterns.ident.ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.ref "patterns.ident.ref")\
\
This can be changed to bind to a reference by using the `ref` keyword, or to a mutable reference using `ref mut`. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let a = Some(10);\
match a {\
    None => (),\
    Some(value) => (),\
}\
\
match a {\
    None => (),\
    Some(ref value) => (),\
}\
}\
```\
\
In the first match expression, the value is copied (or moved). In the second match, a reference to the same memory location is bound to the variable value. This syntax is needed because in destructuring subpatterns the `&` operator can’t be applied to the value’s fields. For example, the following is not valid:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Person {\
   name: String,\
   age: u8,\
}\
let value = Person { name: String::from("John"), age: 23 };\
if let Person { name: &person_name, age: 18..=150 } = value { }\
}\
```\
\
To make it valid, write the following:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Person {\
   name: String,\
   age: u8,\
}\
let value = Person { name: String::from("John"), age: 23 };\
if let Person { name: ref person_name, age: 18..=150 } = value { }\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.ident.ref-ignored\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.ref-ignored "patterns.ident.ref-ignored")\
\
Thus, `ref` is not something that is being matched against. Its objective is exclusively to make the matched binding a reference, instead of potentially copying or moving what was matched.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.precedent\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.precedent "patterns.ident.precedent")\
\
[Path patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-patterns) take precedence over identifier patterns.\
\
> Note\
>\
> When a pattern is a single-segment identifier, the grammar is ambiguous whether it means an [IdentifierPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IdentifierPattern) or a [PathPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathPattern). This ambiguity can only be resolved after [name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#name-resolution).\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> const EXPECTED_VALUE: u8 = 42;\
> //    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ That this constant is in scope affects how the\
> //                   patterns below are treated.\
>\
> fn check_value(x: u8) -> Result<u8, u8> {\
>     match x {\
>         EXPECTED_VALUE => Ok(x),\
>     //  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ Parsed as a `PathPattern` that resolves to\
>     //                 the constant `42`.\
>         other_value => Err(x),\
>     //  ^^^^^^^^^^^ Parsed as an `IdentifierPattern`.\
>     }\
> }\
>\
> // If `EXPECTED_VALUE` were treated as an `IdentifierPattern` above,\
> // that pattern would always match, making the function always return\
> // `Ok(_) regardless of the input.\
> assert_eq!(check_value(42), Ok(42));\
> assert_eq!(check_value(43), Err(43));\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[patterns.ident.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.constraint "patterns.ident.constraint")\
\
It is an error if `ref` or `ref mut` is specified and the identifier shadows a constant.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.refutable "patterns.ident.refutable")\
\
Identifier patterns are irrefutable if the `@` subpattern is irrefutable or the subpattern is not specified.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding "patterns.ident.binding")\
\
### [Binding modes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#binding-modes)\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.intro "patterns.ident.binding.intro")\
\
To service better ergonomics, patterns operate in different _binding modes_ in order to make it easier to bind references to values. When a reference value is matched by a non-reference pattern, it will be automatically treated as a `ref` or `ref mut` binding. Example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x: &Option<i32> = &Some(3);\
if let Some(y) = x {\
    // y was converted to `ref y` and its type is &i32\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.non-reference\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.non-reference "patterns.ident.binding.non-reference")\
\
_Non-reference patterns_ include all patterns except bindings, [wildcard patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#wildcard-pattern) (`_`), [`const` patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-patterns) of reference types, and [reference patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#reference-patterns).\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.default-mode\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.default-mode "patterns.ident.binding.default-mode")\
\
If a binding pattern does not explicitly have `ref`, `ref mut`, or `mut`, then it uses the _default binding mode_ to determine how the variable is bound.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.move\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.move "patterns.ident.binding.move")\
\
The default binding mode starts in “move” mode which uses move semantics.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.top-down\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.top-down "patterns.ident.binding.top-down")\
\
When matching a pattern, the compiler starts from the outside of the pattern and works inwards.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.auto-deref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.auto-deref "patterns.ident.binding.auto-deref")\
\
Each time a reference is matched using a non-reference pattern, it will automatically dereference the value and update the default binding mode.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.ref "patterns.ident.binding.ref")\
\
References will set the default binding mode to `ref`.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.ref-mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.ref-mut "patterns.ident.binding.ref-mut")\
\
Mutable references will set the mode to `ref mut` unless the mode is already `ref` in which case it remains `ref`.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.nested-references\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.nested-references "patterns.ident.binding.nested-references")\
\
If the automatically dereferenced value is still a reference, it is dereferenced and this process repeats.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-binding\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-binding "patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-binding")\
\
The binding pattern may only explicitly specify a `ref` or `ref mut` binding mode, or specify mutability with `mut`, when the default binding mode is “move”. For example, these are not accepted:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let [mut x] = &[()]; //~ ERROR\
let [ref x] = &[()]; //~ ERROR\
let [ref mut x] = &mut [()]; //~ ERROR\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations.edition2024\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations.edition2024 "patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations.edition2024")\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2024 edition, bindings could explicitly specify a `ref` or `ref mut` binding mode even when the default binding mode was not “move”, and they could specify mutability on such bindings with `mut`. In these editions, specifying `mut` on a binding set the binding mode to “move” regardless of the current default binding mode.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-reference\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-reference "patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-reference")\
\
Similarly, a reference pattern may only appear when the default binding mode is “move”. For example, this is not accepted:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let [&x] = &[&()]; //~ ERROR\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-reference.edition2024\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-reference.edition2024 "patterns.ident.binding.mode-limitations-reference.edition2024")\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2024 edition, reference patterns could appear even when the default binding mode was not “move”, and had both the effect of matching against the scrutinee and of causing the default binding mode to be reset to “move”.\
\
[\[patterns.ident.binding.mixed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.binding.mixed "patterns.ident.binding.mixed")\
\
Move bindings and reference bindings can be mixed together in the same pattern. Doing so will result in partial move of the object bound to and the object cannot be used afterwards. This applies only if the type cannot be copied.\
\
In the example below, `name` is moved out of `person`. Trying to use `person` as a whole or `person.name` would result in an error because of _partial move_.\
\
Example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Person {\
   name: String,\
   age: u8,\
}\
let person = Person{ name: String::from("John"), age: 23 };\
// `name` is moved from person and `age` referenced\
let Person { name, ref age } = person;\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.wildcard\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.wildcard "patterns.wildcard")\
\
## [Wildcard pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#wildcard-pattern)\
\
[\[patterns.wildcard.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.wildcard.syntax "patterns.wildcard.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[WildcardPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-WildcardPattern) → \_\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[WildcardPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-WildcardPattern)\
\_\
\
[\[patterns.wildcard.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.wildcard.intro "patterns.wildcard.intro")\
\
The _wildcard pattern_ (an underscore symbol) matches any value. It is used to ignore values when they don’t matter.\
\
[\[patterns.wildcard.struct-matcher\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.wildcard.struct-matcher "patterns.wildcard.struct-matcher")\
\
Inside other patterns it matches a single data field (as opposed to the `..` which matches the remaining fields).\
\
[\[patterns.wildcard.no-binding\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.wildcard.no-binding "patterns.wildcard.no-binding")\
\
Unlike identifier patterns, it does not copy, move or borrow the value it matches.\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = 20;\
let (a, _) = (10, x);   // the x is always matched by _\
assert_eq!(a, 10);\
\
// ignore a function/closure param\
let real_part = |a: f64, _: f64| { a };\
\
// ignore a field from a struct\
struct RGBA {\
   r: f32,\
   g: f32,\
   b: f32,\
   a: f32,\
}\
let color = RGBA{r: 0.4, g: 0.1, b: 0.9, a: 0.5};\
let RGBA{r: red, g: green, b: blue, a: _} = color;\
assert_eq!(color.r, red);\
assert_eq!(color.g, green);\
assert_eq!(color.b, blue);\
\
// accept any Some, with any value\
let x = Some(10);\
if let Some(_) = x {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.wildcard.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.wildcard.refutable "patterns.wildcard.refutable")\
\
The wildcard pattern is always irrefutable.\
\
[\[patterns.rest\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.rest "patterns.rest")\
\
## [Rest pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#rest-pattern)\
\
[\[patterns.rest.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.rest.syntax "patterns.rest.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[RestPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RestPattern) → ..\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[RestPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RestPattern)\
..\
\
[\[patterns.rest.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.rest.intro "patterns.rest.intro")\
\
The _rest pattern_ (the `..` token) acts as a variable-length pattern which matches zero or more elements that haven’t been matched already before and after.\
\
[\[patterns.rest.allowed-patterns\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.rest.allowed-patterns "patterns.rest.allowed-patterns")\
\
It may only be used in [tuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-patterns), [tuple struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-struct-patterns), and [slice](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-patterns) patterns, and may only appear once as one of the elements in those patterns. It is also allowed in an [identifier pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#identifier-patterns) for [slice patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-patterns) only.\
\
[\[patterns.rest.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.rest.refutable "patterns.rest.refutable")\
\
The rest pattern is always irrefutable.\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let words = vec!["a", "b", "c"];\
let slice = &words[..];\
match slice {\
    [] => println!("slice is empty"),\
    [one] => println!("single element {}", one),\
    [head, tail @ ..] => println!("head={} tail={:?}", head, tail),\
}\
\
match slice {\
    // Ignore everything but the last element, which must be "!".\
    [.., "!"] => println!("!!!"),\
\
    // `start` is a slice of everything except the last element, which must be "z".\
    [start @ .., "z"] => println!("starts with: {:?}", start),\
\
    // `end` is a slice of everything but the first element, which must be "a".\
    ["a", end @ ..] => println!("ends with: {:?}", end),\
\
    // 'whole' is the entire slice and `last` is the final element\
    whole @ [.., last] => println!("the last element of {:?} is {}", whole, last),\
\
    rest => println!("{:?}", rest),\
}\
\
if let [.., penultimate, _] = slice {\
    println!("next to last is {}", penultimate);\
}\
\
let tuple = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5);\
// The rest pattern may also be used in tuple and tuple\
// struct patterns.\
match tuple {\
    (1, .., y, z) => println!("y={} z={}", y, z),\
    (.., 5) => println!("tail must be 5"),\
    (..) => println!("matches everything else"),\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.range\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range "patterns.range")\
\
## [Range patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#range-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.range.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.syntax "patterns.range.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[RangePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangePattern) →\
\
[RangeExclusivePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeExclusivePattern)\
\
    \| [RangeInclusivePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeInclusivePattern)\
\
    \| [RangeFromPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFromPattern)\
\
    \| [RangeToExclusivePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeToExclusivePattern)\
\
    \| [RangeToInclusivePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeToInclusivePattern)\
\
    \| [ObsoleteRangePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ObsoleteRangePattern)​[1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-obsolete-range-edition)\
\
[RangeExclusivePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeExclusivePattern) →\
\
[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)..[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)\
\
[RangeInclusivePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeInclusivePattern) →\
\
[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)..=[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)\
\
[RangeFromPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeFromPattern) →\
\
[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)..\
\
[RangeToExclusivePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeToExclusivePattern) →\
\
..[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)\
\
[RangeToInclusivePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RangeToInclusivePattern) →\
\
..=[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)\
\
[ObsoleteRangePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ObsoleteRangePattern) →\
\
[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)...[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)\
\

[\[patterns.range.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.intro "patterns.range.intro")\
\
_Range patterns_ match scalar values within the range defined by their bounds. They comprise a _sigil_ (`..` or `..=`) and a bound on one or both sides.\
\
A bound on the left of the sigil is called a _lower bound_. A bound on the right is called an _upper bound_.\
\
[\[patterns.range.exclusive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.exclusive "patterns.range.exclusive")\
\
The _exclusive range pattern_ matches all values from the lower bound up to, but not including the upper bound. It is written as its lower bound, followed by `..`, followed by the upper bound.\
\
For example, a pattern `'m'..'p'` will match only `'m'`, `'n'` and `'o'`, specifically **not** including `'p'`.\
\
[\[patterns.range.inclusive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.inclusive "patterns.range.inclusive")\
\
The _inclusive range pattern_ matches all values from the lower bound up to and including the upper bound. It is written as its lower bound, followed by `..=`, followed by the upper bound.\
\
For example, a pattern `'m'..='p'` will match only the values `'m'`, `'n'`, `'o'`, and `'p'`.\
\
[\[patterns.range.from\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.from "patterns.range.from")\
\
The _from range pattern_ matches all values greater than or equal to the lower bound. It is written as its lower bound followed by `..`.\
\
For example, `1..` will match any integer greater than or equal to 1, such as 1, 9, or 9001, or 9007199254740991 (if it is of an appropriate size), but not 0, and not negative numbers for signed integers.\
\
[\[patterns.range.to-exclusive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.to-exclusive "patterns.range.to-exclusive")\
\
The _to exclusive range pattern_ matches all values less than the upper bound. It is written as `..` followed by the upper bound.\
\
For example, `..10` will match any integer less than 10, such as 9, 1, 0, and for signed integer types, all negative values.\
\
[\[patterns.range.to-inclusive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.to-inclusive "patterns.range.to-inclusive")\
\
The _to inclusive range pattern_ matches all values less than or equal to the upper bound. It is written as `..=` followed by the upper bound.\
\
For example, `..=10` will match any integer less than or equal to 10, such as 10, 1, 0, and for signed integer types, all negative values.\
\
[\[patterns.range.constraint-nonempty\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.constraint-nonempty "patterns.range.constraint-nonempty")\
\
A range pattern must be nonempty; it must span at least one value in the set of possible values for its type. In other words:\
\
- In `a..=b`, a ≤ b must be the case. For example, it is an error to have a range pattern `10..=0`, but `10..=10` is allowed.\
- In `a..b`, a < b must be the case. For example, it is an error to have a range pattern `10..0` or `10..10`.\
- In `..b`, b must not be the smallest value of its type. For example, it is an error to have a range pattern `..-128i8` or `..f64::NEG_INFINITY`.\
\
[\[patterns.range.bound\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.bound "patterns.range.bound")\
\
A bound is written as one of:\
\
- A character, byte, integer, or float literal.\
- A `-` followed by an integer or float literal.\
- A [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-expressions).\
\
> Note\
>\
> We syntactically accept more than this for a _[RangePatternBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangePatternBound)_. We later reject the other things semantically.\
\
[\[patterns.range.constraint-bound-path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.constraint-bound-path "patterns.range.constraint-bound-path")\
\
If a bound is written as a path, after macro resolution, the path must resolve to a constant item of the type `char`, an integer type, or a float type.\
\
[\[patterns.range.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.type "patterns.range.type")\
\
The range pattern matches the type of its upper and lower bounds, which must be the same type.\
\
[\[patterns.range.path-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.path-value "patterns.range.path-value")\
\
If a bound is a [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-expressions), the bound matches the type and has the value of the [constant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items) the path resolves to.\
\
[\[patterns.range.literal-value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.literal-value "patterns.range.literal-value")\
\
If a bound is a literal, the bound matches the type and has the value of the corresponding [literal expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literal-expressions).\
\
[\[patterns.range.negation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.negation "patterns.range.negation")\
\
If a bound is a literal preceded by a `-`, the bound matches the same type as the corresponding [literal expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literal-expressions) and has the value of [negating](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#negation-operators) the value of the corresponding literal expression.\
\
[\[patterns.range.float-restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.float-restriction "patterns.range.float-restriction")\
\
For float range patterns, the constant may not be a `NaN`.\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let c = 'f';\
let valid_variable = match c {\
    'a'..='z' => true,\
    'A'..='Z' => true,\
    'α'..='ω' => true,\
    _ => false,\
};\
\
let ph = 10;\
println!("{}", match ph {\
    0..7 => "acid",\
    7 => "neutral",\
    8..=14 => "base",\
    _ => unreachable!(),\
});\
\
let uint: u32 = 5;\
match uint {\
    0 => "zero!",\
    1.. => "positive number!",\
};\
\
// using paths to constants:\
const TROPOSPHERE_MIN : u8 = 6;\
const TROPOSPHERE_MAX : u8 = 20;\
\
const STRATOSPHERE_MIN : u8 = TROPOSPHERE_MAX + 1;\
const STRATOSPHERE_MAX : u8 = 50;\
\
const MESOSPHERE_MIN : u8 = STRATOSPHERE_MAX + 1;\
const MESOSPHERE_MAX : u8 = 85;\
\
let altitude = 70;\
\
println!("{}", match altitude {\
    TROPOSPHERE_MIN..=TROPOSPHERE_MAX => "troposphere",\
    STRATOSPHERE_MIN..=STRATOSPHERE_MAX => "stratosphere",\
    MESOSPHERE_MIN..=MESOSPHERE_MAX => "mesosphere",\
    _ => "outer space, maybe",\
});\
\
pub mod binary {\
    pub const MEGA : u64 = 1024*1024;\
    pub const GIGA : u64 = 1024*1024*1024;\
}\
let n_items = 20_832_425;\
let bytes_per_item = 12;\
if let size @ binary::MEGA..=binary::GIGA = n_items * bytes_per_item {\
    println!("It fits and occupies {} bytes", size);\
}\
\
trait MaxValue {\
    const MAX: u64;\
}\
impl MaxValue for u8 {\
    const MAX: u64 = (1 << 8) - 1;\
}\
impl MaxValue for u16 {\
    const MAX: u64 = (1 << 16) - 1;\
}\
impl MaxValue for u32 {\
    const MAX: u64 = (1 << 32) - 1;\
}\
// using qualified paths:\
println!("{}", match 0xfacade {\
    0 ..= <u8 as MaxValue>::MAX => "fits in a u8",\
    0 ..= <u16 as MaxValue>::MAX => "fits in a u16",\
    0 ..= <u32 as MaxValue>::MAX => "fits in a u32",\
    _ => "too big",\
});\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.range.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.refutable "patterns.range.refutable")\
\
Range patterns for fix-width integer and `char` types are irrefutable when they span the entire set of possible values of a type. For example, `0u8..=255u8` is irrefutable.\
\
[\[patterns.range.refutable-integer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.refutable-integer "patterns.range.refutable-integer")\
\
The range of values for an integer type is the closed range from its minimum to maximum value.\
\
[\[patterns.range.refutable-char\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.refutable-char "patterns.range.refutable-char")\
\
The range of values for a `char` type are precisely those ranges containing all Unicode Scalar Values: `'\u{0000}'..='\u{D7FF}'` and `'\u{E000}'..='\u{10FFFF}'`.\
\
[\[patterns.range.constraint-slice\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.constraint-slice "patterns.range.constraint-slice")\
\
[RangeFromPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RangeFromPattern) cannot be used as a top-level pattern for subpatterns in [slice patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-patterns). For example, the pattern `[1.., _]` is not a valid pattern.\
\
[\[patterns.range.edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range.edition2021 "patterns.range.edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2021 edition, range patterns with both a lower and upper bound may also be written using `...` in place of `..=`, with the same meaning.\
\
[\[patterns.ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ref "patterns.ref")\
\
## [Reference patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reference-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.ref.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ref.syntax "patterns.ref.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ReferencePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ReferencePattern) → ( & \| && ) mut?[PatternWithoutRange](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternWithoutRange)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ReferencePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ReferencePattern)\
&\
&&\
mut[PatternWithoutRange](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PatternWithoutRange)\
\
[\[patterns.ref.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ref.intro "patterns.ref.intro")\
\
Reference patterns dereference the pointers that are being matched and, thus, borrow them.\
\
For example, these two matches on `x: &i32` are equivalent:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let int_reference = &3;\
\
let a = match *int_reference { 0 => "zero", _ => "some" };\
let b = match int_reference { &0 => "zero", _ => "some" };\
\
assert_eq!(a, b);\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.ref.ref-ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ref.ref-ref "patterns.ref.ref-ref")\
\
The grammar production for reference patterns has to match the token `&&` to match a reference to a reference because it is a token by itself, not two `&` tokens.\
\
[\[patterns.ref.mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ref.mut "patterns.ref.mut")\
\
Adding the `mut` keyword dereferences a mutable reference. The mutability must match the mutability of the reference.\
\
[\[patterns.ref.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ref.refutable "patterns.ref.refutable")\
\
Reference patterns are always irrefutable.\
\
[\[patterns.struct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.struct "patterns.struct")\
\
## [Struct patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#struct-patterns)\
\

..\
\
[\[patterns.struct.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.struct.intro "patterns.struct.intro")\
\
Struct patterns match struct, enum, and union values that match all criteria defined by its subpatterns. They are also used to [destructure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#destructuring) a struct, enum, or union value.\
\
[\[patterns.struct.ignore-rest\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.struct.ignore-rest "patterns.struct.ignore-rest")\
\
On a struct pattern, the fields are referenced by name, index (in the case of tuple structs) or ignored by use of `..`:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Point {\
    x: u32,\
    y: u32,\
}\
let s = Point {x: 1, y: 1};\
\
match s {\
    Point {x: 10, y: 20} => (),\
    Point {y: 10, x: 20} => (),    // order doesn't matter\
    Point {x: 10, ..} => (),\
    Point {..} => (),\
}\
\
struct PointTuple (\
    u32,\
    u32,\
);\
let t = PointTuple(1, 2);\
\
match t {\
    PointTuple {0: 10, 1: 20} => (),\
    PointTuple {1: 10, 0: 20} => (),   // order doesn't matter\
    PointTuple {0: 10, ..} => (),\
    PointTuple {..} => (),\
}\
\
enum Message {\
    Quit,\
    Move { x: i32, y: i32 },\
}\
let m = Message::Quit;\
\
match m {\
    Message::Quit => (),\
    Message::Move {x: 10, y: 20} => (),\
    Message::Move {..} => (),\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.struct.constraint-struct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.struct.constraint-struct "patterns.struct.constraint-struct")\
\
If `..` is not used, a struct pattern used to match a struct is required to specify all fields:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Struct {\
   a: i32,\
   b: char,\
   c: bool,\
}\
let mut struct_value = Struct{a: 10, b: 'X', c: false};\
\
match struct_value {\
    Struct{a: 10, b: 'X', c: false} => (),\
    Struct{a: 10, b: 'X', ref c} => (),\
    Struct{a: 10, b: 'X', ref mut c} => (),\
    Struct{a: 10, b: 'X', c: _} => (),\
    Struct{a: _, b: _, c: _} => (),\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.struct.constraint-union\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.struct.constraint-union "patterns.struct.constraint-union")\
\
A struct pattern used to match a union must specify exactly one field (see [Pattern matching on unions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#pattern-matching-on-unions)).\
\
[\[patterns.struct.binding-shorthand\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.struct.binding-shorthand "patterns.struct.binding-shorthand")\
\
The [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) syntax matches any value and binds it to a variable with the same name as the given field. It is a shorthand for `fieldname: fieldname`. The `ref` and `mut` qualifiers can be included with the behavior as described in [patterns.ident.ref](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.ident.ref).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Struct {\
   a: i32,\
   b: char,\
   c: bool,\
}\
let struct_value = Struct{a: 10, b: 'X', c: false};\
\
let Struct { a, b, c } = struct_value;\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.struct.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.struct.refutable "patterns.struct.refutable")\
\
A struct pattern is refutable if the [PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathInExpression) resolves to a constructor of an enum with more than one variant, or one of its subpatterns is refutable.\
\
[\[patterns.struct.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.struct.namespace "patterns.struct.namespace")\
\
A struct pattern matches against the struct, union, or enum variant whose constructor is resolved from [PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathInExpression) in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.kinds). See [patterns.tuple-struct.namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple-struct.namespace) for more details.\
\
[\[patterns.tuple-struct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple-struct "patterns.tuple-struct")\
\
## [Tuple struct patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tuple-struct-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.tuple-struct.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple-struct.syntax "patterns.tuple-struct.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\

\
[TupleStructItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleStructItems) → [Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern) ( ,[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern) )\*,?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TupleStructPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleStructPattern)[PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathInExpression)\
([TupleStructItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleStructItems)\
)\
\
[TupleStructItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleStructItems)[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
,[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
,\
\
[\[patterns.tuple-struct.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple-struct.intro "patterns.tuple-struct.intro")\
\
Tuple struct patterns match tuple struct and enum values that match all criteria defined by its subpatterns. They are also used to [destructure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#destructuring) a tuple struct or enum value.\
\
[\[patterns.tuple-struct.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple-struct.refutable "patterns.tuple-struct.refutable")\
\
A tuple struct pattern is refutable if the [PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathInExpression) resolves to a constructor of an enum with more than one variant, or one of its subpatterns is refutable.\
\
[\[patterns.tuple-struct.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple-struct.namespace "patterns.tuple-struct.namespace")\
\
A tuple struct pattern matches against the tuple struct or [tuple-like enum variant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.tuple-expr) whose constructor is resolved from [PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathInExpression) in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.kinds).\
\
> Note\
>\
> Conversely, a struct pattern for a tuple struct or [tuple-like enum variant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.enum.tuple-expr), e.g. `S { 0: _ }`, matches against the tuple struct or variant whose constructor is resolved in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.kinds).\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> enum E1 { V(u16) }\
> enum E2 { V(u32) }\
>\
> // Import `E1::V` from the type namespace only.\
> mod _0 {\
>     const V: () = (); // For namespace masking.\
>     pub(super) use super::E1::*;\
> }\
> use _0::*;\
>\
> // Import `E2::V` from the value namespace only.\
> mod _1 {\
>     struct V {} // For namespace masking.\
>     pub(super) use super::E2::*;\
> }\
> use _1::*;\
>\
> fn f() {\
>     // This struct pattern matches against the tuple-like\
>     // enum variant whose constructor was found in the type\
>     // namespace.\
>     let V { 0: ..=u16::MAX } = (loop {}) else { loop {} };\
>     // This tuple struct pattern matches against the tuple-like\
>     // enum variant whose constructor was found in the value\
>     // namespace.\
>     let V(..=u32::MAX) = (loop {}) else { loop {} };\
> }\
> // Required due to the odd behavior of `super` within functions.\
> fn main() {}\
> ```\
>\
> The Lang team has made certain decisions, such as in [PR #138458](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/138458), that raise questions about the desirability of using the value namespace in this way for patterns, as described in [PR #140593](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/140593#issuecomment-2972338457). It might be prudent to not intentionally rely on this nuance in your code.\
\
[\[patterns.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple "patterns.tuple")\
\
## [Tuple patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tuple-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.tuple.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple.syntax "patterns.tuple.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[TuplePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TuplePattern) → ([TuplePatternItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TuplePatternItems)?)\
\
[TuplePatternItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TuplePatternItems) →\
\
[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern),\
\
    \| [RestPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RestPattern)\
\
    \| [Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern) ( ,[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern) )+,?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TuplePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TuplePattern)\
([TuplePatternItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TuplePatternItems)\
)\
\
[TuplePatternItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TuplePatternItems)[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
,[RestPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RestPattern)[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
,[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
,\
\
[\[patterns.tuple.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple.intro "patterns.tuple.intro")\
\
Tuple patterns match tuple values that match all criteria defined by its subpatterns. They are also used to [destructure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#destructuring) a tuple.\
\
[\[patterns.tuple.rest-syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple.rest-syntax "patterns.tuple.rest-syntax")\
\
The form `(..)` with a single [RestPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RestPattern) is a special form that does not require a comma, and matches a tuple of any size.\
\
[\[patterns.tuple.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.tuple.refutable "patterns.tuple.refutable")\
\
The tuple pattern is refutable when one of its subpatterns is refutable.\
\
An example of using tuple patterns:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let pair = (10, "ten");\
let (a, b) = pair;\
\
assert_eq!(a, 10);\
assert_eq!(b, "ten");\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.paren\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.paren "patterns.paren")\
\
## [Grouped patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#grouped-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.paren.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.paren.syntax "patterns.paren.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[GroupedPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GroupedPattern) → ([Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern))\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[GroupedPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GroupedPattern)\
([Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
)\
\
[\[patterns.paren.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.paren.intro "patterns.paren.intro")\
\
Enclosing a pattern in parentheses can be used to explicitly control the precedence of compound patterns. For example, a reference pattern next to a range pattern such as `&0..=5` is ambiguous and is not allowed, but can be expressed with parentheses.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let int_reference = &3;\
match int_reference {\
    &(0..=5) => (),\
    _ => (),\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.slice\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.slice "patterns.slice")\
\
## [Slice patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#slice-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.slice.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.slice.syntax "patterns.slice.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[SlicePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SlicePattern) → \[[SlicePatternItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SlicePatternItems)?\]\
\
[SlicePatternItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SlicePatternItems) → [Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern) ( ,[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern) )\*,?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[SlicePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SlicePattern)\
\[[SlicePatternItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SlicePatternItems)\
\]\
\
[SlicePatternItems](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SlicePatternItems)[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
,[Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Pattern)\
,\
\
[\[patterns.slice.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.slice.intro "patterns.slice.intro")\
\
Slice patterns can match both arrays of fixed size and slices of dynamic size.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Fixed size\
let arr = [1, 2, 3];\
match arr {\
    [1, _, _] => "starts with one",\
    [a, b, c] => "starts with something else",\
};\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Dynamic size\
let v = vec![1, 2, 3];\
match v[..] {\
    [a, b] => { /* this arm will not apply because the length doesn't match */ }\
    [a, b, c] => { /* this arm will apply */ }\
