    _ => { /* this wildcard is required, since the length is not known statically */ }\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[patterns.slice.refutable-array\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.slice.refutable-array "patterns.slice.refutable-array")\
\
Slice patterns are irrefutable when matching an array as long as each element is irrefutable.\
\
[\[patterns.slice.refutable-slice\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.slice.refutable-slice "patterns.slice.refutable-slice")\
\
When matching a slice, it is irrefutable only in the form with a single `..` [rest pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.rest) or [identifier pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#identifier-patterns) with the `..` rest pattern as a subpattern.\
\
[\[patterns.slice.restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.slice.restriction "patterns.slice.restriction")\
\
Within a slice, a range pattern without both lower and upper bound must be enclosed in parentheses, as in `(a..)`, to clarify it is intended to match against a single slice element. A range pattern with both lower and upper bound, like `a..=b`, is not required to be enclosed in parentheses.\
\
[\[patterns.path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.path "patterns.path")\
\
## [Path patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#path-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.path.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.path.syntax "patterns.path.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[PathPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathPattern) → [PathExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathExpression)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[PathPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathPattern)[PathExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathExpression)\
\
[\[patterns.path.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.path.intro "patterns.path.intro")\
\
_Path patterns_ are patterns that refer either to constant values or to structs or enum variants that have no fields.\
\
[\[patterns.path.unqualified\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.path.unqualified "patterns.path.unqualified")\
\
Unqualified path patterns can refer to:\
\
- enum variants\
- structs\
- constants\
- associated constants\
\
[\[patterns.path.qualified\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.path.qualified "patterns.path.qualified")\
\
Qualified path patterns can only refer to associated constants.\
\
[\[patterns.path.refutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.path.refutable "patterns.path.refutable")\
\
Path patterns are irrefutable when they refer to structs or an enum variant when the enum has only one variant or a constant whose type is irrefutable. They are refutable when they refer to refutable constants or enum variants for enums with multiple variants.\
\
[\[patterns.const\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const "patterns.const")\
\
### [Constant patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#constant-patterns)\
\
[\[patterns.const.partial-eq\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.partial-eq "patterns.const.partial-eq")\
\
When a constant `C` of type `T` is used as a pattern, we first check that `T: PartialEq`.\
\
[\[patterns.const.structural-equality\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.structural-equality "patterns.const.structural-equality")\
\
Furthermore we require that the value of `C` _has (recursive) structural equality_, which is defined recursively as follows:\
\
[\[patterns.const.primitive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.primitive "patterns.const.primitive")\
\
- Integers as well as `str`, `bool` and `char` values always have structural equality.\
\
[\[patterns.const.builtin-aggregate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.builtin-aggregate "patterns.const.builtin-aggregate")\
\
- Tuples, arrays, and slices have structural equality if all their fields/elements have structural equality. (In particular, `()` and `[]` always have structural equality.)\
\
[\[patterns.const.ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.ref "patterns.const.ref")\
\
- References have structural equality if the value they point to has structural equality.\
\
[\[patterns.const.aggregate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.aggregate "patterns.const.aggregate")\
\
- A value of `struct` or `enum` type has structural equality if its `PartialEq` instance is derived via `#[derive(PartialEq)]`, and all fields (for enums: of the active variant) have structural equality.\
\
[\[patterns.const.pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.pointer "patterns.const.pointer")\
\
- A raw pointer has structural equality if it was defined as a constant integer (and then cast/transmuted).\
\
[\[patterns.const.float\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.float "patterns.const.float")\
\
- A float value has structural equality if it is not a `NaN`.\
\
[\[patterns.const.exhaustive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.exhaustive "patterns.const.exhaustive")\
\
- Nothing else has structural equality.\
\
[\[patterns.const.generic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.generic "patterns.const.generic")\
\
In particular, the value of `C` must be known at pattern-building time (which is pre-monomorphization). This means that associated consts that involve generic parameters cannot be used as patterns.\
\
[\[patterns.const.immutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.immutable "patterns.const.immutable")\
\
The value of `C` must not contain any references to mutable statics (`static mut` items or interior mutable `static` items) or `extern` statics.\
\
[\[patterns.const.translation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.const.translation "patterns.const.translation")\
\
After ensuring all conditions are met, the constant value is translated into a pattern, and now behaves exactly as-if that pattern had been written directly. In particular, it fully participates in exhaustiveness checking. (For raw pointers, constants are the only way to write such patterns. Only `_` is ever considered exhaustive for these types.)\
\
[\[patterns.or\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.or "patterns.or")\
\
## [Or-patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#or-patterns)\
\
_Or-patterns_ are patterns that match on one of two or more sub-patterns (for example `A | B | C`). They can nest arbitrarily. Syntactically, or-patterns are allowed in any of the places where other patterns are allowed (represented by the [Pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Pattern) production), with the exceptions of `let`-bindings and function and closure parameters (represented by the [PatternNoTopAlt](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PatternNoTopAlt) production).\
\
[\[patterns.constraints\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.constraints "patterns.constraints")\
\
### [Static semantics](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#static-semantics)\
\
[\[patterns.constraints.pattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.constraints.pattern "patterns.constraints.pattern")\
\
1. Given a pattern `p | q` at some depth for some arbitrary patterns `p` and `q`, the pattern is considered ill-formed if:\
\
\
   - the type inferred for `p` does not unify with the type inferred for `q`, or\
   - the same set of bindings are not introduced in `p` and `q`, or\
   - the type of any two bindings with the same name in `p` and `q` do not unify with respect to types or binding modes.\
\
Unification of types is in all instances aforementioned exact and implicit [type coercions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-coercions) do not apply.\
\
[\[patterns.constraints.match-type-check\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.constraints.match-type-check "patterns.constraints.match-type-check")\
\
2. When type checking an expression `match e_s { a_1 => e_1, ... a_n => e_n }`, for each match arm `a_i` which contains a pattern of form `p_i | q_i`, the pattern `p_i | q_i` is considered ill formed if, at the depth `d` where it exists the fragment of `e_s` at depth `d`, the type of the expression fragment does not unify with `p_i | q_i`.\
\
[\[patterns.constraints.exhaustiveness-or-pattern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.constraints.exhaustiveness-or-pattern "patterns.constraints.exhaustiveness-or-pattern")\
\
3. With respect to exhaustiveness checking, a pattern `p | q` is considered to cover `p` as well as `q`. For some constructor `c(x, ..)` the distributive law applies such that `c(p | q, ..rest)` covers the same set of value as `c(p, ..rest) | c(q, ..rest)` does. This can be applied recursively until there are no more nested patterns of form `p | q` other than those that exist at the top level.\
\
Note that by _“constructor”_ we do not refer to tuple struct patterns, but rather we refer to a pattern for any product type. This includes enum variants, tuple structs, structs with named fields, arrays, tuples, and slices.\
\
\
[\[patterns.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.behavior "patterns.behavior")\
\
### [Dynamic semantics](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#dynamic-semantics)\
\
[\[patterns.behavior.nested-or-patterns\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.behavior.nested-or-patterns "patterns.behavior.nested-or-patterns")\
\
1. The dynamic semantics of pattern matching a scrutinee expression `e_s` against a pattern `c(p | q, ..rest)` at depth `d` where `c` is some constructor, `p` and `q` are arbitrary patterns, and `rest` is optionally any remaining potential factors in `c`, is defined as being the same as that of `c(p, ..rest) | c(q, ..rest)`.\
\
[\[patterns.precedence\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.precedence "patterns.precedence")\
\
### [Precedence with other undelimited patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#precedence-with-other-undelimited-patterns)\
\
As shown elsewhere in this chapter, there are several types of patterns that are syntactically undelimited, including identifier patterns, reference patterns, and or-patterns. Or-patterns always have the lowest-precedence. This allows us to reserve syntactic space for a possible future type ascription feature and also to reduce ambiguity. For example, `x @ A(..) | B(..)` will result in an error that `x` is not bound in all patterns. `&A(x) | B(x)` will result in a type mismatch between `x` in the different subpatterns.\
\
* * *\
\
1. The [ObsoleteRangePattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ObsoleteRangePattern) syntax has been removed in the 2021 edition. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-obsolete-range-edition-1)\
\
\
# [Type system](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#type-system)\
\
[\[type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type "type")\
\
# [Types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#types)\
\
[\[type.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.intro "type.intro")\
\
Every variable, item, and value in a Rust program has a type. The _type_ of a _value_ defines the interpretation of the memory holding it and the operations that may be performed on the value.\
\
[\[type.builtin\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.builtin "type.builtin")\
\
Built-in types are tightly integrated into the language, in nontrivial ways that are not possible to emulate in user-defined types.\
\
[\[type.user-defined\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.user-defined "type.user-defined")\
\
User-defined types have limited capabilities.\
\
[\[type.kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.kinds "type.kinds")\
\
The list of types is:\
\
- Primitive types:\
  - [Boolean](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type) — `bool`\
  - [Numeric](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types) — integer and float\
  - [`char`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#character-type)\
  - [`str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-slice-type)\
  - [Never](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#never-type) — `!` — a type with no values\
- Sequence types:\
  - [Tuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types)\
  - [Array](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types)\
  - [Slice](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-types)\
- User-defined types:\
  - [Struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-types)\
  - [Enum](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerated-types)\
  - [Union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#union-types)\
- Function types:\
  - [Functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-item-types)\
  - [Closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types)\
- Pointer types:\
  - [References](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#shared-references-)\
  - [Raw pointers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-pointers-const-and-mut)\
  - [Function pointers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-pointer-types)\
- Trait types:\
  - [Trait objects](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects)\
  - [Impl trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#impl-trait)\
\
[\[type.name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name "type.name")\
\
## [Type expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#type-expressions)\
\
[\[type.name.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.syntax "type.name.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type) →\
\
[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeNoBounds)\
\
    \| [ImplTraitType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ImplTraitType)\
\
    \| [TraitObjectType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TraitObjectType)\
\
[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeNoBounds) →\
\
[ParenthesizedType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ParenthesizedType)\
\
    \| [ImplTraitTypeOneBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ImplTraitTypeOneBound)\
\
    \| [TraitObjectTypeOneBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TraitObjectTypeOneBound)\
\
    \| [TypePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePath)\
\
    \| [TupleType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleType)\
\
    \| [NeverType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-NeverType)\
\
    \| [RawPointerType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RawPointerType)\
\
    \| [ReferenceType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ReferenceType)\
\
    \| [ArrayType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ArrayType)\
\
    \| [SliceType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SliceType)\
\
    \| [InferredType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InferredType)\
\
    \| [QualifiedPathInType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QualifiedPathInType)\
\
    \| [BareFunctionType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BareFunctionType)\
\
    \| [MacroInvocation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MacroInvocation)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeNoBounds)[ImplTraitType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ImplTraitType)[TraitObjectType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TraitObjectType)\
\
[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeNoBounds)[ParenthesizedType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ParenthesizedType)[ImplTraitTypeOneBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ImplTraitTypeOneBound)[TraitObjectTypeOneBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TraitObjectTypeOneBound)[TypePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePath)[TupleType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleType)[NeverType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-NeverType)[RawPointerType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RawPointerType)[ReferenceType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ReferenceType)[ArrayType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ArrayType)[SliceType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SliceType)[InferredType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-InferredType)[QualifiedPathInType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QualifiedPathInType)[BareFunctionType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BareFunctionType)[MacroInvocation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MacroInvocation)\
\
[\[type.name.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.intro "type.name.intro")\
\
A _type expression_ as defined in the [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type) grammar rule above is the syntax for referring to a type. It may refer to:\
\
[\[type.name.sequence\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.sequence "type.name.sequence")\
\
- Sequence types ( [tuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types), [array](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types), [slice](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-types)).\
\
[\[type.name.path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.path "type.name.path")\
\
- [Type paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths-in-types)which can reference:\
\
  - Primitive types ( [boolean](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type), [numeric](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types), [`char`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#character-type), [`str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-slice-type)).\
  - Paths to an [item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items) ( [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-types), [enum](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerated-types), [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#union-types), [type alias](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-aliases), [trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects)).\
  - [`Self` path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#self-1) where `Self` is the implementing type.\
  - Generic [type parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-parameters).\
\
[\[type.name.pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.pointer "type.name.pointer")\
\
- Pointer types ( [reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#shared-references-), [raw pointer](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#raw-pointers-const-and-mut), [function pointer](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-pointer-types)).\
\
[\[type.name.inference\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.inference "type.name.inference")\
\
- The [inferred type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#inferred-type) which asks the compiler to determine the type.\
\
[\[type.name.grouped\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.grouped "type.name.grouped")\
\
- [Parentheses](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#parenthesized-types) which are used for disambiguation.\
\
[\[type.name.trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.trait "type.name.trait")\
\
- Trait types: [Trait objects](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects) and [impl trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#impl-trait).\
\
[\[type.name.never\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.never "type.name.never")\
\
- The [never](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#never-type) type.\
\
[\[type.name.macro-expansion\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.macro-expansion "type.name.macro-expansion")\
\
- [Macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros) which expand to a type expression.\
\
[\[type.name.parenthesized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.parenthesized "type.name.parenthesized")\
\
### [Parenthesized types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#parenthesized-types)\
\
[\[type.name.parenthesized.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.parenthesized.syntax "type.name.parenthesized.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ParenthesizedType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ParenthesizedType) → ([Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type))\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ParenthesizedType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ParenthesizedType)\
([Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
)\
\
[\[type.name.parenthesized.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.name.parenthesized.intro "type.name.parenthesized.intro")\
\
In some situations the combination of types may be ambiguous. Use parentheses around a type to avoid ambiguity. For example, the `+` operator for [type boundaries](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-and-lifetime-bounds) within a [reference type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#shared-references-) is unclear where the boundary applies, so the use of parentheses is required. Grammar rules that require this disambiguation use the [TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeNoBounds) rule instead of [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::any::Any;\
type T<'a> = &'a (dyn Any + Send);\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.recursive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.recursive "type.recursive")\
\
## [Recursive types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#recursive-types)\
\
[\[type.recursive.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.recursive.intro "type.recursive.intro")\
\
Nominal types — [structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-types), [enumerations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerated-types), and [unions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#union-types) — may be recursive. That is, each `enum` variant or `struct` or `union` field may refer, directly or indirectly, to the enclosing `enum` or `struct` type itself.\
\
[\[type.recursive.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.recursive.constraint "type.recursive.constraint")\
\
Such recursion has restrictions:\
\
- Recursive types must include a nominal type in the recursion (not mere [type aliases](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-aliases), or other structural types such as [arrays](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types) or [tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types)). So `type Rec = &'static [Rec]` is not allowed.\
- The size of a recursive type must be finite; in other words the recursive fields of the type must be [pointer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#pointer-types).\
\
An example of a _recursive_ type and its use:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum List<T> {\
    Nil,\
    Cons(T, Box<List<T>>)\
}\
\
let a: List<i32> = List::Cons(7, Box::new(List::Cons(13, Box::new(List::Nil))));\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.bool\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool "type.bool")\
\
# [Boolean type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#boolean-type)\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let b: bool = true;\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.bool.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.intro "type.bool.intro")\
\
The _boolean type_ or _bool_ is a primitive data type that can take on one of two values, called _true_ and _false_.\
\
[\[type.bool.literal\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.literal "type.bool.literal")\
\
Values of this type may be created using a [literal expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literal-expressions) using the keywords `true` and `false` corresponding to the value of the same name.\
\
[\[type.bool.namespace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.namespace "type.bool.namespace")\
\
This type is a part of the [language prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#language-prelude) with the [name](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#names)`bool`.\
\
[\[type.bool.layout\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.layout "type.bool.layout")\
\
An object with the boolean type has a [size and alignment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#size-and-alignment) of 1 each.\
\
[\[type.bool.repr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.repr "type.bool.repr")\
\
The value false has the bit pattern `0x00` and the value true has the bit pattern `0x01`. It is [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined) for an object with the boolean type to have any other bit pattern.\
\
[\[type.bool.usage\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.usage "type.bool.usage")\
\
The boolean type is the type of many operands in various [expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expressions):\
\
[\[type.bool.usage-condition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.usage-condition "type.bool.usage-condition")\
\
- The condition operand in [if expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-expressions) and [while expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#predicate-loops)\
\
[\[type.bool.usage-lazy-operator\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.usage-lazy-operator "type.bool.usage-lazy-operator")\
\
- The operands in [lazy boolean operator expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lazy-boolean-operators)\
\
> Note\
>\
> The boolean type acts similarly to but is not an [enumerated type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerated-types). In practice, this mostly means that constructors are not associated to the type (e.g. `bool::true`).\
\
[\[type.bool.traits\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.traits "type.bool.traits")\
\
Like all primitives, the boolean type [implements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations) the [traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#clone), [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy), [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sized-1), [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#send), and [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sync).\
\
> Note\
>\
> See the [standard library docs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.bool.html) for library operations.\
\
[\[type.bool.expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr "type.bool.expr")\
\
## [Operations on boolean values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#operations-on-boolean-values)\
\
When using certain operator expressions with a boolean type for its operands, they evaluate using the rules of [boolean logic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boolean_algebra).\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.not\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.not "type.bool.expr.not")\
\
### [Logical not](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#logical-not)\
\
| `b` | [`!b`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#negation-operators) |\
| --- | --- |\
| `true` | `false` |\
| `false` | `true` |\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.or\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.or "type.bool.expr.or")\
\
### [Logical or](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#logical-or)\
\
| `a` | `b` | [`a | b`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#arithmetic-and-logical-binary-operators) |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `true` | `true` | `true` |\
| `true` | `false` | `true` |\
| `false` | `true` | `true` |\
| `false` | `false` | `false` |\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.and\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.and "type.bool.expr.and")\
\
### [Logical and](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#logical-and)\
\
| `a` | `b` | [`a & b`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#arithmetic-and-logical-binary-operators) |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `true` | `true` | `true` |\
| `true` | `false` | `false` |\
| `false` | `true` | `false` |\
| `false` | `false` | `false` |\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.xor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.xor "type.bool.expr.xor")\
\
### [Logical xor](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#logical-xor)\
\
| `a` | `b` | [`a ^ b`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#arithmetic-and-logical-binary-operators) |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `true` | `true` | `false` |\
| `true` | `false` | `true` |\
| `false` | `true` | `true` |\
| `false` | `false` | `false` |\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.cmp\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.cmp "type.bool.expr.cmp")\
\
### [Comparisons](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#comparisons)\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.cmp.eq\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.cmp.eq "type.bool.expr.cmp.eq")\
\
| `a` | `b` | [`a == b`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#comparison-operators) |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `true` | `true` | `true` |\
| `true` | `false` | `false` |\
| `false` | `true` | `false` |\
| `false` | `false` | `true` |\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.cmp.greater\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.cmp.greater "type.bool.expr.cmp.greater")\
\
| `a` | `b` | [`a > b`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#comparison-operators) |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `true` | `true` | `false` |\
| `true` | `false` | `true` |\
| `false` | `true` | `false` |\
| `false` | `false` | `false` |\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.cmp.not-eq\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.cmp.not-eq "type.bool.expr.cmp.not-eq")\
\
- `a != b` is the same as `!(a == b)`\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.cmp.greater-eq\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.cmp.greater-eq "type.bool.expr.cmp.greater-eq")\
\
- `a >= b` is the same as `a == b | a > b`\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.cmp.less\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.cmp.less "type.bool.expr.cmp.less")\
\
- `a < b` is the same as `!(a >= b)`\
\
[\[type.bool.expr.cmp.less-eq\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.expr.cmp.less-eq "type.bool.expr.cmp.less-eq")\
\
- `a <= b` is the same as `a == b | a < b`\
\
[\[type.bool.validity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.bool.validity "type.bool.validity")\
\
## [Bit validity](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#bit-validity)\
\
The single byte of a `bool` is guaranteed to be initialized (in other words, `transmute::<bool, u8>(...)` is always sound – but since some bit patterns are invalid `bool`s, the inverse is not always sound).\
\
[\[type.numeric\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric "type.numeric")\
\
# [Numeric types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#numeric-types)\
\
[\[type.numeric.int\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.int "type.numeric.int")\
\
## [Integer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#integer-types)\
\
[\[type.numeric.int.unsigned\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.int.unsigned "type.numeric.int.unsigned")\
\
The unsigned integer types consist of:\
\
| Type | Minimum | Maximum |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `u8` | 0 | 28-1 |\
| `u16` | 0 | 216-1 |\
| `u32` | 0 | 232-1 |\
| `u64` | 0 | 264-1 |\
| `u128` | 0 | 2128-1 |\
\
[\[type.numeric.int.signed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.int.signed "type.numeric.int.signed")\
\
The signed two’s complement integer types consist of:\
\
| Type | Minimum | Maximum |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `i8` | -(27) | 27-1 |\
| `i16` | -(215) | 215-1 |\
| `i32` | -(231) | 231-1 |\
| `i64` | -(263) | 263-1 |\
| `i128` | -(2127) | 2127-1 |\
\
[\[type.numeric.float\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.float "type.numeric.float")\
\
## [Floating-point types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#floating-point-types)\
\
The IEEE 754-2008 “binary32” and “binary64” floating-point types are `f32` and `f64`, respectively.\
\
[\[type.numeric.int.size\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.int.size "type.numeric.int.size")\
\
## [Machine-dependent integer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#machine-dependent-integer-types)\
\
[\[type.numeric.int.size.usize\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.int.size.usize "type.numeric.int.size.usize")\
\
The `usize` type is an unsigned integer type with the same number of bits as the platform’s pointer type. It can represent every memory address in the process.\
\
> Note\
>\
> While a `usize` can represent every _address_, converting a _pointer_ to a `usize` is not necessarily a reversible operation. For more information, see the documentation for [type cast expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-cast-expressions), [`std::ptr`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ptr/index.html), and [provenance](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ptr/index.html#provenance) in particular.\
\
[\[type.numeric.int.size.isize\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.int.size.isize "type.numeric.int.size.isize")\
\
The `isize` type is a signed two’s complement integer type with the same number of bits as the platform’s pointer type. The theoretical upper bound on object and array size is the maximum `isize` value. This ensures that `isize` can be used to calculate differences between pointers into an object or array and can address every byte within an object along with one byte past the end.\
\
[\[type.numeric.int.size.minimum\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.int.size.minimum "type.numeric.int.size.minimum")\
\
`usize` and `isize` are at least 16-bits wide.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Many pieces of Rust code may assume that pointers, `usize`, and `isize` are either 32-bit or 64-bit. As a consequence, 16-bit pointer support is limited and may require explicit care and acknowledgment from a library to support.\
\
[\[type.numeric.validity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.numeric.validity "type.numeric.validity")\
\
## [Bit validity](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#bit-validity-1)\
\
For every numeric type, `T`, the bit validity of `T` is equivalent to the bit validity of `[u8; size_of::<T>()]`. An uninitialized byte is not a valid `u8`.\
\
[\[type.char\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.char "type.char")\
\
# [Character type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#character-type)\
\
[\[type.char.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.char.intro "type.char.intro")\
\
The `char` type represents a single [Unicode scalar value](http://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value) (i.e., a code point that is not a surrogate).\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> let c: char = 'a';\
> let emoji: char = '😀';\
> let unicode: char = '\u{1F600}';\
> }\
> ```\
\
> Note\
>\
> See [the standard library docs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.char.html) for information on the impls of the `char` type.\
\
[\[type.char.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.char.value "type.char.value")\
\
A value of type `char` is represented as a 32-bit unsigned word in the 0x0000 to 0xD7FF or 0xE000 to 0x10FFFF range. It is immediate [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined) to create a `char` that falls outside this range.\
\
[\[type.char.layout\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.char.layout "type.char.layout")\
\
`char` is guaranteed to have the same size and alignment as `u32` on all platforms.\
\
[\[type.char.validity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.char.validity "type.char.validity")\
\
Every byte of a `char` is guaranteed to be initialized. In other words, `transmute::<char, [u8; size_of::<char>()]>(...)` is always sound – but since some bit patterns are invalid `char`s, the inverse is not always sound.\
\
[\[type.str\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.str "type.str")\
\
# [String slice type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#string-slice-type)\
\
[\[type.str.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.str.intro "type.str.intro")\
\
The string slice (`str`) type represents a sequence of characters.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let greeting1: &str = "Hello, world!";\
let greeting2: &str = "你好，世界";\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> See [the standard library docs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.str.html) for information on the impls of the `str` type.\
\
[\[type.str.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.str.value "type.str.value")\
\
A value of type `str` is represented in the same way as `[u8]`, a slice of 8-bit unsigned bytes.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The standard library makes extra assumptions about `str`: methods working on `str` assume and ensure that the data it contains is valid UTF-8. Calling a `str` method with a non-UTF-8 buffer can cause [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined) now or in the future.\
\
[\[type.str.unsized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.str.unsized "type.str.unsized")\
\
A `str` is a [dynamically sized type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#dynamically-sized-types). It can only be instantiated through a pointer type, such as `&str`. The layout of `&str` is the same as the layout of `&[u8]`.\
\
[\[type.never\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never "type.never")\
\
# [Never type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#never-type)\
\
[\[type.never.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never.syntax "type.never.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[NeverType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-NeverType) → !\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[NeverType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-NeverType)\
!\
\
[\[type.never.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never.intro "type.never.intro")\
\
The never type `!` is a type with no values, representing the result of computations that never complete.\
\
[\[type.never.coercion\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never.coercion "type.never.coercion")\
\
Expressions of type `!` can be coerced into any other type.\
\
[\[type.never.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never.constraint "type.never.constraint")\
\
The `!` type can **only** appear in function return types presently, indicating it is a diverging function that never returns.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn foo() -> ! {\
    panic!("This call never returns.");\
}\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
unsafe extern "C" {\
    pub safe fn no_return_extern_func() -> !;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple "type.tuple")\
\
# [Tuple types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tuple-types)\
\
[\[type.tuple.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.syntax "type.tuple.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[TupleType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TupleType) →\
\
()\
\
    \| ( ( [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type), )+[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)?)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TupleType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TupleType)\
(\
)\
([Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
,[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
)\
\
[\[type.tuple.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.intro "type.tuple.intro")\
\
_Tuple types_ are a family of structural types[1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-1) for heterogeneous lists of other types.\
\
The syntax for a tuple type is a parenthesized, comma-separated list of types.\
\
[\[type.tuple.restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.restriction "type.tuple.restriction")\
\
1-ary tuples require a comma after their element type to be disambiguated with a [parenthesized type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#parenthesized-types).\
\
[\[type.tuple.field-number\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.field-number "type.tuple.field-number")\
\
A tuple type has a number of fields equal to the length of the list of types. This number of fields determines the _arity_ of the tuple. A tuple with `n` fields is called an _n-ary tuple_. For example, a tuple with 2 fields is a 2-ary tuple.\
\
[\[type.tuple.field-name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.field-name "type.tuple.field-name")\
\
Fields of tuples are named using increasing numeric names matching their position in the list of types. The first field is `0`. The second field is `1`. And so on. The type of each field is the type of the same position in the tuple’s list of types.\
\
[\[type.tuple.unit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.unit "type.tuple.unit")\
\
For convenience and historical reasons, the tuple type with no fields (`()`) is often called _unit_ or _the unit type_. Its one value is also called _unit_ or _the unit value_.\
\
Some examples of tuple types:\
\
- `()` (unit)\
- `(i32,)` (1-ary tuple)\
- `(f64, f64)`\
- `(String, i32)`\
- `(i32, String)` (different type from the previous example)\
- `(i32, f64, Vec<String>, Option<bool>)`\
\
[\[type.tuple.constructor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.constructor "type.tuple.constructor")\
\
Values of this type are constructed using a [tuple expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-expressions). Furthermore, various expressions will produce the unit value if there is no other meaningful value for it to evaluate to.\
\
[\[type.tuple.access\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.tuple.access "type.tuple.access")\
\
Tuple fields can be accessed by either a [tuple index expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-indexing-expressions) or [pattern matching](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-patterns).\
\
* * *\
\
1. Structural types are always equivalent if their internal types are equivalent. For a nominal version of tuples, see [tuple structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-types). [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-1-1)\
\
\
[\[type.array\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.array "type.array")\
\
# [Array types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#array-types)\
\
[\[type.array.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.array.syntax "type.array.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ArrayType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ArrayType) → \[[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type);[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Expression)\]\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ArrayType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ArrayType)\
\[[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
;[Expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Expression)\
\]\
\
[\[type.array.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.array.intro "type.array.intro")\
\
An array is a fixed-size sequence of `N` elements of type `T`. The array type is written as `[T; N]`.\
\
[\[type.array.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.array.constraint "type.array.constraint")\
\
The size is a [constant expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-expressions) that evaluates to a [`usize`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#machine-dependent-integer-types).\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// A stack-allocated array\
let array: [i32; 3] = [1, 2, 3];\
\
// A heap-allocated array, coerced to a slice\
let boxed_array: Box<[i32]> = Box::new([1, 2, 3]);\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.array.index\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.array.index "type.array.index")\
\
All elements of arrays are always initialized, and access to an array is always bounds-checked in safe methods and operators.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The [`Vec<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/alloc/vec/struct.Vec.html) standard library type provides a heap-allocated resizable array type.\
\
[\[type.slice\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.slice "type.slice")\
\
# [Slice types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#slice-types)\
\
[\[type.slice.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.slice.syntax "type.slice.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[SliceType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SliceType) → \[[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)\]\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[SliceType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SliceType)\
\[[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
\]\
\
[\[type.slice.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.slice.intro "type.slice.intro")\
\
A slice is a [dynamically sized type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#dynamically-sized-types) representing a ‘view’ into a sequence of elements of type `T`. The slice type is written as `[T]`.\
\
[\[type.slice.unsized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.slice.unsized "type.slice.unsized")\
\
Slice types are generally used through pointer types. For example:\
\
- `&[T]`: a ‘shared slice’, often just called a ‘slice’. It doesn’t own the data it points to; it borrows it.\
- `&mut [T]`: a ‘mutable slice’. It mutably borrows the data it points to.\
- `Box<[T]>`: a ‘boxed slice’\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// A heap-allocated array, coerced to a slice\
let boxed_array: Box<[i32]> = Box::new([1, 2, 3]);\
\
// A (shared) slice into an array\
let slice: &[i32] = &boxed_array[..];\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.slice.safe\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.slice.safe "type.slice.safe")\
\
All elements of slices are always initialized, and access to a slice is always bounds-checked in safe methods and operators.\
\
[\[type.struct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.struct "type.struct")\
\
# [Struct types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#struct-types)\
\
[\[type.struct.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.struct.intro "type.struct.intro")\
\
A `struct` _type_ is a heterogeneous product of other types, called the _fields_ of the type.[1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-structtype)\
\
[\[type.struct.constructor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.struct.constructor "type.struct.constructor")\
\
New instances of a `struct` can be constructed with a [struct expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-expressions).\
\
[\[type.struct.layout\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.struct.layout "type.struct.layout")\
\
The memory layout of a `struct` is undefined by default to allow for compiler optimizations like field reordering, but it can be fixed with the [`repr` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#representations). In either case, fields may be given in any order in a corresponding struct _expression_; the resulting `struct` value will always have the same memory layout.\
\
[\[type.struct.field-visibility\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.struct.field-visibility "type.struct.field-visibility")\
\
The fields of a `struct` may be qualified by [visibility modifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#visibility-and-privacy), to allow access to data in a struct outside a module.\
\
[\[type.struct.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.struct.tuple "type.struct.tuple")\
\
A _tuple struct_ type is just like a struct type, except that the fields are anonymous.\
\
[\[type.struct.unit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.struct.unit "type.struct.unit")\
\
A _unit-like struct_ type is like a struct type, except that it has no fields. The one value constructed by the associated [struct expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-expressions) is the only value that inhabits such a type.\
\
* * *\
\
1. `struct` types are analogous to `struct` types in C, the _record_ types of the ML family, or the _struct_ types of the Lisp family. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-structtype-1)\
\
\
[\[type.enum\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.enum "type.enum")\
\
# [Enumerated types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#enumerated-types)\
\
[\[type.enum.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.enum.intro "type.enum.intro")\
\
An _enumerated type_ is a nominal, heterogeneous disjoint union type, denoted by the name of an [`enum` item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations). [1](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#footnote-enumtype)\
\
[\[type.enum.declaration\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.enum.declaration "type.enum.declaration")\
\
An [`enum` item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations) declares both the type and a number of _variants_, each of which is independently named and has the syntax of a struct, tuple struct or unit-like struct.\
\
[\[type.enum.constructor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.enum.constructor "type.enum.constructor")\
\
New instances of an `enum` can be constructed with a [struct expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-expressions).\
\
[\[type.enum.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.enum.value "type.enum.value")\
\
Any `enum` value consumes as much memory as the largest variant for its corresponding `enum` type, as well as the size needed to store a discriminant.\
\
[\[type.enum.name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.enum.name "type.enum.name")\
\
Enum types cannot be denoted _structurally_ as types, but must be denoted by named reference to an [`enum` item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations).\
\
* * *\
\
1. The `enum` type is analogous to a `data` constructor declaration in Haskell, or a _pick ADT_ in Limbo. [↩](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#fr-enumtype-1)\
\
\
[\[type.union\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.union "type.union")\
\
# [Union types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#union-types)\
\
[\[type.union.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.union.intro "type.union.intro")\
\
A _union type_ is a nominal, heterogeneous C-like union, denoted by the name of a [`union` item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions).\
\
[\[type.union.access\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.union.access "type.union.access")\
\
Unions have no notion of an “active field”. Instead, every union access transmutes parts of the content of the union to the type of the accessed field.\
\
[\[type.union.safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.union.safety "type.union.safety")\
\
Since transmutes can cause unexpected or undefined behaviour, `unsafe` is required to read from a union field.\
\
[\[type.union.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.union.constraint "type.union.constraint")\
\
Union field types are also restricted to a subset of types which ensures that they never need dropping. See the [item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions) documentation for further details.\
\
[\[type.union.layout\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.union.layout "type.union.layout")\
\
The memory layout of a `union` is undefined by default (in particular, fields do _not_ have to be at offset 0), but the `#[repr(...)]` attribute can be used to fix a layout.\
\
[\[type.fn-item\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-item "type.fn-item")\
\
# [Function item types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#function-item-types)\
\
[\[type.fn-item.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-item.intro "type.fn-item.intro")\
\
When referred to, a function item, or the constructor of a tuple-like struct or enum variant, yields a zero-sized value of its _function item type_.\
\
[\[type.fn-item.unique\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-item.unique "type.fn-item.unique")\
\
That type explicitly identifies the function - its name, its type arguments, and its early-bound lifetime arguments (but not its late-bound lifetime arguments, which are only assigned when the function is called) - so the value does not need to contain an actual function pointer, and no indirection is needed when the function is called.\
\
[\[type.fn-item.name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-item.name "type.fn-item.name")\
\
There is no syntax that directly refers to a function item type, but the compiler will display the type as something like `fn(u32) -> i32 {fn_name}` in error messages.\
\
Because the function item type explicitly identifies the function, the item types of different functions - different items, or the same item with different generics - are distinct, and mixing them will create a type error:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn foo<T>() { }\
let x = &mut foo::<i32>;\
*x = foo::<u32>; //~ ERROR mismatched types\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.fn-item.coercion\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-item.coercion "type.fn-item.coercion")\
\
However, there is a [coercion](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-coercions) from function items to [function pointers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-pointer-types) with the same signature, which is triggered not only when a function item is used when a function pointer is directly expected, but also when different function item types with the same signature meet in different arms of the same `if` or `match`:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let want_i32 = false;\
fn foo<T>() { }\
\
// `foo_ptr_1` has function pointer type `fn()` here\
let foo_ptr_1: fn() = foo::<i32>;\
\
// ... and so does `foo_ptr_2` - this type-checks.\
let foo_ptr_2 = if want_i32 {\
    foo::<i32>\
} else {\
    foo::<u32>\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.fn-item.traits\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-item.traits "type.fn-item.traits")\
\
All function items implement [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy), [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#clone), [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#send), and [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sync).\
\
[`Fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.Fn.html), [`FnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnMut.html), and [`FnOnce`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html) are implemented unless the function has any of the following:\
\
- an [`unsafe`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-unsafe.fn) qualifier\
- a [`target_feature` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.codegen.target_feature)\
- an [ABI](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.fn.extern) other than `"Rust"`\
\
[\[type.closure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure "type.closure")\
\
# [Closure types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#closure-types)\
\
[\[type.closure.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.intro "type.closure.intro")\
\
A [closure expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-expressions) produces a closure value with a unique, anonymous type that cannot be written out. A closure type is approximately equivalent to a struct which contains the captured values. For instance, the following closure:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[derive(Debug)]\
struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }\
struct Rectangle { left_top: Point, right_bottom: Point }\
\
fn f<F : FnOnce() -> String> (g: F) {\
    println!("{}", g());\
}\
\
let mut rect = Rectangle {\
    left_top: Point { x: 1, y: 1 },\
    right_bottom: Point { x: 0, y: 0 }\
};\
\
let c = || {\
    rect.left_top.x += 1;\
    rect.right_bottom.x += 1;\
    format!("{:?}", rect.left_top)\
};\
f(c); // Prints "Point { x: 2, y: 1 }".\
}\
```\
\
generates a closure type roughly like the following:\
\
```rust\
\
// Note: This is not exactly how it is translated, this is only for\
// illustration.\
\
struct Closure<'a> {\
    left_top : &'a mut Point,\
    right_bottom_x : &'a mut i32,\
}\
\
impl<'a> FnOnce<()> for Closure<'a> {\
    type Output = String;\
    extern "rust-call" fn call_once(self, args: ()) -> String {\
        self.left_top.x += 1;\
        *self.right_bottom_x += 1;\
        format!("{:?}", self.left_top)\
    }\
}\
```\
\
so that the call to `f` works as if it were:\
\
```rust\
\
f(Closure{ left_top: &mut rect.left_top, right_bottom_x: &mut rect.right_bottom.x });\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture "type.closure.capture")\
\
## [Capture modes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#capture-modes-1)\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.intro "type.closure.capture.intro")\
\
A _capture mode_ determines how a [place expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions) from the environment is borrowed or moved into the closure. The capture modes are:\
\
1. Immutable borrow (`ImmBorrow`) — The place expression is captured as a [shared reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#references--and-mut).\
2. Unique immutable borrow (`UniqueImmBorrow`) — This is similar to an immutable borrow, but must be unique as described [below](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unique-immutable-borrows-in-captures).\
3. Mutable borrow (`MutBorrow`) — The place expression is captured as a [mutable reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#mutable-references-mut).\
4. Move (`ByValue`) — The place expression is captured by [moving the value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#moved-and-copied-types) into the closure.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precedence\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precedence "type.closure.capture.precedence")\
\
Place expressions from the environment are captured from the first mode that is compatible with how the captured value is used inside the closure body. The mode is not affected by the code surrounding the closure, such as the lifetimes of involved variables or fields, or of the closure itself.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.copy "type.closure.capture.copy")\
\
### [`Copy` values](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#copy-values)\
\
Values that implement [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy) that are moved into the closure are captured with the `ImmBorrow` mode.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = [0; 1024];\
let c = || {\
    let y = x; // x captured by ImmBorrow\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.async.input\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.async.input "type.closure.async.input")\
\
### [Async input capture](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#async-input-capture)\
\
Async closures always capture all input arguments, regardless of whether or not they are used within the body.\
\
## [Capture precision](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#capture-precision)\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.capture-path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.capture-path "type.closure.capture.precision.capture-path")\
\
A _capture path_ is a sequence starting with a variable from the environment followed by zero or more place projections from that variable.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.place-projection\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.place-projection "type.closure.capture.precision.place-projection")\
\
A _place projection_ is a [field access](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-access-expressions), [tuple index](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-indexing-expressions), [dereference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator) (and automatic dereferences), [array or slice index](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions) expression, or [pattern destructuring](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.destructure) applied to a variable.\
\
> Note\
>\
> In `rustc`, pattern destructuring desugars into a series of dereferences and field or element accesses.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.intro "type.closure.capture.precision.intro")\
\
The closure borrows or moves the capture path, which may be truncated based on the rules described below.\
\
For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct SomeStruct {\
    f1: (i32, i32),\
}\
let s = SomeStruct { f1: (1, 2) };\
\
let c = || {\
    let x = s.f1.1; // s.f1.1 captured by ImmBorrow\
};\
c();\
}\
```\
\
Here the capture path is the local variable `s`, followed by a field access `.f1`, and then a tuple index `.1`. This closure captures an immutable borrow of `s.f1.1`.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.shared-prefix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.shared-prefix "type.closure.capture.precision.shared-prefix")\
\
### [Shared prefix](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#shared-prefix)\
\
In the case where a capture path and one of the ancestors of that path are both captured by a closure, the ancestor path is captured with the highest capture mode among the two captures, `CaptureMode = max(AncestorCaptureMode, DescendantCaptureMode)`, using the strict weak ordering:\
\
`ImmBorrow < UniqueImmBorrow < MutBorrow < ByValue`\
\
Note that this might need to be applied recursively.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// In this example, there are three different capture paths with a shared ancestor:\
fn move_value<T>(_: T){}\
let s = String::from("S");\
let t = (s, String::from("T"));\
let mut u = (t, String::from("U"));\
\
let c = || {\
    println!("{:?}", u); // u captured by ImmBorrow\
    u.1.truncate(0); // u.0 captured by MutBorrow\
    move_value(u.0.0); // u.0.0 captured by ByValue\
};\
c();\
}\
```\
\
Overall this closure will capture `u` by `ByValue`.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.dereference-shared\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.dereference-shared "type.closure.capture.precision.dereference-shared")\
\
### [Rightmost shared reference truncation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#rightmost-shared-reference-truncation)\
\
The capture path is truncated at the rightmost dereference in the capture path if the dereference is applied to a shared reference.\
\
This truncation is allowed because fields that are read through a shared reference will always be read via a shared reference or a copy. This helps reduce the size of the capture when the extra precision does not yield any benefit from a borrow checking perspective.\
\
The reason it is the _rightmost_ dereference is to help avoid a shorter lifetime than is necessary. Consider the following example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Int(i32);\
struct B<'a>(&'a i32);\
\
struct MyStruct<'a> {\
   a: &'static Int,\
   b: B<'a>,\
}\
\
fn foo<'a, 'b>(m: &'a MyStruct<'b>) -> impl FnMut() + 'static {\
    let c = || drop(&m.a.0);\
    c\
}\
}\
```\
\
If this were to capture `m`, then the closure would no longer outlive `'static`, since `m` is constrained to `'a`. Instead, it captures `(*(*m).a)` by `ImmBorrow`.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard "type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard")\
\
### [Wildcard pattern bindings](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#wildcard-pattern-bindings)\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.reads\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.reads "type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.reads")\
\
Closures only capture data that needs to be read. Binding a value with a [wildcard pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#wildcard-pattern) does not read the value, so the place is not captured.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S; // A non-`Copy` type.\
let x = S;\
let c = || {\
    let _ = x;  // Does not capture `x`.\
};\
let c = || match x {\
    _ => (), // Does not capture `x`.\
};\
x; // OK: `x` can be moved here.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.destructuring\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.destructuring "type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.destructuring")\
\
Destructuring tuples, structs, and single-variant enums does not, by itself, cause a read or the place to be captured.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Enums marked with [`#[non_exhaustive]`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.type-system.non_exhaustive) are always treated as having multiple variants. See _[type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.non\_exhaustive](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.non_exhaustive)_.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S; // A non-`Copy` type.\
\
// Destructuring tuples does not cause a read or capture.\
let x = (S,);\
let c = || {\
    let (..) = x; // Does not capture `x`.\
};\
x; // OK: `x` can be moved here.\
c();\
\
// Destructuring unit structs does not cause a read or capture.\
let x = S;\
let c = || {\
    let S = x; // Does not capture `x`.\
};\
x; // OK: `x` can be moved here.\
c();\
\
// Destructuring structs does not cause a read or capture.\
struct W<T>(T);\
let x = W(S);\
let c = || {\
    let W(..) = x; // Does not capture `x`.\
};\
x; // OK: `x` can be moved here.\
c();\
\
// Destructuring single-variant enums does not cause a read\
// or capture.\
enum E<T> { V(T) }\
let x = E::V(S);\
let c = || {\
    let E::V(..) = x; // Does not capture `x`.\
};\
x; // OK: `x` can be moved here.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.fields\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.fields "type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.fields")\
\
Fields matched against [RestPattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RestPattern) (`..`) or [StructPatternEtCetera](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-StructPatternEtCetera) (also `..`) are not read, and those fields are not captured.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S; // A non-`Copy` type.\
let x = (S, S);\
let c = || {\
    let (x0, ..) = x;  // Captures `x.0` by `ByValue`.\
};\
// Only the first tuple field was captured by the closure.\
x.1; // OK: `x.1` can be moved here.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.array-slice\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.array-slice "type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.array-slice")\
\
Partial captures of arrays and slices are not supported; the entire slice or array is always captured even if used with wildcard pattern matching, indexing, or sub-slicing.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S; // A non-`Copy` type.\
let mut x = [S, S];\
let c = || {\
    let [x0, _] = x; // Captures all of `x` by `ByValue`.\
};\
let _ = &mut x[1]; // ERROR: Borrow of moved value.\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.initialized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.initialized "type.closure.capture.precision.wildcard.initialized")\
\
Values that are matched with wildcards must still be initialized.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x: u8;\
let c = || {\
    let _ = x; // ERROR: Binding `x` isn't initialized.\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants "type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants")\
\
### [Capturing for discriminant reads](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#capturing-for-discriminant-reads)\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.reads\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.reads "type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.reads")\
\
If pattern matching reads a discriminant, the place containing that discriminant is captured by `ImmBorrow`.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.multiple-variant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.multiple-variant "type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.multiple-variant")\
\
Matching against a variant of an enum that has more than one variant reads the discriminant, capturing the place by `ImmBorrow`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S; // A non-`Copy` type.\
let mut x = (Some(S), S);\
let c = || match x {\
    (None, _) => (),\
//   ^^^^\
// This pattern requires reading the discriminant, which\
// causes `x.0` to be captured by `ImmBorrow`.\
    _ => (),\
};\
let _ = &mut x.0; // ERROR: Cannot borrow `x.0` as mutable.\
//           ^^^\
// The closure is still live, so `x.0` is still immutably\
// borrowed here.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S; // A non-`Copy` type.\
let x = (Some(S), S);\
let c = || match x { // Captures `x.0` by `ImmBorrow`.\
    (None, _) => (),\
    _ => (),\
};\
// Though `x.0` is captured due to the discriminant read,\
// `x.1` is not captured.\
x.1; // OK: `x.1` can be moved here.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.single-variant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.single-variant "type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.single-variant")\
\
Matching against the only variant of a single-variant enum does not read the discriminant and does not capture the place.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum E<T> { V(T) } // A single-variant enum.\
let x = E::V(());\
let c = || {\
    let E::V(_) = x; // Does not capture `x`.\
};\
x; // OK: `x` can be moved here.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.non\_exhaustive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.non_exhaustive "type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.non_exhaustive")\
\
If [`#[non_exhaustive]`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.type-system.non_exhaustive) is applied to an enum, the enum is treated as having multiple variants for the purpose of deciding whether a read occurs, even if it actually has only one variant.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.uninhabited-variants\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.uninhabited-variants "type.closure.capture.precision.discriminants.uninhabited-variants")\
\
Even if all variants but the one being matched against are uninhabited, making the pattern [irrefutable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.refutable), the discriminant is still read if it otherwise would be.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
enum Empty {}\
let mut x = Ok::<_, Empty>(42);\
let c = || {\
    let Ok(_) = x; // Captures `x` by `ImmBorrow`.\
};\
let _ = &mut x; // ERROR: Cannot borrow `x` as mutable.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.range-patterns\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.range-patterns "type.closure.capture.precision.range-patterns")\
\
### [Capturing and range patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#capturing-and-range-patterns)\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.range-patterns.reads\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.range-patterns.reads "type.closure.capture.precision.range-patterns.reads")\
\
Matching against a [range pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.range) reads the place being matched, even if the range includes all possible values of the type, and captures the place by `ImmBorrow`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut x = 0u8;\
let c = || {\
    let 0..=u8::MAX = x; // Captures `x` by `ImmBorrow`.\
};\
let _ = &mut x; // ERROR: Cannot borrow `x` as mutable.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns "type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns")\
\
### [Capturing and slice patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#capturing-and-slice-patterns)\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns.slices\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns.slices "type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns.slices")\
\
Matching a slice against a [slice pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.slice) other than one with only a single [rest pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-patterns.rest) (i.e. `[..]`) is treated as a read of the length from the slice and captures the slice by `ImmBorrow`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x: &mut [u8] = &mut [];\
let c = || match x { // Captures `*x` by `ImmBorrow`.\
    &mut [] => (),\
//       ^^\
// This matches a slice of exactly zero elements. To know whether the\
// scrutinee matches, the length must be read, causing the slice to\
// be captured.\
    _ => (),\
};\
let _ = &mut *x; // ERROR: Cannot borrow `*x` as mutable.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x: &mut [u8] = &mut [];\
let c = || match x { // Does not capture `*x`.\
    [..] => (),\
//   ^^ Rest pattern.\
};\
let _ = &mut *x; // OK: `*x` can be borrow here.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Perhaps surprisingly, even though the length is contained in the (wide) _pointer_ to the slice, it is the place of the _pointee_ (the slice) that is treated as read and is captured.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn f<'l: 's, 's>(x: &'s mut &'l [u8]) -> impl Fn() + 'l {\
>     // The closure outlives `'l` because it captures `**x`. If\
>     // instead it captured `*x`, it would not live long enough\
>     // to satisfy the `impl Fn() + 'l` bound.\
>     || match *x { // Captures `**x` by `ImmBorrow`.\
>         &[] => (),\
>         _ => (),\
>     }\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> In this way, the behavior is consistent with dereferencing to the slice in the scrutinee.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn f<'l: 's, 's>(x: &'s mut &'l [u8]) -> impl Fn() + 'l {\
>     || match **x { // Captures `**x` by `ImmBorrow`.\
>         [] => (),\
>         _ => (),\
>     }\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> For details, see [Rust PR #138961](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/138961).\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns.arrays\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns.arrays "type.closure.capture.precision.slice-patterns.arrays")\
\
As the length of an array is fixed by its type, matching an array against a slice pattern does not by itself capture the place.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x: [u8; 1] = [0];\
let c = || match x { // Does not capture `x`.\
    [_] => (), // Length is fixed.\
};\
x; // OK: `x` can be moved here.\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.move-dereference\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.move-dereference "type.closure.capture.precision.move-dereference")\
\
### [Capturing references in move contexts](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#capturing-references-in-move-contexts)\
\
Because it is not allowed to move fields out of a reference, `move` closures will only capture the prefix of a capture path that runs up to, but not including, the first dereference of a reference. The reference itself will be moved into the closure.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct T(String, String);\
\
let mut t = T(String::from("foo"), String::from("bar"));\
let t_mut_ref = &mut t;\
let mut c = move || {\
    t_mut_ref.0.push_str("123"); // captures `t_mut_ref` ByValue\
};\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.raw-pointer-dereference\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.raw-pointer-dereference "type.closure.capture.precision.raw-pointer-dereference")\
\
### [Raw pointer dereference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#raw-pointer-dereference)\
\
Because it is `unsafe` to dereference a raw pointer, closures will only capture the prefix of a capture path that runs up to, but not including, the first dereference of a raw pointer.\
