\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct T(String, String);\
\
let t = T(String::from("foo"), String::from("bar"));\
let t_ptr = &t as *const T;\
\
let c = || unsafe {\
    println!("{}", (*t_ptr).0); // captures `t_ptr` by ImmBorrow\
};\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.union\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.union "type.closure.capture.precision.union")\
\
### [Union fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#union-fields)\
\
Because it is `unsafe` to access a union field, closures will only capture the prefix of a capture path that runs up to the union itself.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
union U {\
    a: (i32, i32),\
    b: bool,\
}\
let u = U { a: (123, 456) };\
\
let c = || {\
    let x = unsafe { u.a.0 }; // captures `u` ByValue\
};\
c();\
\
// This also includes writing to fields.\
let mut u = U { a: (123, 456) };\
\
let mut c = || {\
    u.b = true; // captures `u` with MutBorrow\
};\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.unaligned\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.unaligned "type.closure.capture.precision.unaligned")\
\
### [Reference into unaligned `struct`s](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reference-into-unaligned-structs)\
\
Because it is [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined) to create references to unaligned fields in a structure, closures will only capture the prefix of the capture path that runs up to, but not including, the first field access into a structure that uses [the `packed` representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-alignment-modifiers). This includes all fields, even those that are aligned, to protect against compatibility concerns should any of the fields in the structure change in the future.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(packed)]\
struct T(i32, i32);\
\
let t = T(2, 5);\
let c = || {\
    let a = t.0; // captures `t` with ImmBorrow\
};\
// Copies out of `t` are ok.\
let (a, b) = (t.0, t.1);\
c();\
}\
```\
\
Similarly, taking the address of an unaligned field also captures the entire struct:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(packed)]\
struct T(String, String);\
\
let mut t = T(String::new(), String::new());\
let c = || {\
    let a = std::ptr::addr_of!(t.1); // captures `t` with ImmBorrow\
};\
let a = t.0; // ERROR: cannot move out of `t.0` because it is borrowed\
c();\
}\
```\
\
but the above works if it is not packed since it captures the field precisely:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct T(String, String);\
\
let mut t = T(String::new(), String::new());\
let c = || {\
    let a = std::ptr::addr_of!(t.1); // captures `t.1` with ImmBorrow\
};\
// The move here is allowed.\
let a = t.0;\
c();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.box-deref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.box-deref "type.closure.capture.precision.box-deref")\
\
### [`Box` vs other `Deref` implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#box-vs-other-deref-implementations)\
\
The implementation of the [`Deref`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#deref-and-derefmut) trait for [`Box`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boxt) is treated differently from other `Deref` implementations, as it is considered a special entity.\
\
For example, let us look at examples involving `Rc` and `Box`. The `*rc` is desugared to a call to the trait method `deref` defined on `Rc`, but since `*box` is treated differently, it is possible to do a precise capture of the contents of the `Box`.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.box-non-move.not-moved\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.box-non-move.not-moved "type.closure.capture.precision.box-non-move.not-moved")\
\
#### [`Box` with non-`move` closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#box-with-non-move-closure)\
\
In a non-`move` closure, if the contents of the `Box` are not moved into the closure body, the contents of the `Box` are precisely captured.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S(String);\
\
let b = Box::new(S(String::new()));\
let c_box = || {\
    let x = &(*b).0; // captures `(*b).0` by ImmBorrow\
};\
c_box();\
\
// Contrast `Box` with another type that implements Deref:\
let r = std::rc::Rc::new(S(String::new()));\
let c_rc = || {\
    let x = &(*r).0; // captures `r` by ImmBorrow\
};\
c_rc();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.box-non-move.moved\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.box-non-move.moved "type.closure.capture.precision.box-non-move.moved")\
\
However, if the contents of the `Box` are moved into the closure, then the box is entirely captured. This is done so the amount of data that needs to be moved into the closure is minimized.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// This is the same as the example above except the closure\
// moves the value instead of taking a reference to it.\
\
struct S(String);\
\
let b = Box::new(S(String::new()));\
let c_box = || {\
    let x = (*b).0; // captures `b` with ByValue\
};\
c_box();\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.box-move.read\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.box-move.read "type.closure.capture.precision.box-move.read")\
\
#### [`Box` with move closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#box-with-move-closure)\
\
Similarly to moving contents of a `Box` in a non-`move` closure, reading the contents of a `Box` in a `move` closure will capture the `Box` entirely.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S(i32);\
\
let b = Box::new(S(10));\
let c_box = move || {\
    let x = (*b).0; // captures `b` with ByValue\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.unique-immutable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.unique-immutable "type.closure.unique-immutable")\
\
## [Unique immutable borrows in captures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unique-immutable-borrows-in-captures)\
\
Captures can occur by a special kind of borrow called a _unique immutable borrow_, which cannot be used anywhere else in the language and cannot be written out explicitly. It occurs when modifying the referent of a mutable reference, as in the following example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut b = false;\
let x = &mut b;\
let mut c = || {\
    // An ImmBorrow and a MutBorrow of `x`.\
    let a = &x;\
    *x = true; // `x` captured by UniqueImmBorrow\
};\
// The following line is an error:\
// let y = &x;\
c();\
// However, the following is OK.\
let z = &x;\
}\
```\
\
In this case, borrowing `x` mutably is not possible, because `x` is not `mut`. But at the same time, borrowing `x` immutably would make the assignment illegal, because a `& &mut` reference might not be unique, so it cannot safely be used to modify a value. So a unique immutable borrow is used: it borrows `x` immutably, but like a mutable borrow, it must be unique.\
\
In the above example, uncommenting the declaration of `y` will produce an error because it would violate the uniqueness of the closure’s borrow of `x`; the declaration of z is valid because the closure’s lifetime has expired at the end of the block, releasing the borrow.\
\
[\[type.closure.call\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.call "type.closure.call")\
\
## [Call traits and coercions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#call-traits-and-coercions)\
\
[\[type.closure.call.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.call.intro "type.closure.call.intro")\
\
Closure types all implement [`FnOnce`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html), indicating that they can be called once by consuming ownership of the closure. Additionally, some closures implement more specific call traits:\
\
[\[type.closure.call.fn-mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.call.fn-mut "type.closure.call.fn-mut")\
\
- A closure which does not move out of any captured variables implements [`FnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnMut.html), indicating that it can be called by mutable reference.\
\
[\[type.closure.call.fn\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.call.fn "type.closure.call.fn")\
\
- A closure which does not mutate or move out of any captured variables implements [`Fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.Fn.html), indicating that it can be called by shared reference.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `move` closures may still implement [`Fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.Fn.html) or [`FnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnMut.html), even though they capture variables by move. This is because the traits implemented by a closure type are determined by what the closure does with captured values, not how it captures them.\
\
[\[type.closure.non-capturing\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.non-capturing "type.closure.non-capturing")\
\
_Non-capturing closures_ are closures that don’t capture anything from their environment. Non-async, non-capturing closures can be coerced to function pointers (e.g., `fn()`) with the matching signature.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let add = |x, y| x + y;\
\
let mut x = add(5,7);\
\
type Binop = fn(i32, i32) -> i32;\
let bo: Binop = add;\
x = bo(5,7);\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.async.traits\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.async.traits "type.closure.async.traits")\
\
### [Async closure traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#async-closure-traits)\
\
[\[type.closure.async.traits.fn-family\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.async.traits.fn-family "type.closure.async.traits.fn-family")\
\
Async closures have a further restriction of whether or not they implement [`FnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnMut.html) or [`Fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.Fn.html).\
\
The [`Future`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/future/future/trait.Future.html) returned by the async closure has similar capturing characteristics as a closure. It captures place expressions from the async closure based on how they are used. The async closure is said to be _lending_ to its [`Future`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/future/future/trait.Future.html) if it has either of the following properties:\
\
- The `Future` includes a mutable capture.\
- The async closure captures by value, except when the value is accessed with a dereference projection.\
\
If the async closure is lending to its `Future`, then [`FnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnMut.html) and [`Fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.Fn.html) are _not_ implemented. [`FnOnce`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html) is always implemented.\
\
> **Example**: The first clause for a mutable capture can be illustrated with the following:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn takes_callback<Fut: Future>(c: impl FnMut() -> Fut) {}\
>\
> fn f() {\
>     let mut x = 1i32;\
>     let c = async || {\
>         x = 2;  // x captured with MutBorrow\
>     };\
>     takes_callback(c);  // ERROR: async closure does not implement `FnMut`\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> The second clause for a regular value capture can be illustrated with the following:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn takes_callback<Fut: Future>(c: impl Fn() -> Fut) {}\
>\
> fn f() {\
>     let x = &1i32;\
>     let c = async move || {\
>         let a = x + 2;  // x captured ByValue\
>     };\
>     takes_callback(c);  // ERROR: async closure does not implement `Fn`\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> The exception of the the second clause can be illustrated by using a dereference, which does allow `Fn` and `FnMut` to be implemented:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn takes_callback<Fut: Future>(c: impl Fn() -> Fut) {}\
>\
> fn f() {\
>     let x = &1i32;\
>     let c = async move || {\
>         let a = *x + 2;\
>     };\
>     takes_callback(c);  // OK: implements `Fn`\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[type.closure.async.traits.async-family\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.async.traits.async-family "type.closure.async.traits.async-family")\
\
Async closures implement [`AsyncFn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/async_function/trait.AsyncFn.html), [`AsyncFnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/async_function/trait.AsyncFnMut.html), and [`AsyncFnOnce`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/async_function/trait.AsyncFnOnce.html) in an analogous way as regular closures implement [`Fn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.Fn.html), [`FnMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnMut.html), and [`FnOnce`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/function/trait.FnOnce.html); that is, depending on the use of the captured variables in its body.\
\
[\[type.closure.traits\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.traits "type.closure.traits")\
\
### [Other traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#other-traits)\
\
[\[type.closure.traits.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.traits.intro "type.closure.traits.intro")\
\
All closure types implement [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sized-1). Additionally, closure types implement the following traits if allowed to do so by the types of the captures it stores:\
\
- [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#clone)\
- [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy)\
- [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sync)\
- [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#send)\
\
[\[type.closure.traits.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.traits.behavior "type.closure.traits.behavior")\
\
The rules for [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#send) and [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sync) match those for normal struct types, while [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#clone) and [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy) behave as if [derived](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive). For [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#clone), the order of cloning of the captured values is left unspecified.\
\
Because captures are often by reference, the following general rules arise:\
\
- A closure is [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sync) if all captured values are [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sync).\
- A closure is [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#send) if all values captured by non-unique immutable reference are [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sync), and all values captured by unique immutable or mutable reference, copy, or move are [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#send).\
- A closure is [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#clone) or [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy) if it does not capture any values by unique immutable or mutable reference, and if all values it captures by copy or move are [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#clone) or [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#copy), respectively.\
\
[\[type.closure.drop-order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.drop-order "type.closure.drop-order")\
\
## [Drop order](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#drop-order)\
\
If a closure captures a field of a composite types such as structs, tuples, and enums by value, the field’s lifetime would now be tied to the closure. As a result, it is possible for disjoint fields of a composite types to be dropped at different times.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
{\
    let tuple =\
      (String::from("foo"), String::from("bar")); // --+\
    { //                                               |\
        let c = || { // ----------------------------+  |\
            // tuple.0 is captured into the closure |  |\
            drop(tuple.0); //                       |  |\
        }; //                                       |  |\
    } // 'c' and 'tuple.0' dropped here ------------+  |\
} // tuple.1 dropped here -----------------------------+\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.entirety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.entirety "type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.entirety")\
\
## [Edition 2018 and before](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#edition-2018-and-before)\
\
### [Closure types difference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#closure-types-difference)\
\
In Edition 2018 and before, closures always capture a variable in its entirety, without its precise capture path. This means that for the example used in the [Closure types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types) section, the generated closure type would instead look something like this:\
\
```rust\
\
struct Closure<'a> {\
    rect : &'a mut Rectangle,\
}\
\
impl<'a> FnOnce<()> for Closure<'a> {\
    type Output = String;\
    extern "rust-call" fn call_once(self, args: ()) -> String {\
        self.rect.left_top.x += 1;\
        self.rect.right_bottom.x += 1;\
        format!("{:?}", self.rect.left_top)\
    }\
}\
```\
\
and the call to `f` would work as follows:\
\
```rust\
\
f(Closure { rect: rect });\
```\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.composite\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.composite "type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.composite")\
\
### [Capture precision difference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#capture-precision-difference)\
\
Composite types such as structs, tuples, and enums are always captured in its entirety, not by individual fields. As a result, it may be necessary to borrow into a local variable in order to capture a single field:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::collections::HashSet;\
\
struct SetVec {\
    set: HashSet<u32>,\
    vec: Vec<u32>\
}\
\
impl SetVec {\
    fn populate(&mut self) {\
        let vec = &mut self.vec;\
        self.set.iter().for_each(|&n| {\
            vec.push(n);\
        })\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
If, instead, the closure were to use `self.vec` directly, then it would attempt to capture `self` by mutable reference. But since `self.set` is already borrowed to iterate over, the code would not compile.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.move\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.move "type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.move")\
\
If the `move` keyword is used, then all captures are by move or, for `Copy` types, by copy, regardless of whether a borrow would work. The `move` keyword is usually used to allow the closure to outlive the captured values, such as if the closure is being returned or used to spawn a new thread.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.wildcard\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.wildcard "type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.wildcard")\
\
Regardless of if the data will be read by the closure, i.e. in case of wild card patterns, if a variable defined outside the closure is mentioned within the closure the variable will be captured in its entirety.\
\
[\[type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.drop-order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.drop-order "type.closure.capture.precision.edition2018.drop-order")\
\
### [Drop order difference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#drop-order-difference)\
\
As composite types are captured in their entirety, a closure which captures one of those composite types by value would drop the entire captured variable at the same time as the closure gets dropped.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
{\
    let tuple =\
      (String::from("foo"), String::from("bar"));\
    {\
        let c = || { // --------------------------+\
            // tuple is captured into the closure |\
            drop(tuple.0); //                     |\
        }; //                                     |\
    } // 'c' and 'tuple' dropped here ------------+\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer "type.pointer")\
\
# [Pointer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pointer-types)\
\
[\[type.pointer.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.intro "type.pointer.intro")\
\
All pointers are explicit first-class values. They can be moved or copied, stored into data structs, and returned from functions.\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference "type.pointer.reference")\
\
## [References (`&` and `&mut`)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#references--and-mut)\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference.syntax "type.pointer.reference.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ReferenceType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ReferenceType) → &[Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Lifetime)?mut?[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeNoBounds)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ReferenceType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ReferenceType)\
&[Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Lifetime)\
mut[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeNoBounds)\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference.shared\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference.shared "type.pointer.reference.shared")\
\
### [Shared references (`&`)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#shared-references-)\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference.shared.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference.shared.intro "type.pointer.reference.shared.intro")\
\
Shared references point to memory which is owned by some other value.\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference.shared.constraint-mutation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference.shared.constraint-mutation "type.pointer.reference.shared.constraint-mutation")\
\
When a shared reference to a value is created, it prevents direct mutation of the value. [Interior mutability](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#interior-mutability) provides an exception for this in certain circumstances. As the name suggests, any number of shared references to a value may exist. A shared reference type is written `&type`, or `&'a type` when you need to specify an explicit lifetime.\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference.shared.copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference.shared.copy "type.pointer.reference.shared.copy")\
\
Copying a reference is a “shallow” operation: it involves only copying the pointer itself, that is, pointers are `Copy`. Releasing a reference has no effect on the value it points to, but referencing of a [temporary value](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#temporaries) will keep it alive during the scope of the reference itself.\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference.mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference.mut "type.pointer.reference.mut")\
\
### [Mutable references (`&mut`)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#mutable-references-mut)\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference.mut.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference.mut.intro "type.pointer.reference.mut.intro")\
\
Mutable references point to memory which is owned by some other value. A mutable reference type is written `&mut type` or `&'a mut type`.\
\
[\[type.pointer.reference.mut.copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.reference.mut.copy "type.pointer.reference.mut.copy")\
\
A mutable reference (that hasn’t been borrowed) is the only way to access the value it points to, so is not `Copy`.\
\
[\[type.pointer.raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.raw "type.pointer.raw")\
\
## [Raw pointers (`*const` and `*mut`)](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#raw-pointers-const-and-mut)\
\
[\[type.pointer.raw.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.raw.syntax "type.pointer.raw.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[RawPointerType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-RawPointerType) → \* ( mut \| const ) [TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeNoBounds)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[RawPointerType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-RawPointerType)\
\*\
mut\
const[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeNoBounds)\
\
[\[type.pointer.raw.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.raw.intro "type.pointer.raw.intro")\
\
Raw pointers are pointers without safety or liveness guarantees. Raw pointers are written as `*const T` or `*mut T`. For example `*const i32` means a raw pointer to a 32-bit integer.\
\
[\[type.pointer.raw.copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.raw.copy "type.pointer.raw.copy")\
\
Copying or dropping a raw pointer has no effect on the lifecycle of any other value.\
\
[\[type.pointer.raw.safety\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.raw.safety "type.pointer.raw.safety")\
\
Dereferencing a raw pointer is an [`unsafe` operation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unsafety).\
\
This can also be used to convert a raw pointer to a reference by reborrowing it (`&*` or `&mut *`). Raw pointers are generally discouraged; they exist to support interoperability with foreign code, and writing performance-critical or low-level functions.\
\
[\[type.pointer.raw.cmp\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.raw.cmp "type.pointer.raw.cmp")\
\
When comparing raw pointers they are compared by their address, rather than by what they point to. When comparing raw pointers to [dynamically sized types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#dynamically-sized-types) they also have their additional data compared.\
\
[\[type.pointer.raw.constructor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.raw.constructor "type.pointer.raw.constructor")\
\
Raw pointers can be created directly using `&raw const` for `*const` pointers and `&raw mut` for `*mut` pointers.\
\
[\[type.pointer.smart\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.smart "type.pointer.smart")\
\
## [Smart pointers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#smart-pointers)\
\
The standard library contains additional ‘smart pointer’ types beyond references and raw pointers.\
\
[\[type.pointer.validity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.validity "type.pointer.validity")\
\
## [Bit validity](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#bit-validity-2)\
\
[\[type.pointer.validity.pointer-fragment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.validity.pointer-fragment "type.pointer.validity.pointer-fragment")\
\
Despite pointers and references being similar to `usize`s in the machine code emitted on most platforms, the semantics of transmuting a reference or pointer type to a non-pointer type is currently undecided. Thus, it may not be valid to transmute a pointer or reference type, `P`, to a `[u8; size_of::<P>()]`.\
\
[\[type.pointer.validity.raw\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.pointer.validity.raw "type.pointer.validity.raw")\
\
For thin raw pointers (i.e., for `P = *const T` or `P = *mut T` for `T: Sized`), the inverse direction (transmuting from an integer or array of integers to `P`) is always valid. However, the pointer produced via such a transmutation may not be dereferenced (not even if `T` has size zero).\
\
[\[type.fn-pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-pointer "type.fn-pointer")\
\
# [Function pointer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#function-pointer-types)\
\
[\[type.fn-pointer.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-pointer.syntax "type.fn-pointer.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[BareFunctionType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BareFunctionType) →\
\
[ForLifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ForLifetimes)?[FunctionTypeQualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FunctionTypeQualifiers)fn\
\
([FunctionParametersMaybeNamedVariadic](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-FunctionParametersMaybeNamedVariadic)?)[BareFunctionReturnType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BareFunctionReturnType)?\
\
[FunctionTypeQualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FunctionTypeQualifiers) → unsafe? ( extern[Abi](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Abi)? )?\
\
[BareFunctionReturnType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BareFunctionReturnType) → ->[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeNoBounds)\
\
[FunctionParametersMaybeNamedVariadic](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FunctionParametersMaybeNamedVariadic) →\
\
[MaybeNamedFunctionParameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MaybeNamedFunctionParameters) \| [MaybeNamedFunctionParametersVariadic](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MaybeNamedFunctionParametersVariadic)\
\
[MaybeNamedFunctionParameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MaybeNamedFunctionParameters) →\
\
[MaybeNamedParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MaybeNamedParam) ( ,[MaybeNamedParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MaybeNamedParam) )\*,?\
\
[MaybeNamedParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MaybeNamedParam) →\
\
[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\* ( ( [IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| \_ ) : )?[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)\
\
[MaybeNamedFunctionParametersVariadic](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-MaybeNamedFunctionParametersVariadic) →\
\
    ( [MaybeNamedParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MaybeNamedParam), )\*[MaybeNamedParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MaybeNamedParam),[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-OuterAttribute)\*...\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[BareFunctionType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BareFunctionType)[ForLifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ForLifetimes)[FunctionTypeQualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FunctionTypeQualifiers)\

([FunctionParametersMaybeNamedVariadic](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-FunctionParametersMaybeNamedVariadic)\
)[BareFunctionReturnType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BareFunctionReturnType)\

\

\

,\
\
[MaybeNamedParam](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MaybeNamedParam)[OuterAttribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-OuterAttribute)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
\_\
:[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
\
