...\
\
[\[type.fn-pointer.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-pointer.intro "type.fn-pointer.intro")\
\
A function pointer type, written using the `fn` keyword, refers to a function whose identity is not necessarily known at compile-time.\
\
An example where `Binop` is defined as a function pointer type:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn add(x: i32, y: i32) -> i32 {\
    x + y\
}\
\
let mut x = add(5,7);\
\
type Binop = fn(i32, i32) -> i32;\
let bo: Binop = add;\
x = bo(5,7);\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.fn-pointer.coercion\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-pointer.coercion "type.fn-pointer.coercion")\
\
Function pointers can be created via a coercion from both [function items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-item-types) and non-capturing, non-async [closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types).\
\
[\[type.fn-pointer.qualifiers\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-pointer.qualifiers "type.fn-pointer.qualifiers")\
\
The `unsafe` qualifier indicates that the type’s value is an [unsafe function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-unsafe-keyword), and the `extern` qualifier indicates it is an [extern function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-function-qualifier).\
\
[\[type.fn-pointer.constraint-variadic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-pointer.constraint-variadic "type.fn-pointer.constraint-variadic")\
\
For the function to be variadic, its `extern` ABI must be one of those listed in [items.extern.variadic.conventions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.extern.variadic.conventions).\
\
[\[type.fn-pointer.attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.fn-pointer.attributes "type.fn-pointer.attributes")\
\
## [Attributes on function pointer parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#attributes-on-function-pointer-parameters)\
\
Attributes on function pointer parameters follow the same rules and restrictions as [regular function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-on-function-parameters).\
\
[\[type.trait-object\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object "type.trait-object")\
\
# [Trait objects](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#trait-objects)\
\
[\[type.trait-object.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.syntax "type.trait-object.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[TraitObjectType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TraitObjectType) → dyn?[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds)\
\
[TraitObjectTypeOneBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TraitObjectTypeOneBound) → dyn?[TraitBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TraitBound)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TraitObjectType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TraitObjectType)\
dyn[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeParamBounds)\
\
[TraitObjectTypeOneBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TraitObjectTypeOneBound)\
dyn[TraitBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TraitBound)\
\
[\[type.trait-object.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.intro "type.trait-object.intro")\
\
A _trait object_ is an opaque value of another type that implements a set of traits. The set of traits is made up of a [dyn compatible](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#dyn-compatibility) _base trait_ plus any number of [auto traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#auto-traits).\
\
[\[type.trait-object.impls\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.impls "type.trait-object.impls")\
\
Trait objects implement the base trait, its auto traits, and any [supertraits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#supertraits) of the base trait.\
\
[\[type.trait-object.name\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.name "type.trait-object.name")\
\
Trait objects are written as the keyword `dyn` followed by a set of trait bounds, but with the following restrictions on the trait bounds.\
\
[\[type.trait-object.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.constraint "type.trait-object.constraint")\
\
There may not be more than one non-auto trait, no more than one lifetime, and opt-out bounds (e.g. `?Sized`) are not allowed. Furthermore, paths to traits may be parenthesized.\
\
For example, given a trait `Trait`, the following are all trait objects:\
\
- `dyn Trait`\
- `dyn Trait + Send`\
- `dyn Trait + Send + Sync`\
- `dyn Trait + 'static`\
- `dyn Trait + Send + 'static`\
- `dyn Trait +`\
- `dyn 'static + Trait`.\
- `dyn (Trait)`\
\
[\[type.trait-object.syntax-edition2021\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.syntax-edition2021 "type.trait-object.syntax-edition2021")\
\
> 2021 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2021 edition, the `dyn` keyword may be omitted.\
\
[\[type.trait-object.syntax-edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.syntax-edition2018 "type.trait-object.syntax-edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, if the first bound of the trait object is a path that starts with `::`, then the `dyn` will be treated as a part of the path. The first path can be put in parenthesis to get around this. As such, if you want a trait object with the trait `::your_module::Trait`, you should write it as `dyn (::your_module::Trait)`.\
>\
> Beginning in the 2018 edition, `dyn` is a true keyword and is not allowed in paths, so the parentheses are not necessary.\
\
[\[type.trait-object.alias\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.alias "type.trait-object.alias")\
\
Two trait object types alias each other if the base traits alias each other and if the sets of auto traits are the same and the lifetime bounds are the same. For example, `dyn Trait + Send + UnwindSafe` is the same as `dyn Trait + UnwindSafe + Send`.\
\
[\[type.trait-object.unsized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.unsized "type.trait-object.unsized")\
\
Due to the opaqueness of which concrete type the value is of, trait objects are [dynamically sized types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#dynamically-sized-types). Like all DSTs, trait objects are used behind some type of pointer; for example `&dyn SomeTrait` or `Box<dyn SomeTrait>`. Each instance of a pointer to a trait object includes:\
\
- a pointer to an instance of a type `T` that implements `SomeTrait`\
- a _virtual method table_, often just called a _vtable_, which contains, for each method of `SomeTrait` and its [supertraits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#supertraits) that `T` implements, a pointer to `T`’s implementation (i.e. a function pointer).\
\
The purpose of trait objects is to permit “late binding” of methods. Calling a method on a trait object results in virtual dispatch at runtime: that is, a function pointer is loaded from the trait object vtable and invoked indirectly. The actual implementation for each vtable entry can vary on an object-by-object basis.\
\
An example of a trait object:\
\
```rust\
\
trait Printable {\
    fn stringify(&self) -> String;\
}\
\
impl Printable for i32 {\
    fn stringify(&self) -> String { self.to_string() }\
}\
\
fn print(a: Box<dyn Printable>) {\
    println!("{}", a.stringify());\
}\
\
fn main() {\
    print(Box::new(10) as Box<dyn Printable>);\
}\
```\
\
In this example, the trait `Printable` occurs as a trait object in both the type signature of `print`, and the cast expression in `main`.\
\
[\[type.trait-object.lifetime-bounds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.trait-object.lifetime-bounds "type.trait-object.lifetime-bounds")\
\
## [Trait object lifetime bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#trait-object-lifetime-bounds)\
\
Since a trait object can contain references, the lifetimes of those references need to be expressed as part of the trait object. This lifetime is written as `Trait + 'a`. There are [defaults](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#default-trait-object-lifetimes) that allow this lifetime to usually be inferred with a sensible choice.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait "type.impl-trait")\
\
# [Impl trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#impl-trait)\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.syntax "type.impl-trait.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ImplTraitType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ImplTraitType) → impl[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds)\
\
[ImplTraitTypeOneBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ImplTraitTypeOneBound) → impl[TraitBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TraitBound)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ImplTraitType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ImplTraitType)\
impl[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeParamBounds)\
\
[ImplTraitTypeOneBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ImplTraitTypeOneBound)\
impl[TraitBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TraitBound)\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.intro "type.impl-trait.intro")\
\
`impl Trait` provides ways to specify unnamed but concrete types that implement a specific trait. It can appear in two sorts of places: argument position (where it can act as an anonymous type parameter to functions), and return position (where it can act as an abstract return type).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Trait {}\
impl Trait for () {}\
\
// argument position: anonymous type parameter\
fn foo(arg: impl Trait) {\
}\
\
// return position: abstract return type\
fn bar() -> impl Trait {\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.param\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.param "type.impl-trait.param")\
\
## [Anonymous type parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#anonymous-type-parameters)\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is often called “impl Trait in argument position”. (The term “parameter” is more correct here, but “impl Trait in argument position” is the phrasing used during the development of this feature, and it remains in parts of the implementation.)\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.param.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.param.intro "type.impl-trait.param.intro")\
\
Functions can use `impl` followed by a set of trait bounds to declare a parameter as having an anonymous type. The caller must provide a type that satisfies the bounds declared by the anonymous type parameter, and the function can only use the methods available through the trait bounds of the anonymous type parameter.\
\
For example, these two forms are almost equivalent:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Trait {}\
\
// generic type parameter\
fn with_generic_type<T: Trait>(arg: T) {\
}\
\
// impl Trait in argument position\
fn with_impl_trait(arg: impl Trait) {\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.param.generic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.param.generic "type.impl-trait.param.generic")\
\
That is, `impl Trait` in argument position is syntactic sugar for a generic type parameter like `<T: Trait>`, except that the type is anonymous and doesn’t appear in the [GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams) list.\
\
> Note\
>\
> For function parameters, generic type parameters and `impl Trait` are not exactly equivalent. With a generic parameter such as `<T: Trait>`, the caller has the option to explicitly specify the generic argument for `T` at the call site using [GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgs), for example, `foo::<usize>(1)`. Changing a parameter from either one to the other can constitute a breaking change for the callers of a function, since this changes the number of generic arguments.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.return\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.return "type.impl-trait.return")\
\
## [Abstract return types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#abstract-return-types)\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is often called “impl Trait in return position”.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.return.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.return.intro "type.impl-trait.return.intro")\
\
Functions can use `impl Trait` to return an abstract return type. These types stand in for another concrete type where the caller may only use the methods declared by the specified `Trait`.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.return.constraint-body\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.return.constraint-body "type.impl-trait.return.constraint-body")\
\
Each possible return value from the function must resolve to the same concrete type.\
\
`impl Trait` in return position allows a function to return an unboxed abstract type. This is particularly useful with [closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types) and iterators. For example, closures have a unique, un-writable type. Previously, the only way to return a closure from a function was to use a [trait object](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects):\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn returns_closure() -> Box<dyn Fn(i32) -> i32> {\
    Box::new(|x| x + 1)\
}\
}\
```\
\
This could incur performance penalties from heap allocation and dynamic dispatch. It wasn’t possible to fully specify the type of the closure, only to use the `Fn` trait. That means that the trait object is necessary. However, with `impl Trait`, it is possible to write this more simply:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn returns_closure() -> impl Fn(i32) -> i32 {\
    |x| x + 1\
}\
}\
```\
\
which also avoids the drawbacks of using a boxed trait object.\
\
Similarly, the concrete types of iterators could become very complex, incorporating the types of all previous iterators in a chain. Returning `impl Iterator` means that a function only exposes the `Iterator` trait as a bound on its return type, instead of explicitly specifying all of the other iterator types involved.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.return-in-trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.return-in-trait "type.impl-trait.return-in-trait")\
\
## [Return-position `impl Trait` in traits and trait implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#return-position-impl-trait-in-traits-and-trait-implementations)\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.return-in-trait.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.return-in-trait.intro "type.impl-trait.return-in-trait.intro")\
\
Functions in traits may also use `impl Trait` as a syntax for an anonymous associated type.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.return-in-trait.desugaring\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.return-in-trait.desugaring "type.impl-trait.return-in-trait.desugaring")\
\
Every `impl Trait` in the return type of an associated function in a trait is desugared to an anonymous associated type. The return type that appears in the implementation’s function signature is used to determine the value of the associated type.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-captures\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-captures "type.impl-trait.generic-captures")\
\
## [Capturing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#capturing)\
\
Behind each return-position `impl Trait` abstract type is some hidden concrete type. For this concrete type to use a generic parameter, that generic parameter must be _captured_ by the abstract type.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.auto\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.auto "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.auto")\
\
## [Automatic capturing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#automatic-capturing)\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.auto.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.auto.intro "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.auto.intro")\
\
Return-position `impl Trait` abstract types automatically capture all in-scope generic parameters, including generic type, const, and lifetime parameters (including higher-ranked ones).\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.edition2024\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.edition2024 "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.edition2024")\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2024 edition, on free functions and on associated functions and methods of inherent impls, generic lifetime parameters that do not appear in the bounds of the abstract return type are not automatically captured.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise")\
\
## [Precise capturing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#precise-capturing)\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.use "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.use")\
\
The set of generic parameters captured by a return-position `impl Trait` abstract type may be explicitly controlled with a [`use<..>` bound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-bounds). If present, only the generic parameters listed in the `use<..>` bound will be captured. E.g.:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn capture<'a, 'b, T>(x: &'a (), y: T) -> impl Sized + use<'a, T> {\
  //                                      ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\
  //                                     Captures `'a` and `T` only.\
  (x, y)\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-single\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-single "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-single")\
\
Currently, only one `use<..>` bound may be present in a bounds list, all in-scope type and const generic parameters must be included, and all lifetime parameters that appear in other bounds of the abstract type must be included.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-lifetime\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-lifetime "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-lifetime")\
\
Within the `use<..>` bound, any lifetime parameters present must appear before all type and const generic parameters, and the elided lifetime (`'_`) may be present if it is otherwise allowed to appear within the `impl Trait` return type.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-param-impl-trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-param-impl-trait "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-param-impl-trait")\
\
Because all in-scope type parameters must be included by name, a `use<..>` bound may not be used in the signature of items that use argument-position `impl Trait`, as those items have anonymous type parameters in scope.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-in-trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-in-trait "type.impl-trait.generic-capture.precise.constraint-in-trait")\
\
Any `use<..>` bound that is present in an associated function in a trait definition must include all generic parameters of the trait, including the implicit `Self` generic type parameter of the trait.\
\
## [Differences between generics and `impl Trait` in return position](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#differences-between-generics-and-impl-trait-in-return-position)\
\
In argument position, `impl Trait` is very similar in semantics to a generic type parameter. However, there are significant differences between the two in return position. With `impl Trait`, unlike with a generic type parameter, the function chooses the return type, and the caller cannot choose the return type.\
\
The function:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Trait {}\
fn foo<T: Trait>() -> T {\
    // ...\
panic!()\
}\
}\
```\
\
allows the caller to determine the return type, `T`, and the function returns that type.\
\
The function:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Trait {}\
impl Trait for () {}\
fn foo() -> impl Trait {\
    // ...\
}\
}\
```\
\
doesn’t allow the caller to determine the return type. Instead, the function chooses the return type, but only promises that it will implement `Trait`.\
\
[\[type.impl-trait.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.impl-trait.constraint "type.impl-trait.constraint")\
\
## [Limitations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#limitations-1)\
\
`impl Trait` can only appear as a parameter or return type of a non-`extern` function. It cannot be the type of a `let` binding, field type, or appear inside a type alias.\
\
[\[type.generic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.generic "type.generic")\
\
# [Type parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#type-parameters)\
\
Within the body of an item that has type parameter declarations, the names of its type parameters are types:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn to_vec<A: Clone>(xs: &[A]) -> Vec<A> {\
    if xs.is_empty() {\
        return vec![];\
    }\
    let first: A = xs[0].clone();\
    let mut rest: Vec<A> = to_vec(&xs[1..]);\
    rest.insert(0, first);\
    rest\
}\
}\
```\
\
Here, `first` has type `A`, referring to `to_vec`’s `A` type parameter; and `rest` has type `Vec<A>`, a vector with element type `A`.\
\
[\[type.inferred\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.inferred "type.inferred")\
\
# [Inferred type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#inferred-type)\
\
[\[type.inferred.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.inferred.syntax "type.inferred.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[InferredType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-InferredType) → \_\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[InferredType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InferredType)\
\_\
\
[\[type.inferred.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.inferred.intro "type.inferred.intro")\
\
The inferred type asks the compiler to infer the type if possible based on the surrounding information available.\
\
> Example\
>\
> The inferred type is often used in generic arguments:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> let x: Vec<_> = (0..10).collect();\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[type.inferred.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.inferred.constraint "type.inferred.constraint")\
\
The inferred type cannot be used in item signatures.\
\
[\[dynamic-sized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-dynamic-sized "dynamic-sized")\
\
# [Dynamically sized types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#dynamically-sized-types)\
\
[\[dynamic-sized.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-dynamic-sized.intro "dynamic-sized.intro")\
\
Most types have a fixed size that is known at compile time and implement the trait [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#sized-1). A type with a size that is known only at run-time is called a _dynamically sized type_ ( _DST_) or, informally, an unsized type. [Slices](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-types), [trait objects](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects), and [str](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-slice-type) are examples of DSTs.\
\
[\[dynamic-sized.restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-dynamic-sized.restriction "dynamic-sized.restriction")\
\
Such types can only be used in certain cases:\
\
[\[dynamic-sized.pointer-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-dynamic-sized.pointer-types "dynamic-sized.pointer-types")\
\
- [Pointer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#pointer-types) to DSTs are sized but have twice the size of pointers to sized types\
\
  - Pointers to slices and `str` also store the number of elements.\
  - Pointers to trait objects also store a pointer to a vtable.\
\
[\[dynamic-sized.question-sized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-dynamic-sized.question-sized "dynamic-sized.question-sized")\
\
- DSTs can be provided as type arguments to generic type parameters having the special `?Sized` bound. They can also be used for associated type definitions when the corresponding associated type declaration has a `?Sized` bound. By default, any type parameter or associated type has a `Sized` bound, unless it is relaxed using `?Sized`.\
\
[\[dynamic-sized.trait-impl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-dynamic-sized.trait-impl "dynamic-sized.trait-impl")\
\
- Traits may be implemented for DSTs. Unlike with generic type parameters, `Self: ?Sized` is the default in trait definitions.\
\
[\[dynamic-sized.struct-field\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-dynamic-sized.struct-field "dynamic-sized.struct-field")\
\
- Structs may contain a DST as the last field; this makes the struct itself a DST.\
\
> Note\
>\
> [Variables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variables), function parameters, [const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items) items, and [static](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items) items must be `Sized`.\
\
[\[layout\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout "layout")\
\
# [Type layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#type-layout)\
\
[\[layout.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.intro "layout.intro")\
\
The layout of a type is its size, alignment, and the relative offsets of its fields. For enums, how the discriminant is laid out and interpreted is also part of type layout.\
\
[\[layout.guarantees\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.guarantees "layout.guarantees")\
\
Type layout can be changed with each compilation. Instead of trying to document exactly what is done, we only document what is guaranteed today.\
\
Note that even types with the same layout can still differ in how they are passed across function boundaries. For function call ABI compatibility of types, see [here](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/primitive.fn.html#abi-compatibility).\
\
[\[layout.properties\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.properties "layout.properties")\
\
## [Size and alignment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#size-and-alignment)\
\
All values have an alignment and size.\
\
[\[layout.properties.align\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.properties.align "layout.properties.align")\
\
The _alignment_ of a value specifies what addresses are valid to store the value at. A value of alignment `n` must only be stored at an address that is a multiple of n. For example, a value with an alignment of 2 must be stored at an even address, while a value with an alignment of 1 can be stored at any address. Alignment is measured in bytes, and must be at least 1, and always a power of 2. The alignment of a value can be checked with the [`align_of_val`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/fn.align_of_val.html) function.\
\
[\[layout.properties.size\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.properties.size "layout.properties.size")\
\
The _size_ of a value is the offset in bytes between successive elements in an array with that item type including alignment padding. The size of a value is always a multiple of its alignment. Note that some types are zero-sized; 0 is considered a multiple of any alignment (for example, on some platforms, the type `[u16; 0]` has size 0 and alignment 2). The size of a value can be checked with the [`size_of_val`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/fn.size_of_val.html) function.\
\
[\[layout.properties.sized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.properties.sized "layout.properties.sized")\
\
Types where all values have the same size and alignment, and both are known at compile time, implement the [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Sized.html) trait and can be checked with the [`size_of`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/fn.size_of.html) and [`align_of`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/fn.align_of.html) functions. Types that are not [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Sized.html) are known as [dynamically sized types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#dynamically-sized-types). Since all values of a `Sized` type share the same size and alignment, we refer to those shared values as the size of the type and the alignment of the type respectively.\
\
[\[layout.primitive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.primitive "layout.primitive")\
\
## [Primitive data layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#primitive-data-layout)\
\
[\[layout.primitive.size\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.primitive.size "layout.primitive.size")\
\
The size of most primitives is given in this table.\
\
| Type | `size_of::<Type>()` |\
| --- | --- |\
| `bool` | 1 |\
| `u8` / `i8` | 1 |\
| `u16` / `i16` | 2 |\
| `u32` / `i32` | 4 |\
| `u64` / `i64` | 8 |\
| `u128` / `i128` | 16 |\
| `usize` / `isize` | See below |\
| `f32` | 4 |\
| `f64` | 8 |\
| `char` | 4 |\
\
[\[layout.primitive.size-minimum\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.primitive.size-minimum "layout.primitive.size-minimum")\
\
`usize` and `isize` have a size big enough to contain every address on the target platform. For example, on a 32 bit target, this is 4 bytes, and on a 64 bit target, this is 8 bytes.\
\
[\[layout.primitive.size-align\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.primitive.size-align "layout.primitive.size-align")\
\
`usize` and `isize` have the same size and alignment.\
\
[\[layout.primitive.platform-specific-alignment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.primitive.platform-specific-alignment "layout.primitive.platform-specific-alignment")\
\
The alignment of primitives is platform-specific. In most cases, their alignment is equal to their size, but it may be less. In particular, `i128` and `u128` are often aligned to 4 or 8 bytes even though their size is 16, and on many 32-bit platforms, `i64`, `u64`, and `f64` are only aligned to 4 bytes, not 8.\
\
[\[layout.primitive.integer-alignment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.primitive.integer-alignment "layout.primitive.integer-alignment")\
\
Alignment is guaranteed to be the same for fixed-width signed and unsigned integer variants of the same indicated size — that is, for a given size `N`, `align_of::<uN>() == align_of::<iN>()`.\
\
[\[layout.pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.pointer "layout.pointer")\
\
## [Pointers and references layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pointers-and-references-layout)\
\
[\[layout.pointer.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.pointer.intro "layout.pointer.intro")\
\
Pointers and references have the same layout. Mutability of the pointer or reference does not change the layout.\
\
[\[layout.pointer.thin\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.pointer.thin "layout.pointer.thin")\
\
Pointers to sized types have the same size and alignment as `usize`.\
\
[\[layout.pointer.unsized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.pointer.unsized "layout.pointer.unsized")\
\
Pointers to unsized types are sized. The size and alignment of a pointer to an unsized type are each guaranteed to be greater than or equal to those of a pointer to a sized type.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Though you should not rely on this, all pointers to DSTs are currently twice the size of the size of `usize` and have the same alignment.\
\
[\[layout.array\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.array "layout.array")\
\
## [Array layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#array-layout)\
\
An array of `[T; N]` has a size of `size_of::<T>() * N` and the same alignment of `T`. Arrays are laid out so that the zero-based `nth` element of the array is offset from the start of the array by `n * size_of::<T>()` bytes.\
\
[\[layout.slice\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.slice "layout.slice")\
\
## [Slice layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#slice-layout)\
\
Slices have the same layout as the section of the array they slice.\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is about the raw `[T]` type, not pointers (`&[T]`, `Box<[T]>`, etc.) to slices.\
\
[\[layout.str\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.str "layout.str")\
\
## [`str` Layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#str-layout)\
\
String slices are a UTF-8 representation of characters that have the same layout as slices of type `[u8]`. A reference `&str` has the same layout as a reference `&[u8]`.\
\
[\[layout.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.tuple "layout.tuple")\
\
## [Tuple layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tuple-layout)\
\
[\[layout.tuple.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.tuple.def "layout.tuple.def")\
\
Tuples are laid out according to the [`Rust` representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-rust-representation).\
\
[\[layout.tuple.unit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.tuple.unit "layout.tuple.unit")\
\
The exception to this is the unit tuple (`()`), which is guaranteed as a zero-sized type to have a size of 0 and an alignment of 1.\
\
[\[layout.trait-object\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.trait-object "layout.trait-object")\
\
## [Trait object layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#trait-object-layout)\
\
Trait objects have the same layout as the value the trait object is of.\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is about the raw trait object types, not pointers (`&dyn Trait`, `Box<dyn Trait>`, etc.) to trait objects.\
\
[\[layout.closure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.closure "layout.closure")\
\
## [Closure layout](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#closure-layout)\
\
Closures have no layout guarantees.\
\
[\[layout.repr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr "layout.repr")\
\
## [Representations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#representations)\
\
[\[layout.repr.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.intro "layout.repr.intro")\
\
All user-defined composite types (`struct`s, `enum`s, and `union`s) have a _representation_ that specifies what the layout is for the type.\
\
[\[layout.repr.kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.kinds "layout.repr.kinds")\
\
The possible representations for a type are:\
\
- [`Rust`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-rust-representation) (default)\
- [`C`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-c-representation)\
- The [primitive representations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#primitive-representations)\
- [`transparent`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-transparent-representation)\
\
[\[layout.repr.attribute\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.attribute "layout.repr.attribute")\
\
The representation of a type can be changed by applying the `repr` attribute to it. The following example shows a struct with a `C` representation.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(C)]\
struct ThreeInts {\
    first: i16,\
    second: i8,\
    third: i32\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[layout.repr.align-packed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.align-packed "layout.repr.align-packed")\
\
The alignment may be raised or lowered with the `align` and `packed` modifiers respectively. They alter the representation specified in the attribute. If no representation is specified, the default one is altered.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Default representation, alignment lowered to 2.\
#[repr(packed(2))]\
struct PackedStruct {\
    first: i16,\
    second: i8,\
    third: i32\
}\
\
// C representation, alignment raised to 8\
#[repr(C, align(8))]\
struct AlignedStruct {\
    first: i16,\
    second: i8,\
    third: i32\
}\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> As a consequence of the representation being an attribute on the item, the representation does not depend on generic parameters. Any two types with the same name have the same representation. For example, `Foo<Bar>` and `Foo<Baz>` both have the same representation.\
\
[\[layout.repr.inter-field\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.inter-field "layout.repr.inter-field")\
\
The representation of a type can change the padding between fields, but does not change the layout of the fields themselves. For example, a struct with a `C` representation that contains a struct `Inner` with the `Rust` representation will not change the layout of `Inner`.\
\
[\[layout.repr.rust\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.rust "layout.repr.rust")\
\
### [The `Rust` representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-rust-representation)\
\
[\[layout.repr.rust.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.rust.intro "layout.repr.rust.intro")\
\
The `Rust` representation is the default representation for nominal types without a `repr` attribute. Using this representation explicitly through a `repr` attribute is guaranteed to be the same as omitting the attribute entirely.\
\
[\[layout.repr.rust.layout\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.rust.layout "layout.repr.rust.layout")\
\
The only data layout guarantees made by this representation are those required for soundness. These are:\
\
1. The offset of a field is divisible by that field’s alignment.\
2. The alignment of the type is at least the maximum alignment of its fields.\
\
[\[layout.repr.rust.layout.struct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.rust.layout.struct "layout.repr.rust.layout.struct")\
\
For [structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), it is further guaranteed that the fields do not overlap. That is, the fields can be ordered such that the offset plus the size of any field is less than or equal to the offset of the next field in the ordering. The ordering does not have to be the same as the order in which the fields are specified in the declaration of the type.\
\
Be aware that this guarantee does not imply that the fields have distinct addresses: zero-sized types may have the same address as other fields in the same struct.\
\
[\[layout.repr.rust.unspecified\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.rust.unspecified "layout.repr.rust.unspecified")\
\
There are no other guarantees of data layout made by this representation.\
\
[\[layout.repr.c\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c "layout.repr.c")\
\
### [The `C` representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-c-representation)\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.intro "layout.repr.c.intro")\
\
The `C` representation is designed for dual purposes. One purpose is for creating types that are interoperable with the C Language. The second purpose is to create types that you can soundly perform operations on that rely on data layout such as reinterpreting values as a different type.\
\
Because of this dual purpose, it is possible to create types that are not useful for interfacing with the C programming language.\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.constraint "layout.repr.c.constraint")\
\
This representation can be applied to structs, unions, and enums. The exception is [zero-variant enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#zero-variant-enums) for which the `C` representation is an error.\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.struct\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.struct "layout.repr.c.struct")\
\
#### [`\#[repr(C)]` Structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reprc-structs)\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.struct.align\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.struct.align "layout.repr.c.struct.align")\
\
The alignment of the struct is the alignment of the most-aligned field in it.\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.struct.size-field-offset\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.struct.size-field-offset "layout.repr.c.struct.size-field-offset")\
\
The size and offset of fields is determined by the following algorithm.\
\
Start with a current offset of 0 bytes.\
\
For each field in declaration order in the struct, first determine the size and alignment of the field. If the current offset is not a multiple of the field’s alignment, then add padding bytes to the current offset until it is a multiple of the field’s alignment. The offset for the field is what the current offset is now. Then increase the current offset by the size of the field.\
\
Finally, the size of the struct is the current offset rounded up to the nearest multiple of the struct’s alignment.\
\
Here is this algorithm described in pseudocode.\
\
```rust\
\
/// Returns the amount of padding needed after `offset` to ensure that the\
/// following address will be aligned to `alignment`.\
fn padding_needed_for(offset: usize, alignment: usize) -> usize {\
    let misalignment = offset % alignment;\
    if misalignment > 0 {\
        // round up to next multiple of `alignment`\
        alignment - misalignment\
    } else {\
        // already a multiple of `alignment`\
        0\
    }\
}\
\
struct.alignment = struct.fields().map(|field| field.alignment).max();\
\
let current_offset = 0;\
\
for field in struct.fields_in_declaration_order() {\
    // Increase the current offset so that it's a multiple of the alignment\
    // of this field. For the first field, this will always be zero.\
    // The skipped bytes are called padding bytes.\
    current_offset += padding_needed_for(current_offset, field.alignment);\
\
    struct[field].offset = current_offset;\
\
    current_offset += field.size;\
}\
\
struct.size = current_offset + padding_needed_for(current_offset, struct.alignment);\
```\
\
> Warning\
>\
> This pseudocode uses a naive algorithm that ignores overflow issues for the sake of clarity. To perform memory layout computations in actual code, use [`Layout`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/alloc/layout/struct.Layout.html).\
\
> Note\
>\
> This algorithm can produce zero-sized structs. In C, an empty struct declaration like `struct Foo { }` is illegal. However, both gcc and clang support options to enable such structs, and assign them size zero. C++, in contrast, gives empty structs a size of 1, unless they are inherited from or they are fields that have the `[[no_unique_address]]` attribute, in which case they do not increase the overall size of the struct.\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.union\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.union "layout.repr.c.union")\
\
#### [`\#[repr(C)]` Unions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reprc-unions)\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.union.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.union.intro "layout.repr.c.union.intro")\
\
A union declared with `#[repr(C)]` will have the same size and alignment as an equivalent C union declaration in the C language for the target platform.\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.union.size-align\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.union.size-align "layout.repr.c.union.size-align")\
\
The union will have a size of the maximum size of all of its fields rounded to its alignment, and an alignment of the maximum alignment of all of its fields. These maximums may come from different fields. Each field lives at byte offset 0 from the beginning of the union.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(C)]\
union Union {\
    f1: u16,\
    f2: [u8; 4],\
}\
\
assert_eq!(std::mem::size_of::<Union>(), 4);  // From f2\
assert_eq!(std::mem::align_of::<Union>(), 2); // From f1\
\
assert_eq!(std::mem::offset_of!(Union, f1), 0);\
assert_eq!(std::mem::offset_of!(Union, f2), 0);\
\
#[repr(C)]\
union SizeRoundedUp {\
   a: u32,\
   b: [u16; 3],\
}\
\
assert_eq!(std::mem::size_of::<SizeRoundedUp>(), 8);  // Size of 6 from b,\
                                                      // rounded up to 8 from\
                                                      // alignment of a.\
assert_eq!(std::mem::align_of::<SizeRoundedUp>(), 4); // From a\
\
assert_eq!(std::mem::offset_of!(SizeRoundedUp, a), 0);\
assert_eq!(std::mem::offset_of!(SizeRoundedUp, b), 0);\
}\
```\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.enum\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.enum "layout.repr.c.enum")\
\
#### [`\#[repr(C)]` Field-less Enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reprc-field-less-enums)\
\
For [field-less enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-less-enum), the `C` representation has the size and alignment of the default `enum` size and alignment for the target platform’s C ABI.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The enum representation in C is implementation defined, so this is really a “best guess”. In particular, this may be incorrect when the C code of interest is compiled with certain flags.\
\
> Warning\
>\
> There are crucial differences between an `enum` in the C language and Rust’s [field-less enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-less-enum) with this representation. An `enum` in C is mostly a `typedef` plus some named constants; in other words, an object of an `enum` type can hold any integer value. For example, this is often used for bitflags in `C`. In contrast, Rust’s [field-less enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-less-enum) can only legally hold the discriminant values, everything else is [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined). Therefore, using a field-less enum in FFI to model a C `enum` is often wrong.\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.adt\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.adt "layout.repr.c.adt")\
\
#### [`\#[repr(C)]` Enums With Fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#reprc-enums-with-fields)\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.adt.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.adt.intro "layout.repr.c.adt.intro")\
\
The representation of a `repr(C)` enum with fields is a `repr(C)` struct with two fields, also called a “tagged union” in C:\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.adt.tag\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.adt.tag "layout.repr.c.adt.tag")\
\
- a `repr(C)` version of the enum with all fields removed (“the tag”)\
\
[\[layout.repr.c.adt.fields\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.c.adt.fields "layout.repr.c.adt.fields")\
\
- a `repr(C)` union of `repr(C)` structs for the fields of each variant that had them (“the payload”)\
\
> Note\
>\
> Due to the representation of `repr(C)` structs and unions, if a variant has a single field there is no difference between putting that field directly in the union or wrapping it in a struct; any system which wishes to manipulate such an `enum`’s representation may therefore use whichever form is more convenient or consistent for them.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// This Enum has the same representation as ...\
#[repr(C)]\
enum MyEnum {\
    A(u32),\
    B(f32, u64),\
    C { x: u32, y: u8 },\
    D,\
 }\
\
// ... this struct.\
#[repr(C)]\
struct MyEnumRepr {\
    tag: MyEnumDiscriminant,\
    payload: MyEnumFields,\
}\
\
// This is the discriminant enum.\
#[repr(C)]\
enum MyEnumDiscriminant { A, B, C, D }\
\
// This is the variant union.\
#[repr(C)]\
union MyEnumFields {\
    A: MyAFields,\
    B: MyBFields,\
    C: MyCFields,\
    D: MyDFields,\
}\
\
#[repr(C)]\
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]\
struct MyAFields(u32);\
\
#[repr(C)]\
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]\
struct MyBFields(f32, u64);\
\
#[repr(C)]\
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]\
struct MyCFields { x: u32, y: u8 }\
\
// This struct could be omitted (it is a zero-sized type), and it must be in\
// C/C++ headers.\
#[repr(C)]\
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]\
struct MyDFields;\
}\
```\
\
[\[layout.repr.primitive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.primitive "layout.repr.primitive")\
\
### [Primitive representations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#primitive-representations)\
\
[\[layout.repr.primitive.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.primitive.intro "layout.repr.primitive.intro")\
\
The _primitive representations_ are the representations with the same names as the primitive integer types. That is: `u8`, `u16`, `u32`, `u64`, `u128`, `usize`, `i8`, `i16`, `i32`, `i64`, `i128`, and `isize`.\
\
[\[layout.repr.primitive.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.primitive.constraint "layout.repr.primitive.constraint")\
\
Primitive representations can only be applied to enumerations and have different behavior whether the enum has fields or no fields. It is an error for [zero-variant enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#zero-variant-enums) to have a primitive representation. Combining two primitive representations together is an error.\
\
[\[layout.repr.primitive.enum\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.primitive.enum "layout.repr.primitive.enum")\
\
#### [Primitive representation of field-less enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#primitive-representation-of-field-less-enums)\
\
For [field-less enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-less-enum), primitive representations set the size and alignment to be the same as the primitive type of the same name. For example, a field-less enum with a `u8` representation can only have discriminants between 0 and 255 inclusive.\
\
[\[layout.repr.primitive.adt\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.primitive.adt "layout.repr.primitive.adt")\
\
#### [Primitive representation of enums with fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#primitive-representation-of-enums-with-fields)\
\
The representation of a primitive representation enum is a `repr(C)` union of `repr(C)` structs for each variant with a field. The first field of each struct in the union is the primitive representation version of the enum with all fields removed (“the tag”) and the remaining fields are the fields of that variant.\
\
> Note\
>\
> This representation is unchanged if the tag is given its own member in the union, should that make manipulation more clear for you (although to follow the C++ standard the tag member should be wrapped in a `struct`).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// This enum has the same representation as ...\
#[repr(u8)]\
enum MyEnum {\
    A(u32),\
    B(f32, u64),\
    C { x: u32, y: u8 },\
    D,\
 }\
\
// ... this union.\
#[repr(C)]\
union MyEnumRepr {\
    A: MyVariantA,\
    B: MyVariantB,\
    C: MyVariantC,\
    D: MyVariantD,\
}\
\
// This is the discriminant enum.\
#[repr(u8)]\
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]\
enum MyEnumDiscriminant { A, B, C, D }\
\
#[repr(C)]\
#[derive(Clone, Copy)]\
struct MyVariantA(MyEnumDiscriminant, u32);\
\
#[repr(C)]\
#[derive(Clone, Copy)]\
struct MyVariantB(MyEnumDiscriminant, f32, u64);\
\
#[repr(C)]\
#[derive(Clone, Copy)]\
struct MyVariantC { tag: MyEnumDiscriminant, x: u32, y: u8 }\
\
#[repr(C)]\
#[derive(Clone, Copy)]\
struct MyVariantD(MyEnumDiscriminant);\
}\
```\
\
[\[layout.repr.primitive-c\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.primitive-c "layout.repr.primitive-c")\
\
#### [Combining primitive representations of enums with fields and `\#[repr(C)]`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#combining-primitive-representations-of-enums-with-fields-and-reprc)\
\
For enums with fields, it is also possible to combine `repr(C)` and a primitive representation (e.g., `repr(C, u8)`). This modifies the [`repr(C)`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#reprc-enums-with-fields) by changing the representation of the discriminant enum to the chosen primitive instead. So, if you chose the `u8` representation, then the discriminant enum would have a size and alignment of 1 byte.\
\
The discriminant enum from the example [earlier](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#reprc-enums-with-fields) then becomes:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(C, u8)] // `u8` was added\
enum MyEnum {\
    A(u32),\
    B(f32, u64),\
    C { x: u32, y: u8 },\
    D,\
 }\
\
// ...\
\
#[repr(u8)] // So `u8` is used here instead of `C`\
enum MyEnumDiscriminant { A, B, C, D }\
\
// ...\
}\
```\
\
For example, with a `repr(C, u8)` enum it is not possible to have 257 unique discriminants (“tags”) whereas the same enum with only a `repr(C)` attribute will compile without any problems.\
\
Using a primitive representation in addition to `repr(C)` can change the size of an enum from the `repr(C)` form:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#[repr(C)]\
enum EnumC {\
    Variant0(u8),\
    Variant1,\
}\
\
#[repr(C, u8)]\
enum Enum8 {\
    Variant0(u8),\
    Variant1,\
}\
\
#[repr(C, u16)]\
enum Enum16 {\
    Variant0(u8),\
    Variant1,\
}\
\
// The size of the C representation is platform dependent\
assert_eq!(std::mem::size_of::<EnumC>(), 8);\
// One byte for the discriminant and one byte for the value in Enum8::Variant0\
assert_eq!(std::mem::size_of::<Enum8>(), 2);\
// Two bytes for the discriminant and one byte for the value in Enum16::Variant0\
// plus one byte of padding.\
assert_eq!(std::mem::size_of::<Enum16>(), 4);\
}\
```\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment "layout.repr.alignment")\
\
### [The alignment modifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-alignment-modifiers)\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment.intro "layout.repr.alignment.intro")\
\
The `align` and `packed` modifiers can be used to respectively raise or lower the alignment of `struct`s and `union`s. `packed` may also alter the padding between fields (although it will not alter the padding inside of any field). On their own, `align` and `packed` do not provide guarantees about the order of fields in the layout of a struct or the layout of an enum variant, although they may be combined with representations (such as `C`) which do provide such guarantees.\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment.constraint-alignment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment.constraint-alignment "layout.repr.alignment.constraint-alignment")\
\
The alignment is specified as an integer parameter in the form of `#[repr(align(x))]` or `#[repr(packed(x))]`. The alignment value must be a power of two from 1 up to 229. For `packed`, if no value is given, as in `#[repr(packed)]`, then the value is 1.\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment.align\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment.align "layout.repr.alignment.align")\
\
For `align`, if the specified alignment is less than the alignment of the type without the `align` modifier, then the alignment is unaffected.\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment.packed\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment.packed "layout.repr.alignment.packed")\
\
For `packed`, if the specified alignment is greater than the type’s alignment without the `packed` modifier, then the alignment and layout is unaffected.\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment.packed-fields\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment.packed-fields "layout.repr.alignment.packed-fields")\
\
The alignments of each field, for the purpose of positioning fields, is the smaller of the specified alignment and the alignment of the field’s type.\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment.packed-padding\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment.packed-padding "layout.repr.alignment.packed-padding")\
\
Inter-field padding is guaranteed to be the minimum required in order to satisfy each field’s (possibly altered) alignment (although note that, on its own, `packed` does not provide any guarantee about field ordering). An important consequence of these rules is that a type with `#[repr(packed(1))]` (or `#[repr(packed)]`) will have no inter-field padding.\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment.constraint-exclusive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment.constraint-exclusive "layout.repr.alignment.constraint-exclusive")\
\
The `align` and `packed` modifiers cannot be applied on the same type and a `packed` type cannot transitively contain another `align`ed type. `align` and `packed` may only be applied to the [`Rust`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-rust-representation) and [`C`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-c-representation) representations.\
\
[\[layout.repr.alignment.enum\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.alignment.enum "layout.repr.alignment.enum")\
\
The `align` modifier can also be applied on an `enum`. When it is, the effect on the `enum`’s alignment is the same as if the `enum` was wrapped in a newtype `struct` with the same `align` modifier.\
\
> Note\
>\
> References to unaligned fields are not allowed because it is [undefined behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined). When fields are unaligned due to an alignment modifier, consider the following options for using references and dereferences:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> #[repr(packed)]\
> struct Packed {\
>     f1: u8,\
>     f2: u16,\
> }\
> let mut e = Packed { f1: 1, f2: 2 };\
> // Instead of creating a reference to a field, copy the value to a local variable.\
> let x = e.f2;\
> // Or in situations like `println!` which creates a reference, use braces\
> // to change it to a copy of the value.\
> println!("{}", {e.f2});\
> // Or if you need a pointer, use the unaligned methods for reading and writing\
> // instead of dereferencing the pointer directly.\
> let ptr: *const u16 = &raw const e.f2;\
> let value = unsafe { ptr.read_unaligned() };\
> let mut_ptr: *mut u16 = &raw mut e.f2;\
> unsafe { mut_ptr.write_unaligned(3) }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[layout.repr.transparent\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.transparent "layout.repr.transparent")\
\
### [The `transparent` representation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-transparent-representation)\
\
[\[layout.repr.transparent.constraint-field\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.transparent.constraint-field "layout.repr.transparent.constraint-field")\
\
The `transparent` representation can only be used on a [`struct`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs) or an [`enum`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations) with a single variant that has:\
\
- any number of fields with size 0 and alignment 1 (e.g. [`PhantomData<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#phantomdatat)), and\
- at most one other field.\
\
[\[layout.repr.transparent.layout-abi\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.transparent.layout-abi "layout.repr.transparent.layout-abi")\
\
Structs and enums with this representation have the same layout and ABI as the only non-size 0 non-alignment 1 field, if present, or unit otherwise.\
\
This is different than the `C` representation because a struct with the `C` representation will always have the ABI of a `C``struct` while, for example, a struct with the `transparent` representation with a primitive field will have the ABI of the primitive field.\
\
[\[layout.repr.transparent.constraint-exclusive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-layout.repr.transparent.constraint-exclusive "layout.repr.transparent.constraint-exclusive")\
\
Because this representation delegates type layout to another type, it cannot be used with any other representation.\
\
[\[interior-mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut "interior-mut")\
\
# [Interior mutability](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#interior-mutability)\
\
[\[interior-mut.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut.intro "interior-mut.intro")\
\
Sometimes a type needs to be mutated while having multiple aliases. In Rust this is achieved using a pattern called _interior mutability_.\
\
[\[interior-mut.shared-ref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut.shared-ref "interior-mut.shared-ref")\
\
A type has interior mutability if its internal state can be changed through a [shared reference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#shared-references-) to it.\
\
[\[interior-mut.no-constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut.no-constraint "interior-mut.no-constraint")\
\
This goes against the usual [requirement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#behavior-considered-undefined) that the value pointed to by a shared reference is not mutated.\
\
[\[interior-mut.unsafe-cell\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut.unsafe-cell "interior-mut.unsafe-cell")\
\
[`std::cell::UnsafeCell<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/cell/struct.UnsafeCell.html) type is the only allowed way to disable this requirement. When `UnsafeCell<T>` is immutably aliased, it is still safe to mutate, or obtain a mutable reference to, the `T` it contains.\
\
[\[interior-mut.mut-unsafe-cell\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut.mut-unsafe-cell "interior-mut.mut-unsafe-cell")\
\
As with all other types, it is undefined behavior to have multiple `&mut UnsafeCell<T>` aliases.\
\
[\[interior-mut.abstraction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut.abstraction "interior-mut.abstraction")\
\
Other types with interior mutability can be created by using `UnsafeCell<T>` as a field. The standard library provides a variety of types that provide safe interior mutability APIs.\
\
[\[interior-mut.ref-cell\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut.ref-cell "interior-mut.ref-cell")\
\
For example, [`std::cell::RefCell<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/cell/struct.RefCell.html) uses run-time borrow checks to ensure the usual rules around multiple references.\
\
[\[interior-mut.atomic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-interior-mut.atomic "interior-mut.atomic")\
\
The [`std::sync::atomic`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/sync/atomic/index.html) module contains types that wrap a value that is only accessed with atomic operations, allowing the value to be shared and mutated across threads.\
\
[\[subtype\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtype "subtype")\
\
# [Subtyping and variance](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#subtyping-and-variance)\
\
[\[subtype.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtype.intro "subtype.intro")\
\
Subtyping is implicit and can occur at any stage in type checking or inference.\
\
[\[subtype.kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtype.kinds "subtype.kinds")\
\
Subtyping is restricted to two cases: variance with respect to lifetimes and between types with higher ranked lifetimes. If we were to erase lifetimes from types, then the only subtyping would be due to type equality.\
\
Consider the following example: string literals always have `'static` lifetime. Nevertheless, we can assign `s` to `t`:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn bar<'a>() {\
    let s: &'static str = "hi";\
    let t: &'a str = s;\
}\
}\
```\
\
Since `'static` outlives the lifetime parameter `'a`, `&'static str` is a subtype of `&'a str`.\
\
[\[subtype.higher-ranked\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtype.higher-ranked "subtype.higher-ranked")\
\
[Higher-ranked](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon/hrtb.html) [function pointers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-pointer-types) and [trait objects](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects) have another subtype relation. They are subtypes of types that are given by substitutions of the higher-ranked lifetimes. Some examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Here 'a is substituted for 'static\
let subtype: &(for<'a> fn(&'a i32) -> &'a i32) = &((|x| x) as fn(&_) -> &_);\
let supertype: &(fn(&'static i32) -> &'static i32) = subtype;\
\
// This works similarly for trait objects\
let subtype: &(dyn for<'a> Fn(&'a i32) -> &'a i32) = &|x| x;\
let supertype: &(dyn Fn(&'static i32) -> &'static i32) = subtype;\
\
// We can also substitute one higher-ranked lifetime for another\
let subtype: &(for<'a, 'b> fn(&'a i32, &'b i32)) = &((|x, y| {}) as fn(&_, &_));\
let supertype: &for<'c> fn(&'c i32, &'c i32) = subtype;\
}\
```\
\
[\[subtyping.variance\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtyping.variance "subtyping.variance")\
\
## [Variance](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#variance)\
\
[\[subtyping.variance.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtyping.variance.intro "subtyping.variance.intro")\
\
Variance is a property that generic types have with respect to their arguments. A generic type’s _variance_ in a parameter is how the subtyping of the parameter affects the subtyping of the type.\
\
[\[subtyping.variance.covariant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtyping.variance.covariant "subtyping.variance.covariant")\
\
- `F<T>` is _covariant_ over `T` if `T` being a subtype of `U` implies that `F<T>` is a subtype of `F<U>` (subtyping “passes through”)\
\
[\[subtyping.variance.contravariant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtyping.variance.contravariant "subtyping.variance.contravariant")\
\
- `F<T>` is _contravariant_ over `T` if `T` being a subtype of `U` implies that `F<U>` is a subtype of `F<T>`\
\
[\[subtyping.variance.invariant\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtyping.variance.invariant "subtyping.variance.invariant")\
\
- `F<T>` is _invariant_ over `T` otherwise (no subtyping relation can be derived)\
\
[\[subtyping.variance.builtin-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtyping.variance.builtin-types "subtyping.variance.builtin-types")\
\
Variance of types is automatically determined as follows\
\
| Type | Variance in `'a` | Variance in `T` |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| `&'a T` | covariant | covariant |\
| `&'a mut T` | covariant | invariant |\
| `*const T` |  | covariant |\
| `*mut T` |  | invariant |\
| `[T]` and `[T; n]` |  | covariant |\
| `fn() -> T` |  | covariant |\
| `fn(T) -> ()` |  | contravariant |\
| `std::cell::UnsafeCell<T>` |  | invariant |\
| `std::marker::PhantomData<T>` |  | covariant |\
| `dyn Trait<T> + 'a` | covariant | invariant |\
\
[\[subtyping.variance.user-composite-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtyping.variance.user-composite-types "subtyping.variance.user-composite-types")\
\
The variance of other `struct`, `enum`, and `union` types is decided by looking at the variance of the types of their fields. If the parameter is used in positions with different variances then the parameter is invariant. For example the following struct is covariant in `'a` and `T` and invariant in `'b`, `'c`, and `U`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::cell::UnsafeCell;\
struct Variance<'a, 'b, 'c, T, U: 'a> {\
    x: &'a U,               // This makes `Variance` covariant in 'a, and would\
                            // make it covariant in U, but U is used later\
    y: *const T,            // Covariant in T\
    z: UnsafeCell<&'b f64>, // Invariant in 'b\
    w: *mut U,              // Invariant in U, makes the whole struct invariant\
\
    f: fn(&'c ()) -> &'c () // Both co- and contravariant, makes 'c invariant\
                            // in the struct.\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[subtyping.variance.builtin-composite-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-subtyping.variance.builtin-composite-types "subtyping.variance.builtin-composite-types")\
\
When used outside of an `struct`, `enum`, or `union`, the variance for parameters is checked at each location separately.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::cell::UnsafeCell;\
fn generic_tuple<'short, 'long: 'short>(\
    // 'long is used inside of a tuple in both a co- and invariant position.\
    x: (&'long u32, UnsafeCell<&'long u32>),\
) {\
    // As the variance at these positions is computed separately,\
    // we can freely shrink 'long in the covariant position.\
    let _: (&'short u32, UnsafeCell<&'long u32>) = x;\
}\
\
fn takes_fn_ptr<'short, 'middle: 'short>(\
    // 'middle is used in both a co- and contravariant position.\
    f: fn(&'middle ()) -> &'middle (),\
) {\
    // As the variance at these positions is computed separately,\
    // we can freely shrink 'middle in the covariant position\
    // and extend it in the contravariant position.\
    let _: fn(&'static ()) -> &'short () = f;\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound "bound")\
\
# [Trait and lifetime bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#trait-and-lifetime-bounds)\
\
[\[bound.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.syntax "bound.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\

\

\
[UseBoundGenericArg](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-UseBoundGenericArg)[Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Lifetime)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
Self\
\
[\[bound.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.intro "bound.intro")\
\
[Trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-bounds) and lifetime bounds provide a way for [generic items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters) to restrict which types and lifetimes are used as their parameters. Bounds can be provided on any type in a [where clause](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#where-clauses). There are also shorter forms for certain common cases:\
\
- Bounds written after declaring a [generic parameter](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters): `fn f<A: Copy>() {}` is the same as `fn f<A>() where A: Copy {}`.\
- In trait declarations as [supertraits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#supertraits): `trait Circle : Shape {}` is equivalent to `trait Circle where Self : Shape {}`.\
- In trait declarations as bounds on [associated types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types): `trait A { type B: Copy; }` is equivalent to `trait A where Self::B: Copy { type B; }`.\
\
[\[bound.satisfaction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.satisfaction "bound.satisfaction")\
\
Bounds on an item must be satisfied when using the item. When type checking and borrow checking a generic item, the bounds can be used to determine that a trait is implemented for a type. For example, given `Ty: Trait`\
\
- In the body of a generic function, methods from `Trait` can be called on `Ty` values. Likewise associated constants on the `Trait` can be used.\
- Associated types from `Trait` can be used.\
- Generic functions and types with a `T: Trait` bounds can be used with `Ty` being used for `T`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
type Surface = i32;\
trait Shape {\
    fn draw(&self, surface: Surface);\
    fn name() -> &'static str;\
}\
\
fn draw_twice<T: Shape>(surface: Surface, sh: T) {\
    sh.draw(surface);           // Can call method because T: Shape\
    sh.draw(surface);\
}\
\
fn copy_and_draw_twice<T: Copy>(surface: Surface, sh: T) where T: Shape {\
    let shape_copy = sh;        // doesn't move sh because T: Copy\
    draw_twice(surface, sh);    // Can use generic function because T: Shape\
}\
\
struct Figure<S: Shape>(S, S);\
\
fn name_figure<U: Shape>(\
    figure: Figure<U>,          // Type Figure<U> is well-formed because U: Shape\
) {\
    println!(\
        "Figure of two {}",\
        U::name(),              // Can use associated function\
    );\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound.trivial\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.trivial "bound.trivial")\
\
Bounds that don’t use the item’s parameters or [higher-ranked lifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#higher-ranked-trait-bounds) are checked when the item is defined. It is an error for such a bound to be false.\
\
[\[bound.special\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.special "bound.special")\
\
[`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Copy.html), [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/clone/trait.Clone.html), and [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Sized.html) bounds are also checked for certain generic types when using the item, even if the use does not provide a concrete type. It is an error to have `Copy` or `Clone` as a bound on a mutable reference, [trait object](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects), or [slice](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-types). It is an error to have `Sized` as a bound on a trait object or slice.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct A<'a, T>\
where\
    i32: Default,           // Allowed, but not useful\
    i32: Iterator,          // Error: `i32` is not an iterator\
    &'a mut T: Copy,        // (at use) Error: the trait bound is not satisfied\
    [T]: Sized,             // (at use) Error: size cannot be known at compilation\
{\
    f: &'a T,\
}\
struct UsesA<'a, T>(A<'a, T>);\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound.trait-object\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.trait-object "bound.trait-object")\
\
Trait and lifetime bounds are also used to name [trait objects](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects).\
\
[\[bound.sized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.sized "bound.sized")\
\
## [`?Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#sized)\
\
`?` is only used to relax the implicit [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Sized.html) trait bound for [type parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-parameters) or [associated types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types). `?Sized` may not be used as a bound for other types.\
\
[\[bound.lifetime\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.lifetime "bound.lifetime")\
\
## [Lifetime bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#lifetime-bounds)\
\
[\[bound.lifetime.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.lifetime.intro "bound.lifetime.intro")\
\
Lifetime bounds can be applied to types or to other lifetimes.\
\
[\[bound.lifetime.outlive-lifetime\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.lifetime.outlive-lifetime "bound.lifetime.outlive-lifetime")\
\
The bound `'a: 'b` is usually read as `'a` _outlives_`'b`. `'a: 'b` means that `'a` lasts at least as long as `'b`, so a reference `&'a ()` is valid whenever `&'b ()` is valid.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn f<'a, 'b>(x: &'a i32, mut y: &'b i32) where 'a: 'b {\
    y = x;                      // &'a i32 is a subtype of &'b i32 because 'a: 'b\
    let r: &'b &'a i32 = &&0;   // &'b &'a i32 is well formed because 'a: 'b\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound.lifetime.outlive-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.lifetime.outlive-type "bound.lifetime.outlive-type")\
\
`T: 'a` means that all lifetime parameters of `T` outlive `'a`. For example, if `'a` is an unconstrained lifetime parameter, then `i32: 'static` and `&'static str: 'a` are satisfied, but `Vec<&'a ()>: 'static` is not.\
\
[\[bound.higher-ranked\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.higher-ranked "bound.higher-ranked")\
\
## [Higher-ranked trait bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#higher-ranked-trait-bounds)\
\
[\[bound.higher-ranked.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.higher-ranked.syntax "bound.higher-ranked.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[ForLifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-ForLifetimes) → for[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[ForLifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-ForLifetimes)\
for[GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericParams)\
\
[\[bound.higher-ranked.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.higher-ranked.intro "bound.higher-ranked.intro")\
\
Trait bounds may be _higher ranked_ over lifetimes. These bounds specify a bound that is true _for all_ lifetimes. For example, a bound such as `for<'a> &'a T: PartialEq<i32>` would require an implementation like\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct T;\
impl<'a> PartialEq<i32> for &'a T {\
    // ...\
   fn eq(&self, other: &i32) -> bool {true}\
}\
}\
```\
\
and could then be used to compare a `&'a T` with any lifetime to an `i32`.\
\
Only a higher-ranked bound can be used here, because the lifetime of the reference is shorter than any possible lifetime parameter on the function:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn call_on_ref_zero<F>(f: F) where for<'a> F: Fn(&'a i32) {\
    let zero = 0;\
    f(&zero);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound.higher-ranked.trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.higher-ranked.trait "bound.higher-ranked.trait")\
\
Higher-ranked lifetimes may also be specified just before the trait: the only difference is the [scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#higher-ranked-trait-bound-scopes) of the lifetime parameter, which extends only to the end of the following trait instead of the whole bound. This function is equivalent to the last one.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn call_on_ref_zero<F>(f: F) where F: for<'a> Fn(&'a i32) {\
    let zero = 0;\
    f(&zero);\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound.implied\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.implied "bound.implied")\
\
## [Implied bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#implied-bounds)\
\
[\[bound.implied.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.implied.intro "bound.implied.intro")\
\
Lifetime bounds required for types to be well-formed are sometimes inferred.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
