fn requires_t_outlives_a<'a, T>(x: &'a T) {}\
}\
```\
\
The type parameter `T` is required to outlive `'a` for the type `&'a T` to be well-formed. This is inferred because the function signature contains the type `&'a T` which is only valid if `T: 'a` holds.\
\
[\[bound.implied.context\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.implied.context "bound.implied.context")\
\
Implied bounds are added for all parameters and outputs of functions. Inside of `requires_t_outlives_a` you can assume `T: 'a` to hold even if you don’t explicitly specify this:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn requires_t_outlives_a_not_implied<'a, T: 'a>() {}\
\
fn requires_t_outlives_a<'a, T>(x: &'a T) {\
    // This compiles, because `T: 'a` is implied by\
    // the reference type `&'a T`.\
    requires_t_outlives_a_not_implied::<'a, T>();\
}\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn requires_t_outlives_a_not_implied<'a, T: 'a>() {}\
fn not_implied<'a, T>() {\
    // This errors, because `T: 'a` is not implied by\
    // the function signature.\
    requires_t_outlives_a_not_implied::<'a, T>();\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound.implied.trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.implied.trait "bound.implied.trait")\
\
Only lifetime bounds are implied, trait bounds still have to be explicitly added. The following example therefore causes an error:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::fmt::Debug;\
struct IsDebug<T: Debug>(T);\
// error[E0277]: `T` doesn't implement `Debug`\
fn doesnt_specify_t_debug<T>(x: IsDebug<T>) {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound.implied.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.implied.def "bound.implied.def")\
\
Lifetime bounds are also inferred for type definitions and impl blocks for any type:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Struct<'a, T> {\
    // This requires `T: 'a` to be well-formed\
    // which is inferred by the compiler.\
    field: &'a T,\
}\
\
enum Enum<'a, T> {\
    // This requires `T: 'a` to be well-formed,\
    // which is inferred by the compiler.\
    //\
    // Note that `T: 'a` is required even when only\
    // using `Enum::OtherVariant`.\
    SomeVariant(&'a T),\
    OtherVariant,\
}\
\
trait Trait<'a, T: 'a> {}\
\
// This would error because `T: 'a` is not implied by any type\
// in the impl header.\
//     impl<'a, T> Trait<'a, T> for () {}\
\
// This compiles as `T: 'a` is implied by the self type `&'a T`.\
impl<'a, T> Trait<'a, T> for &'a T {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[bound.use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-bound.use "bound.use")\
\
## [Use bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#use-bounds)\
\
Certain bounds lists may include a `use<..>` bound to control which generic parameters are captured by the `impl Trait` [abstract return type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#abstract-return-types). See [precise capturing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#precise-capturing) for more details.\
\
[\[coerce\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce "coerce")\
\
# [Type coercions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#type-coercions)\
\
[\[coerce.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.intro "coerce.intro")\
\
**Type coercions** are implicit operations that change the type of a value. They happen automatically at specific locations and are highly restricted in what types actually coerce.\
\
[\[coerce.as\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.as "coerce.as")\
\
Any conversions allowed by coercion can also be explicitly performed by the [type cast operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-cast-expressions), `as`.\
\
Coercions are originally defined in [RFC 401](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/text/0401-coercions.md) and expanded upon in [RFC 1558](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/text/1558-closure-to-fn-coercion.md).\
\
[\[coerce.site\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site "coerce.site")\
\
## [Coercion sites](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#coercion-sites)\
\
[\[coerce.site.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.intro "coerce.site.intro")\
\
A coercion can only occur at certain coercion sites in a program; these are typically places where the desired type is explicit or can be derived by propagation from explicit types (without type inference). Possible coercion sites are:\
\
[\[coerce.site.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.let "coerce.site.let")\
\
- `let` statements where an explicit type is given.\
\
For example, `&mut 42` is coerced to have type `&i8` in the following:\
\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let _: &i8 = &mut 42;\
}\
```\
\
\
[\[coerce.site.value\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.value "coerce.site.value")\
\
- `static` and `const` item declarations (similar to `let` statements).\
\
[\[coerce.site.argument\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.argument "coerce.site.argument")\
\
- Arguments for function calls\
\
The value being coerced is the actual parameter, and it is coerced to the type of the formal parameter.\
\
For example, `&mut 42` is coerced to have type `&i8` in the following:\
\
\
```rust\
\
fn bar(_: &i8) { }\
\
fn main() {\
      bar(&mut 42);\
}\
```\
\
\
For method calls, the receiver (`self` parameter) type is coerced differently, see the documentation on [method-call expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#method-call-expressions) for details.\
\
\
[\[coerce.site.constructor\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.constructor "coerce.site.constructor")\
\
- Instantiations of struct, union, or enum variant fields\
\
For example, `&mut 42` is coerced to have type `&i8` in the following:\
\
\
```rust\
\
struct Foo<'a> { x: &'a i8 }\
\
fn main() {\
      Foo { x: &mut 42 };\
}\
```\
\
\
[\[coerce.site.return\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.return "coerce.site.return")\
\
- Function results—either the final line of a block if it is not semicolon-terminated or any expression in a `return` statement\
\
For example, `x` is coerced to have type `&dyn Display` in the following:\
\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::fmt::Display;\
fn foo(x: &u32) -> &dyn Display {\
      x\
}\
}\
```\
\
\
[\[coerce.site.assignment\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.assignment "coerce.site.assignment")\
\
- Assigned value operands in assignment expressions\
\
For example, `y` is coerced to have type `&i8` in the following:\
\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let mut x = &0i8;\
let y = &mut 42i8;\
x = y;\
}\
```\
\
\
[\[coerce.site.subexpr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.subexpr "coerce.site.subexpr")\
\
If the expression in one of these coercion sites is a coercion-propagating expression, then the relevant sub-expressions in that expression are also coercion sites. Propagation recurses from these new coercion sites. Propagating expressions and their relevant sub-expressions are:\
\
[\[coerce.site.array\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.array "coerce.site.array")\
\
- Array literals, where the array has type `[U; n]`. Each sub-expression in the array literal is a coercion site for coercion to type `U`.\
\
[\[coerce.site.repeat\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.repeat "coerce.site.repeat")\
\
- Array literals with repeating syntax, where the array has type `[U; n]`. The repeated sub-expression is a coercion site for coercion to type `U`.\
\
[\[coerce.site.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.tuple "coerce.site.tuple")\
\
- Tuples, where a tuple is a coercion site to type `(U_0, U_1, ..., U_n)`. Each sub-expression is a coercion site to the respective type, e.g. the zeroth sub-expression is a coercion site to type `U_0`.\
\
[\[coerce.site.parenthesis\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.parenthesis "coerce.site.parenthesis")\
\
- Parenthesized sub-expressions (`(e)`): if the expression has type `U`, then the sub-expression is a coercion site to `U`.\
\
[\[coerce.site.block\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.site.block "coerce.site.block")\
\
- Blocks: if a block has type `U`, then the last expression in the block (if it is not semicolon-terminated) is a coercion site to `U`. This includes blocks which are part of control flow statements, such as `if`/`else`, if the block has a known type.\
\
[\[coerce.types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types "coerce.types")\
\
## [Coercion types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#coercion-types)\
\
[\[coerce.types.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.intro "coerce.types.intro")\
\
Coercion is allowed between the following types:\
\
[\[coerce.types.reflexive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.reflexive "coerce.types.reflexive")\
\
- `T` to `U` if `T` is a [subtype](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#subtyping-and-variance) of `U` ( _reflexive case_)\
\
[\[coerce.types.transitive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.transitive "coerce.types.transitive")\
\
- `T_1` to `T_3` where `T_1` coerces to `T_2` and `T_2` coerces to `T_3` ( _transitive case_)\
\
Note that this is not fully supported yet.\
\
\
[\[coerce.types.mut-reborrow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.mut-reborrow "coerce.types.mut-reborrow")\
\
- `&mut T` to `&T`\
\
[\[coerce.types.mut-pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.mut-pointer "coerce.types.mut-pointer")\
\
- `*mut T` to `*const T`\
\
[\[coerce.types.ref-to-pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.ref-to-pointer "coerce.types.ref-to-pointer")\
\
- `&T` to `*const T`\
\
[\[coerce.types.mut-to-pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.mut-to-pointer "coerce.types.mut-to-pointer")\
\
- `&mut T` to `*mut T`\
\
[\[coerce.types.deref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.deref "coerce.types.deref")\
\
- `&T` or `&mut T` to `&U` if `T` implements `Deref<Target = U>`. For example:\
\
\
```rust\
\
use std::ops::Deref;\
\
struct CharContainer {\
      value: char,\
}\
\
impl Deref for CharContainer {\
      type Target = char;\
\
      fn deref<'a>(&'a self) -> &'a char {\
          &self.value\
      }\
}\
\
fn foo(arg: &char) {}\
\
fn main() {\
      let x = &mut CharContainer { value: 'y' };\
      foo(x); //&mut CharContainer is coerced to &char.\
}\
```\
\
\
[\[coerce.types.deref-mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.deref-mut "coerce.types.deref-mut")\
\
- `&mut T` to `&mut U` if `T` implements `DerefMut<Target = U>`.\
\
[\[coerce.types.unsize\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.unsize "coerce.types.unsize")\
\
- TyCtor(`T`) to TyCtor(`U`), where TyCtor(`T`) is one of\
\
\
  - `&T`\
  - `&mut T`\
  - `*const T`\
  - `*mut T`\
  - `Box<T>`\
\
and where `U` can be obtained from `T` by [unsized coercion](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unsized-coercions).\
\
[\[coerce.types.fn\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.fn "coerce.types.fn")\
\
- Function item types to `fn` pointers\
\
[\[coerce.types.closure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.closure "coerce.types.closure")\
\
- Non capturing closures to `fn` pointers\
\
[\[coerce.types.never\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.types.never "coerce.types.never")\
\
- `!` to any `T`\
\
[\[coerce.unsize\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.unsize "coerce.unsize")\
\
### [Unsized coercions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unsized-coercions)\
\
[\[coerce.unsize.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.unsize.intro "coerce.unsize.intro")\
\
The following coercions are called `unsized coercions`, since they relate to converting types to unsized types, and are permitted in a few cases where other coercions are not, as described above. They can still happen anywhere else a coercion can occur.\
\
[\[coerce.unsize.trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.unsize.trait "coerce.unsize.trait")\
\
Two traits, [`Unsize`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Unsize.html) and [`CoerceUnsized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/unsize/trait.CoerceUnsized.html), are used to assist in this process and expose it for library use. The following coercions are built-ins and, if `T` can be coerced to `U` with one of them, then an implementation of `Unsize<U>` for `T` will be provided:\
\
[\[coerce.unsize.slice\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.unsize.slice "coerce.unsize.slice")\
\
- `[T; n]` to `[T]`.\
\
[\[coerce.unsize.trait-object\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.unsize.trait-object "coerce.unsize.trait-object")\
\
- `T` to `dyn U`, when `T` implements `U + Sized`, and `U` is [dyn compatible](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#dyn-compatibility).\
\
[\[coerce.unsize.trait-upcast\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.unsize.trait-upcast "coerce.unsize.trait-upcast")\
\
- `dyn T` to `dyn U`, when `U` is one of `T`’s [supertraits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#supertraits).\
\
  - This allows dropping auto traits, i.e. `dyn T + Auto` to `dyn U` is allowed.\
  - This allows adding auto traits if the principal trait has the auto trait as a super trait, i.e. given `trait T: U + Send {}`, `dyn T` to `dyn T + Send` or to `dyn U + Send` coercions are allowed.\
\
[\[coerce.unsized.composite\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.unsized.composite "coerce.unsized.composite")\
\
- `Foo<..., T, ...>` to `Foo<..., U, ...>`, when:\
\
  - `Foo` is a struct.\
  - `T` implements `Unsize<U>`.\
  - The last field of `Foo` has a type involving `T`.\
  - If that field has type `Bar<T>`, then `Bar<T>` implements `Unsize<Bar<U>>`.\
  - T is not part of the type of any other fields.\
\
[\[coerce.unsized.pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.unsized.pointer "coerce.unsized.pointer")\
\
Additionally, a type `Foo<T>` can implement `CoerceUnsized<Foo<U>>` when `T` implements `Unsize<U>` or `CoerceUnsized<Foo<U>>`. This allows it to provide an unsized coercion to `Foo<U>`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> While the definition of the unsized coercions and their implementation has been stabilized, the traits themselves are not yet stable and therefore can’t be used directly in stable Rust.\
\
[\[coerce.least-upper-bound\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound "coerce.least-upper-bound")\
\
## [Least upper bound coercions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#least-upper-bound-coercions)\
\
[\[coerce.least-upper-bound.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound.intro "coerce.least-upper-bound.intro")\
\
In some contexts, the compiler must coerce together multiple types to try and find the most general type. This is called a “Least Upper Bound” coercion. LUB coercion is used and only used in the following situations:\
\
- To find the common type for a series of if branches.\
- To find the common type for a series of match arms.\
- To find the common type for array elements.\
- To find the common type for a [labeled block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels) among the break operands and the final block operand.\
- To find the common type for an [`loop` expression with break expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-value) among the break operands.\
- To find the type for the return type of a closure with multiple return statements.\
- To check the type for the return type of a function with multiple return statements.\
\
[\[coerce.least-upper-bound.target\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound.target "coerce.least-upper-bound.target")\
\
In each such case, there are a set of types `T0..Tn` to be mutually coerced to some target type `T_t`, which is unknown to start.\
\
[\[coerce.least-upper-bound.computation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound.computation "coerce.least-upper-bound.computation")\
\
Computing the LUB coercion is done iteratively. The target type `T_t` begins as the type `T0`. For each new type `Ti`, we consider whether\
\
[\[coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-identity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-identity "coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-identity")\
\
- If `Ti` can be coerced to the current target type `T_t`, then no change is made.\
\
[\[coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-replace\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-replace "coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-replace")\
\
- Otherwise, check whether `T_t` can be coerced to `Ti`; if so, the `T_t` is changed to `Ti`. (This check is also conditioned on whether all of the source expressions considered thus far have implicit coercions.)\
\
[\[coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-unify\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-unify "coerce.least-upper-bound.computation-unify")\
\
- If not, try to compute a mutual supertype of `T_t` and `Ti`, which will become the new target type.\
\
### [Examples:](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#examples-3)\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let (a, b, c) = (0, 1, 2);\
// For if branches\
let bar = if true {\
    a\
} else if false {\
    b\
} else {\
    c\
};\
\
// For match arms\
let baw = match 42 {\
    0 => a,\
    1 => b,\
    _ => c,\
};\
\
// For array elements\
let bax = [a, b, c];\
\
// For closure with multiple return statements\
let clo = || {\
    if true {\
        a\
    } else if false {\
        b\
    } else {\
        c\
    }\
};\
let baz = clo();\
\
// For type checking of function with multiple return statements\
fn foo() -> i32 {\
    let (a, b, c) = (0, 1, 2);\
    match 42 {\
        0 => a,\
        1 => b,\
        _ => c,\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
In these examples, types of the `ba*` are found by LUB coercion. And the compiler checks whether LUB coercion result of `a`, `b`, `c` is `i32` in the processing of the function `foo`.\
\
### [Caveat](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#caveat)\
\
This description is obviously informal. Making it more precise is expected to proceed as part of a general effort to specify the Rust type checker more precisely.\
\
[\[divergence\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence "divergence")\
\
# [Divergence](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#divergence)\
\
[\[divergence.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence.intro "divergence.intro")\
\
A _diverging expression_ is an expression that never completes normal execution.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn diverges() -> ! {\
    panic!("This function never returns!");\
}\
\
fn example() {\
    let x: i32 = diverges(); // This line never completes.\
    println!("This is never printed: {x}");\
}\
}\
```\
\
See the following rules for specific expression divergence behavior:\
\
- [expr.block.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.diverging) — Block expressions.\
- [expr.if.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.if.diverging) — `if` expressions.\
- [expr.loop.block-labels.type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels.type) — Labeled block expressions with `break`.\
- [expr.loop.break-value.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break-value.diverging) — `loop` expressions with `break`.\
- [expr.loop.break.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.break.diverging) — `break` expressions.\
- [expr.loop.continue.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.continue.diverging) — `continue` expressions.\
- [expr.loop.infinite.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.infinite.diverging) — Infinite `loop` expressions.\
- [expr.match.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.diverging) — `match` expressions.\
- [expr.match.empty](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.empty) — Empty `match` expressions.\
- [expr.return.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.return.diverging) — `return` expressions.\
- [type.never.constraint](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never.constraint) — Function calls returning `!`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The [`panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.panic.html) macro and related panic-generating macros like [`unreachable!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.unreachable.html) also have the type [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never) and are diverging.\
\
[\[divergence.never\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence.never "divergence.never")\
\
Any expression of type [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never) is a diverging expression. However, diverging expressions are not limited to type [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never); expressions of other types may also diverge (e.g., `Some(loop {})` has type `Option<!>`).\
\
> Note\
>\
> Though `!` is considered an uninhabited type, a type being uninhabited is not sufficient for it to diverge.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> enum Empty {}\
> fn make_never() -> ! {loop{}}\
> fn make_empty() -> Empty {loop{}}\
>\
> fn diverging() -> ! {\
>     // This has a type of `!`.\
>     // So, the entire function is considered diverging.\
>     make_never();\
>     // OK: The type of the body is `!` which matches the return type.\
> }\
> fn not_diverging() -> ! {\
>     // This type is uninhabited.\
>     // However, the entire function is not considered diverging.\
>     make_empty();\
>     // ERROR: The type of the body is `()` but expected type `!`.\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Divergence can propagate to the surrounding block. See [expr.block.diverging](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.diverging).\
\
[\[divergence.fallback\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-divergence.fallback "divergence.fallback")\
\
## [Fallback](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#fallback)\
\
If a type to be inferred is only unified with diverging expressions, then that type will be inferred to be [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.never).\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn foo() -> i32 { 22 }\
> match foo() {\
>     // ERROR: The trait bound `!: Default` is not satisfied.\
>     4 => Default::default(),\
>     _ => return,\
> };\
> }\
> ```\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2024 edition, the type was inferred to instead be `()`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Importantly, type unification may happen _structurally_, so the fallback `!` may be part of a larger type. The following compiles:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> fn foo() -> i32 { 22 }\
> // This has the type `Option<!>`, not `!`\
> match foo() {\
>     4 => Default::default(),\
>     _ => Some(return),\
> };\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[destructors\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors "destructors")\
\
# [Destructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#destructors)\
\
[\[destructors.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.intro "destructors.intro")\
\
When an [initialized](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#initialized) [variable](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variables) or [temporary](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#temporaries) goes out of [scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#drop-scopes), its _destructor_ is run or it is _dropped_. [Assignment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#assignment-expressions) also runs the destructor of its left-hand operand, if it’s initialized. If a variable has been partially initialized, only its initialized fields are dropped.\
\
[\[destructors.operation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.operation "destructors.operation")\
\
The destructor of a type `T` consists of:\
\
1. If `T: Drop`, calling [`<T as core::ops::Drop>::drop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/drop/trait.Drop.html#tymethod.drop)\
2. Recursively running the destructor of all of its fields.\
   - The fields of a [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-types) are dropped in declaration order.\
   - The fields of the active [enum variant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerated-types) are dropped in declaration order.\
   - The fields of a [tuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types) are dropped in order.\
   - The elements of an [array](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types) or owned [slice](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-types) are dropped from the first element to the last.\
   - The variables that a [closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types) captures by move are dropped in an unspecified order.\
   - [Trait objects](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects) run the destructor of the underlying type.\
   - Other types don’t result in any further drops.\
\
[\[destructors.drop\_in\_place\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.drop_in_place "destructors.drop_in_place")\
\
If a destructor must be run manually, such as when implementing your own smart pointer, [`core::ptr::drop_in_place`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ptr/fn.drop_in_place.html) can be used.\
\
Some examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct PrintOnDrop(&'static str);\
\
impl Drop for PrintOnDrop {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("{}", self.0);\
    }\
}\
\
let mut overwritten = PrintOnDrop("drops when overwritten");\
overwritten = PrintOnDrop("drops when scope ends");\
\
let tuple = (PrintOnDrop("Tuple first"), PrintOnDrop("Tuple second"));\
\
let moved;\
// No destructor run on assignment.\
moved = PrintOnDrop("Drops when moved");\
// Drops now, but is then uninitialized.\
moved;\
\
// Uninitialized does not drop.\
let uninitialized: PrintOnDrop;\
\
// After a partial move, only the remaining fields are dropped.\
let mut partial_move = (PrintOnDrop("first"), PrintOnDrop("forgotten"));\
// Perform a partial move, leaving only `partial_move.0` initialized.\
core::mem::forget(partial_move.1);\
// When partial_move's scope ends, only the first field is dropped.\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope "destructors.scope")\
\
## [Drop scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#drop-scopes)\
\
[\[destructors.scope.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.intro "destructors.scope.intro")\
\
Each variable or temporary is associated to a _drop scope_. When control flow leaves a drop scope all variables associated to that scope are dropped in reverse order of declaration (for variables) or creation (for temporaries).\
\
[\[destructors.scope.desugaring\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.desugaring "destructors.scope.desugaring")\
\
Drop scopes can be determined by replacing [`for`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#iterator-loops), [`if`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-expressions), and [`while`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#predicate-loops) expressions with equivalent expressions using [`match`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions), [`loop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#infinite-loops) and `break`.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.operators\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.operators "destructors.scope.operators")\
\
Overloaded operators are not distinguished from built-in operators and [binding modes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#binding-modes) are not considered.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.list\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.list "destructors.scope.list")\
\
Given a function, or closure, there are drop scopes for:\
\
[\[destructors.scope.function\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.function "destructors.scope.function")\
\
- The entire function\
\
[\[destructors.scope.statement\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.statement "destructors.scope.statement")\
\
- Each [statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#statements)\
\
[\[destructors.scope.expression\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.expression "destructors.scope.expression")\
\
- Each [expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expressions)\
\
[\[destructors.scope.block\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.block "destructors.scope.block")\
\
- Each block, including the function body\
  - In the case of a [block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions), the scope for the block and the expression are the same scope.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.match-arm\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.match-arm "destructors.scope.match-arm")\
\
- Each arm of a `match` expression\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting "destructors.scope.nesting")\
\
Drop scopes are nested within one another as follows. When multiple scopes are left at once, such as when returning from a function, variables are dropped from the inside outwards.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.function\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.function "destructors.scope.nesting.function")\
\
- The entire function scope is the outer most scope.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.function-body\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.function-body "destructors.scope.nesting.function-body")\
\
- The function body block is contained within the scope of the entire function.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.expr-statement\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.expr-statement "destructors.scope.nesting.expr-statement")\
\
- The parent of the expression in an expression statement is the scope of the statement.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.let-initializer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.let-initializer "destructors.scope.nesting.let-initializer")\
\
- The parent of the initializer of a [`let` statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#let-statements) is the `let` statement’s scope.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.statement\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.statement "destructors.scope.nesting.statement")\
\
- The parent of a statement scope is the scope of the block that contains the statement.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.match-guard\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.match-guard "destructors.scope.nesting.match-guard")\
\
- The parent of the expression for a `match` guard is the scope of the arm that the guard is for.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.match-arm\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.match-arm "destructors.scope.nesting.match-arm")\
\
- The parent of the expression after the `=>` in a `match` expression is the scope of the arm that it’s in.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.match\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.match "destructors.scope.nesting.match")\
\
- The parent of the arm scope is the scope of the `match` expression that it belongs to.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.nesting.other\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.nesting.other "destructors.scope.nesting.other")\
\
- The parent of all other scopes is the scope of the immediately enclosing expression.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.params\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.params "destructors.scope.params")\
\
### [Scopes of function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#scopes-of-function-parameters)\
\
All function parameters are in the scope of the entire function body, so are dropped last when evaluating the function. Each actual function parameter is dropped after any bindings introduced in that parameter’s pattern.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct PrintOnDrop(&'static str);\
impl Drop for PrintOnDrop {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("drop({})", self.0);\
    }\
}\
// Drops `y`, then the second parameter, then `x`, then the first parameter\
fn patterns_in_parameters(\
    (x, _): (PrintOnDrop, PrintOnDrop),\
    (_, y): (PrintOnDrop, PrintOnDrop),\
) {}\
\
// drop order is 3 2 0 1\
patterns_in_parameters(\
    (PrintOnDrop("0"), PrintOnDrop("1")),\
    (PrintOnDrop("2"), PrintOnDrop("3")),\
);\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.bindings\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.bindings "destructors.scope.bindings")\
\
### [Scopes of local variables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#scopes-of-local-variables)\
\
[\[destructors.scope.bindings.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.bindings.let "destructors.scope.bindings.let")\
\
Local variables declared in a `let` statement are associated to the scope of the block that contains the `let` statement.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct PrintOnDrop(&'static str);\
impl Drop for PrintOnDrop {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("drop({})", self.0);\
    }\
}\
let declared_first = PrintOnDrop("Dropped last in outer scope");\
{\
    let declared_in_block = PrintOnDrop("Dropped in inner scope");\
}\
let declared_last = PrintOnDrop("Dropped first in outer scope");\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.bindings.match-arm\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.bindings.match-arm "destructors.scope.bindings.match-arm")\
\
Local variables declared in a `match` expression or pattern-matching `match` guard are associated to the arm scope of the `match` arm that they are declared in.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#![allow(irrefutable_let_patterns)]\
struct PrintOnDrop(&'static str);\
impl Drop for PrintOnDrop {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("drop({})", self.0);\
    }\
}\
match PrintOnDrop("Dropped last in the first arm's scope") {\
    // When guard evaluation succeeds, control-flow stays in the arm and\
    // values may be moved from the scrutinee into the arm's bindings,\
    // causing them to be dropped in the arm's scope.\
    x if let y = PrintOnDrop("Dropped second in the first arm's scope")\
        && let z = PrintOnDrop("Dropped first in the first arm's scope") =>\
    {\
        let declared_in_block = PrintOnDrop("Dropped in inner scope");\
        // Pattern-matching guards' bindings and temporaries are dropped in\
        // reverse order, dropping each guard condition operand's bindings\
        // before its temporaries. Lastly, variables bound by the arm's\
        // pattern are dropped.\
    }\
    _ => unreachable!(),\
}\
\
match PrintOnDrop("Dropped in the enclosing temporary scope") {\
    // When guard evaluation fails, control-flow leaves the arm scope,\
    // causing bindings and temporaries from earlier pattern-matching\
    // guard condition operands to be dropped. This occurs before evaluating\
    // the next arm's guard or body.\
    _ if let y = PrintOnDrop("Dropped in the first arm's scope")\
        && false => unreachable!(),\
    // When a guard is executed multiple times due to self-overlapping\
    // or-patterns, control-flow leaves the arm scope when the guard fails\
    // and re-enters the arm scope before executing the guard again.\
    _ | _ if let y = PrintOnDrop("Dropped in the second arm's scope twice")\
        && false => unreachable!(),\
    _ => {},\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.bindings.patterns\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.bindings.patterns "destructors.scope.bindings.patterns")\
\
Variables in patterns are dropped in reverse order of declaration within the pattern.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct PrintOnDrop(&'static str);\
impl Drop for PrintOnDrop {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("drop({})", self.0);\
    }\
}\
let (declared_first, declared_last) = (\
    PrintOnDrop("Dropped last"),\
    PrintOnDrop("Dropped first"),\
);\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.bindings.or-patterns\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.bindings.or-patterns "destructors.scope.bindings.or-patterns")\
\
For the purpose of drop order, [or-patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#or-patterns) declare bindings in the order given by the first subpattern.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct PrintOnDrop(&'static str);\
impl Drop for PrintOnDrop {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("drop({})", self.0);\
    }\
}\
// Drops `x` before `y`.\
fn or_pattern_drop_order<T>(\
    (Ok([x, y]) | Err([y, x])): Result<[T; 2], [T; 2]>\
//   ^^^^^^^^^^   ^^^^^^^^^^^ This is the second subpattern.\
//   |\
//   This is the first subpattern.\
//\
//   In the first subpattern, `x` is declared before `y`. Since it is\
//   the first subpattern, that is the order used even if the second\
//   subpattern, where the bindings are declared in the opposite\
//   order, is matched.\
) {}\
\
// Here we match the first subpattern, and the drops happen according\
// to the declaration order in the first subpattern.\
or_pattern_drop_order(Ok([\
    PrintOnDrop("Declared first, dropped last"),\
    PrintOnDrop("Declared last, dropped first"),\
]));\
\
// Here we match the second subpattern, and the drops still happen\
// according to the declaration order in the first subpattern.\
or_pattern_drop_order(Err([\
    PrintOnDrop("Declared last, dropped first"),\
    PrintOnDrop("Declared first, dropped last"),\
]));\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.temporary\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary "destructors.scope.temporary")\
\
### [Temporary scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#temporary-scopes)\
\
[\[destructors.scope.temporary.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary.intro "destructors.scope.temporary.intro")\
\
The _temporary scope_ of an expression is the scope that is used for the temporary variable that holds the result of that expression when used in a [place context](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#place-expressions-and-value-expressions), unless it is [promoted](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-promotion).\
\
[\[destructors.scope.temporary.enclosing\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary.enclosing "destructors.scope.temporary.enclosing")\
\
Apart from lifetime extension, the temporary scope of an expression is the smallest scope that contains the expression and is one of the following:\
\
- The entire function.\
- A statement.\
- The body of an [`if`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-expressions), [`while`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#predicate-loops) or [`loop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#infinite-loops) expression.\
- The `else` block of an `if` expression.\
- The non-pattern matching condition expression of an `if` or `while` expression or a non-pattern-matching `match` [guard condition operand](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-guard-chains).\
- The pattern-matching guard, if present, and body expression for a `match` arm.\
- Each operand of a [lazy boolean expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lazy-boolean-operators).\
- The pattern-matching condition(s) and consequent body of [`if`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-expressions) ( [destructors.scope.temporary.edition2024](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary.edition2024)).\
- The pattern-matching condition and loop body of [`while`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#predicate-loops).\
- The entirety of the tail expression of a block ( [destructors.scope.temporary.edition2024](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary.edition2024)).\
\
> Note\
>\
> The [scrutinee](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scrutinee) of a `match` expression is not a temporary scope, so temporaries in the scrutinee can be dropped after the `match` expression. For example, the temporary for `1` in `match 1 { ref mut z => z };` lives until the end of the statement.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The desugaring of a [destructuring assignment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.assign.destructure) restricts the temporary scope of its assigned value operand (the RHS). For details, see [expr.assign.destructure.tmp-scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.assign.destructure.tmp-scopes).\
\
[\[destructors.scope.temporary.edition2024\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary.edition2024 "destructors.scope.temporary.edition2024")\
\
> 2024 Edition differences\
>\
> The 2024 edition added two new temporary scope narrowing rules: `if let` temporaries are dropped before the `else` block, and temporaries of tail expressions of blocks are dropped immediately after the tail expression is evaluated.\
\
Some examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
#![allow(irrefutable_let_patterns)]\
struct PrintOnDrop(&'static str);\
impl Drop for PrintOnDrop {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("drop({})", self.0);\
    }\
}\
let local_var = PrintOnDrop("local var");\
\
// Dropped once the condition has been evaluated\
if PrintOnDrop("If condition").0 == "If condition" {\
    // Dropped at the end of the block\
    PrintOnDrop("If body").0\
} else {\
    unreachable!()\
};\
\
if let "if let scrutinee" = PrintOnDrop("if let scrutinee").0 {\
    PrintOnDrop("if let consequent").0\
    // `if let consequent` dropped here\
}\
// `if let scrutinee` is dropped here\
else {\
    PrintOnDrop("if let else").0\
    // `if let else` dropped here\
};\
\
while let x = PrintOnDrop("while let scrutinee").0 {\
    PrintOnDrop("while let loop body").0;\
    break;\
    // `while let loop body` dropped here.\
    // `while let scrutinee` dropped here.\
}\
\
// Dropped before the first ||\
(PrintOnDrop("first operand").0 == ""\
// Dropped before the )\
|| PrintOnDrop("second operand").0 == "")\
// Dropped before the ;\
|| PrintOnDrop("third operand").0 == "";\
\
// Scrutinee is dropped at the end of the function, before local variables\
// (because this is the tail expression of the function body block).\
match PrintOnDrop("Matched value in final expression") {\
    // Non-pattern-matching guards' temporaries are dropped once the\
    // condition has been evaluated\
    _ if PrintOnDrop("guard condition").0 == "" => (),\
    // Pattern-matching guards' temporaries are dropped when leaving the\
    // arm's scope\
    _ if let "guard scrutinee" = PrintOnDrop("guard scrutinee").0 => {\
        let _ = &PrintOnDrop("lifetime-extended temporary in inner scope");\
        // `lifetime-extended temporary in inner scope` is dropped here\
    }\
    // `guard scrutinee` is dropped here\
    _ => (),\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.operands\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.operands "destructors.scope.operands")\
\
### [Operands](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#operands)\
\
Temporaries are also created to hold the result of operands to an expression while the other operands are evaluated. The temporaries are associated to the scope of the expression with that operand. Since the temporaries are moved from once the expression is evaluated, dropping them has no effect unless one of the operands to an expression breaks out of the expression, returns, or [panics](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#panic-1).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct PrintOnDrop(&'static str);\
impl Drop for PrintOnDrop {\
    fn drop(&mut self) {\
        println!("drop({})", self.0);\
    }\
}\
loop {\
    // Tuple expression doesn't finish evaluating so operands drop in reverse order\
    (\
        PrintOnDrop("Outer tuple first"),\
        PrintOnDrop("Outer tuple second"),\
        (\
            PrintOnDrop("Inner tuple first"),\
            PrintOnDrop("Inner tuple second"),\
            break,\
        ),\
        PrintOnDrop("Never created"),\
    );\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.const-promotion\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.const-promotion "destructors.scope.const-promotion")\
\
### [Constant promotion](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#constant-promotion)\
\
Promotion of a value expression to a `'static` slot occurs when the expression could be written in a constant and borrowed, and that borrow could be dereferenced where the expression was originally written, without changing the runtime behavior. That is, the promoted expression can be evaluated at compile-time and the resulting value does not contain [interior mutability](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#interior-mutability) or [destructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#destructors) (these properties are determined based on the value where possible, e.g. `&None` always has the type `&'static Option<_>`, as it contains nothing disallowed).\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension")\
\
### [Temporary lifetime extension](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#temporary-lifetime-extension)\
\
> Note\
>\
> The exact rules for temporary lifetime extension are subject to change. This is describing the current behavior only.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.let "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.let")\
\
The temporary scopes for expressions in `let` statements are sometimes _extended_ to the scope of the block containing the `let` statement. This is done when the usual temporary scope would be too small, based on certain syntactic rules. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
let x = &mut 0;\
// Usually a temporary would be dropped by now, but the temporary for `0` lives\
// to the end of the block.\
println!("{}", x);\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.static "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.static")\
\
Lifetime extension also applies to `static` and `const` items, where it makes temporaries live until the end of the program. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
const C: &Vec<i32> = &Vec::new();\
// Usually this would be a dangling reference as the `Vec` would only\
// exist inside the initializer expression of `C`, but instead the\
// borrow gets lifetime-extended so it effectively has `'static` lifetime.\
println!("{:?}", C);\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.sub-expressions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.sub-expressions "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.sub-expressions")\
\
If a [borrow](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.operator.borrow), [dereference](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator), [field](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-access-expressions), or [tuple indexing expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-indexing-expressions) has an extended temporary scope, then so does its operand. If an [indexing expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions) has an extended temporary scope, then the indexed expression also has an extended temporary scope.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns")\
\
#### [Extending based on patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#extending-based-on-patterns)\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns.extending\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns.extending "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns.extending")\
\
An _extending pattern_ is either:\
\
- An [identifier pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#identifier-patterns) that binds by reference or mutable reference.\
\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
let ref x = temp(); // Binds by reference.\
x;\
let ref mut x = temp(); // Binds by mutable reference.\
x;\
}\
```\
\
- A [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-patterns), [tuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-patterns), [tuple struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-struct-patterns), [slice](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#slice-patterns), or [or-pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#or-patterns) where at least one of the direct subpatterns is an extending pattern.\
\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use core::sync::atomic::{AtomicU64, Ordering::Relaxed};\
static X: AtomicU64 = AtomicU64::new(0);\
struct W<T>(T);\
impl<T> Drop for W<T> { fn drop(&mut self) { X.fetch_add(1, Relaxed); } }\
let W { 0: ref x } = W(()); // Struct pattern.\
x;\
let W(ref x) = W(()); // Tuple struct pattern.\
x;\
let (W(ref x),) = (W(()),); // Tuple pattern.\
x;\
let [W(ref x), ..] = [W(())]; // Slice pattern.\
x;\
let (Ok(W(ref x)) | Err(&ref x)) = Ok(W(())); // Or pattern.\
x;\
//\
// All of the temporaries above are still live here.\
assert_eq!(0, X.load(Relaxed));\
}\
```\
\
\
So `ref x`, `V(ref x)` and `[ref x, y]` are all extending patterns, but `x`, `&ref x` and `&(ref x,)` are not.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns.let "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.patterns.let")\
\
If the pattern in a `let` statement is an extending pattern then the temporary scope of the initializer expression is extended.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// This is an extending pattern, so the temporary scope is extended.\
let ref x = *&temp(); // OK\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// This is neither an extending pattern nor an extending expression,\
// so the temporary is dropped at the semicolon.\
let &ref x = *&&temp(); // ERROR\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// This is not an extending pattern but it is an extending expression,\
// so the temporary lives beyond the `let` statement.\
let &ref x = &*&temp(); // OK\
x;\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs")\
\
Tests\
\
Tests with this rule:\
\
- [tests/ui/lifetimes/temporary-lifetime-extension-tuple-ctor.rs](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/1.96.0/tests/ui/lifetimes/temporary-lifetime-extension-tuple-ctor.rs)\
\
#### [Extending based on expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#extending-based-on-expressions)\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.extending\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.extending "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.extending")\
\
For a let statement with an initializer, an _extending expression_ is an expression which is one of the following:\
\
- The initializer expression.\
- The operand of an extending [borrow](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.operator.borrow) expression.\
- The [super operands](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros) of an extending [super macro call](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros) expression.\
- The operand(s) of an extending [array](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-expressions), [cast](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-cast-expressions), [braced struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#struct-expressions), or [tuple](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-expressions) expression.\
- The arguments to an extending [tuple struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.struct.tuple) or [tuple enum variant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-type.enum.declaration) constructor expression.\
- The final expression of an extending [block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#block-expressions) except for an [async block expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.block.async).\
- The final expression of an extending [`if`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-expressions) expression’s consequent, `else if`, or `else` block.\
- An arm expression of an extending [`match`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions) expression.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The desugaring of a [destructuring assignment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.assign.destructure) makes its assigned value operand (the RHS) an extending expression within a newly-introduced block. For details, see [expr.assign.destructure.tmp-ext](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.assign.destructure.tmp-ext).\
\
So the borrow expressions in `&mut 0`, `(&1, &mut 2)`, and `Some(&mut 3)` are all extending expressions. The borrows in `&0 + &1` and `f(&mut 0)` are not.\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.borrows\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.borrows "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.borrows")\
\
The operand of an extending [borrow](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.operator.borrow) expression has its [temporary scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary) [extended](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension).\
\
[\[destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.super-macros\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.super-macros "destructors.scope.lifetime-extension.exprs.super-macros")\
\
The [super temporaries](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros) of an extending [super macro call](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.super-macros) expression have their [scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.temporary) [extended](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.scope.lifetime-extension).\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` does not treat [array repeat operands](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.array.repeat-operand) of extending [array](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-types) expressions as extending expressions. Whether it should is an open question.\
>\
> For details, see [Rust issue #146092](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/146092).\
\
#### [Examples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#examples-4)\
\
Here are some examples where expressions have extended temporary scopes:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use core::pin::pin;\
use core::sync::atomic::{AtomicU64, Ordering::Relaxed};\
static X: AtomicU64 = AtomicU64::new(0);\
#[derive(Debug)] struct S;\
impl Drop for S { fn drop(&mut self) { X.fetch_add(1, Relaxed); } }\
const fn temp() -> S { S }\
let x = &temp(); // Operand of borrow.\
x;\
let x = &raw const *&temp(); // Operand of raw borrow.\
assert_eq!(X.load(Relaxed), 0);\
let x = &temp() as &dyn Send; // Operand of cast.\
x;\
let x = (&*&temp(),); // Operand of tuple constructor.\
x;\
struct W<T>(T);\
let x = W(&temp()); // Argument to tuple struct constructor.\
x;\
let x = Some(&temp()); // Argument to tuple enum variant constructor.\
x;\
let x = { [Some(&temp())] }; // Final expr of block.\
x;\
let x = const { &temp() }; // Final expr of `const` block.\
x;\
let x = unsafe { &temp() }; // Final expr of `unsafe` block.\
x;\
let x = if true { &temp() } else { &temp() };\
//              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\
//           Final exprs of `if`/`else` blocks.\
x;\
let x = match () { _ => &temp() }; // `match` arm expression.\
x;\
let x = pin!(temp()); // Super operand of super macro call expression.\
x;\
let x = pin!({ &mut temp() }); // As above.\
x;\
let x = format_args!("{:?}", temp()); // As above.\
x;\
//\
// All of the temporaries above are still live here.\
assert_eq!(0, X.load(Relaxed));\
}\
```\
\
Here are some examples where expressions don’t have extended temporary scopes:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// Arguments to function calls are not extending expressions. The\
// temporary is dropped at the semicolon.\
let x = core::convert::identity(&temp()); // ERROR\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
trait Use { fn use_temp(&self) -> &Self { self } }\
impl Use for () {}\
// Receivers of method calls are not extending expressions.\
let x = (&temp()).use_temp(); // ERROR\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// Scrutinees of match expressions are not extending expressions.\
let x = match &temp() { x => x }; // ERROR\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// Final expressions of `async` blocks are not extending expressions.\
let x = async { &temp() }; // ERROR\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// Final expressions of closures are not extending expressions.\
let x = || &temp(); // ERROR\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// Operands of loop breaks are not extending expressions.\
let x = loop { break &temp() }; // ERROR\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// Operands of breaks to labels are not extending expressions.\
let x = 'a: { break 'a &temp() }; // ERROR\
x;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use core::pin::pin;\
fn temp() {}\
// The argument to `pin!` is only an extending expression if the call\
// is an extending expression. Since it's not, the inner block is not\
// an extending expression, so the temporaries in its trailing\
// expression are dropped immediately.\
pin!({ &temp() }); // ERROR\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn temp() {}\
// As above.\
format_args!("{:?}", { &temp() }); // ERROR\
}\
```\
\
[\[destructors.forget\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.forget "destructors.forget")\
\
## [Not running destructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#not-running-destructors)\
\
[\[destructors.manually-suppressing\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.manually-suppressing "destructors.manually-suppressing")\
\
### [Manually suppressing destructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#manually-suppressing-destructors)\
\
[`core::mem::forget`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/fn.forget.html) can be used to prevent the destructor of a variable from being run, and [`core::mem::ManuallyDrop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/manually_drop/struct.ManuallyDrop.html) provides a wrapper to prevent a variable or field from being dropped automatically.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Preventing a destructor from being run via [`core::mem::forget`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/mem/fn.forget.html) or other means is safe even if it has a type that isn’t `'static`. Besides the places where destructors are guaranteed to run as defined by this document, types may _not_ safely rely on a destructor being run for soundness.\
\
[\[destructors.process-termination\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-destructors.process-termination "destructors.process-termination")\
\
### [Process termination without unwinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#process-termination-without-unwinding)\
\
There are some ways to terminate the process without [unwinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unwinding-1), in which case destructors will not be run.\
\
The standard library provides [`std::process::exit`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/process/fn.exit.html) and [`std::process::abort`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/process/fn.abort.html) to do this explicitly. Additionally, if the [panic handler](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.panic_handler.std) is set to `abort`, panicking will always terminate the process without destructors being run.\
\
There is one additional case to be aware of: when a panic reaches a [non-unwinding ABI boundary](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unwinding), either no destructors will run, or all destructors up until the ABI boundary will run.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision "lifetime-elision")\
\
# [Lifetime elision](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#lifetime-elision)\
\
Rust has rules that allow lifetimes to be elided in various places where the compiler can infer a sensible default choice.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.function\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.function "lifetime-elision.function")\
\
## [Lifetime elision in functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#lifetime-elision-in-functions)\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.function.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.function.intro "lifetime-elision.function.intro")\
\
In order to make common patterns more ergonomic, lifetime arguments can be _elided_ in [function item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-item-types), [function pointer](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-pointer-types), and [closure trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types) signatures. The following rules are used to infer lifetime parameters for elided lifetimes.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.function.lifetimes-not-inferred\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.function.lifetimes-not-inferred "lifetime-elision.function.lifetimes-not-inferred")\
\
It is an error to elide lifetime parameters that cannot be inferred.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.function.explicit-placeholder\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.function.explicit-placeholder "lifetime-elision.function.explicit-placeholder")\
\
The placeholder lifetime, `'_`, can also be used to have a lifetime inferred in the same way. For lifetimes in paths, using `'_` is preferred.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.function.only-functions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.function.only-functions "lifetime-elision.function.only-functions")\
\
Trait object lifetimes follow different rules discussed [below](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#default-trait-object-lifetimes).\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.function.implicit-lifetime-parameters\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.function.implicit-lifetime-parameters "lifetime-elision.function.implicit-lifetime-parameters")\
\
- Each elided lifetime in the parameters becomes a distinct lifetime parameter.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.function.output-lifetime\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.function.output-lifetime "lifetime-elision.function.output-lifetime")\
\
- If there is exactly one lifetime used in the parameters (elided or not), that lifetime is assigned to _all_ elided output lifetimes.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.function.receiver-lifetime\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.function.receiver-lifetime "lifetime-elision.function.receiver-lifetime")\
\
In method signatures there is another rule\
\
- If the receiver has type `&Self` or `&mut Self`, then the lifetime of that reference to `Self` is assigned to all elided output lifetime parameters.\
\
Examples:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait T {}\
trait ToCStr {}\
struct Thing<'a> {f: &'a i32}\
struct Command;\
\
trait Example {\
fn print1(s: &str);                                   // elided\
fn print2(s: &'_ str);                                // also elided\
fn print3<'a>(s: &'a str);                            // expanded\
\
fn debug1(lvl: usize, s: &str);                       // elided\
fn debug2<'a>(lvl: usize, s: &'a str);                // expanded\
\
fn substr1(s: &str, until: usize) -> &str;            // elided\
fn substr2<'a>(s: &'a str, until: usize) -> &'a str;  // expanded\
\
fn get_mut1(&mut self) -> &mut dyn T;                 // elided\
fn get_mut2<'a>(&'a mut self) -> &'a mut dyn T;       // expanded\
\
fn args1<T: ToCStr>(&mut self, args: &[T]) -> &mut Command;                  // elided\
fn args2<'a, 'b, T: ToCStr>(&'a mut self, args: &'b [T]) -> &'a mut Command; // expanded\
\
fn other_args1<'a>(arg: &str) -> &'a str;             // elided\
fn other_args2<'a, 'b>(arg: &'b str) -> &'a str;      // expanded\
\
fn new1(buf: &mut [u8]) -> Thing<'_>;                 // elided - preferred\
fn new2(buf: &mut [u8]) -> Thing;                     // elided\
fn new3<'a>(buf: &'a mut [u8]) -> Thing<'a>;          // expanded\
}\
\
type FunPtr1 = fn(&str) -> &str;                      // elided\
type FunPtr2 = for<'a> fn(&'a str) -> &'a str;        // expanded\
\
type FunTrait1 = dyn Fn(&str) -> &str;                // elided\
type FunTrait2 = dyn for<'a> Fn(&'a str) -> &'a str;  // expanded\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// The following examples show situations where it is not allowed to elide the\
// lifetime parameter.\
\
trait Example {\
// Cannot infer, because there are no parameters to infer from.\
fn get_str() -> &str;                                 // ILLEGAL\
\
// Cannot infer, ambiguous if it is borrowed from the first or second parameter.\
fn frob(s: &str, t: &str) -> &str;                    // ILLEGAL\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object "lifetime-elision.trait-object")\
\
## [Default trait object lifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#default-trait-object-lifetimes)\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.intro "lifetime-elision.trait-object.intro")\
\
The assumed lifetime of references held by a [trait object](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects) is called its _default object lifetime bound_. These were defined in [RFC 599](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/text/0599-default-object-bound.md) and amended in [RFC 1156](https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/text/1156-adjust-default-object-bounds.md).\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.explicit-bound\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.explicit-bound "lifetime-elision.trait-object.explicit-bound")\
\
These default object lifetime bounds are used instead of the lifetime parameter elision rules defined above when the lifetime bound is omitted entirely.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.explicit-placeholder\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.explicit-placeholder "lifetime-elision.trait-object.explicit-placeholder")\
\
If `'_` is used as the lifetime bound then the bound follows the usual elision rules.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type "lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type")\
\
If the trait object is used as a type argument of a generic type then the containing type is first used to try to infer a bound.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type-unique\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type-unique "lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type-unique")\
\
- If there is a unique bound from the containing type then that is the default.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type-explicit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type-explicit "lifetime-elision.trait-object.containing-type-explicit")\
\
- If there is more than one bound from the containing type then an explicit bound must be specified.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.trait-bounds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.trait-bounds "lifetime-elision.trait-object.trait-bounds")\
\
If neither of those rules apply, then the bounds on the trait are used:\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.trait-unique\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.trait-unique "lifetime-elision.trait-object.trait-unique")\
\
- If the trait is defined with a single lifetime _bound_ then that bound is used.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.static-lifetime\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.static-lifetime "lifetime-elision.trait-object.static-lifetime")\
\
- If `'static` is used for any lifetime bound then `'static` is used.\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.default\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.default "lifetime-elision.trait-object.default")\
\
- If the trait has no lifetime bounds, then the lifetime is inferred in expressions and is `'static` outside of expressions.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// For the following trait...\
trait Foo { }\
\
// These two are the same because Box<T> has no lifetime bound on T\
type T1 = Box<dyn Foo>;\
type T2 = Box<dyn Foo + 'static>;\
\
// ...and so are these:\
impl dyn Foo {}\
impl dyn Foo + 'static {}\
\
// ...so are these, because &'a T requires T: 'a\
type T3<'a> = &'a dyn Foo;\
type T4<'a> = &'a (dyn Foo + 'a);\
\
// std::cell::Ref<'a, T> also requires T: 'a, so these are the same\
type T5<'a> = std::cell::Ref<'a, dyn Foo>;\
type T6<'a> = std::cell::Ref<'a, dyn Foo + 'a>;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// This is an example of an error.\
trait Foo { }\
struct TwoBounds<'a, 'b, T: ?Sized + 'a + 'b> {\
    f1: &'a i32,\
    f2: &'b i32,\
    f3: T,\
}\
type T7<'a, 'b> = TwoBounds<'a, 'b, dyn Foo>;\
//                                  ^^^^^^^\
// Error: the lifetime bound for this object type cannot be deduced from context\
}\
```\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.trait-object.innermost-type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.trait-object.innermost-type "lifetime-elision.trait-object.innermost-type")\
\
Note that the innermost object sets the bound, so `&'a Box<dyn Foo>` is still `&'a Box<dyn Foo + 'static>`.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// For the following trait...\
trait Bar<'a>: 'a { }\
\
// ...these two are the same:\
type T1<'a> = Box<dyn Bar<'a>>;\
type T2<'a> = Box<dyn Bar<'a> + 'a>;\
\
// ...and so are these:\
impl<'a> dyn Bar<'a> {}\
impl<'a> dyn Bar<'a> + 'a {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.const-static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.const-static "lifetime-elision.const-static")\
\
## [`const` and `static` elision](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#const-and-static-elision)\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.const-static.implicit-static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.const-static.implicit-static "lifetime-elision.const-static.implicit-static")\
\
Both [constant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items) and [static](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items) declarations of reference types have _implicit_`'static` lifetimes unless an explicit lifetime is specified. As such, the constant declarations involving `'static` above may be written without the lifetimes.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// STRING: &'static str\
const STRING: &str = "bitstring";\
\
struct BitsNStrings<'a> {\
    mybits: [u32; 2],\
    mystring: &'a str,\
}\
\
// BITS_N_STRINGS: BitsNStrings<'static>\
const BITS_N_STRINGS: BitsNStrings<'_> = BitsNStrings {\
    mybits: [1, 2],\
    mystring: STRING,\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[lifetime-elision.const-static.fn-references\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lifetime-elision.const-static.fn-references "lifetime-elision.const-static.fn-references")\
\
Note that if the `static` or `const` items include function or closure references, which themselves include references, the compiler will first try the standard elision rules. If it is unable to resolve the lifetimes by its usual rules, then it will error. By way of example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Foo;\
struct Bar;\
struct Baz;\
fn somefunc(a: &Foo, b: &Bar, c: &Baz) -> usize {42}\
// Resolved as `for<'a> fn(&'a str) -> &'a str`.\
const RESOLVED_SINGLE: fn(&str) -> &str = |x| x;\
\
// Resolved as `for<'a, 'b, 'c> Fn(&'a Foo, &'b Bar, &'c Baz) -> usize`.\
const RESOLVED_MULTIPLE: &dyn Fn(&Foo, &Bar, &Baz) -> usize = &somefunc;\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct Foo;\
struct Bar;\
struct Baz;\
fn somefunc<'a,'b>(a: &'a Foo, b: &'b Bar) -> &'a Baz {unimplemented!()}\
// There is insufficient information to bound the return reference lifetime\
// relative to the argument lifetimes, so this is an error.\
const RESOLVED_STATIC: &dyn Fn(&Foo, &Bar) -> &Baz = &somefunc;\
//                                            ^\
// this function's return type contains a borrowed value, but the signature\
// does not say whether it is borrowed from argument 1 or argument 2\
}\
```\
\
[\[lang-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types "lang-types")\
\
# [Special types and traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#special-types-and-traits)\
\
[\[lang-types.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.intro "lang-types.intro")\
\
Certain types and traits that exist in [the standard library](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/index.html) are known to the\
Rust compiler. This chapter documents the special features of these types and\
traits.\
\
[\[lang-types.box\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.box "lang-types.box")\
\
## [`Box<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#boxt)\
\
[\[lang-types.box.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.box.intro "lang-types.box.intro")\
\
[`Box<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/alloc/boxed/struct.Box.html) has a few special features that Rust doesn’t currently allow for user\
defined types.\
\
[\[lang-types.box.deref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.box.deref "lang-types.box.deref")\
\
- The [dereference operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-dereference-operator) for `Box<T>` produces a place which can be [moved\\
from](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.move.movable-place). This means that the `*` operator and the destructor of `Box<T>` are\
built-in to the language.\
\
[\[lang-types.box.receiver\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.box.receiver "lang-types.box.receiver")\
\
- [Methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-functions-and-methods) can take `Box<Self>` as a receiver.\
\
[\[lang-types.box.fundamental\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.box.fundamental "lang-types.box.fundamental")\
\
- A trait may be implemented for `Box<T>` in the same crate as `T`, which the\
[orphan rules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-implementation-coherence) prevent for other generic types.\
\
[\[lang-types.rc\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.rc "lang-types.rc")\
\
## [`Rc<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#rct)\
\
[\[lang-types.rc.receiver\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.rc.receiver "lang-types.rc.receiver")\
\
[Methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-functions-and-methods) can take [`Rc<Self>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/alloc/rc/struct.Rc.html) as a receiver.\
\
[\[lang-types.arc\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.arc "lang-types.arc")\
\
## [`Arc<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#arct)\
\
[\[lang-types.arc.receiver\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.arc.receiver "lang-types.arc.receiver")\
\
[Methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-functions-and-methods) can take [`Arc<Self>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/alloc/sync/struct.Arc.html) as a receiver.\
\
[\[lang-types.pin\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.pin "lang-types.pin")\
\
## [`Pin<P>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pinp)\
\
[\[lang-types.pin.receiver\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.pin.receiver "lang-types.pin.receiver")\
\
[Methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-functions-and-methods) can take [`Pin<P>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/pin/struct.Pin.html) as a receiver.\
\
[\[lang-types.unsafe-cell\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.unsafe-cell "lang-types.unsafe-cell")\
\
## [`UnsafeCell<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unsafecellt)\
\
[\[lang-types.unsafe-cell.interior-mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.unsafe-cell.interior-mut "lang-types.unsafe-cell.interior-mut")\
\
[`std::cell::UnsafeCell<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/cell/struct.UnsafeCell.html) is used for [interior mutability](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#interior-mutability). It ensures that\
the compiler doesn’t perform optimisations that are incorrect for such types.\
\
[\[lang-types.unsafe-cell.read-only-alloc\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.unsafe-cell.read-only-alloc "lang-types.unsafe-cell.read-only-alloc")\
\
It also ensures that [`static` items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items) which have a type with interior\
