mutability aren’t placed in memory marked as read only.\
\
[\[lang-types.phantom-data\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.phantom-data "lang-types.phantom-data")\
\
## [`PhantomData<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#phantomdatat)\
\
[`std::marker::PhantomData<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/struct.PhantomData.html) is a zero-sized, minimum alignment, type that\
is considered to own a `T` for the purposes of [variance](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#variance), [drop check](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon/dropck.html), and\
[auto traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#auto-traits).\
\
[\[lang-types.ops\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.ops "lang-types.ops")\
\
## [Operator traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#operator-traits)\
\
The traits in [`std::ops`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/index.html) and [`std::cmp`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/cmp/index.html) are used to overload [operators](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#operator-expressions),\
[indexing expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions), and [call expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#call-expressions).\
\
[\[lang-types.deref\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.deref "lang-types.deref")\
\
## [`Deref` and `DerefMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#deref-and-derefmut)\
\
As well as overloading the unary `*` operator, [`Deref`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/deref/trait.Deref.html) and [`DerefMut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/deref/trait.DerefMut.html) are\
also used in [method resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#method-call-expressions) and [deref coercions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#coercion-types).\
\
[\[lang-types.drop\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.drop "lang-types.drop")\
\
## [`Drop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#drop)\
\
The [`Drop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ops/drop/trait.Drop.html) trait provides a [destructor](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#destructors), to be run whenever a value of this\
type is to be destroyed.\
\
[\[lang-types.copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy "lang-types.copy")\
\
## [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#copy)\
\
[\[lang-types.copy.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy.intro "lang-types.copy.intro")\
\
The [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Copy.html) trait changes the semantics of a type implementing it.\
\
[\[lang-types.copy.behavior\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy.behavior "lang-types.copy.behavior")\
\
Values whose type implements `Copy` are copied rather than moved upon assignment.\
\
[\[lang-types.copy.constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy.constraint "lang-types.copy.constraint")\
\
`Copy` can only be implemented for types which do not implement `Drop`, and whose fields are all `Copy`.\
For enums, this means all fields of all variants have to be `Copy`.\
For unions, this means all variants have to be `Copy`.\
\
[\[lang-types.copy.builtin-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy.builtin-types "lang-types.copy.builtin-types")\
\
`Copy` is implemented by the compiler for\
\
[\[lang-types.copy.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy.tuple "lang-types.copy.tuple")\
\
- [Tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types) of `Copy` types\
\
[\[lang-types.copy.fn-pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy.fn-pointer "lang-types.copy.fn-pointer")\
\
- [Function pointers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-pointer-types)\
\
[\[lang-types.copy.fn-item\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy.fn-item "lang-types.copy.fn-item")\
\
- [Function items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-item-types)\
\
[\[lang-types.copy.closure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.copy.closure "lang-types.copy.closure")\
\
- [Closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types) that capture no values or that only capture values of `Copy` types\
\
[\[lang-types.clone\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.clone "lang-types.clone")\
\
## [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#clone)\
\
[\[lang-types.clone.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.clone.intro "lang-types.clone.intro")\
\
The [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/clone/trait.Clone.html) trait is a supertrait of `Copy`, so it also needs compiler\
generated implementations.\
\
[\[lang-types.clone.builtin-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.clone.builtin-types "lang-types.clone.builtin-types")\
\
It is implemented by the compiler for the following types:\
\
[\[lang-types.clone.builtin-copy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.clone.builtin-copy "lang-types.clone.builtin-copy")\
\
- Types with a built-in `Copy` implementation (see above)\
\
[\[lang-types.clone.tuple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.clone.tuple "lang-types.clone.tuple")\
\
- [Tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tuple-types) of `Clone` types\
\
[\[lang-types.clone.closure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.clone.closure "lang-types.clone.closure")\
\
- [Closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-types) that only capture values of `Clone` types or capture no values from the environment\
\
[\[lang-types.send\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.send "lang-types.send")\
\
## [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#send)\
\
The [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Send.html) trait indicates that a value of this type is safe to send from one\
thread to another.\
\
[\[lang-types.sync\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.sync "lang-types.sync")\
\
## [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#sync)\
\
[\[lang-types.sync.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.sync.intro "lang-types.sync.intro")\
\
The [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Sync.html) trait indicates that a value of this type is safe to share between\
multiple threads.\
\
[\[lang-types.sync.static-constraint\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.sync.static-constraint "lang-types.sync.static-constraint")\
\
This trait must be implemented for all types used in immutable [`static` items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items).\
\
[\[lang-types.termination\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.termination "lang-types.termination")\
\
## [`Termination`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#termination)\
\
The [`Termination`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/process/trait.Termination.html) trait indicates the acceptable return types for the [main function](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#main-functions) and [test functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-test-attribute).\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits "lang-types.auto-traits")\
\
## [Auto traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#auto-traits)\
\
The [`Send`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Send.html), [`Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Sync.html), [`Unpin`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Unpin.html), [`UnwindSafe`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/panic/unwind_safe/trait.UnwindSafe.html), and [`RefUnwindSafe`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/panic/unwind_safe/trait.RefUnwindSafe.html) traits are _auto_\
_traits_. Auto traits have special properties.\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits.auto-impl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits.auto-impl "lang-types.auto-traits.auto-impl")\
\
If no explicit implementation or negative implementation is written out for an\
auto trait for a given type, then the compiler implements it automatically\
according to the following rules:\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits.builtin-composite\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits.builtin-composite "lang-types.auto-traits.builtin-composite")\
\
- `&T`, `&mut T`, `*const T`, `*mut T`, `[T; n]`, and `[T]` implement the trait\
if `T` does.\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits.fn-item-pointer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits.fn-item-pointer "lang-types.auto-traits.fn-item-pointer")\
\
- Function item types and function pointers automatically implement the trait.\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits.aggregate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits.aggregate "lang-types.auto-traits.aggregate")\
\
- Structs, enums, unions, and tuples implement the trait if all of their fields\
do.\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits.closure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits.closure "lang-types.auto-traits.closure")\
\
- Closures implement the trait if the types of all of their captures do. A\
closure that captures a `T` by shared reference and a `U` by value implements\
any auto traits that both `&T` and `U` do.\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits.generic-impl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits.generic-impl "lang-types.auto-traits.generic-impl")\
\
For generic types (counting the built-in types above as generic over `T`), if a\
generic implementation is available, then the compiler does not automatically\
implement it for types that could use the implementation except that they do not\
meet the requisite trait bounds. For instance, the standard library implements\
`Send` for all `&T` where `T` is `Sync`; this means that the compiler will not\
implement `Send` for `&T` if `T` is `Send` but not `Sync`.\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits.negative\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits.negative "lang-types.auto-traits.negative")\
\
Auto traits can also have negative implementations, shown as `impl !AutoTrait for T` in the standard library documentation, that override the automatic\
implementations. For example `*mut T` has a negative implementation of `Send`,\
and so `*mut T` is not `Send`, even if `T` is. There is currently no stable way\
to specify additional negative implementations; they exist only in the standard\
library.\
\
[\[lang-types.auto-traits.trait-object-marker\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.auto-traits.trait-object-marker "lang-types.auto-traits.trait-object-marker")\
\
Auto traits may be added as an additional bound to any [trait object](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-objects), even\
though normally only one trait is allowed. For instance, `Box<dyn Debug + Send + UnwindSafe>` is a valid type.\
\
[\[lang-types.sized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.sized "lang-types.sized")\
\
## [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#sized-1)\
\
[\[lang-types.sized.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.sized.intro "lang-types.sized.intro")\
\
The [`Sized`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/marker/trait.Sized.html) trait indicates that the size of this type is known at compile-time; that is, it’s not a [dynamically sized type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#dynamically-sized-types).\
\
[\[lang-types.sized.implicit-sized\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.sized.implicit-sized "lang-types.sized.implicit-sized")\
\
[Type parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-parameters) (except `Self` in traits) are `Sized` by default, as are [associated types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types).\
\
[\[lang-types.sized.implicit-impl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.sized.implicit-impl "lang-types.sized.implicit-impl")\
\
`Sized` is always implemented automatically by the compiler, not by [implementation items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations).\
\
[\[lang-types.sized.relaxation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-lang-types.sized.relaxation "lang-types.sized.relaxation")\
\
These implicit `Sized` bounds may be relaxed by using the special `?Sized` bound.\
\
[\[names\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names "names")\
\
# [Names](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#names)\
\
[\[names.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.intro "names.intro")\
\
An _entity_ is a language construct that can be referred to in some way within the source program, usually via a [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths). Entities include [types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#types), [items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items), [generic parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters), [variable bindings](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns), [loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels), [lifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lifetimes-and-loop-labels), [fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-access-expressions), [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1), and [lints](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes).\
\
A _declaration_ is a syntactical construct that can introduce a _name_ to refer to an entity. Entity names are valid within a [_scope_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scopes) — a region of source text where that name may be referenced.\
\
Some entities are [explicitly declared](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#explicitly-declared-entities) in the source code, and some are [implicitly declared](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implicitly-declared-entities) as part of the language or compiler extensions.\
\
[_Paths_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) are used to refer to an entity, possibly in another module or type.\
\
Lifetimes and loop labels use a [dedicated syntax](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lifetimes-and-loop-labels) using a leading quote.\
\
Names are segregated into different [_namespaces_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces), allowing entities in different namespaces to share the same name without conflict.\
\
[_Name resolution_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#name-resolution) is the compile-time process of tying paths, identifiers, and labels to entity declarations.\
\
Access to certain names may be restricted based on their [_visibility_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#visibility-and-privacy).\
\
[\[names.explicit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit "names.explicit")\
\
## [Explicitly declared entities](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#explicitly-declared-entities)\
\
[\[names.explicit.list\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.list "names.explicit.list")\
\
Entities that explicitly introduce a name in the source code are:\
\
[\[names.explicit.item-decl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.item-decl "names.explicit.item-decl")\
\
- [Items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items):\
\
  - [Module declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules)\
  - [External crate declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-crate-declarations)\
  - [Use declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations)\
  - [Function declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions) and [function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-parameters)\
  - [Type aliases](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-aliases)\
  - [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions), [enum](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations), enum variant declarations, and their named fields\
  - [Constant item declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items)\
  - [Static item declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items)\
  - [Trait item declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) and their [associated items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-items)\
  - [External block items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#external-blocks)\
  - [`macro_rules` declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example) and [matcher metavariables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#metavariables)\
  - [Implementation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations) associated items\
\
[\[names.explicit.expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.expr "names.explicit.expr")\
\
- [Expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expressions):\
\
  - [Closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-expressions) parameters\
  - [`while let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#while-let-patterns) pattern bindings\
  - [`for`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#iterator-loops) pattern bindings\
  - [`if let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-let-patterns) pattern bindings\
  - [`match`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions) pattern bindings\
  - [Loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels)\
\
[\[names.explicit.generics\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.generics "names.explicit.generics")\
\
- [Generic parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters)\
\
[\[names.explicit.higher-ranked-bounds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.higher-ranked-bounds "names.explicit.higher-ranked-bounds")\
\
- [Higher ranked trait bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#higher-ranked-trait-bounds)\
\
[\[names.explicit.binding\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.binding "names.explicit.binding")\
\
- [`let` statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#let-statements) pattern bindings\
\
[\[names.explicit.macro\_use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.macro_use "names.explicit.macro_use")\
\
- The [`macro_use` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_use-attribute) can introduce macro names from another crate\
\
[\[names.explicit.macro\_export\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.macro_export "names.explicit.macro_export")\
\
- The [`macro_export` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_export-attribute) can introduce an alias for the macro into the crate root\
\
[\[names.explicit.macro-invocation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.explicit.macro-invocation "names.explicit.macro-invocation")\
\
Additionally, [macro invocations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro-invocation) and [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1) can introduce names by expanding to one of the above items.\
\
[\[names.implicit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit "names.implicit")\
\
## [Implicitly declared entities](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#implicitly-declared-entities)\
\
[\[names.implicit.list\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.list "names.implicit.list")\
\
The following entities are implicitly defined by the language, or are introduced by compiler options and extensions:\
\
[\[names.implicit.primitive-types\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.primitive-types "names.implicit.primitive-types")\
\
- [Language prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#language-prelude):\
\
  - [Boolean type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type) — `bool`\
  - Textual types — [`char`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#character-type) and [`str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-slice-type)\
  - [Integer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#integer-types) — `i8`, `i16`, `i32`, `i64`, `i128`, `u8`, `u16`, `u32`, `u64`, `u128`\
  - [Machine-dependent integer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#machine-dependent-integer-types) — `usize` and `isize`\
  - [floating-point types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#floating-point-types) — `f32` and `f64`\
\
[\[names.implicit.builtin-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.builtin-attributes "names.implicit.builtin-attributes")\
\
- [Built-in attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#built-in-attributes-index)\
\
[\[names.implicit.prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.prelude "names.implicit.prelude")\
\
- [Standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#standard-library-prelude) items, attributes, and macros\
\
[\[names.implicit.stdlib\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.stdlib "names.implicit.stdlib")\
\
- [Standard library](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude) crates in the root module\
\
[\[names.implicit.extern-prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.extern-prelude "names.implicit.extern-prelude")\
\
- [External crates](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude) linked by the compiler\
\
[\[names.implicit.tool-attributes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.tool-attributes "names.implicit.tool-attributes")\
\
- [Tool attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-attributes)\
\
[\[names.implicit.lints\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.lints "names.implicit.lints")\
\
- [Lints](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lint-check-attributes) and [tool lint attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-lint-attributes)\
\
[\[names.implicit.derive-helpers\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.derive-helpers "names.implicit.derive-helpers")\
\
- [Derive helper attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive-macro-helper-attributes) are valid within an item without being explicitly imported\
\
[\[names.implicit.lifetime-static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.lifetime-static "names.implicit.lifetime-static")\
\
- The [`'static`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#weak-keywords) lifetime\
\
[\[names.implicit.root\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.implicit.root "names.implicit.root")\
\
Additionally, the crate root module does not have a name, but can be referred to with certain [path qualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#path-qualifiers) or aliases.\
\
[\[names.namespaces\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces "names.namespaces")\
\
# [Namespaces](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#namespaces)\
\
[\[names.namespaces.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.intro "names.namespaces.intro")\
\
A _namespace_ is a logical grouping of declared [names](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#names). Names are segregated into separate namespaces based on the kind of entity the name refers to. Namespaces allow the occurrence of a name in one namespace to not conflict with the same name in another namespace.\
\
There are several different namespaces that each contain different kinds of entities. The usage of a name will look for the declaration of that name in different namespaces, based on the context, as described in the [name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#name-resolution) chapter.\
\
[\[names.namespaces.kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.kinds "names.namespaces.kinds")\
\
The following is a list of namespaces, with their corresponding entities:\
\
- Type Namespace\
  - [Module declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules)\
  - [External crate declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-crate-declarations)\
  - [External crate prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude) items\
  - [Struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions), [enum](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations), enum variant declarations\
  - [Trait item declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits)\
  - [Type aliases](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#type-aliases)\
  - [Associated type declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-types)\
  - Built-in types: [boolean](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type), [numeric](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#numeric-types), [`char`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#character-type), and [`str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-slice-type)\
  - [Generic type parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters)\
  - [`Self` type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#self-1)\
  - [Tool attribute modules](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-attributes)\
- Value Namespace\
  - [Function declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#functions)\
  - [Constant item declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items)\
  - [Static item declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items)\
  - [Struct constructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs)\
  - [Enum variant constructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations)\
  - [`Self` constructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#self-1)\
  - [Generic const parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#const-generics)\
  - [Associated const declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-constants)\
  - [Associated function declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-functions-and-methods)\
  - Local bindings — [`let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#let-statements), [`if let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-let-patterns), [`while let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#while-let-patterns), [`for`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#iterator-loops), [`match`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions) arms, [function parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-parameters), [closure parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-expressions)\
  - Captured [closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-expressions) variables\
- Macro Namespace\
  - [`macro_rules` declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example)\
  - [Built-in attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#built-in-attributes-index)\
  - [Tool attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-attributes)\
  - [Function-like procedural macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-proc_macro-attribute)\
  - [Derive macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive)\
  - [Derive macro helpers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive-macro-helper-attributes)\
  - [Attribute macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-proc_macro_attribute-attribute)\
- Lifetime Namespace\
  - [Generic lifetime parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameters)\
- Label Namespace\
  - [Loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels)\
  - [Block labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.loop.block-labels)\
\
An example of how overlapping names in different namespaces can be used unambiguously:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Foo introduces a type in the type namespace and a constructor in the value\
// namespace.\
struct Foo(u32);\
\
// The `Foo` macro is declared in the macro namespace.\
macro_rules! Foo {\
    () => {};\
}\
\
// `Foo` in the `f` parameter type refers to `Foo` in the type namespace.\
// `'Foo` introduces a new lifetime in the lifetime namespace.\
fn example<'Foo>(f: Foo) {\
    // `Foo` refers to the `Foo` constructor in the value namespace.\
    let ctor = Foo;\
    // `Foo` refers to the `Foo` macro in the macro namespace.\
    Foo!{}\
    // `'Foo` introduces a label in the label namespace.\
    'Foo: loop {\
        // `'Foo` refers to the `'Foo` lifetime parameter, and `Foo`\
        // refers to the type namespace.\
        let x: &'Foo Foo;\
        // `'Foo` refers to the label.\
        break 'Foo;\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.namespaces.without\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.without "names.namespaces.without")\
\
## [Named entities without a namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#named-entities-without-a-namespace)\
\
The following entities have explicit names, but the names are not a part of any specific namespace.\
\
### [Fields](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#fields)\
\
[\[names.namespaces.without.fields\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.without.fields "names.namespaces.without.fields")\
\
Even though struct, enum, and union fields are named, the named fields do not live in an explicit namespace. They can only be accessed via a [field expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#field-access-expressions), which only inspects the field names of the specific type being accessed.\
\
### [Use declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#use-declarations-1)\
\
[\[names.namespaces.without.use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.without.use "names.namespaces.without.use")\
\
A [use declaration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations) has named aliases that it imports into scope, but the `use` item itself does not belong to a specific namespace. Instead, it can introduce aliases into multiple namespaces, depending on the item kind being imported.\
\
[\[names.namespaces.sub-namespaces\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.sub-namespaces "names.namespaces.sub-namespaces")\
\
## [Sub-namespaces](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#sub-namespaces)\
\
[\[names.namespaces.sub-namespaces.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.sub-namespaces.intro "names.namespaces.sub-namespaces.intro")\
\
The macro namespace is split into two sub-namespaces: one for [bang-style macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros) and one for [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1). When an attribute is resolved, any bang-style macros in scope will be ignored. And conversely resolving a bang-style macro will ignore attribute macros in scope. This prevents one style from shadowing another.\
\
For example, the [`cfg` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg-attribute) and the [`cfg` macro](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-cfg-macro) are two different entities with the same name in the macro namespace, but they can still be used in their respective context.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `use` imports still cannot create duplicate bindings of the same name in a module or block, regardless of sub-namespace.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #[macro_export]\
> macro_rules! mymac {\
>     () => {};\
> }\
>\
> use myattr::mymac; // error[E0252]: the name `mymac` is defined multiple times.\
> ```\
\
[\[names.scopes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes "names.scopes")\
\
# [Scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#scopes)\
\
[\[names.scopes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.intro "names.scopes.intro")\
\
A _scope_ is the region of source text where a named [entity](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#names) may be referenced with that name. The following sections provide details on the scoping rules and behavior, which depend on the kind of entity and where it is declared. The process of how names are resolved to entities is described in the [name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#name-resolution) chapter. More information on “drop scopes” used for the purpose of running destructors may be found in the [destructors](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#destructors) chapter.\
\
[\[names.scopes.items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.items "names.scopes.items")\
\
## [Item scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#item-scopes)\
\
[\[names.scopes.items.module\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.items.module "names.scopes.items.module")\
\
The name of an [item](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items) declared directly in a [module](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#modules) has a scope that extends from the start of the module to the end of the module. These items are also members of the module and can be referred to with a [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) leading from their module.\
\
[\[names.scopes.items.statement\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.items.statement "names.scopes.items.statement")\
\
The name of an item declared as a [statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#statements) has a scope that extends from the start of the block the item statement is in until the end of the block.\
\
[\[names.scopes.items.duplicate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.items.duplicate "names.scopes.items.duplicate")\
\
It is an error to introduce an item with a duplicate name of another item in the same [namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) within the same module or block. [Asterisk glob imports](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations) have special behavior for dealing with duplicate names and shadowing, see the linked chapter for more details.\
\
[\[names.scopes.items.shadow-prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.items.shadow-prelude "names.scopes.items.shadow-prelude")\
\
Items in a module may shadow items in a [prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#prelude-scopes).\
\
[\[names.scopes.items.nested-modules\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.items.nested-modules "names.scopes.items.nested-modules")\
\
Item names from outer modules are not in scope within a nested module. A [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) may be used to refer to an item in another module.\
\
[\[names.scopes.associated-items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.associated-items "names.scopes.associated-items")\
\
### [Associated item scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#associated-item-scopes)\
\
[\[names.scopes.associated-items.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.associated-items.scope "names.scopes.associated-items.scope")\
\
[Associated items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-items) are not scoped and can only be referred to by using a [path](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#paths) leading from the type or trait they are associated with. [Methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#methods) can also be referred to via [call expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#call-expressions).\
\
[\[names.scopes.associated-items.duplicate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.associated-items.duplicate "names.scopes.associated-items.duplicate")\
\
Similar to items within a module or block, it is an error to introduce an item within a trait or implementation that is a duplicate of another item in the trait or impl in the same namespace.\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings "names.scopes.pattern-bindings")\
\
## [Pattern binding scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pattern-binding-scopes)\
\
The scope of a local variable [pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns) binding depends on where it is used:\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.let "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.let")\
\
- [`let` statement](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#let-statements) bindings range from just after the `let` statement until the end of the block where it is declared.\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.parameter\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.parameter "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.parameter")\
\
- [Function parameter](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#function-parameters) bindings are within the body of the function.\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.closure\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.closure "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.closure")\
\
- [Closure parameter](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-expressions) bindings are within the closure body.\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.loop\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.loop "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.loop")\
\
- [`for`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#iterator-loops) bindings are within the loop body.\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.let-chains\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.let-chains "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.let-chains")\
\
- [`if let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-let-patterns) and [`while let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#while-let-patterns) bindings are valid in the following conditions as well as the consequent block.\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.match-arm\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.match-arm "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.match-arm")\
\
- [`match` arms](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-expressions) bindings are within the [match guard](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#match-guards) and the match arm expression.\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.match-guard-let\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.match-guard-let "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.match-guard-let")\
\
- [`match` guard `let`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-expr.match.guard.let) bindings are valid in the following guard conditions and the match arm expression.\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.items "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.items")\
\
Local variable scopes do not extend into item declarations.\
\
### [Pattern binding shadowing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pattern-binding-shadowing)\
\
[\[names.scopes.pattern-bindings.shadow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.pattern-bindings.shadow "names.scopes.pattern-bindings.shadow")\
\
Pattern bindings are allowed to shadow any name in scope with the following exceptions which are an error:\
\
- [Const generic parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#const-generics)\
- [Static items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items)\
- [Const items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items)\
- Constructors for [structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs) and [enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations)\
\
The following example illustrates how local bindings can shadow item declarations:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn shadow_example() {\
    // Since there are no local variables in scope yet, this resolves to the function.\
    foo(); // prints `function`\
    let foo = || println!("closure");\
    fn foo() { println!("function"); }\
    // This resolves to the local closure since it shadows the item.\
    foo(); // prints `closure`\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.scopes.generic-parameters\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.generic-parameters "names.scopes.generic-parameters")\
\
## [Generic parameter scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#generic-parameter-scopes)\
\
[\[names.scopes.generic-parameters.param-list\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.generic-parameters.param-list "names.scopes.generic-parameters.param-list")\
\
Generic parameters are declared in a [GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams) list. The scope of a generic parameter is within the item it is declared on.\
\
[\[names.scopes.generic-parameters.order-independent\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.generic-parameters.order-independent "names.scopes.generic-parameters.order-independent")\
\
All parameters are in scope within the generic parameter list regardless of the order they are declared. The following shows some examples where a parameter may be referenced before it is declared:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// The 'b bound is referenced before it is declared.\
fn params_scope<'a: 'b, 'b>() {}\
\
trait SomeTrait<const Z: usize> {}\
// The const N is referenced in the trait bound before it is declared.\
fn f<T: SomeTrait<N>, const N: usize>() {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.scopes.generic-parameters.bounds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.generic-parameters.bounds "names.scopes.generic-parameters.bounds")\
\
Generic parameters are also in scope for type bounds and where clauses, for example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait SomeTrait<'a, T> {}\
// The <'a, U> for `SomeTrait` refer to the 'a and U parameters of `bounds_scope`.\
fn bounds_scope<'a, T: SomeTrait<'a, U>, U>() {}\
\
fn where_scope<'a, T, U>()\
    where T: SomeTrait<'a, U>\
{}\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.scopes.generic-parameters.inner-items\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.generic-parameters.inner-items "names.scopes.generic-parameters.inner-items")\
\
It is an error for [items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items) declared inside a function to refer to a generic parameter from their outer scope.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn example<T>() {\
    fn inner(x: T) {} // ERROR: can't use generic parameters from outer function\
}\
}\
```\
\
### [Generic parameter shadowing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#generic-parameter-shadowing)\
\
[\[names.scopes.generic-parameters.shadow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.generic-parameters.shadow "names.scopes.generic-parameters.shadow")\
\
It is an error to shadow a generic parameter with the exception that items declared within functions are allowed to shadow generic parameter names from the function.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn example<'a, T, const N: usize>() {\
    // Items within functions are allowed to shadow generic parameter in scope.\
    fn inner_lifetime<'a>() {} // OK\
    fn inner_type<T>() {} // OK\
    fn inner_const<const N: usize>() {} // OK\
}\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait SomeTrait<'a, T, const N: usize> {\
    fn example_lifetime<'a>() {} // ERROR: 'a is already in use\
    fn example_type<T>() {} // ERROR: T is already in use\
    fn example_const<const N: usize>() {} // ERROR: N is already in use\
    fn example_mixed<const T: usize>() {} // ERROR: T is already in use\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.scopes.lifetimes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.lifetimes "names.scopes.lifetimes")\
\
### [Lifetime scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#lifetime-scopes)\
\
Lifetime parameters are declared in a [GenericParams](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericParams) list and [higher-ranked trait bounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#higher-ranked-trait-bounds).\
\
[\[names.scopes.lifetimes.special\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.lifetimes.special "names.scopes.lifetimes.special")\
\
The `'static` lifetime and [placeholder lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#lifetime-elision)`'_` have a special meaning and cannot be declared as a parameter.\
\
#### [Lifetime generic parameter scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#lifetime-generic-parameter-scopes)\
\
[\[names.scopes.lifetimes.generic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.lifetimes.generic "names.scopes.lifetimes.generic")\
\
[Constant](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#constant-items) and [static](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#static-items) items and [const contexts](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#const-context) only ever allow `'static` lifetime references, so no other lifetime may be in scope within them. [Associated consts](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-constants) do allow referring to lifetimes declared in their trait or implementation.\
\
#### [Higher-ranked trait bound scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#higher-ranked-trait-bound-scopes)\
\
[\[names.scopes.lifetimes.higher-ranked\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.lifetimes.higher-ranked "names.scopes.lifetimes.higher-ranked")\
\
The scope of a lifetime parameter declared as a [higher-ranked trait bound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#higher-ranked-trait-bounds) depends on the scenario where it is used.\
\
- As a [TypeBoundWhereClauseItem](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeBoundWhereClauseItem) the declared lifetimes are in scope in the type and the type bounds.\
- As a [TraitBound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TraitBound) the declared lifetimes are in scope within the bound type path.\
- As a [BareFunctionType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BareFunctionType) the declared lifetimes are in scope within the function parameters and return type.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Trait<'a>{}\
\
fn where_clause<T>()\
    // 'a is in scope in both the type and the type bounds.\
    where for <'a> &'a T: Trait<'a>\
{}\
\
fn bound<T>()\
    // 'a is in scope within the bound.\
    where T: for <'a> Trait<'a>\
{}\
\
struct Example<'a> {\
    field: &'a u32\
}\
\
// 'a is in scope in both the parameters and return type.\
type FnExample = for<'a> fn(x: Example<'a>) -> Example<'a>;\
}\
```\
\
#### [Impl trait restrictions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#impl-trait-restrictions)\
\
[\[names.scopes.lifetimes.impl-trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.lifetimes.impl-trait "names.scopes.lifetimes.impl-trait")\
\
[Impl trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#impl-trait) types can only reference lifetimes declared on a function or implementation.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait Trait1 {\
    type Item;\
}\
trait Trait2<'a> {}\
\
struct Example;\
\
impl Trait1 for Example {\
    type Item = Element;\
}\
\
struct Element;\
impl<'a> Trait2<'a> for Element {}\
\
// The `impl Trait2` here is not allowed to refer to 'b but it is allowed to\
// refer to 'a.\
fn foo<'a>() -> impl for<'b> Trait1<Item = impl Trait2<'a> + use<'a>> {\
    // ...\
   Example\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.scopes.loop-label\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.loop-label "names.scopes.loop-label")\
\
## [Loop label scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#loop-label-scopes)\
\
[\[names.scopes.loop-label.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.loop-label.scope "names.scopes.loop-label.scope")\
\
[Loop labels](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loop-labels) may be declared by a [loop expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#loops-and-other-breakable-expressions). The scope of a loop label is from the point it is declared till the end of the loop expression. The scope does not extend into [items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items), [closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#closure-expressions), [async blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#async-blocks), [const arguments](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#const-generics), [const contexts](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#const-context), and the iterator expression of the defining [`for` loop](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#iterator-loops).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
'a: for n in 0..3 {\
    if n % 2 == 0 {\
        break 'a;\
    }\
    fn inner() {\
        // Using 'a here would be an error.\
        // break 'a;\
    }\
}\
\
// The label is in scope for the expression of `while` loops.\
'a: while break 'a {}         // Loop does not run.\
'a: while let _ = break 'a {} // Loop does not run.\
\
// The label is not in scope in the defining `for` loop:\
'a: for outer in 0..5 {\
    // This will break the outer loop, skipping the inner loop and stopping\
    // the outer loop.\
    'a: for inner in { break 'a; 0..1 } {\
        println!("{}", inner); // This does not run.\
    }\
    println!("{}", outer); // This does not run, either.\
}\
\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.scopes.loop-label.shadow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.loop-label.shadow "names.scopes.loop-label.shadow")\
\
Loop labels may shadow labels of the same name in outer scopes. References to a label refer to the closest definition.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Loop label shadowing example.\
'a: for outer in 0..5 {\
    'a: for inner in 0..5 {\
        // This terminates the inner loop, but the outer loop continues to run.\
        break 'a;\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.scopes.prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.prelude "names.scopes.prelude")\
\
## [Prelude scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#prelude-scopes)\
\
[\[names.scopes.prelude.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.prelude.intro "names.scopes.prelude.intro")\
\
[Preludes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#preludes) bring entities into scope of every module. The entities are not members of the module, but are implicitly queried during [name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#name-resolution).\
\
[\[names.scopes.prelude.shadow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.prelude.shadow "names.scopes.prelude.shadow")\
\
The prelude names may be shadowed by declarations in a module.\
\
[\[names.scopes.prelude.layers\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.prelude.layers "names.scopes.prelude.layers")\
\
The preludes are layered such that one shadows another if they contain entities of the same name. The order that preludes may shadow other preludes is the following where earlier entries may shadow later ones:\
\
1. [Extern prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude)\
2. [Tool prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-prelude)\
3. [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro_use-prelude)\
4. [Standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#standard-library-prelude)\
5. [Language prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#language-prelude)\
\
[\[names.scopes.macro\_rules\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.macro_rules "names.scopes.macro_rules")\
\
## [`macro_rules` scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#macro_rules-scopes)\
\
The scope of `macro_rules` macros is described in the [Macros By Example](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example) chapter. The behavior depends on the use of the [`macro_use`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_use-attribute) and [`macro_export`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_export-attribute) attributes.\
\
[\[names.scopes.derive\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.derive "names.scopes.derive")\
\
## [Derive macro helper attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#derive-macro-helper-attributes-1)\
\
[\[names.scopes.derive.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.derive.scope "names.scopes.derive.scope")\
\
[Derive macro helper attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive-macro-helper-attributes) are in scope in the item where their corresponding [`derive` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#derive) is specified. The scope extends from just after the `derive` attribute to the end of the item.\
\
[\[names.scopes.derive.shadow\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.derive.shadow "names.scopes.derive.shadow")\
\
Helper attributes shadow other attributes of the same name in scope.\
\
[\[names.scopes.self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.self "names.scopes.self")\
\
## [`Self` scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#self-scope)\
\
[\[names.scopes.self.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.self.intro "names.scopes.self.intro")\
\
Although [`Self`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#self-1) is a keyword with special meaning, it interacts with name resolution in a way similar to normal names.\
\
[\[names.scopes.self.def-scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.self.def-scope "names.scopes.self.def-scope")\
\
The implicit `Self` type in the definition of a [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), [enum](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations), [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions), [trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits), or [implementation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations) is treated similarly to a [generic parameter](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#generic-parameter-scopes), and is in scope in the same way as a generic type parameter.\
\
[\[names.scopes.self.impl-scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.scopes.self.impl-scope "names.scopes.self.impl-scope")\
\
The implicit `Self` constructor in the value [namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces) of an [implementation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations) is in scope within the body of the implementation (the implementation’s [associated items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#associated-items)).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Self type within struct definition.\
struct Recursive {\
    f1: Option<Box<Self>>\
}\
\
// Self type within generic parameters.\
struct SelfGeneric<T: Into<Self>>(T);\
\
// Self value constructor within an implementation.\
struct ImplExample();\
impl ImplExample {\
    fn example() -> Self { // Self type\
        Self() // Self value constructor\
    }\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.preludes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes "names.preludes")\
\
# [Preludes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#preludes)\
\
[\[names.preludes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.intro "names.preludes.intro")\
\
A _prelude_ is a collection of names that are automatically brought into scope of every module in a crate.\
\
These prelude names are not part of the module itself: they are implicitly queried during [name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#name-resolution). For example, even though something like [`Box`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/alloc/boxed/struct.Box.html) is in scope in every module, you cannot refer to it as `self::Box` because it is not a member of the current module.\
\
[\[names.preludes.kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.kinds "names.preludes.kinds")\
\
There are several different preludes:\
\
- [Standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std)\
- [Extern prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude)\
- [Language prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#language-prelude)\
- [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro_use-prelude)\
- [Tool prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-prelude)\
\
[\[names.preludes.std\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std "names.preludes.std")\
\
## [Standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#standard-library-prelude)\
\
[\[names.preludes.std.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std.intro "names.preludes.std.intro")\
\
Each crate has a standard library prelude, which consists of the names from a single standard library module.\
\
[\[names.preludes.std.module\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std.module "names.preludes.std.module")\
\
The module used depends on the crate’s edition, and on whether the [`no_std` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_std-attribute) is applied to the crate:\
\
| Edition | `no_std` not applied | `no_std` applied |\
| --- | --- | --- |\
| 2015 | [`std::prelude::rust_2015`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/prelude/rust_2015/index.html) | [`core::prelude::rust_2015`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/prelude/rust_2015/index.html) |\
| 2018 | [`std::prelude::rust_2018`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/prelude/rust_2018/index.html) | [`core::prelude::rust_2018`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/prelude/rust_2018/index.html) |\
| 2021 | [`std::prelude::rust_2021`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/prelude/rust_2021/index.html) | [`core::prelude::rust_2021`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/prelude/rust_2021/index.html) |\
| 2024 | [`std::prelude::rust_2024`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/prelude/rust_2024/index.html) | [`core::prelude::rust_2024`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/prelude/rust_2024/index.html) |\
\
> Note\
>\
> [`std::prelude::rust_2015`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/prelude/rust_2015/index.html) and [`std::prelude::rust_2018`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/prelude/rust_2018/index.html) have the same contents as [`std::prelude::v1`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/prelude/v1/index.html).\
>\
> [`core::prelude::rust_2015`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/prelude/rust_2015/index.html) and [`core::prelude::rust_2018`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/prelude/rust_2018/index.html) have the same contents as [`core::prelude::v1`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/prelude/v1/index.html).\
\
> Note\
>\
> When one of [`core::panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.panic.html) or [`std::panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.panic.html) is brought into scope due to the [standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std), and a user-written [glob import](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob) brings the other into scope, `rustc` currently allows use of `panic!`, even though it is ambiguous. The user-written glob import takes precedence to resolve this ambiguity.\
>\
> For details, see [names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.panic-hack](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.panic-hack).\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern "names.preludes.extern")\
\
## [Extern prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#extern-prelude)\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.intro "names.preludes.extern.intro")\
\
External crates imported with [`extern crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-crate-declarations) in the root module or provided to the compiler (as with the `--extern` flag with `rustc`) are added to the _extern prelude_. If imported with an alias such as `extern crate orig_name as new_name`, then the symbol `new_name` is instead added to the prelude.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.core\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.core "names.preludes.extern.core")\
\
The [`core`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/index.html) crate is always added to the extern prelude.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.std\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.std "names.preludes.extern.std")\
\
The [`std`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/index.html) crate is added as long as the [`no_std` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_std-attribute) is not specified in the crate root.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.edition2018 "names.preludes.extern.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, crates in the extern prelude cannot be referenced via [use declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations), so it is generally standard practice to include `extern crate` declarations to bring them into scope.\
>\
> Beginning in the 2018 edition, [use declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations) can reference crates in the extern prelude, so it is considered unidiomatic to use `extern crate`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Additional crates that ship with `rustc`, such as [`alloc`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/alloc/index.html), and [`test`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/test/index.html), are not automatically included with the `--extern` flag when using Cargo. They must be brought into scope with an `extern crate` declaration, even in the 2018 edition.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> extern crate alloc;\
> use alloc::rc::Rc;\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> Cargo does bring in `proc_macro` to the extern prelude for proc-macro crates only.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.no\_std\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.no_std "names.preludes.extern.no_std")\
\
### [The `no_std` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-no_std-attribute)\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.no\_std.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.no_std.intro "names.preludes.extern.no_std.intro")\
\
The _`no_std` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes)_ causes the [`std`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/index.html) crate to not be linked automatically and the [standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std) to instead use the `core` prelude.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![no_std]\
> ```\
\
> Note\
>\
> Using `no_std` is useful when either the crate is targeting a platform that does not support the standard library or is purposefully not using the capabilities of the standard library. Those capabilities are mainly dynamic memory allocation (e.g. `Box` and `Vec`) and file and network capabilities (e.g. `std::fs` and `std::io`).\
\
> Warning\
>\
> Using `no_std` does not prevent the standard library from being linked. It is still valid to write `extern crate std` in the crate or in one of its dependencies; this will cause the compiler to link the `std` crate into the program.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.no\_std.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.no_std.syntax "names.preludes.extern.no_std.syntax")\
\
The `no_std` attribute uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) syntax.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.no\_std.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.no_std.allowed-positions "names.preludes.extern.no_std.allowed-positions")\
\
The `no_std` attribute may only be applied to the crate root.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.no\_std.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.no_std.duplicates "names.preludes.extern.no_std.duplicates")\
\
The `no_std` attribute may be used any number of times on a form.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` lints against any use following the first.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.no\_std.module\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.no_std.module "names.preludes.extern.no_std.module")\
\
The `no_std` attribute changes the [standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std) to use the `core` prelude instead of the `std` prelude.\
\
[\[names.preludes.extern.no\_std.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.extern.no_std.edition2018 "names.preludes.extern.no_std.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> Before the 2018 edition, `std` is injected into the crate root by default. If `no_std` is specified, `core` is injected instead. Starting with the 2018 edition, regardless of `no_std` being specified, neither is injected into the crate root.\
\
[\[names.preludes.lang\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.lang "names.preludes.lang")\
\
## [Language prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#language-prelude)\
\
[\[names.preludes.lang.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.lang.intro "names.preludes.lang.intro")\
\
The language prelude includes names of types and attributes that are built-in to the language. The language prelude is always in scope.\
\
[\[names.preludes.lang.entities\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.lang.entities "names.preludes.lang.entities")\
\
It includes the following:\
\
- [Type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces)\
  - [Boolean type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#boolean-type) — `bool`\
  - [`char`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#character-type)\
  - [`str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#string-slice-type)\
  - [Integer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#integer-types) — `i8`, `i16`, `i32`, `i64`, `i128`, `u8`, `u16`, `u32`, `u64`, `u128`\
  - [Machine-dependent integer types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#machine-dependent-integer-types) — `usize` and `isize`\
  - [floating-point types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#floating-point-types) — `f32` and `f64`\
- [Macro namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces)\
  - [Built-in attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#built-in-attributes-index)\
  - [Built-in derive macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-attributes.derive.built-in)\
\
[\[names.preludes.macro\_use\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.macro_use "names.preludes.macro_use")\
\
## [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#macro_use-prelude)\
\
[\[names.preludes.macro\_use.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.macro_use.intro "names.preludes.macro_use.intro")\
\
The `macro_use` prelude includes macros from external crates that were imported by the [`macro_use` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-macro_use-attribute) applied to an [`extern crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-crate-declarations).\
\
[\[names.preludes.tool\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.tool "names.preludes.tool")\
\
## [Tool prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#tool-prelude)\
\
[\[names.preludes.tool.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.tool.intro "names.preludes.tool.intro")\
\
The tool prelude includes tool names for external tools in the [type namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces). See the [tool attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-attributes) section for more details.\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude")\
\
## [The `no_implicit_prelude` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-no_implicit_prelude-attribute)\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.intro "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.intro")\
\
The _`no_implicit_prelude` [attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1)_ is used to prevent implicit preludes from being brought into scope.\
\
> Example\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> // The attribute can be applied to the crate root to affect\
> // all modules.\
> #![no_implicit_prelude]\
>\
> // Or it can be applied to a module to only affect that module\
> // and its descendants.\
> #[no_implicit_prelude]\
> mod example {\
>     // ...\
> }\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.syntax "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.syntax")\
\
The `no_implicit_prelude` attribute uses the [MetaWord](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-MetaWord) syntax.\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.allowed-positions "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.allowed-positions")\
\
The `no_implicit_prelude` attribute may only be applied to the crate or to a module.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` ignores use in other positions but lints against it. This may become an error in the future.\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude.duplicates\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.duplicates "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.duplicates")\
\
The `no_implicit_prelude` attribute may be used any number of times on a form.\
\
> Note\
>\
> `rustc` lints against any use following the first.\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude.excluded-preludes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.excluded-preludes "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.excluded-preludes")\
\
The `no_implicit_prelude` attribute prevents the [standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std), [extern prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude), [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro_use-prelude), and the [tool prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#tool-prelude) from being brought into scope for the module and its descendants.\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude.implicitly-imported-macros\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.implicitly-imported-macros "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.implicitly-imported-macros")\
\
> Note\
>\
> Despite `#![no_implicit_prelude]`, `rustc` currently brings certain macros implicitly into scope. Those macros are:\
>\
> - [`assert!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.assert.html)\
> - [`cfg!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.cfg.html)\
> - [`cfg_select!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macros/macro.cfg_select.html)\
> - [`column!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.column.html)\
> - [`compile_error!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.compile_error.html)\
> - [`concat!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.concat.html)\
> - [`concat_bytes!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.concat_bytes.html)\
> - [`env!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.env.html)\
> - [`file!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.file.html)\
> - [`format_args!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.format_args.html)\
> - [`include!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.include.html)\
> - [`include_bytes!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.include_bytes.html)\
> - [`include_str!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.include_str.html)\
> - [`line!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.line.html)\
> - [`module_path!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.module_path.html)\
> - [`option_env!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.option_env.html)\
> - [`panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.panic.html)\
> - [`stringify!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.stringify.html)\
> - [`unreachable!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.unreachable.html)\
>\
> E.g., this works:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![no_implicit_prelude]\
> fn main() { assert!(true); }\
> ```\
>\
> Don’t rely on this behavior; it may be removed in the future. Always bring the items you need into scope explicitly when using `#![no_implicit_prelude]`.\
>\
> For details, see [Rust PR #62086](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/62086) and [Rust PR #139493](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/139493).\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude.lang\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.lang "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.lang")\
\
The `no_implicit_prelude` attribute does not affect the [language prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#language-prelude).\
\
[\[names.preludes.no\_implicit\_prelude.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.edition2018 "names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 edition, the `no_implicit_prelude` attribute does not affect the [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro_use-prelude), and all macros exported from the standard library are still included in the `macro_use` prelude. Starting in the 2018 edition, the attribute does remove the `macro_use` prelude.\
\
[\[paths\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths "paths")\
\
# [Paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#paths)\
\
[\[paths.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.intro "paths.intro")\
\
A _path_ is a sequence of one or more path segments separated by `::` tokens. Paths are used to refer to [items](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#items), values, [types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#types), [macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros), and [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1).\
\
Two examples of simple paths consisting of only identifier segments:\
\
```rust\
\
x;\
x::y::z;\
```\
\
## [Types of paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#types-of-paths)\
\
[\[paths.simple\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.simple "paths.simple")\
\
### [Simple paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#simple-paths)\
\
[\[paths.simple.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.simple.syntax "paths.simple.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath) →\
\
::?[SimplePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePathSegment) ( ::[SimplePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePathSegment) )\*\
\
[SimplePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePathSegment) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| super \| self \| crate \| $crate\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath)\
::[SimplePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePathSegment)\
::[SimplePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePathSegment)\
\
[SimplePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePathSegment)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
super\
self\
crate\
$crate\
\
[\[paths.simple.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.simple.intro "paths.simple.intro")\
\
Simple paths are used in [visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#visibility-and-privacy) markers, [attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#attributes-1), [macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example), and [`use`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations) items. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
use std::io::{self, Write};\
mod m {\
    #[clippy::cyclomatic_complexity = "0"]\
    pub (in super) fn f1() {}\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[paths.expr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.expr "paths.expr")\
\
### [Paths in expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#paths-in-expressions)\
\
[\[paths.expr.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.expr.syntax "paths.expr.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathInExpression) →\
\
::?[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathExprSegment) ( ::[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathExprSegment) )\*\
\
[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathExprSegment) →\
\
[PathIdentSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathIdentSegment) ( ::[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgs) )?\
\
[PathIdentSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathIdentSegment) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER) \| super \| self \| Self \| crate \| $crate\
\
[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgs) →\
\
<>\
\
