    \| < ( [GenericArg](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArg), )\*[GenericArg](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArg),?>\
\
[GenericArg](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArg) →\
\
[Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Lifetime) \| [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) \| [GenericArgsConst](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgsConst) \| [GenericArgsBinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgsBinding) \| [GenericArgsBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgsBounds)\
\
[GenericArgsConst](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgsConst) →\
\
[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-BlockExpression)\
\
    \| [LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LiteralExpression)\
\
    \| -[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-LiteralExpression)\
\
    \| [SimplePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePathSegment)\
\
[GenericArgsBinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgsBinding) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgs)?=[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type)\
\
[GenericArgsBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgsBounds) →\
\
[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-IDENTIFIER)[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgs)?:[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeParamBounds)\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathInExpression)\
::[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathExprSegment)\
::[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathExprSegment)\
\
[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathExprSegment)[PathIdentSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathIdentSegment)\
::[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgs)\
\
[PathIdentSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathIdentSegment)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)\
super\
self\
Self\
crate\
$crate\
\
[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgs)\
<\
>\
<[GenericArg](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArg)\
,[GenericArg](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArg)\
,\
>\
\
[GenericArg](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArg)[Lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Lifetime)[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type) [GenericArgsConst](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgsConst) [GenericArgsBinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgsBinding) [GenericArgsBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgsBounds)\
\
[GenericArgsConst](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgsConst)[BlockExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-BlockExpression)[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LiteralExpression)\
-[LiteralExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-LiteralExpression)[SimplePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePathSegment)\
\
[GenericArgsBinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgsBinding)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgs)\
=[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
\
[GenericArgsBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgsBounds)[IDENTIFIER](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-IDENTIFIER)[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgs)\
:[TypeParamBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeParamBounds)\
\
[\[paths.expr.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.expr.intro "paths.expr.intro")\
\
Paths in expressions allow for paths with generic arguments to be specified. They are used in various places in [expressions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#expressions) and [patterns](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#patterns).\
\
[\[paths.expr.turbofish\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.expr.turbofish "paths.expr.turbofish")\
\
The `::` token is required before the opening `<` for generic arguments to avoid ambiguity with the less-than operator. This is colloquially known as “turbofish” syntax.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
(0..10).collect::<Vec<_>>();\
Vec::<u8>::with_capacity(1024);\
}\
```\
\
[\[paths.expr.argument-order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.expr.argument-order "paths.expr.argument-order")\
\
The order of generic arguments is restricted to lifetime arguments, then type arguments, then const arguments, then equality constraints.\
\
[\[paths.expr.complex-const-params\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.expr.complex-const-params "paths.expr.complex-const-params")\
\
Const arguments must be surrounded by braces unless they are a [literal](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#literal-expressions), an [inferred const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred), or a single segment path. An [inferred const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred) may not be surrounded by braces.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod m {\
    pub const C: usize = 1;\
}\
const C: usize = m::C;\
fn f<const N: usize>() -> [u8; N] { [0; N] }\
\
let _ = f::<1>(); // Literal.\
let _: [_; 1] = f::<_>(); // Inferred const.\
let _: [_; 1] = f::<(((_)))>(); // Inferred const.\
let _ = f::<C>(); // Single segment path.\
let _ = f::<{ m::C }>(); // Multi-segment path must be braced.\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn f<const N: usize>() -> [u8; N] { [0; _] }\
let _: [_; 1] = f::<{ _ }>();\
//                    ^ ERROR `_` not allowed here\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> In a generic argument list, an [inferred const](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.inferred) is parsed as an [inferred type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-InferredType) but then semantically treated as a separate kind of [const generic argument](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.generics.const.argument).\
\
[\[paths.expr.impl-trait-params\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.expr.impl-trait-params "paths.expr.impl-trait-params")\
\
The synthetic type parameters corresponding to `impl Trait` types are implicit, and these cannot be explicitly specified.\
\
[\[paths.qualified\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualified "paths.qualified")\
\
## [Qualified paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#qualified-paths)\
\
[\[paths.qualified.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualified.syntax "paths.qualified.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[QualifiedPathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QualifiedPathInExpression) → [QualifiedPathType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QualifiedPathType) ( ::[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathExprSegment) )+\
\
[QualifiedPathType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QualifiedPathType) → <[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) ( as[TypePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePath) )?>\
\
[QualifiedPathInType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QualifiedPathInType) → [QualifiedPathType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QualifiedPathType) ( ::[TypePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePathSegment) )+\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[QualifiedPathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QualifiedPathInExpression)[QualifiedPathType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QualifiedPathType)\
::[PathExprSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathExprSegment)\
\
[QualifiedPathType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QualifiedPathType)\
<[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
as[TypePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePath)\
>\
\
[QualifiedPathInType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-QualifiedPathInType)[QualifiedPathType](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-QualifiedPathType)\
::[TypePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePathSegment)\
\
[\[paths.qualified.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualified.intro "paths.qualified.intro")\
\
Fully qualified paths allow for disambiguating the path for [trait implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-implementations) and for specifying [canonical paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#canonical-paths). When used in a type specification, it supports using the type syntax specified below.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
struct S;\
impl S {\
    fn f() { println!("S"); }\
}\
trait T1 {\
    fn f() { println!("T1 f"); }\
}\
impl T1 for S {}\
trait T2 {\
    fn f() { println!("T2 f"); }\
}\
impl T2 for S {}\
S::f();  // Calls the inherent impl.\
<S as T1>::f();  // Calls the T1 trait function.\
<S as T2>::f();  // Calls the T2 trait function.\
}\
```\
\
[\[paths.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.type "paths.type")\
\
### [Paths in types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#paths-in-types)\
\
[\[paths.type.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.type.syntax "paths.type.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[TypePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePath) → ::?[TypePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePathSegment) ( ::[TypePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePathSegment) )\*\
\
[TypePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePathSegment) → [PathIdentSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathIdentSegment) ( ::? ( [GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-GenericArgs) \| [TypePathFn](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePathFn) ) )?\
\
[TypePathFn](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePathFn) → ([TypePathFnInputs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePathFnInputs)?) ( ->[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypeNoBounds) )?\
\
[TypePathFnInputs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePathFnInputs) → [Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) ( ,[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Type) )\*,?\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[TypePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePath)\
::[TypePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePathSegment)\
::[TypePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePathSegment)\
\
[TypePathSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePathSegment)[PathIdentSegment](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-PathIdentSegment)\
::[GenericArgs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-GenericArgs) [TypePathFn](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePathFn)\
\
[TypePathFn](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePathFn)\
([TypePathFnInputs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypePathFnInputs)\
)\
->[TypeNoBounds](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-TypeNoBounds)\
\
[TypePathFnInputs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-TypePathFnInputs)[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
,[Type](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Type)\
,\
\
[\[paths.type.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.type.intro "paths.type.intro")\
\
Type paths are used within type definitions, trait bounds, type parameter bounds, and qualified paths.\
\
[\[paths.type.turbofish\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.type.turbofish "paths.type.turbofish")\
\
Although the `::` token is allowed before the generics arguments, it is not required because there is no ambiguity like there is in [PathInExpression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-PathInExpression).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod ops {\
    pub struct Range<T> {f1: T}\
    pub trait Index<T> {}\
    pub struct Example<'a> {f1: &'a i32}\
}\
struct S;\
impl ops::Index<ops::Range<usize>> for S { /*...*/ }\
fn i<'a>() -> impl Iterator<Item = ops::Example<'a>> {\
    // ...\
   const EXAMPLE: Vec<ops::Example<'static>> = Vec::new();\
   EXAMPLE.into_iter()\
}\
type G = std::boxed::Box<dyn std::ops::FnOnce(isize) -> isize>;\
}\
```\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers "paths.qualifiers")\
\
## [Path qualifiers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#path-qualifiers)\
\
Paths can be denoted with various leading qualifiers to change the meaning of how it is resolved.\
\
> Note\
>\
> [`use` declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations) have additional behaviors and restrictions for `self`, `super`, `crate`, and `$crate`.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.global-root\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.global-root "paths.qualifiers.global-root")\
\
### [`::`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#)\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.global-root.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.global-root.intro "paths.qualifiers.global-root.intro")\
\
Paths starting with `::` are considered to be _global paths_ where the segments of the path start being resolved from a place which differs based on edition. Each identifier in the path must resolve to an item.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.global-root.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.global-root.edition2018 "paths.qualifiers.global-root.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> In the 2015 Edition, identifiers resolve from the “crate root” (`crate::` in the 2018 edition), which contains a variety of different items, including external crates, default crates such as `std` or `core`, and items in the top level of the crate (including `use` imports).\
>\
> Beginning with the 2018 Edition, paths starting with `::` resolve from crates in the [extern prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#extern-prelude). That is, they must be followed by the name of a crate.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
pub fn foo() {\
    // In the 2018 edition, this accesses `std` via the extern prelude.\
    // In the 2015 edition, this accesses `std` via the crate root.\
    let now = ::std::time::Instant::now();\
    println!("{:?}", now);\
}\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
// 2015 Edition\
mod a {\
    pub fn foo() {}\
}\
mod b {\
    pub fn foo() {\
        ::a::foo(); // call `a`'s foo function\
        // In Rust 2018, `::a` would be interpreted as the crate `a`.\
    }\
}\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.mod-self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.mod-self "paths.qualifiers.mod-self")\
\
### [`self`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#self)\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.mod-self.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.mod-self.intro "paths.qualifiers.mod-self.intro")\
\
`self` resolves the path relative to the current module.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.mod-self.restriction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.mod-self.restriction "paths.qualifiers.mod-self.restriction")\
\
`self` can only be used as the first segment, without a preceding `::`.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.self-pat\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.self-pat "paths.qualifiers.self-pat")\
\
In a method body, a path which consists of a single `self` segment resolves to the method’s self parameter.\
\
```rust\
\
fn foo() {}\
fn bar() {\
    self::foo();\
}\
struct S(bool);\
impl S {\
  fn baz(self) {\
        self.0;\
    }\
}\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.type-self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.type-self "paths.qualifiers.type-self")\
\
### [`Self`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#self-1)\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.type-self.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.type-self.intro "paths.qualifiers.type-self.intro")\
\
`Self`, with a capital “S”, is used to refer to the current type being implemented or defined. It may be used in the following situations:\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.type-self.trait\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.type-self.trait "paths.qualifiers.type-self.trait")\
\
- In a [trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#traits) definition, it refers to the type implementing the trait.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.type-self.impl\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.type-self.impl "paths.qualifiers.type-self.impl")\
\
- In an [implementation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations), it refers to the type being implemented. When implementing a tuple or unit [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), it also refers to the constructor in the [value namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces).\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.type-self.type\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.type-self.type "paths.qualifiers.type-self.type")\
\
- In the definition of a [struct](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#structs), [enumeration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#enumerations), or [union](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unions), it refers to the type being defined. The definition is not allowed to be infinitely recursive (there must be an indirection).\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.type-self.scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.type-self.scope "paths.qualifiers.type-self.scope")\
\
The scope of `Self` behaves similarly to a generic parameter; see the [`Self` scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#self-scope) section for more details.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.type-self.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.type-self.allowed-positions "paths.qualifiers.type-self.allowed-positions")\
\
`Self` can only be used as the first segment, without a preceding `::`.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.type-self.no-generics\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.type-self.no-generics "paths.qualifiers.type-self.no-generics")\
\
The `Self` path cannot include generic arguments (as in `Self::<i32>`).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
trait T {\
    type Item;\
    const C: i32;\
    // `Self` will be whatever type that implements `T`.\
    fn new() -> Self;\
    // `Self::Item` will be the type alias in the implementation.\
    fn f(&self) -> Self::Item;\
}\
struct S;\
impl T for S {\
    type Item = i32;\
    const C: i32 = 9;\
    fn new() -> Self {           // `Self` is the type `S`.\
        S\
    }\
    fn f(&self) -> Self::Item {  // `Self::Item` is the type `i32`.\
        Self::C                  // `Self::C` is the constant value `9`.\
    }\
}\
\
// `Self` is in scope within the generics of a trait definition,\
// to refer to the type being defined.\
trait Add<Rhs = Self> {\
    type Output;\
    // `Self` can also reference associated items of the\
    // type being implemented.\
    fn add(self, rhs: Rhs) -> Self::Output;\
}\
\
struct NonEmptyList<T> {\
    head: T,\
    // A struct can reference itself (as long as it is not\
    // infinitely recursive).\
    tail: Option<Box<Self>>,\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.super\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.super "paths.qualifiers.super")\
\
### [`super`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#super)\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.super.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.super.intro "paths.qualifiers.super.intro")\
\
`super` in a path resolves to the parent module.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.super.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.super.allowed-positions "paths.qualifiers.super.allowed-positions")\
\
It may only be used in leading segments of the path, possibly after an initial `self` segment.\
\
```rust\
\
mod a {\
    pub fn foo() {}\
}\
mod b {\
    pub fn foo() {\
        super::a::foo(); // call a's foo function\
    }\
}\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.super.repetition\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.super.repetition "paths.qualifiers.super.repetition")\
\
`super` may be repeated several times after the first `super` or `self` to refer to ancestor modules.\
\
```rust\
\
mod a {\
    fn foo() {}\
\
    mod b {\
        mod c {\
            fn foo() {\
                super::super::foo(); // call a's foo function\
                self::super::super::foo(); // call a's foo function\
            }\
        }\
    }\
}\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.crate "paths.qualifiers.crate")\
\
### [`crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#crate)\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.crate.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.crate.intro "paths.qualifiers.crate.intro")\
\
`crate` resolves the path relative to the current crate.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.crate.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.crate.allowed-positions "paths.qualifiers.crate.allowed-positions")\
\
`crate` can only be used as the first segment, without a preceding `::`.\
\
```rust\
\
fn foo() {}\
mod a {\
    fn bar() {\
        crate::foo();\
    }\
}\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.macro-crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.macro-crate "paths.qualifiers.macro-crate")\
\
### [`$crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#crate-1)\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.macro-crate.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.macro-crate.allowed-positions "paths.qualifiers.macro-crate.allowed-positions")\
\
[`$crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.crate) is only used within [macro transcribers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macros-by-example), and can only be used as the first segment, without a preceding `::`.\
\
[\[paths.qualifiers.macro-crate.hygiene\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.qualifiers.macro-crate.hygiene "paths.qualifiers.macro-crate.hygiene")\
\
[`$crate`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.hygiene.crate) will expand to a path to access items from the top level of the crate where the macro is defined, regardless of which crate the macro is invoked.\
\
```rust\
\
pub fn increment(x: u32) -> u32 {\
    x + 1\
}\
\
#[macro_export]\
macro_rules! inc {\
    ($x:expr) => ( $crate::increment($x) )\
}\
fn main() { }\
```\
\
[\[paths.canonical\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical "paths.canonical")\
\
## [Canonical paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#canonical-paths)\
\
[\[paths.canonical.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical.intro "paths.canonical.intro")\
\
Each item defined in a module or implementation has a _canonical path_ that corresponds to where within its crate it is defined.\
\
[\[paths.canonical.alias\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical.alias "paths.canonical.alias")\
\
All other paths to these items are aliases.\
\
[\[paths.canonical.def\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical.def "paths.canonical.def")\
\
The canonical path is defined as a _path prefix_ appended by the path segment the item itself defines.\
\
[\[paths.canonical.non-canonical\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical.non-canonical "paths.canonical.non-canonical")\
\
[Implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#implementations) and [use declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations) do not have canonical paths, although the items that implementations define do have them. Items defined in block expressions do not have canonical paths. Items defined in a module that does not have a canonical path do not have a canonical path. Associated items defined in an implementation that refers to an item without a canonical path, e.g. as the implementing type, the trait being implemented, a type parameter or bound on a type parameter, do not have canonical paths.\
\
[\[paths.canonical.module-prefix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical.module-prefix "paths.canonical.module-prefix")\
\
The path prefix for modules is the canonical path to that module.\
\
[\[paths.canonical.bare-impl-prefix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical.bare-impl-prefix "paths.canonical.bare-impl-prefix")\
\
For bare implementations, it is the canonical path of the item being implemented surrounded by angle (`<>`) brackets.\
\
[\[paths.canonical.trait-impl-prefix\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical.trait-impl-prefix "paths.canonical.trait-impl-prefix")\
\
For [trait implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#trait-implementations), it is the canonical path of the item being implemented followed by `as` followed by the canonical path to the trait all surrounded in angle (`<>`) brackets.\
\
[\[paths.canonical.local-canonical-path\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-paths.canonical.local-canonical-path "paths.canonical.local-canonical-path")\
\
The canonical path is only meaningful within a given crate. There is no global namespace across crates; an item’s canonical path merely identifies it within the crate.\
\
```rust\
\
// Comments show the canonical path of the item.\
\
mod a { // crate::a\
    pub struct Struct; // crate::a::Struct\
\
    pub trait Trait { // crate::a::Trait\
        fn f(&self); // crate::a::Trait::f\
    }\
\
    impl Trait for Struct {\
        fn f(&self) {} // <crate::a::Struct as crate::a::Trait>::f\
    }\
\
    impl Struct {\
        fn g(&self) {} // <crate::a::Struct>::g\
    }\
}\
\
mod without { // crate::without\
    fn canonicals() { // crate::without::canonicals\
        struct OtherStruct; // None\
\
        trait OtherTrait { // None\
            fn g(&self); // None\
        }\
\
        impl OtherTrait for OtherStruct {\
            fn g(&self) {} // None\
        }\
\
        impl OtherTrait for crate::a::Struct {\
            fn g(&self) {} // None\
        }\
\
        impl crate::a::Trait for OtherStruct {\
            fn f(&self) {} // None\
        }\
    }\
}\
\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
[\[names.resolution\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution "names.resolution")\
\
# [Name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#name-resolution)\
\
[\[names.resolution.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.intro "names.resolution.intro")\
\
_Name resolution_ is the process of tying paths and other identifiers to the declarations of those entities. Names are segregated into different [namespaces](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#namespaces), allowing entities in different namespaces to share the same name without conflict. Each name is valid within a [scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#scopes), or a region of source text where that name may be referenced. Access to a name may be restricted based on its [visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#visibility-and-privacy).\
\
Name resolution is split into three stages throughout the compilation process. The first stage, _expansion-time resolution_, resolves all [`use` declarations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#use-declarations) and [macro invocations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#macro-invocation). The second stage, _primary resolution_, resolves all names that have not yet been resolved and that do not depend on type information to resolve. The last stage, _type-relative resolution_, resolves the remaining names once type information is available.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Expansion-time resolution is also known as _early resolution_. Primary resolution is also known as _late resolution_.\
\
[\[names.resolution.general\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.general "names.resolution.general")\
\
## [General](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#general)\
\
[\[names.resolution.general.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.general.intro "names.resolution.general.intro")\
\
The rules within this section apply to all stages of name resolution.\
\
[\[names.resolution.general.scopes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.general.scopes "names.resolution.general.scopes")\
\
### [Scopes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#scopes-1)\
\
[\[names.resolution.general.scopes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.general.scopes.intro "names.resolution.general.scopes.intro")\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is a placeholder for future expansion about resolution of names within various scopes.\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion "names.resolution.expansion")\
\
## [Expansion-time name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#expansion-time-name-resolution)\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.intro "names.resolution.expansion.intro")\
\
Expansion-time name resolution is the stage of name resolution necessary to complete macro expansion and fully generate a crate’s [AST](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-glossary.ast). This stage requires the resolution of macro invocations and `use` declarations. Resolving `use` declarations is required for macro invocations that resolve via [path-based scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.name-resolution). Resolving macro invocations is required in order to expand them.\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.unresolved-invocations\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.unresolved-invocations "names.resolution.expansion.unresolved-invocations")\
\
After expansion-time name resolution, the AST must not contain any unexpanded macro invocations. Every macro invocation resolves to a valid definition that exists in the final AST or in an external crate.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
m!(); // ERROR: Cannot find macro `m` in this scope.\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.expansion-order-stability\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.expansion-order-stability "names.resolution.expansion.expansion-order-stability")\
\
The resolution of names must be stable. After expansion, names in the fully expanded AST must resolve to the same definition regardless of the order in which macros are expanded and imports are resolved.\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.speculation\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.speculation "names.resolution.expansion.speculation")\
\
All name resolution candidates selected during macro expansion are considered speculative. Once the crate has been fully expanded, all speculative import resolutions are validated to ensure that macro expansion did not introduce any new ambiguities.\
\
> Note\
>\
> Due to the iterative nature of macro expansion, this causes so-called time traveling ambiguities, such as when a macro or glob import introduces an item that is ambiguous with its own base path.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> fn main() {}\
> macro_rules! f {\
>     () => {\
>         mod m {\
>             pub(crate) use f;\
>         }\
>     }\
> }\
> f!();\
>\
> const _: () = {\
>     // Initially, we speculatively resolve `m` to the module in\
>     // the crate root.\
>     //\
>     // Expansion of `f` introduces a second `m` module inside this\
>     // body.\
>     //\
>     // Expansion-time resolution finalizes resolutions by re-\
>     // resolving all imports and macro invocations, sees the\
>     // introduced ambiguity and reports it as an error.\
>     m::f!(); // ERROR: `m` is ambiguous.\
> };\
> ```\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports "names.resolution.expansion.imports")\
\
### [Imports](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#imports)\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.intro "names.resolution.expansion.imports.intro")\
\
All `use` declarations are fully resolved during this stage of resolution. [Type-relative paths](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.type-relative) cannot be resolved at this stage and will produce an error.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod m {\
    pub const C: () = ();\
    pub enum E { V }\
    pub type A = E;\
    impl E {\
        pub const C: () = ();\
    }\
}\
\
// Valid imports resolved at expansion-time:\
use m::C; // OK.\
use m::E; // OK.\
use m::A; // OK.\
use m::E::V; // OK.\
\
// Valid expressions resolved during type-relative resolution:\
let _ = m::A::V; // OK.\
let _ = m::E::C; // OK.\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod m {\
    pub const C: () = ();\
    pub enum E { V }\
    pub type A = E;\
    impl E {\
        pub const C: () = ();\
    }\
}\
// Invalid type-relative imports that can't resolve at expansion-time:\
use m::A::V; // ERROR: Unresolved import `m::A::V`.\
use m::E::C; // ERROR: Unresolved import `m::E::C`.\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.shadowing\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.shadowing "names.resolution.expansion.imports.shadowing")\
\
Names introduced via `use` declarations in an [outer scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.general.scopes) are shadowed by candidates in the same namespace with the same name from an inner scope except where otherwise restricted by [name resolution ambiguities](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
pub mod m1 {\
    pub mod ambig {\
        pub const C: u8 = 1;\
    }\
}\
\
pub mod m2 {\
    pub mod ambig {\
        pub const C: u8 = 2;\
    }\
}\
\
// This introduces the name `ambig` in the outer scope.\
use m1::ambig;\
const _: () = {\
    // This shadows `ambig` in the inner scope.\
    use m2::ambig;\
    // The inner candidate is selected here\
    // as the resolution of `ambig`.\
    use ambig::C;\
    assert!(C == 2);\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.shadowing.shared-scope\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.shadowing.shared-scope "names.resolution.expansion.imports.shadowing.shared-scope")\
\
Shadowing of names introduced via `use` declarations within a single scope is permitted in the following situations:\
\
- [`use` glob shadowing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob.shadowing)\
- [Macro textual scope shadowing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.textual.shadow)\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity "names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity")\
\
#### [Ambiguities](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#ambiguities)\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.intro "names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.intro")\
\
There are certain situations during expansion-time resolution where there are multiple macro definitions, `use` declarations, or modules an import or macro invocation’s name could refer to where the compiler cannot consistently determine which candidate should shadow the other. Shadowing cannot be permitted in these situations and the compiler instead emits ambiguity errors.\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.glob-vs-glob\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.glob-vs-glob "names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.glob-vs-glob")\
\
Names may not be resolved through ambiguous glob imports. Glob imports are allowed to import conflicting names in the same namespace as long as the name is not used. Names with conflicting candidates from ambiguous glob imports may still be shadowed by non-glob imports and used without producing an error. The errors occur at time of use, not time of import.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod m1 {\
    pub struct Ambig;\
}\
\
mod m2 {\
    pub struct Ambig;\
}\
\
// OK: This brings conficting names in the same namespace into scope\
// but they have not been used yet.\
use m1::*;\
use m2::*;\
\
const _: () = {\
    // The error happens when the name with the conflicting candidates\
    // is used.\
    let x = Ambig; // ERROR: `Ambig` is ambiguous.\
};\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod m1 {\
    pub struct Ambig;\
}\
\
mod m2 {\
    pub struct Ambig;\
}\
\
use m1::*;\
use m2::*; // OK: No name conflict.\
const _: () = {\
    // This is permitted, since resolution is not through the\
    // ambiguous globs.\
    struct Ambig;\
    let x = Ambig; // OK.\
};\
}\
```\
\
Multiple glob imports are allowed to import the same name, and that name is allowed to be used if the imports are of the same item (following reexports). The visibility of the name is the maximum visibility of the imports.\
\
```rust\
\
mod m1 {\
    pub struct Ambig;\
}\
\
mod m2 {\
    // This reexports the same `Ambig` item from a second module.\
    pub use super::m1::Ambig;\
}\
\
mod m3 {\
    // These both import the same `Ambig`.\
    //\
    // The visibility of `Ambig` is `pub` because that is the\
    // maximum visibility between these two `use` declarations.\
    pub use super::m1::*;\
    use super::m2::*;\
}\
\
mod m4 {\
    // `Ambig` can be used through the `m3` globs and still has\
    // `pub` visibility.\
    pub use crate::m3::Ambig;\
}\
\
const _: () = {\
    // Therefore, we can use it here.\
    let _ = m4::Ambig; // OK.\
};\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.glob-vs-outer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.glob-vs-outer "names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.glob-vs-outer")\
\
Names in imports and macro invocations may not be resolved through glob imports when there is another candidate available in an [outer scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.general.scopes).\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.panic-hack\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.panic-hack "names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.panic-hack")\
\
> Note\
>\
> When one of [`core::panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.panic.html) or [`std::panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.panic.html) is brought into scope due to the [standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std), and a user-written [glob import](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-items.use.glob) brings the other into scope, `rustc` currently allows use of `panic!`, even though it is ambiguous. The user-written glob import takes precedence to resolve this ambiguity.\
>\
> In Rust 2021 and later, [`core::panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/macro.panic.html) and [`std::panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.panic.html) operate identically. But in earlier editions, they differ; only [`std::panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.panic.html) accepts a [`String`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/alloc/string/struct.String.html) as the format argument.\
>\
> E.g., this is an error:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> extern crate core;\
> use ::core::prelude::v1::*;\
> fn main() {\
>     panic!(std::string::String::new()); // ERROR.\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> And this is accepted:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![no_std]\
> extern crate std;\
> use ::std::prelude::v1::*;\
> fn main() {\
>     panic!(std::string::String::new()); // OK.\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> Don’t rely on this behavior; the plan is to remove it.\
>\
> For details, see [Rust issue #147319](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/147319).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
mod glob {\
    pub mod ambig {\
        pub struct Name;\
    }\
}\
\
// Outer `ambig` candidate.\
pub mod ambig {\
    pub struct Name;\
}\
\
const _: () = {\
    // Cannot resolve `ambig` through this glob\
    // because of the outer `ambig` candidate above.\
    use glob::*;\
    use ambig::Name; // ERROR: `ambig` is ambiguous.\
};\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// As above, but with macros.\
pub mod m {\
    macro_rules! f {\
        () => {};\
    }\
    pub(crate) use f;\
}\
pub mod glob {\
    macro_rules! f {\
        () => {};\
    }\
    pub(crate) use f as ambig;\
}\
\
use m::f as ambig;\
\
const _: () = {\
    use glob::*;\
    ambig!(); // ERROR: `ambig` is ambiguous.\
};\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> These ambiguity errors are specific to expansion-time resolution. Having multiple candidates available for a given name during later stages of resolution is not considered an error. So long as none of the imports themselves are ambiguous, there will always be a single unambiguous closest resolution.\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> #![allow(unused)]\
> fn main() {\
> mod glob {\
>     pub const AMBIG: u8 = 1;\
> }\
>\
> mod outer {\
>     pub const AMBIG: u8 = 2;\
> }\
>\
> use outer::AMBIG;\
>\
> const C: () = {\
>     use glob::*;\
>     assert!(AMBIG == 1);\
>     //      ^---- This `AMBIG` is resolved during primary resolution.\
> };\
> }\
> ```\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.path-vs-textual-macro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.path-vs-textual-macro "names.resolution.expansion.imports.ambiguity.path-vs-textual-macro")\
\
Names may not be resolved through ambiguous macro reexports. Macro reexports are ambiguous when they would shadow a textual macro candidate for the same name in an [outer scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.general.scopes).\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
// Textual macro candidate.\
macro_rules! ambig {\
    () => {}\
}\
\
// Path-based macro candidate.\
macro_rules! path_based {\
    () => {}\
}\
\
pub fn f() {\
    // This reexport of the `path_based` macro definition\
    // as `ambig` may not shadow the `ambig` macro definition\
    // which is resolved via textual macro scope.\
    use path_based as ambig;\
    ambig!(); // ERROR: `ambig` is ambiguous.\
}\
}\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> This restriction is needed due to implementation details in the compiler, specifically the current scope visitation logic and the complexity of supporting this behavior. This ambiguity error may be removed in the future.\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.macros\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.macros "names.resolution.expansion.macros")\
\
### [Macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#macros-1)\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.macros.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.macros.intro "names.resolution.expansion.macros.intro")\
\
Macros are resolved by iterating through the available scopes to find the available candidates. Macros are split into two sub-namespaces, one for function-like macros, and the other for attributes and derives. Resolution candidates from the incorrect sub-namespace are ignored.\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.macros.visitation-order\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.macros.visitation-order "names.resolution.expansion.macros.visitation-order")\
\
The available scope kinds are visited in the following order. Each of these scope kinds represent one or more scopes.\
\
- [Derive helpers](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.attributes)\
- [Textual scope macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.decl.scope.textual)\
- [Path-based scope macros](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.invocation.name-resolution)\
- [`macro_use` prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.macro_use)\
- [Standard library prelude](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.std)\
- [Builtin attributes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.lang)\
\
> Note\
>\
> The compiler will attempt to resolve derive helpers that are used before their associated macro introduces them into scope. This scope is visited after the scope for resolving derive helper candidates that are correctly in scope. This behavior is slated for removal.\
>\
> For more info see [derive helper scope](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-macro.proc.derive.attributes.scope).\
\
> Note\
>\
> This visitation order may change in the future, such as interleaving the visitation of textual and path-based scope candidates based on their lexical scopes.\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> Starting in edition 2018 the `#[macro_use]` prelude is not visited when [`#[no_implicit_prelude]`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.preludes.no_implicit_prelude) is present.\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.macros.reserved-names\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.macros.reserved-names "names.resolution.expansion.macros.reserved-names")\
\
The names `cfg` and `cfg_attr` are reserved in the macro attribute [sub-namespace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.namespaces.sub-namespaces).\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity "names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity")\
\
#### [Ambiguities](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#ambiguities-1)\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity.more-expanded-vs-outer\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity.more-expanded-vs-outer "names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity.more-expanded-vs-outer")\
\
Names may not be resolved through ambiguous candidates inside of macro expansions. Candidates inside of macro expansions are ambiguous when they would shadow a candidate for the same name from outside of the first candidate’s macro expansion and the invocation of the name being resolved is also from outside of the first candidate’s macro expansion.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! define_ambig {\
    () => {\
        macro_rules! ambig {\
            () => {}\
        }\
    }\
}\
\
// Introduce outer candidate definition for `ambig` macro invocation.\
macro_rules! ambig {\
    () => {}\
}\
\
// Introduce a second candidate definition for `ambig` inside of a\
// macro expansion.\
define_ambig!();\
\
// The definition of `ambig` from the second invocation\
// of `define_ambig` is the innermost canadidate.\
//\
// The definition of `ambig` from the first invocation of\
// `define_ambig` is the second candidate.\
//\
// The compiler checks that the first candidate is inside of a macro\
// expansion, that the second candidate is not from within the same\
// macro expansion, and that the name being resolved is not from\
// within the same macro expansion.\
ambig!(); // ERROR: `ambig` is ambiguous.\
}\
```\
\
The reverse is not considered ambiguous.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! define_ambig {\
    () => {\
        macro_rules! ambig {\
            () => {}\
        }\
    }\
}\
// Swap order of definitions.\
define_ambig!();\
macro_rules! ambig {\
    () => {}\
}\
// The innermost candidate is now less expanded so it may shadow more\
// the macro expanded definition above it.\
ambig!();\
}\
```\
\
Nor is it ambiguous if the invocation being resolved is within the innermost candidate’s expansion.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! ambig {\
    () => {}\
}\
\
macro_rules! define_and_invoke_ambig {\
    () => {\
        // Define innermost candidate.\
        macro_rules! ambig {\
            () => {}\
        }\
\
        // Invocation of `ambig` is in the same expansion as the\
        // innermost candidate.\
        ambig!(); // OK\
    }\
}\
\
define_and_invoke_ambig!();\
}\
```\
\
It doesn’t matter if both definitions come from invocations of the same macro; the outermost candidate is still considered “less expanded” because it is not within the expansion containing the innermost candidate’s definition.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! define_ambig {\
    () => {\
        macro_rules! ambig {\
            () => {}\
        }\
    }\
}\
define_ambig!();\
define_ambig!();\
ambig!(); // ERROR: `ambig` is ambiguous.\
}\
```\
\
This also applies to imports so long as the innermost candidate for the name is from within a macro expansion.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
macro_rules! define_ambig {\
    () => {\
        mod ambig {\
            pub struct Name;\
        }\
    }\
}\
\
mod ambig {\
    pub struct Name;\
}\
\
const _: () = {\
    // Introduce innermost candidate for\
    // `ambig` mod in this macro expansion.\
    define_ambig!();\
    use ambig::Name; // ERROR: `ambig` is ambiguous.\
};\
}\
```\
\
[\[names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity.built-in-attr\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity.built-in-attr "names.resolution.expansion.macros.ambiguity.built-in-attr")\
\
User-defined attributes or derive macros may not shadow built-in non-macro attributes (e.g. inline).\
\
```rust\
\
// with-helper/src/lib.rs\
use proc_macro::TokenStream;\
#[proc_macro_derive(WithHelperAttr, attributes(non_exhaustive))]\
//                                             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^\
//                                   User-defined attribute candidate.\
// ...\
pub fn derive_with_helper_attr(_item: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {\
    TokenStream::new()\
}\
```\
\
```rust\
\
// src/lib.rs\
#[derive(with_helper::WithHelperAttr)]\
#[non_exhaustive] // ERROR: `non_exhaustive` is ambiguous.\
struct S;\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> This applies regardless of the name the built-in attribute is a candidate for:\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> // with-helper/src/lib.rs\
> use proc_macro::TokenStream;\
>\
> #[proc_macro_derive(WithHelperAttr, attributes(helper))]\
> //                                             ^^^^^^\
> //                                 User-defined attribute candidate.\
> // ...\
> pub fn derive_with_helper_attr(_item: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {\
>     TokenStream::new()\
> }\
> ```\
>\
> ```rust\
>\
> // src/lib.rs\
> use inline as helper;\
> //            ^----- Built-in attribute candidate via reexport.\
>\
> #[derive(with_helper::WithHelperAttr)]\
> #[helper] // ERROR: `helper` is ambiguous.\
> struct S;\
> ```\
\
[\[names.resolution.primary\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.primary "names.resolution.primary")\
\
## [Primary name resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#primary-name-resolution)\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is a placeholder for future expansion about primary name resolution.\
\
[\[names.resolution.type-relative\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-names.resolution.type-relative "names.resolution.type-relative")\
\
## [Type-relative resolution](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#type-relative-resolution)\
\
> Note\
>\
> This is a placeholder for future expansion about type-dependent resolution.\
\
[\[vis\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis "vis")\
\
# [Visibility and privacy](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#visibility-and-privacy)\
\
[\[vis.syntax\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.syntax "vis.syntax")\
\
**Syntax**\
\
[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-Visibility) →\
\
pub\
\
    \| pub(crate)\
\
    \| pub(self)\
\
    \| pub(super)\
\
    \| pub(in[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-SimplePath))\
\
Show syntax diagrams\
\
[Visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#grammar-Visibility)\
pub\
pub\
(\
crate\
)\
pub\
(\
self\
)\
pub\
(\
super\
)\
pub\
(\
in[SimplePath](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#railroad-SimplePath)\
)\
\
[\[vis.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.intro "vis.intro")\
\
These two terms are often used interchangeably, and what they are attempting to convey is the answer to the question “Can this item be used at this location?”\
\
[\[vis.name-hierarchy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.name-hierarchy "vis.name-hierarchy")\
\
Rust’s name resolution operates on a global hierarchy of namespaces. Each level in the hierarchy can be thought of as some item. The items are one of those mentioned above, but also include external crates. Declaring or defining a new module can be thought of as inserting a new tree into the hierarchy at the location of the definition.\
\
[\[vis.privacy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.privacy "vis.privacy")\
\
To control whether interfaces can be used across modules, Rust checks each use of an item to see whether it should be allowed or not. This is where privacy warnings are generated, or otherwise “you used a private item of another module and weren’t allowed to.”\
\
[\[vis.default\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.default "vis.default")\
\
By default, everything is _private_, with two exceptions: Associated items in a `pub` Trait are public by default; Enum variants in a `pub` enum are also public by default. When an item is declared as `pub`, it can be thought of as being accessible to the outside world. For example:\
\
```rust\
\
fn main() {}\
// Declare a private struct\
struct Foo;\
\
// Declare a public struct with a private field\
pub struct Bar {\
    field: i32,\
}\
\
// Declare a public enum with two public variants\
pub enum State {\
    PubliclyAccessibleState,\
    PubliclyAccessibleState2,\
}\
```\
\
[\[vis.access\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.access "vis.access")\
\
With the notion of an item being either public or private, Rust allows item accesses in two cases:\
\
1. If an item is public, then it can be accessed externally from some module `m` if you can access all the item’s ancestor modules from `m`. You can also potentially be able to name the item through re-exports. See below.\
2. If an item is private, it may be accessed by the current module and its descendants.\
\
These two cases are surprisingly powerful for creating module hierarchies exposing public APIs while hiding internal implementation details. To help explain, here’s a few use cases and what they would entail:\
\
- A library developer needs to expose functionality to crates which link against their library. As a consequence of the first case, this means that anything which is usable externally must be `pub` from the root down to the destination item. Any private item in the chain will disallow external accesses.\
\
- A crate needs a global available “helper module” to itself, but it doesn’t want to expose the helper module as a public API. To accomplish this, the root of the crate’s hierarchy would have a private module which then internally has a “public API”. Because the entire crate is a descendant of the root, then the entire local crate can access this private module through the second case.\
\
- When writing unit tests for a module, it’s often a common idiom to have an immediate child of the module to-be-tested named `mod test`. This module could access any items of the parent module through the second case, meaning that internal implementation details could also be seamlessly tested from the child module.\
\
\
In the second case, it mentions that a private item “can be accessed” by the current module and its descendants, but the exact meaning of accessing an item depends on what the item is.\
\
[\[vis.usage\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.usage "vis.usage")\
\
Accessing a module, for example, would mean looking inside of it (to import more items). On the other hand, accessing a function would mean that it is invoked. Additionally, path expressions and import statements are considered to access an item in the sense that the import/expression is only valid if the destination is in the current visibility scope.\
\
Here’s an example of a program which exemplifies the three cases outlined above:\
\
```rust\
\
// This module is private, meaning that no external crate can access this\
// module. Because it is private at the root of this current crate, however, any\
// module in the crate may access any publicly visible item in this module.\
mod crate_helper_module {\
\
    // This function can be used by anything in the current crate\
    pub fn crate_helper() {}\
\
    // This function *cannot* be used by anything else in the crate. It is not\
    // publicly visible outside of the `crate_helper_module`, so only this\
    // current module and its descendants may access it.\
    fn implementation_detail() {}\
}\
\
// This function is "public to the root" meaning that it's available to external\
// crates linking against this one.\
pub fn public_api() {}\
\
// Similarly to 'public_api', this module is public so external crates may look\
// inside of it.\
pub mod submodule {\
    use crate::crate_helper_module;\
\
    pub fn my_method() {\
        // Any item in the local crate may invoke the helper module's public\
        // interface through a combination of the two rules above.\
        crate_helper_module::crate_helper();\
    }\
\
    // This function is hidden to any module which is not a descendant of\
    // `submodule`\
    fn my_implementation() {}\
\
    #[cfg(test)]\
    mod test {\
\
        #[test]\
        fn test_my_implementation() {\
            // Because this module is a descendant of `submodule`, it's allowed\
            // to access private items inside of `submodule` without a privacy\
            // violation.\
            super::my_implementation();\
        }\
    }\
}\
\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
For a Rust program to pass the privacy checking pass, all paths must be valid accesses given the two rules above. This includes all use statements, expressions, types, etc.\
\
[\[vis.scoped\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.scoped "vis.scoped")\
\
## [`pub(in path)`, `pub(crate)`, `pub(super)`, and `pub(self)`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#pubin-path-pubcrate-pubsuper-and-pubself)\
\
[\[vis.scoped.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.scoped.intro "vis.scoped.intro")\
\
In addition to public and private, Rust allows users to declare an item as visible only within a given scope. The rules for `pub` restrictions are as follows:\
\
[\[vis.scoped.in\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.scoped.in "vis.scoped.in")\
\
- `pub(in path)` makes an item visible within the provided `path`. `path` must be a simple path which resolves to an ancestor module of the item whose visibility is being declared. Each identifier in `path` must refer directly to a module (not to a name introduced by a `use` statement).\
\
[\[vis.scoped.crate\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.scoped.crate "vis.scoped.crate")\
\
- `pub(crate)` makes an item visible within the current crate.\
\
[\[vis.scoped.super\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.scoped.super "vis.scoped.super")\
\
- `pub(super)` makes an item visible to the parent module. This is equivalent to `pub(in super)`.\
\
[\[vis.scoped.self\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.scoped.self "vis.scoped.self")\
\
- `pub(self)` makes an item visible to the current module. This is equivalent to `pub(in self)` or not using `pub` at all.\
\
[\[vis.scoped.edition2018\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.scoped.edition2018 "vis.scoped.edition2018")\
\
> 2018 Edition differences\
>\
> Starting with the 2018 edition, paths for `pub(in path)` must start with `crate`, `self`, or `super`. The 2015 edition may also use paths starting with `::` or modules from the crate root.\
\
Here’s an example:\
\
```rust\
\
pub mod outer_mod {\
    pub mod inner_mod {\
        // This function is visible within `outer_mod`\
        pub(in crate::outer_mod) fn outer_mod_visible_fn() {}\
        // Same as above, this is only valid in the 2015 edition.\
        pub(in outer_mod) fn outer_mod_visible_fn_2015() {}\
\
        // This function is visible to the entire crate\
        pub(crate) fn crate_visible_fn() {}\
\
        // This function is visible within `outer_mod`\
        pub(super) fn super_mod_visible_fn() {\
            // This function is visible since we're in the same `mod`\
            inner_mod_visible_fn();\
        }\
\
        // This function is visible only within `inner_mod`,\
        // which is the same as leaving it private.\
        pub(self) fn inner_mod_visible_fn() {}\
    }\
    pub fn foo() {\
        inner_mod::outer_mod_visible_fn();\
        inner_mod::crate_visible_fn();\
        inner_mod::super_mod_visible_fn();\
\
        // This function is no longer visible since we're outside of `inner_mod`\
        // Error! `inner_mod_visible_fn` is private\
        //inner_mod::inner_mod_visible_fn();\
    }\
}\
\
fn bar() {\
    // This function is still visible since we're in the same crate\
    outer_mod::inner_mod::crate_visible_fn();\
\
    // This function is no longer visible since we're outside of `outer_mod`\
    // Error! `super_mod_visible_fn` is private\
    //outer_mod::inner_mod::super_mod_visible_fn();\
\
    // This function is no longer visible since we're outside of `outer_mod`\
    // Error! `outer_mod_visible_fn` is private\
    //outer_mod::inner_mod::outer_mod_visible_fn();\
\
    outer_mod::foo();\
}\
\
fn main() { bar() }\
```\
\
> Note\
>\
> This syntax only adds another restriction to the visibility of an item. It does not guarantee that the item is visible within all parts of the specified scope. To access an item, all of its parent items up to the current scope must still be visible as well.\
\
[\[vis.reexports\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.reexports "vis.reexports")\
\
## [Re-exporting and visibility](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#re-exporting-and-visibility)\
\
[\[vis.reexports.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.reexports.intro "vis.reexports.intro")\
\
Rust allows publicly re-exporting items through a `pub use` directive. Because this is a public directive, this allows the item to be used in the current module through the rules above. It essentially allows public access into the re-exported item. For example, this program is valid:\
\
```rust\
\
pub use self::implementation::api;\
\
mod implementation {\
    pub mod api {\
        pub fn f() {}\
    }\
}\
\
fn main() {}\
```\
\
This means that any external crate referencing `implementation::api::f` would receive a privacy violation, while the path `api::f` would be allowed.\
\
[\[vis.reexports.private-item\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-vis.reexports.private-item "vis.reexports.private-item")\
\
When re-exporting a private item, it can be thought of as allowing the “privacy chain” being short-circuited through the reexport instead of passing through the namespace hierarchy as it normally would.\
\
[\[memory\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-memory "memory")\
\
# [Memory model](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#memory-model)\
\
> Warning\
>\
> The memory model of Rust is incomplete and not fully decided.\
\
[\[memory.bytes\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-memory.bytes "memory.bytes")\
\
## [Bytes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#bytes)\
\
[\[memory.bytes.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-memory.bytes.intro "memory.bytes.intro")\
\
The most basic unit of memory in Rust is a byte.\
\
> Note\
>\
> While bytes are typically lowered to hardware bytes, Rust uses an “abstract” notion of bytes that can make distinctions which are absent in hardware, such as being uninitialized, or storing part of a pointer. Those distinctions can affect whether your program has undefined behavior, so they still have tangible impact on how compiled Rust programs behave.\
\
[\[memory.bytes.contents\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-memory.bytes.contents "memory.bytes.contents")\
\
Each byte may have one of the following values:\
\
[\[memory.bytes.init\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-memory.bytes.init "memory.bytes.init")\
\
- An initialized byte containing a `u8` value and optional [provenance](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/ptr/index.html#provenance),\
\
[\[memory.bytes.uninit\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-memory.bytes.uninit "memory.bytes.uninit")\
\
- An uninitialized byte.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The above list is not yet guaranteed to be exhaustive.\
\
[\[alloc\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-alloc "alloc")\
\
# [Memory allocation and lifetime](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#memory-allocation-and-lifetime)\
\
[\[alloc.static\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-alloc.static "alloc.static")\
\
The _items_ of a program are those functions, modules, and types that have their\
value calculated at compile-time and stored uniquely in the memory image of the\
rust process. Items are neither dynamically allocated nor freed.\
\
[\[alloc.dynamic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-alloc.dynamic "alloc.dynamic")\
\
The _heap_ is a general term that describes boxes. The lifetime of an\
allocation in the heap depends on the lifetime of the box values pointing to\
it. Since box values may themselves be passed in and out of frames, or stored\
in the heap, heap allocations may outlive the frame they are allocated within.\
An allocation in the heap is guaranteed to reside at a single location in the\
heap for the whole lifetime of the allocation - it will never be relocated as\
a result of moving a box value.\
\
[\[variable\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-variable "variable")\
\
# [Variables](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#variables)\
\
[\[variable.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-variable.intro "variable.intro")\
\
A _variable_ is a component of a stack frame, either a named function parameter,\
an anonymous [temporary](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#temporaries), or a named local\
variable.\
\
[\[variable.local\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-variable.local "variable.local")\
\
A _local variable_ (or _stack-local_ allocation) holds a value directly,\
allocated within the stack’s memory. The value is a part of the stack frame.\
\
[\[variable.local-mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-variable.local-mut "variable.local-mut")\
\
Local variables are immutable unless declared otherwise. For example:\
`let mut x = ...`.\
\
[\[variable.param-mut\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-variable.param-mut "variable.param-mut")\
\
Function parameters are immutable unless declared with `mut`. The `mut` keyword\
applies only to the following parameter. For example: `|mut x, y|` and\
`fn f(mut x: Box<i32>, y: Box<i32>)` declare one mutable variable `x` and one\
immutable variable `y`.\
\
[\[variable.init\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-variable.init "variable.init")\
\
Local variables are not initialized when allocated. Instead, the entire frame\
worth of local variables are allocated, on frame-entry, in an uninitialized\
state. Subsequent statements within a function may or may not initialize the\
local variables. Local variables can be used only after they have been\
initialized through all reachable control flow paths.\
\
In this next example, `init_after_if` is initialized after the [`if` expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#if-expressions)\
while `uninit_after_if` is not because it is not initialized in the `else` case.\
\
```rust\
\
#![allow(unused)]\
fn main() {\
fn random_bool() -> bool { true }\
fn initialization_example() {\
    let init_after_if: ();\
    let uninit_after_if: ();\
\
    if random_bool() {\
        init_after_if = ();\
        uninit_after_if = ();\
    } else {\
        init_after_if = ();\
    }\
\
    init_after_if; // ok\
    // uninit_after_if; // err: use of possibly uninitialized `uninit_after_if`\
}\
}\
```\
\
[\[panic\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic "panic")\
\
# [Panic](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#panic-1)\
\
[\[panic.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.intro "panic.intro")\
\
Rust provides a mechanism to prevent a function from returning normally, and instead “panic,” which is a response to an error condition that is typically not expected to be recoverable within the context in which the error is encountered.\
\
[\[panic.lang-ops\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.lang-ops "panic.lang-ops")\
\
Some language constructs, such as out-of-bounds [array indexing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#array-and-slice-indexing-expressions), panic automatically.\
\
[\[panic.control\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.control "panic.control")\
\
There are also language features that provide a level of control over panic behavior:\
\
- A [_panic handler_](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-panic_handler-attribute) defines the behavior of a panic.\
- [FFI ABIs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unwinding) may alter how panics behave.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The standard library provides the capability to explicitly panic via the [`panic!` macro](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.panic.html).\
\
[\[panic.panic\_handler\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.panic_handler "panic.panic_handler")\
\
## [The `panic_handler` attribute](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#the-panic_handler-attribute)\
\
[\[panic.panic\_handler.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.panic_handler.intro "panic.panic_handler.intro")\
\
The _`panic_handler` attribute_ can be applied to a function to define the behavior of panics.\
\
[\[panic.panic\_handler.allowed-positions\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.panic_handler.allowed-positions "panic.panic_handler.allowed-positions")\
\
The `panic_handler` attribute can only be applied to a function with signature `fn(&PanicInfo) -> !`.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The [`PanicInfo`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/core/panic/panic_info/struct.PanicInfo.html) struct contains information about the location of the panic.\
\
[\[panic.panic\_handler.unique\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.panic_handler.unique "panic.panic_handler.unique")\
\
There must be a single `panic_handler` function in the dependency graph.\
\
Below is shown a `panic_handler` function that logs the panic message and then halts the thread.\
\
```rust\
\
#![no_std]\
\
use core::fmt::{self, Write};\
use core::panic::PanicInfo;\
\
struct Sink {\
    // ..\
   _0: (),\
}\
\
impl Sink {\
    fn new() -> Sink { Sink { _0: () }}\
}\
\
impl fmt::Write for Sink {\
    fn write_str(&mut self, _: &str) -> fmt::Result { Ok(()) }\
}\
\
#[panic_handler]\
fn panic(info: &PanicInfo) -> ! {\
    let mut sink = Sink::new();\
\
    // logs "panicked at '$reason', src/main.rs:27:4" to some `sink`\
    let _ = writeln!(sink, "{}", info);\
\
    loop {}\
}\
```\
\
[\[panic.panic\_handler.std\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.panic_handler.std "panic.panic_handler.std")\
\
### [Standard behavior](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#standard-behavior)\
\
[\[panic.panic\_handler.std.kinds\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.panic_handler.std.kinds "panic.panic_handler.std.kinds")\
\
`std` provides two different panic handlers:\
\
- `unwind` — unwinds the stack and is potentially recoverable.\
- `abort` –– aborts the process and is non-recoverable.\
\
Not all targets may provide the `unwind` handler.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The panic handler used when linking with `std` can be set with the [`-C panic`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/codegen-options/index.html#panic) CLI flag. The default for most targets is `unwind`.\
>\
> The standard library’s panic behavior can be modified at runtime with the [`std::panic::set_hook`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/panicking/fn.set_hook.html) function.\
\
[\[panic.panic\_handler.std.no\_std\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.panic_handler.std.no_std "panic.panic_handler.std.no_std")\
\
Linking a [`no_std`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-no_std-attribute) binary, dylib, cdylib, or staticlib will require specifying your own panic handler.\
\
[\[panic.strategy\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.strategy "panic.strategy")\
\
## [Panic strategy](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#panic-strategy)\
\
[\[panic.strategy.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.strategy.intro "panic.strategy.intro")\
\
The _panic strategy_ defines the kind of panic behavior that a crate is built to support.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The panic strategy can be chosen in `rustc` with the [`-C panic`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/codegen-options/index.html#panic) CLI flag.\
>\
> When generating a binary, dylib, cdylib, or staticlib and linking with `std`, the `-C panic` CLI flag also influences which [panic handler](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#the-panic_handler-attribute) is used.\
\
> Note\
>\
> When compiling code with the `abort` panic strategy, the optimizer may assume that unwinding across Rust frames is impossible, which can result in both code-size and runtime speed improvements.\
\
> Note\
>\
> See [link.unwinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-link.unwinding) for restrictions on linking crates with different panic strategies. An implication is that crates built with the `unwind` strategy can use the `abort` panic handler, but the `abort` strategy cannot use the `unwind` panic handler.\
\
[\[panic.unwind\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.unwind "panic.unwind")\
\
## [Unwinding](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unwinding-1)\
\
[\[panic.unwind.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.unwind.intro "panic.unwind.intro")\
\
Panicking may either be recoverable or non-recoverable, though it can be configured (by choosing a non-unwinding panic handler) to always be non-recoverable. (The converse is not true: the `unwind` handler does not guarantee that all panics are recoverable, only that panicking via the `panic!` macro and similar standard library mechanisms is recoverable.)\
\
[\[panic.unwind.destruction\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.unwind.destruction "panic.unwind.destruction")\
\
When a panic occurs, the `unwind` handler “unwinds” Rust frames, just as C++’s `throw` unwinds C++ frames, until the panic reaches the point of recovery (for instance at a thread boundary). This means that as the panic traverses Rust frames, live objects in those frames that [implement `Drop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#destructors) will have their `drop` methods called. Thus, when normal execution resumes, no-longer-accessible objects will have been “cleaned up” just as if they had gone out of scope normally.\
\
> Note\
>\
> As long as this guarantee of resource-cleanup is preserved, “unwinding” may be implemented without actually using the mechanism used by C++ for the target platform.\
\
> Note\
>\
> The standard library provides two mechanisms for recovering from a panic, [`std::panic::catch_unwind`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/panic/fn.catch_unwind.html) (which enables recovery within the panicking thread) and [`std::thread::spawn`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/thread/functions/fn.spawn.html) (which automatically sets up panic recovery for the spawned thread so that other threads may continue running).\
\
[\[panic.unwind.ffi\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.unwind.ffi "panic.unwind.ffi")\
\
### [Unwinding across FFI boundaries](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html\#unwinding-across-ffi-boundaries)\
\
[\[panic.unwind.ffi.intro\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.unwind.ffi.intro "panic.unwind.ffi.intro")\
\
It is possible to unwind across FFI boundaries using an [appropriate ABI declaration](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#unwinding). While useful in certain cases, this creates unique opportunities for undefined behavior, especially when multiple language runtimes are involved.\
\
[\[panic.unwind.ffi.undefined\]](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/print.html#r-panic.unwind.ffi.undefined "panic.unwind.ffi.undefined")\
\
Unwinding with the wrong ABI is undefined behavior:\
\
